Experiments and visualizations demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in mitigating catastrophic forgetting and preserving visual concepts. It achieves state-of-the-art performance and exhibits robust generalizability across diverse CL scenarios.

</details>


### [54] [SOMA-1M: A Large-Scale SAR-Optical Multi-resolution Alignment Dataset for Multi-Task Remote Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05480)
*Peihao Wu,Yongxiang Yao,Yi Wan,Wenfei Zhang,Ruipeng Zhao,Jiayuan Li,Yongjun Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SOMA-1M introduces a large, precisely aligned SAR–Optical dataset (≥1.3M pairs, 512×512 patches) across multiple sensors and resolutions, enabling multi-task benchmarks and foundation-model research for cross-modal remote sensing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmark datasets suffer from single resolution, limited scale, and poor alignment, hindering training and generalization of multi-scale, cross-modal foundation models. A pixel-level, multi-resolution dataset with diverse land-cover scenes addresses these gaps.

Method: Build a coarse-to-fine image-matching framework to achieve pixel-level alignment across Sentinel-1, PIESAT-1, Capella Space, and Google Earth data; produce 512×512 patches spanning 0.5–10 m resolution; annotate 12 land-cover categories; design four hierarchical vision benchmarks (image matching, image fusion, SAR-assisted cloud removal, cross-modal translation) and evaluate 30+ algorithms; release dataset publicly.

Result: Supervised training on SOMA-1M yields significant performance gains across all benchmarks; MRSI matching reaches state-of-the-art levels; demonstrates robustness and generalization potential for multimodal RS tasks and foundation-model development.

Conclusion: SOMA-1M is a foundational resource that enables robust cross-modal RS algorithms and foundation-model research; public release will catalyze advances in multimodal fusion, alignment, and translation tasks.

Abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) and optical imagery provide complementary strengths that constitute the critical foundation for transcending single-modality constraints and facilitating cross-modal collaborative processing and intelligent interpretation. However, existing benchmark datasets often suffer from limitations such as single spatial resolution, insufficient data scale, and low alignment accuracy, making them inadequate for supporting the training and generalization of multi-scale foundation models. To address these challenges, we introduce SOMA-1M (SAR-Optical Multi-resolution Alignment), a pixel-level precisely aligned dataset containing over 1.3 million pairs of georeferenced images with a specification of 512 x 512 pixels. This dataset integrates imagery from Sentinel-1, PIESAT-1, Capella Space, and Google Earth, achieving global multi-scale coverage from 0.5 m to 10 m. It encompasses 12 typical land cover categories, effectively ensuring scene diversity and complexity. To address multimodal projection deformation and massive data registration, we designed a rigorous coarse-to-fine image matching framework ensuring pixel-level alignment. Based on this dataset, we established comprehensive evaluation benchmarks for four hierarchical vision tasks, including image matching, image fusion, SAR-assisted cloud removal, and cross-modal translation, involving over 30 mainstream algorithms. Experimental results demonstrate that supervised training on SOMA-1M significantly enhances performance across all tasks. Notably, multimodal remote sensing image (MRSI) matching performance achieves current state-of-the-art (SOTA) levels. SOMA-1M serves as a foundational resource for robust multimodal algorithms and remote sensing foundation models. The dataset will be released publicly at: https://github.com/PeihaoWu/SOMA-1M.

</details>


### [55] [Feature points evaluation on omnidirectional vision with a photorealistic fisheye sequence -- A report on experiments done in 2014](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05487)
*Julien Moreau,S. Ambellouis,Yassine Ruichek*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A 2014 draft report about fisheye camera calibration/visual odometry, introducing a PFSeq dataset for Photorealistic Fisheye Sequence. It aims to identify suitable feature detectors/descriptors for fisheye imagery in self-calibration but does not propose new algorithms, lacks peer review, and does not re-run experiments or update for later state-of-the-art.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine which feature detectors and descriptors perform well on fisheye images for reliable self-calibration and subsequent fisheye VSO/stereovision in urban driving, addressing the bottleneck where a good projection model is needed for calibration and robust features are needed for projection.

Method: Descriptive analysis and experiments using existing features on fisheye data; introduces PFSeq dataset and a detailed bibliography; does not implement novel feature detectors, nor compare standard detectors with omnidirectional-specific methods; experiments were not repeated or updated to reflect newer methods as of 2014.

Result: No peer-reviewed results or published comparisons. The report provides a dataset and a bibliography, but experiments have not been re-run, and conclusions are limited and not extended beyond the 2014 state-of-the-art.

Conclusion: The work is a draft with potential utility for fisheye CV research (via PFSeq) but lacks rigorous evaluation, reproducibility, and contemporary benchmarking. To be impactful, it would need updated experiments, clear methodology, and comparisons against omnidirectional feature methods and projection models.

Abstract: What is this report: This is a scientific report, contributing with a detailed bibliography, a dataset which we will call now PFSeq for ''Photorealistic Fisheye Sequence'' and make available at https://doi.org/10. 57745/DYIVVU, and comprehensive experiments. This work should be considered as a draft, and has been done during my PhD thesis ''Construction of 3D models from fisheye video data-Application to the localisation in urban area'' in 2014 [Mor16]. These results have never been published. The aim was to find the best features detector and descriptor for fisheye images, in the context of selfcalibration, with cameras mounted on the top of a car and aiming at the zenith (to proceed then fisheye visual odometry and stereovision in urban scenes). We face a chicken and egg problem, because we can not take advantage of an accurate projection model for an optimal features detection and description, and we rightly need good features to perform the calibration (i.e. to compute the accurate projection model of the camera). What is not this report: It does not contribute with new features algorithm. It does not compare standard features algorithms to algorithms designed for omnidirectional images (unfortunately). It has not been peer-reviewed. Discussions have been translated and enhanced but the experiments have not been run again and the report has not been updated accordingly to the evolution of the state-of-the-art (read this as a 2014 report).

</details>


### [56] [VGGT-Motion: Motion-Aware Calibration-Free Monocular SLAM for Long-Range Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05508)
*Zhuang Xiong,Chen Zhang,Qingshan Xu,Wenbing Tao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VGGT-Motion: a calibration-free monocular SLAM system enabling scalable long-range global consistency via motion-aware submap construction, anchor-driven direct Sim(3 registration, and linear-complexity submap pose-graph optimization, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on kilometer-scale trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address scale drift and computational cost in calibration-free monocular SLAM over long sequences. Motion-agnostic partitioning harms contextual coherence and causes zero-motion drift; traditional geometric alignment is expensive. A scalable, robust solution is needed for kilometer-scale trajectories.

Method: 1) Motion-aware submap construction guided by optical flow for adaptive partitioning, removal of static redundancy, and encapsulation of turns to stabilize local geometry. 2) Anchor-driven direct Sim(3) registration with context-balanced anchors enabling search-free, pixel-wise dense alignment and efficient loop closure without costly feature matching. 3) Lightweight, submap-level pose graph optimization with linear complexity to enforce global consistency on large scales.

Result: Empirical results show marked improvements in trajectory accuracy and efficiency, achieving state-of-the-art performance in zero-shot, long-range calibration-free monocular SLAM.

Conclusion: VGGT-Motion demonstrates that motion-aware partitioning, anchor-based direct alignment, and linear-time submap optimization jointly yield scalable, robust global consistency for kilometer-scale monocular SLAM without calibration.

Abstract: Despite recent progress in calibration-free monocular SLAM via 3D vision foundation models, scale drift remains severe on long sequences. Motion-agnostic partitioning breaks contextual coherence and causes zero-motion drift, while conventional geometric alignment is computationally expensive. To address these issues, we propose VGGT-Motion, a calibration-free SLAM system for efficient and robust global consistency over kilometer-scale trajectories. Specifically, we first propose a motion-aware submap construction mechanism that uses optical flow to guide adaptive partitioning, prune static redundancy, and encapsulate turns for stable local geometry. We then design an anchor-driven direct Sim(3) registration strategy. By exploiting context-balanced anchors, it achieves search-free, pixel-wise dense alignment and efficient loop closure without costly feature matching. Finally, a lightweight submap-level pose graph optimization enforces global consistency with linear complexity, enabling scalable long-range operation. Experiments show that VGGT-Motion markedly improves trajectory accuracy and efficiency, achieving state-of-the-art performance in zero-shot, long-range calibration-free monocular SLAM.

</details>


### [57] [Mapper-GIN: Lightweight Structural Graph Abstraction for Corrupted 3D Point Cloud Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05522)
*Jeongbin You,Donggun Kim,Sejun Park,Seungsang Oh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight Mapper-GIN pipeline partitions a 3D point cloud into overlapping regions via Mapper, builds a region-graph from overlaps, and classifies with a Graph Isomorphism Network; achieves competitive robustness on ModelNet40-C with ~0.5M parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether topology-based structural abstraction alone can improve robustness in 3D point-cloud classification, reducing reliance on larger backbones or extensive data augmentation.

Method: Apply Mapper with PCA lens, cubical cover, and density-based clustering to partition the point cloud into overlapping regions. Construct a region-graph from region overlaps. Use a Graph Isomorphism Network to perform graph classification on the region graph.

Result: On ModelNet40-C, Mapper-GIN yields competitive and stable accuracy under Noise and Transformation corruptions with a small model (~0.5M parameters). Robustness arises from region-level graph abstraction and GIN message passing rather than heavier architectures or extra robustness mechanisms.

Conclusion: Region-graph topology provides an efficient and interpretable source of robustness for 3D recognition, supporting the view that topological abstraction can enhance robustness without heavy models.

Abstract: Robust 3D point cloud classification is often pursued by scaling up backbones or relying on specialized data augmentation. We instead ask whether structural abstraction alone can improve robustness, and study a simple topology-inspired decomposition based on the Mapper algorithm. We propose Mapper-GIN, a lightweight pipeline that partitions a point cloud into overlapping regions using Mapper (PCA lens, cubical cover, and followed by density-based clustering), constructs a region graph from their overlaps, and performs graph classification with a Graph Isomorphism Network. On the corruption benchmark ModelNet40-C, Mapper-GIN achieves competitive and stable accuracy under Noise and Transformation corruptions with only 0.5M parameters. In contrast to prior approaches that require heavier architectures or additional mechanisms to gain robustness, Mapper-GIN attains strong corruption robustness through simple region-level graph abstraction and GIN message passing. Overall, our results suggest that region-graph structure offers an efficient and interpretable source of robustness for 3D visual recognition.

</details>


### [58] [Generalization of Self-Supervised Vision Transformers for Protein Localization Across Microscopy Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05527)
*Ben Isselmann,Dilara Göksu,Andreas Weinmann*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DINO self-supervised backbones pretrained on ImageNet-1k, HPA, and OpenCell transfer well to OpenCell protein localization; the domain-specific HPA SSL yields the best macro F1 (~0.822), slightly outperforming a model trained on OpenCell itself.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess cross-domain transferability of SSL representations in microscopy across different staining protocols and channel configurations, and evaluate whether domain-relevant SSL can generalize to related datasets with limited labeled data.

Method: Generate embeddings using three DINO backbones pretrained on ImageNet-1k, HPA, and OpenCell. Train a supervised classification head on OpenCell labels and evaluate with macro F1.

Result: All pretrained models transfer well. HPA-pretrained model achieves the best mean macro F1 = 0.8221 ± 0.0062, slightly outperforming a DINO model trained on OpenCell (0.8057 ± 0.0090).

Conclusion: Domain-relevant SSL representations can generalize to related microscopy datasets, indicating the value of large-scale pretraining and enabling strong downstream performance even when task-specific labeled data are limited.

Abstract: Task-specific microscopy datasets are often too small to train deep learning models that learn robust feature representations. Self-supervised learning (SSL) can mitigate this by pretraining on large unlabeled datasets, but it remains unclear how well such representations transfer across microscopy domains with different staining protocols and channel configurations. We investigate the cross-domain transferability of DINO-pretrained Vision Transformers for protein localization on the OpenCell dataset. We generate image embeddings using three DINO backbones pretrained on ImageNet-1k, the Human Protein Atlas (HPA), and OpenCell, and evaluate them by training a supervised classification head on OpenCell labels. All pretrained models transfer well, with the microscopy-specific HPA-pretrained model achieving the best performance (mean macro $F_1$-score = 0.8221 \pm 0.0062), slightly outperforming a DINO model trained directly on OpenCell (0.8057 \pm 0.0090). These results highlight the value of large-scale pretraining and indicate that domain-relevant SSL representations can generalize effectively to related but distinct microscopy datasets, enabling strong downstream performance even when task-specific labeled data are limited.

</details>


### [59] [SSG: Scaled Spatial Guidance for Multi-Scale Visual Autoregressive Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05534)
*Youngwoo Shin,Jiwan Hur,Junmo Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free inference-time guidance for visual autoregressive models to preserve a coarse-to-fine hierarchy by emphasizing high-frequency semantic residual using frequency-domain Discrete Spatial Enhancement (DSE); improves fidelity and diversity with low latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In practice, VAR hierarchies drift during inference due to limited capacity and accumulated errors. An information-theoretic view suggests each scale should add high-frequency content not explained by earlier scales; addressing this reduces train–inference discrepancy.

Method: Propose Scaled Spatial Guidance (SSG), a training-free inference-time technique that steers generation toward the intended hierarchy by amplifying target high-frequency content (semantic residual) isolated from a coarser prior. Use Discrete Spatial Enhancement (DSE), a frequency-domain procedure, to sharpen and isolate this semantic residual. The approach is tokenization- and conditioning-agnostic and applicable to VAR models with discrete visual tokens.

Result: Empirical results show SSG yields consistent improvements in fidelity and diversity while maintaining low latency, indicating efficient leverage of the coarse-to-fine generation process.

Conclusion: SSG provides a broadly applicable, training-free mechanism to enforce a coarse-to-fine hierarchy in VAR models through frequency-aware guidance, with potential for wide impact on generation quality and efficiency. The approach is model- and modality-agnostic and readily usable in existing pipelines.

Abstract: Visual autoregressive (VAR) models generate images through next-scale prediction, naturally achieving coarse-to-fine, fast, high-fidelity synthesis mirroring human perception. In practice, this hierarchy can drift at inference time, as limited capacity and accumulated error cause the model to deviate from its coarse-to-fine nature. We revisit this limitation from an information-theoretic perspective and deduce that ensuring each scale contributes high-frequency content not explained by earlier scales mitigates the train-inference discrepancy. With this insight, we propose Scaled Spatial Guidance (SSG), training-free, inference-time guidance that steers generation toward the intended hierarchy while maintaining global coherence. SSG emphasizes target high-frequency signals, defined as the semantic residual, isolated from a coarser prior. To obtain this prior, we leverage a principled frequency-domain procedure, Discrete Spatial Enhancement (DSE), which is devised to sharpen and better isolate the semantic residual through frequency-aware construction. SSG applies broadly across VAR models leveraging discrete visual tokens, regardless of tokenization design or conditioning modality. Experiments demonstrate SSG yields consistent gains in fidelity and diversity while preserving low latency, revealing untapped efficiency in coarse-to-fine image generation. Code is available at https://github.com/Youngwoo-git/SSG.

</details>


### [60] [A Comparative Study of 3D Person Detection: Sensor Modalities and Robustness in Diverse Indoor and Outdoor Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05538)
*Malaz Tamim,Andrea Matic-Flierl,Karsten Roscher*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Sensor fusion (camera-LiDAR) yields the best 3D person detection across diverse indoor/outdoor scenes on JRDB; among single modalities, LiDAR outperforms camera; fusion improves robustness to occlusion and distance but remains vulnerable to misalignment and LiDAR-specific corruptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Systematically evaluate 3D person detection across modalities beyond autonomous driving, focusing on robustness to occlusion, distance, and sensor misalignment using JRDB.

Method: Compare BEVDepth (camera), PointPillars (LiDAR), and DAL (camera-LiDAR fusion) on JRDB; analyze performance under varying occlusion and distance; assess robustness to sensor corruptions and misalignment; report comparative results.

Result: Fusion-based DAL consistently outperforms single-modality models, especially in challenging scenarios. DAL shows improved resilience to some perturbations but remains sensitive to sensor misalignment and LiDAR-specific corruptions; BEVDepth is the weakest and most affected by occlusion, distance, and noise.

Conclusion: Sensor fusion is advantageous for robust 3D person detection; however, further research is needed to mitigate vulnerabilities such as misalignment sensitivity and specific sensor corruptions.

Abstract: Accurate 3D person detection is critical for safety in applications such as robotics, industrial monitoring, and surveillance. This work presents a systematic evaluation of 3D person detection using camera-only, LiDAR-only, and camera-LiDAR fusion. While most existing research focuses on autonomous driving, we explore detection performance and robustness in diverse indoor and outdoor scenes using the JRDB dataset. We compare three representative models - BEVDepth (camera), PointPillars (LiDAR), and DAL (camera-LiDAR fusion) - and analyze their behavior under varying occlusion and distance levels. Our results show that the fusion-based approach consistently outperforms single-modality models, particularly in challenging scenarios. We further investigate robustness against sensor corruptions and misalignments, revealing that while DAL offers improved resilience, it remains sensitive to sensor misalignment and certain LiDAR-based corruptions. In contrast, the camera-based BEVDepth model showed the lowest performance and was most affected by occlusion, distance, and noise. Our findings highlight the importance of utilizing sensor fusion for enhanced 3D person detection, while also underscoring the need for ongoing research to address the vulnerabilities inherent in these systems.

</details>


### [61] [FastVMT: Eliminating Redundancy in Video Motion Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05551)
*Yue Ma,Zhikai Wang,Tianhao Ren,Mingzhe Zheng,Hongyu Liu,Jiayi Guo,Mark Fong,Yuxuan Xue,Zixiang Zhao,Konrad Schindler,Qifeng Chen,Linfeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Identify and remove two structural redundancies in Diffusion Transformer (DiT) for video motion transfer: motion redundancy and gradient redundancy. Propose local attention masking and gradient reuse to accelerate inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computational waste in DiT-based video motion transfer by exploiting intrinsic properties of video dynamics (small, smooth motion) and diffusion gradients (slowly changing along steps) to achieve faster runtimes without sacrificing quality.

Method:  Introduce FastVMT: (1) mask attention to a local neighborhood to curb motion redundancy, (2) reuse gradients from previous diffusion steps and skip unnecessary gradient computations to exploit gradient redundancy.

Result: Achieves an average 3.43x speedup with no degradation in visual fidelity or temporal consistency of the generated videos.

Conclusion: Targeting structural redundancies in DiT-based video motion transfer is an effective route to substantial speedups while maintaining quality.

Abstract: Video motion transfer aims to synthesize videos by generating visual content according to a text prompt while transferring the motion pattern observed in a reference video. Recent methods predominantly use the Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architecture. To achieve satisfactory runtime, several methods attempt to accelerate the computations in the DiT, but fail to address structural sources of inefficiency. In this work, we identify and remove two types of computational redundancy in earlier work: motion redundancy arises because the generic DiT architecture does not reflect the fact that frame-to-frame motion is small and smooth; gradient redundancy occurs if one ignores that gradients change slowly along the diffusion trajectory. To mitigate motion redundancy, we mask the corresponding attention layers to a local neighborhood such that interaction weights are not computed unnecessarily distant image regions. To exploit gradient redundancy, we design an optimization scheme that reuses gradients from previous diffusion steps and skips unwarranted gradient computations. On average, FastVMT achieves a 3.43x speedup without degrading the visual fidelity or the temporal consistency of the generated videos.

</details>


### [62] [Visual Implicit Geometry Transformer for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05573)
*Arsenii Shirokov,Mikhail Kuznetsov,Danila Stepochkin,Egor Evdokimov,Daniil Glazkov,Nikolay Patakin,Anton Konushin,Dmitry Senushkin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces ViGT, a calibration-free Visual Implicit Geometry Transformer that estimates continuous 3D BEV occupancy fields from surround-view cameras using self-supervised training on synchronized image-LiDAR data; demonstrates strong generalization across diverse datasets and state-of-the-art performance on pointmap tasks, with competitive results on Occ3D-nuScenes; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for scalable, simple, and generalizable 3D occupancy estimation for autonomous driving across diverse sensor configurations. Overcome calibration dependence and pixel-aligned constraints by learning a unified, BEV-centric geometric representation that serves multiple downstream tasks.

Method: A calibration-free architecture (ViGT) that fuses multi-view camera inputs into a continuous 3D occupancy field in a BEV frame using a Visual Implicit Geometry Transformer. Trained with self-supervision from synchronized image-LiDAR pairs, enabling learning without manual annotations. Trains on a mixture of five large-scale datasets (NuScenes, Waymo, NuPlan, ONCE, Argoverse) for broad generalization.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on the pointmap estimation task with the best average rank among baselines; on Occ3D-nuScenes, ViGT attains competitive/supervised-level performance. Public code available at the provided repository.

Conclusion: Demonstrates scalability and generalization of a calibration-free, BEV-based geometric model for autonomous driving, enabling a single model to handle multiple geometric tasks with a common representation. Supports negative data annotation overhead through self-supervision and shows strong cross-dataset transferability.

Abstract: We introduce the Visual Implicit Geometry Transformer (ViGT), an autonomous driving geometric model that estimates continuous 3D occupancy fields from surround-view camera rigs. ViGT represents a step towards foundational geometric models for autonomous driving, prioritizing scalability, architectural simplicity, and generalization across diverse sensor configurations. Our approach achieves this through a calibration-free architecture, enabling a single model to adapt to different sensor setups. Unlike general-purpose geometric foundational models that focus on pixel-aligned predictions, ViGT estimates a continuous 3D occupancy field in a birds-eye-view (BEV) addressing domain-specific requirements. ViGT naturally infers geometry from multiple camera views into a single metric coordinate frame, providing a common representation for multiple geometric tasks. Unlike most existing occupancy models, we adopt a self-supervised training procedure that leverages synchronized image-LiDAR pairs, eliminating the need for costly manual annotations. We validate the scalability and generalizability of our approach by training our model on a mixture of five large-scale autonomous driving datasets (NuScenes, Waymo, NuPlan, ONCE, and Argoverse) and achieving state-of-the-art performance on the pointmap estimation task, with the best average rank across all evaluated baselines. We further evaluate ViGT on the Occ3D-nuScenes benchmark, where ViGT achieves comparable performance with supervised methods. The source code is publicly available at \href{https://github.com/whesense/ViGT}{https://github.com/whesense/ViGT}.

</details>


### [63] [A Hybrid CNN and ML Framework for Multi-modal Classification of Movement Disorders Using MRI and Brain Structural Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05574)
*Mengyu Li,Ingibjörg Kristjánsdóttir,Thilo van Eimeren,Kathrin Giehl,Lotta M. Ellingsen,the ASAP Neuroimaging Initiative*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-modal CNN+ML framework fuses T1 MRI, 12 deep-brain structure segmentation masks, and their volumes to classify APD subtypes versus PD and differentiate PSP/MSA subtypes, achieving AUCs of 0.95 (PSP vs PD), 0.86 (MSA vs PD), and 0.92 (PSP vs MSA).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The overlap of clinical features in early APD stages with PD reduces diagnostic reliability. Reliable imaging biomarkers are needed for early differential diagnosis among APD subtypes and PD.

Method: A hybrid approach that combines CNN-derived image features with volume-based ML inputs using multi-modal data: (1) T1-weighted MRI, (2) segmentation masks for 12 APD-related deep structures, and (3) their volumetric measurements. The model fuses image, segmentation, and volume features to perform subtype classification.

Result: The integrated framework achieved AUCs of 0.95 (PSP vs PD), 0.86 (MSA vs PD), and 0.92 (PSP vs MSA), indicating that combining spatial image information with quantitative structural features enhances subtype differentiation.

Conclusion: Fusing CNN-based image features with volume-based inputs improves early differential diagnosis of APD subtypes versus PD and among subtypes, suggesting clinical potential for more reliable early-stage diagnosis and targeted interventions.

Abstract: Atypical Parkinsonian Disorders (APD), also known as Parkinson-plus syndrome, are a group of neurodegenerative diseases that include progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP) and multiple system atrophy (MSA). In the early stages, overlapping clinical features often lead to misdiagnosis as Parkinson's disease (PD). Identifying reliable imaging biomarkers for early differential diagnosis remains a critical challenge. In this study, we propose a hybrid framework combining convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with machine learning (ML) techniques to classify APD subtypes versus PD and distinguish between the subtypes themselves: PSP vs. PD, MSA vs. PD, and PSP vs. MSA. The model leverages multi-modal input data, including T1-weighted magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), segmentation masks of 12 deep brain structures associated with APD, and their corresponding volumetric measurements. By integrating these complementary modalities, including image data, structural segmentation masks, and quantitative volume features, the hybrid approach achieved promising classification performance with area under the curve (AUC) scores of 0.95 for PSP vs. PD, 0.86 for MSA vs. PD, and 0.92 for PSP vs. MSA. These results highlight the potential of combining spatial and structural information for robust subtype differentiation. In conclusion, this study demonstrates that fusing CNN-based image features with volume-based ML inputs improves classification accuracy for APD subtypes. The proposed approach may contribute to more reliable early-stage diagnosis, facilitating timely and targeted interventions in clinical practice.

</details>


### [64] [LocateEdit-Bench: A Benchmark for Instruction-Based Editing Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05577)
*Shiyu Wu,Shuyan Li,Jing Li,Jing Liu,Yequan Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces LocateEdit-Bench, a large-scale dataset to benchmark forgery localization for instruction-driven image editing, with 231K edited images, four editing models, three edit types, and two evaluation protocols; open-sourced on acceptance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing forgery localization methods largely target inpainting-based manipulations and struggle with instruction-driven edits; a dedicated benchmark is needed to track progress as editing techniques evolve.

Method: Construct LocateEdit-Bench comprising 231K edited images produced by four state-of-the-art editing models across three edit types; develop two multi-metric evaluation protocols to assess localization methods; perform dataset analysis.

Result: Proposes a comprehensive dataset and evaluation framework enabling robust benchmarking of localization methods against instruction-driven edits; provides baseline analyses and insights.

Conclusion: Dataset lays the foundation for measuring and improving forgery localization in the evolving landscape of image editing and will be open-sourced upon acceptance.

Abstract: Recent advancements in image editing have enabled highly controllable and semantically-aware alteration of visual content, posing unprecedented challenges to manipulation localization. However, existing AI-generated forgery localization methods primarily focus on inpainting-based manipulations, making them ineffective against the latest instruction-based editing paradigms. To bridge this critical gap, we propose LocateEdit-Bench, a large-scale dataset comprising $231$K edited images, designed specifically to benchmark localization methods against instruction-driven image editing. Our dataset incorporates four cutting-edge editing models and covers three common edit types. We conduct a detailed analysis of the dataset and develop two multi-metric evaluation protocols to assess existing localization methods. Our work establishes a foundation to keep pace with the evolving landscape of image editing, thereby facilitating the development of effective methods for future forgery localization. Dataset will be open-sourced upon acceptance.

</details>


### [65] [LoGoSeg: Integrating Local and Global Features for Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05578)
*Junyang Chen,Xiangbo Lv,Zhiqiang Kou,Xingdong Sheng,Ning Xu,Yiguo Qiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LoGoSeg is a single-stage open-vocabulary semantic segmentation model that combines an object existence prior, region-aware alignment, and dual-stream fusion to improve spatial precision and reduce hallucinations, without external proposals or extra data, achieving competitive generalization on six benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limits of image-level pretraining in OVSS, which often leads to imprecise spatial alignment and hallucinations in cluttered scenes due to weak object priors and lack of region-level constraints.

Method: Three core components: (i) an object existence prior that weights categories by global image-text similarity to suppress unlikely classes; (ii) a region-aware alignment module that establishes precise region-text correspondences; (iii) a dual-stream fusion mechanism that combines local structural cues with global semantic context. The framework is single-stage and does not rely on external mask proposals, extra backbones, or additional datasets.

Result: Empirical evaluation on six benchmarks (A-847, PC-459, A-150, PC-59, PAS-20, PAS-20b) showing competitive performance and strong generalization in open-vocabulary settings.

Conclusion: LoGoSeg offers an efficient OVSS solution that mitigates object hallucination and missed detections by integrating global and region-level priors with a unified fusion strategy, without increasing architectural or data requirements.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVSS) extends traditional closed-set segmentation by enabling pixel-wise annotation for both seen and unseen categories using arbitrary textual descriptions. While existing methods leverage vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP, their reliance on image-level pretraining often results in imprecise spatial alignment, leading to mismatched segmentations in ambiguous or cluttered scenes. However, most existing approaches lack strong object priors and region-level constraints, which can lead to object hallucination or missed detections, further degrading performance. To address these challenges, we propose LoGoSeg, an efficient single-stage framework that integrates three key innovations: (i) an object existence prior that dynamically weights relevant categories through global image-text similarity, effectively reducing hallucinations; (ii) a region-aware alignment module that establishes precise region-level visual-textual correspondences; and (iii) a dual-stream fusion mechanism that optimally combines local structural information with global semantic context. Unlike prior works, LoGoSeg eliminates the need for external mask proposals, additional backbones, or extra datasets, ensuring efficiency. Extensive experiments on six benchmarks (A-847, PC-459, A-150, PC-59, PAS-20, and PAS-20b) demonstrate its competitive performance and strong generalization in open-vocabulary settings.

</details>


### [66] [Geometric Observability Index: An Operator-Theoretic Framework for Per-Feature Sensitivity, Weak Observability, and Dynamic Effects in SE(3) Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05582)
*Joe-Mei Feng,Sheng-Wei Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Geometric Observability Index (GOI) for SE(3) to quantify per-feature influence on camera pose estimation using a matrix-Lie-group extension of influence functions; links to Fisher information geometry; unifies conditioning, influence functions, and dynamic scene detectability; enables lightweight, training-free diagnostics in SLAM/SfM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of understanding of how individual image features influence pose estimates in SLAM/SfM, especially under dynamic observations and weak observability; extend classical sensitivity tools to matrix Lie groups to explain feature influence, degeneracies, and dynamic amplification.

Method: Develop intrinsic perturbation operator for left-trivialized M-estimators on SE(3); derive curvature operator in the observable subspace; define GOI via spectral decomposition of the observable curvature; relate GOI to Fisher information geometry in the population limit; analyze degeneracies (pure rotation, vanishing parallax) and dynamic feature amplification; integrate into Gauss-Newton pipelines as lightweight diagnostics.

Result: GOI provides a per-measurement influence measure with a spectral decomposition along principal observable curvature directions; identifies weak observability directions and how measurements amplify sensitivity; in the population limit, GOI aligns with SE(3) Fisher information geometry, yielding a single-measurement analogue of the Cramer-Rao bound; explains classical degeneracies and dynamic feature amplification; enables training-free diagnostics for dynamic features and weak observability without modifying SLAM architectures.

Conclusion: GOI offers a geometrically consistent framework that unifies conditioning analyses, Fisher information geometry, influence-function theory, and dynamic scene detectability on SE(3); its integration with Gauss-Newton pipelines provides practical, low-cost diagnostics for SLAM/SfM systems and deepens understanding of measurement influence and observability on Lie groups.

Abstract: We present a unified operator-theoretic framework for analyzing per-feature sensitivity in camera pose estimation on the Lie group SE(3). Classical sensitivity tools - conditioning analyses, Euclidean perturbation arguments, and Fisher information bounds - do not explain how individual image features influence the pose estimate, nor why dynamic or inconsistent observations can disproportionately distort modern SLAM and structure-from-motion systems. To address this gap, we extend influence function theory to matrix Lie groups and derive an intrinsic perturbation operator for left-trivialized M-estimators on SE(3).
  The resulting Geometric Observability Index (GOI) quantifies the contribution of a single measurement through the curvature operator and the Lie algebraic structure of the observable subspace. GOI admits a spectral decomposition along the principal directions of the observable curvature, revealing a direct correspondence between weak observability and amplified sensitivity. In the population regime, GOI coincides with the Fisher information geometry on SE(3), yielding a single-measurement analogue of the Cramer-Rao bound.
  The same spectral mechanism explains classical degeneracies such as pure rotation and vanishing parallax, as well as dynamic feature amplification along weak curvature directions. Overall, GOI provides a geometrically consistent description of measurement influence that unifies conditioning analysis, Fisher information geometry, influence function theory, and dynamic scene detectability through the spectral geometry of the curvature operator. Because these quantities arise directly within Gauss-Newton pipelines, the curvature spectrum and GOI also yield lightweight, training-free diagnostic signals for identifying dynamic features and detecting weak observability configurations without modifying existing SLAM architectures.

</details>


### [67] [A Mixed Reality System for Robust Manikin Localization in Childbirth Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05588)
*Haojie Cheng,Chang Liu,Abhiram Kanneganti,Mahesh Arjandas Choolani,Arundhati Tushar Gosavi,Eng Tat Khoo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MR-based childbirth training overlays virtual guiding hands onto a real childbirth manikin via a standalone headset, enabling independent practice with haptic feedback; it outperforms VR in a large study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address constraints in obstetric training (short rotations, patient reluctance, unpredictable labor) by reducing instructional burden while preserving authentic haptics and enabling independent practice.

Method: Develop a mixed reality system with enhanced passthrough via calibrated RGB-D camera; employ a coarse-to-fine localization: align maternal manikin with fiducials to define delivery region, then register neonatal head; overlay virtual guidance with haptic reinforcement; operate on a standalone headset. Validate on 83 fourth-year medical students; four senior obstetricians assess using standardized criteria.

Result: MR training produced significantly higher scores in delivery, post-delivery, and overall performance; trainees preferred MR to VR.

Conclusion: MR-based childbirth training is more effective and preferred than VR, feasible on standalone hardware, and can reduce supervision needs while enhancing learning efficiency.

Abstract: Opportunities for medical students to gain practical experience in vaginal births are increasingly constrained by shortened clinical rotations, patient reluctance, and the unpredictable nature of labour. To alleviate clinicians' instructional burden and enhance trainees' learning efficiency, we introduce a mixed reality (MR) system for childbirth training that combines virtual guidance with tactile manikin interaction, thereby preserving authentic haptic feedback while enabling independent practice without continuous on-site expert supervision. The system extends the passthrough capability of commercial head-mounted displays (HMDs) by spatially calibrating an external RGB-D camera, allowing real-time visual integration of physical training objects. Building on this capability, we implement a coarse-to-fine localization pipeline that first aligns the maternal manikin with fiducial markers to define a delivery region and then registers the pre-scanned neonatal head within this area. This process enables spatially accurate overlay of virtual guiding hands near the manikin, allowing trainees to follow expert trajectories reinforced by haptic interaction. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that the system achieves accurate and stable manikin localization on a standalone headset, ensuring practical deployment without external computing resources. A large-scale user study involving 83 fourth-year medical students was subsequently conducted to compare MR-based and virtual reality (VR)-based childbirth training. Four senior obstetricians independently assessed performance using standardized criteria. Results showed that MR training achieved significantly higher scores in delivery, post-delivery, and overall task performance, and was consistently preferred by trainees over VR training.

</details>


### [68] [EgoPoseVR: Spatiotemporal Multi-Modal Reasoning for Egocentric Full-Body Pose in Virtual Reality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05590)
*Haojie Cheng,Shaun Jing Heng Ong,Shaoyu Cai,Aiden Tat Yang Koh,Fuxi Ouyang,Eng Tat Khoo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EgoPoseVR is an end-to-end dual-modality framework that fuses headset motion cues with egocentric RGB-D to achieve accurate, temporally coherent full-body pose estimation for VR, using a spatiotemporal encoder with cross-attention and a kinematic optimization stage, trained on a large synthetic dataset; it outperforms baselines and yields better user-perceived accuracy, stability, and embodiment without extra sensors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the instability and poor lower-body estimation of current egocentric HMD-based pose methods, and achieve real-time, robust full-body tracking by leveraging headset motion signals alongside RGB-D data.

Method: An end-to-end EgoPoseVR pipeline that (1) encodes frame- and joint-level representations with a spatiotemporal encoder, (2) fuses modalities via cross-attention, (3) refines poses with a kinematic optimization module constrained by HMD signals, and (4) trains on a large synthetic dataset of 1.8M aligned HMD-RGBD frames.

Result: EgoPoseVR outperforms state-of-the-art egocentric pose estimation models and, in a real-world user study, achieves higher subjective ratings in accuracy, stability, embodiment, and intention for future use; demonstrates robust full-body pose tracking without additional wearables or room-scale tracking.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a practical, accurate, and stable solution for VR embodiment by effectively integrating headset motion cues with egocentric RGB-D data and leveraging kinematic constraints, enabling robust full-body pose tracking in VR.

Abstract: Immersive virtual reality (VR) applications demand accurate, temporally coherent full-body pose tracking. Recent head-mounted camera-based approaches show promise in egocentric pose estimation, but encounter challenges when applied to VR head-mounted displays (HMDs), including temporal instability, inaccurate lower-body estimation, and the lack of real-time performance. To address these limitations, we present EgoPoseVR, an end-to-end framework for accurate egocentric full-body pose estimation in VR that integrates headset motion cues with egocentric RGB-D observations through a dual-modality fusion pipeline. A spatiotemporal encoder extracts frame- and joint-level representations, which are fused via cross-attention to fully exploit complementary motion cues across modalities. A kinematic optimization module then imposes constraints from HMD signals, enhancing the accuracy and stability of pose estimation. To facilitate training and evaluation, we introduce a large-scale synthetic dataset of over 1.8 million temporally aligned HMD and RGB-D frames across diverse VR scenarios. Experimental results show that EgoPoseVR outperforms state-of-the-art egocentric pose estimation models. A user study in real-world scenes further shows that EgoPoseVR achieved significantly higher subjective ratings in accuracy, stability, embodiment, and intention for future use compared to baseline methods. These results show that EgoPoseVR enables robust full-body pose tracking, offering a practical solution for accurate VR embodiment without requiring additional body-worn sensors or room-scale tracking systems.

</details>


### [69] [CAViT -- Channel-Aware Vision Transformer for Dynamic Feature Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05598)
*Aon Safdar,Mohamed Saadeldin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CAViT replaces static MLPs with dynamic, attention-based channel mixing in ViTs, adding a dual-attention block (spatial then channel-wise) to enable content-aware token mixing without depth increase, yielding accuracy gains and efficiency gains across natural and medical datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ViTs' channel mixing is fixed via MLPs and lacks adaptability to input content; dynamic, context-driven interaction can improve representation with minimal overhead.

Method: In each Transformer block, apply spatial self-attention, then perform channel-wise self-attention for feature interaction; a unified, content-aware token mixing mechanism that replaces the static MLP without increasing depth or complexity.

Result: Outperforms ViT baseline by up to 3.6% accuracy on five datasets (natural and medical); reduces parameters and FLOPs by over 30%; qualitative attention maps show sharper, semantically meaningful activations.

Conclusion: Dynamic, attention-driven channel mixing enhances representational expressiveness and efficiency in ViTs, validating the approach across diverse domains and providing interpretable attention patterns.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have demonstrated strong performance across a range of computer vision tasks by modeling long-range spatial interactions via self-attention. However, channel-wise mixing in ViTs remains static, relying on fixed multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) that lack adaptability to input content. We introduce 'CAViT', a dual-attention architecture that replaces the static MLP with a dynamic, attention-based mechanism for feature interaction. Each Transformer block in CAViT performs spatial self-attention followed by channel-wise self-attention, allowing the model to dynamically recalibrate feature representations based on global image context. This unified and content-aware token mixing strategy enhances representational expressiveness without increasing depth or complexity. We validate CAViT across five benchmark datasets spanning both natural and medical domains, where it outperforms the standard ViT baseline by up to +3.6% in accuracy, while reducing parameter count and FLOPs by over 30%. Qualitative attention maps reveal sharper and semantically meaningful activation patterns, validating the effectiveness of our attention-driven token mixing.

</details>


### [70] [Multi-instance robust fitting for non-classical geometric models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05602)
*Zongliang Zhang,Shuxiang Li,Xingwang Huang,Zongyue Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-instance robust fitting framework for non-classical models that uses a non-differentiable, threshold-free model-to-data estimator paired with a meta-heuristic optimizer to recover multiple instances from noisy data; demonstrated on diverse non-classical models with code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robust fitting methods mainly target classical models (lines, circles, planes) and single-instance reconstruction. There is a gap for robustly fitting non-classical models (e.g., spiral curves, procedural characters, free-form surfaces) and for recovering multiple instances from noisy data without relying on predefined error thresholds.

Method: Introduce an estimator based on model-to-data error that is robust to outliers and does not require a fixed error threshold. Because the estimator is non-differentiable with respect to model parameters, employ a meta-heuristic optimizer to search for the global optimum. Extend the framework to multi-instance fitting across various non-classical models.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate effectiveness on diverse non-classical models. The authors also provide code for reproducibility at a GitHub repository.

Conclusion: The work presents a general multi-instance robust fitting approach for non-classical models, combining a threshold-free, non-differentiable estimator with a global-search meta-heuristic optimizer, validated experimentally and released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Most existing robust fitting methods are designed for classical models, such as lines, circles, and planes. In contrast, fewer methods have been developed to robustly handle non-classical models, such as spiral curves, procedural character models, and free-form surfaces. Furthermore, existing methods primarily focus on reconstructing a single instance of a non-classical model. This paper aims to reconstruct multiple instances of non-classical models from noisy data. We formulate this multi-instance fitting task as an optimization problem, which comprises an estimator and an optimizer. Specifically, we propose a novel estimator based on the model-to-data error, capable of handling outliers without a predefined error threshold. Since the proposed estimator is non-differentiable with respect to the model parameters, we employ a meta-heuristic algorithm as the optimizer to seek the global optimum. The effectiveness of our method are demonstrated through experimental results on various non-classical models. The code is available at https://github.com/zhangzongliang/fitting.

</details>


### [71] [Unified Sensor Simulation for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05617)
*Nikolay Patakin,Arsenii Shirokov,Anton Konushin,Dmitry Senushkin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: XSIM is a sensor simulation framework for autonomous driving that extends 3DGUT splatting with generalized rolling-shutter modeling, introduces phase-aware handling of azimuth boundary discontinuities for spherical cameras, and adds a dual-opacity 3D Gaussian representation, yielding improved geometric consistency and photorealism; validated with Waymo, Argoverse 2, and PandaSet, with code released publicly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for realistic and robust sensor simulation in autonomous driving to accurately model dynamic scenes, rolling-shutter distortions, and the interaction between geometry and appearance. Existing 3DGUT approaches suffer from cyclic projection and time discontinuities at azimuth boundaries for spherical cameras (e.g., LiDARs), leading to incorrect particle projections and inconsistent rendering.

Method: XSIM extends 3DGUT splatting by (1) incorporating a generalized rolling-shutter model tailored for autonomous driving; (2) introducing a phase modeling mechanism to account for temporal and shape discontinuities of Gaussians projected by the Unscented Transform at azimuth borders; (3) proposing an extended 3D Gaussian representation with two opacity parameters to align geometry and color distributions; (4) providing a unified and flexible framework for appearance and geometric sensor modeling to render complex sensor distortions in dynamic environments.

Result: Comprehensive evaluation on Waymo Open Dataset, Argoverse 2, and PandaSet shows consistent improvements over strong baselines and state-of-the-art performance across all datasets.

Conclusion: XSIM delivers enhanced scene representations with improved geometric consistency and photorealistic appearance for autonomous driving sensor simulation, with public code available to foster adoption and evaluation.

Abstract: In this work, we introduce \textbf{XSIM}, a sensor simulation framework for autonomous driving. XSIM extends 3DGUT splatting with a generalized rolling-shutter modeling tailored for autonomous driving applications. Our framework provides a unified and flexible formulation for appearance and geometric sensor modeling, enabling rendering of complex sensor distortions in dynamic environments. We identify spherical cameras, such as LiDARs, as a critical edge case for existing 3DGUT splatting due to cyclic projection and time discontinuities at azimuth boundaries leading to incorrect particle projection. To address this issue, we propose a phase modeling mechanism that explicitly accounts temporal and shape discontinuities of Gaussians projected by the Unscented Transform at azimuth borders. In addition, we introduce an extended 3D Gaussian representation that incorporates two distinct opacity parameters to resolve mismatches between geometry and color distributions. As a result, our framework provides enhanced scene representations with improved geometric consistency and photorealistic appearance. We evaluate our framework extensively on multiple autonomous driving datasets, including Waymo Open Dataset, Argoverse 2, and PandaSet. Our framework consistently outperforms strong recent baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance across all datasets. The source code is publicly available at \href{https://github.com/whesense/XSIM}{https://github.com/whesense/XSIM}.

</details>


### [72] [ROMAN: Reward-Orchestrated Multi-Head Attention Network for Autonomous Driving System Testing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05629)
*Jianlei Chi,Yuzhen Wu,Jiaxuan Hou,Xiaodong Zhang,Ming Fan,Suhui Sun,Weijun Dai,Bo Li,Jianguo Sun,Jun Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ROMAN is a scenario generation approach for ADS testing that uses a multi-head attention network and a traffic law weighting mechanism to generate high-risk violation scenarios. It leverages an LLM-based risk weighting module to evaluate violations on severity and occurrence, and demonstrates improved violation generation and diversity over ABLE and LawBreaker in CARLA with Baidu Apollo, covering all traffic-law clauses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current ADS testing struggles to generate complex, high-risk law-breaking scenarios and to capture multi-vehicle interactions and critical situations, limiting thorough safety evaluation under real-world traffic laws.

Method: Integrates a multi-head attention network to model interactions among vehicles, signals, and environmental factors, with a traffic law weighting workflow powered by an LLM-based risk weighting module to assess violations by severity and occurrence. Generates violation-rich scenarios and tests within the CARLA simulation for Baidu Apollo.

Result: ROMAN achieves higher average violation counts than ABLE (7.91% more) and LawBreaker (55.96% more), with greater scenario diversity. It also uniquely generates violations for every clause of the input traffic laws, enabling broader high-risk violation detection.

Conclusion: ROMAN is effective for targeted ADS evaluation by generating diverse, high-risk violation scenarios aligned with traffic laws, outperforming prior approaches and ensuring clause-level law coverage.

Abstract: Automated Driving System (ADS) acts as the brain of autonomous vehicles, responsible for their safety and efficiency. Safe deployment requires thorough testing in diverse real-world scenarios and compliance with traffic laws like speed limits, signal obedience, and right-of-way rules. Violations like running red lights or speeding pose severe safety risks. However, current testing approaches face significant challenges: limited ability to generate complex and high-risk law-breaking scenarios, and failing to account for complex interactions involving multiple vehicles and critical situations. To address these challenges, we propose ROMAN, a novel scenario generation approach for ADS testing that combines a multi-head attention network with a traffic law weighting mechanism. ROMAN is designed to generate high-risk violation scenarios to enable more thorough and targeted ADS evaluation. The multi-head attention mechanism models interactions among vehicles, traffic signals, and other factors. The traffic law weighting mechanism implements a workflow that leverages an LLM-based risk weighting module to evaluate violations based on the two dimensions of severity and occurrence. We have evaluated ROMAN by testing the Baidu Apollo ADS within the CARLA simulation platform and conducting extensive experiments to measure its performance. Experimental results demonstrate that ROMAN surpassed state-of-the-art tools ABLE and LawBreaker by achieving 7.91% higher average violation count than ABLE and 55.96% higher than LawBreaker, while also maintaining greater scenario diversity. In addition, only ROMAN successfully generated violation scenarios for every clause of the input traffic laws, enabling it to identify more high-risk violations than existing approaches.

</details>


### [73] [UniSurg: A Video-Native Foundation Model for Universal Understanding of Surgical Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05638)
*Jinlin Wu,Felix Holm,Chuxi Chen,An Wang,Yaxin Hu,Xiaofan Ye,Zelin Zang,Miao Xu,Lihua Zhou,Huai Liao,Danny T. M. Chan,Ming Feng,Wai S. Poon,Hongliang Ren,Dong Yi,Nassir Navab,Gaofeng Meng,Jiebo Luo,Hongbin Liu,Zhen Lei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UniSurg is a motion-prediction video foundation model for surgical videos that shifts from pixel-level reconstruction to latent motion prediction. Built on V-JEPA with three innovations (motion-guided latent prediction, spatiotemporal affinity self-distillation, feature diversity regularization), trained on UniSurg-15M (3,658 hours, 50 sources, 13 regions). It achieves large performance gains across 17 benchmarks, setting a new standard for motion-oriented surgical video understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current surgical video models rely mainly on pixel-level reconstruction, wasting capacity on low-level details irrelevant to semantic surgical understanding. There is a need for models that prioritize semantically meaningful motion and relations to improve tasks like recognition, segmentation, and depth estimation.

Method: Extend Video Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (V-JEPA) with: (1) motion-guided latent prediction to emphasize semantically meaningful regions; (2) spatiotemporal affinity self-distillation to enforce relational consistency; (3) feature diversity regularization to avoid representation collapse in sparse textures. Pretrain on UniSurg-15M (3,658 hours, 50 sources, 13 anatomical regions).

Result: On 17 benchmarks, UniSurg outperforms state-of-the-art methods, e.g., +14.6% F1 on EgoSurgery, +10.3% on PitVis; 39.54% mAP-IVT on CholecT50 (action triplets); improvements extend to skill assessment, polyp segmentation, and depth estimation.

Conclusion: UniSurg establishes a new standard for universal, motion-oriented surgical video understanding by optimizing latent motion representations and relational consistency across diverse surgical tasks.

Abstract: While foundation models have advanced surgical video analysis, current approaches rely predominantly on pixel-level reconstruction objectives that waste model capacity on low-level visual details - such as smoke, specular reflections, and fluid motion - rather than semantic structures essential for surgical understanding. We present UniSurg, a video-native foundation model that shifts the learning paradigm from pixel-level reconstruction to latent motion prediction. Built on the Video Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (V-JEPA), UniSurg introduces three key technical innovations tailored to surgical videos: 1) motion-guided latent prediction to prioritize semantically meaningful regions, 2) spatiotemporal affinity self-distillation to enforce relational consistency, and 3) feature diversity regularization to prevent representation collapse in texture-sparse surgical scenes. To enable large-scale pretraining, we curate UniSurg-15M, the largest surgical video dataset to date, comprising 3,658 hours of video from 50 sources across 13 anatomical regions. Extensive experiments across 17 benchmarks demonstrate that UniSurg significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on surgical workflow recognition (+14.6% F1 on EgoSurgery, +10.3% on PitVis), action triplet recognition (39.54% mAP-IVT on CholecT50), skill assessment, polyp segmentation, and depth estimation. These results establish UniSurg as a new standard for universal, motion-oriented surgical video understanding.

</details>


### [74] [Enhancing Personality Recognition by Comparing the Predictive Power of Traits, Facets, and Nuances](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05650)
*Amir Ansari,Jana Subirana,Bruna Silva,Sergio Escalera,David Gallardo-Pujol,Cristina Palmero*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Nuance-level (granular) personality modeling improves audiovisual personality recognition over facet/trait models, using a transformer with cross-modal and dyad-aware attention on UDIVA v0.5; up to 74% MSE reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Broad trait scores are a coarse ground truth; context-specific behaviors can manifest similar trait scores. Investigating finer-grained facets/nuances may enhance generalization and predictive accuracy in personality recognition from audiovisual data.

Method: Transformer-based model trained on UDIVA v0.5 with cross-modal (audiovisual) and cross-subject (dyad-aware) attention; comparisons across nuance-, facet-, and trait-level representations.

Result: Nuance-level models consistently outperform facet- and trait-level models, achieving substantial MSE reductions (up to 74%) across different interaction scenarios.

Conclusion: Hierarchical nuance-level personality representations provide superior predictive power for audiovisual interaction data, suggesting that finer-grained personality modeling improves recognition and generalization over coarse trait scores.

Abstract: Personality is a complex, hierarchical construct typically assessed through item-level questionnaires aggregated into broad trait scores. Personality recognition models aim to infer personality traits from different sources of behavioral data. However, reliance on broad trait scores as ground truth, combined with limited training data, poses challenges for generalization, as similar trait scores can manifest through diverse, context dependent behaviors. In this work, we explore the predictive impact of the more granular hierarchical levels of the Big-Five Personality Model, facets and nuances, to enhance personality recognition from audiovisual interaction data. Using the UDIVA v0.5 dataset, we trained a transformer-based model including cross-modal (audiovisual) and cross-subject (dyad-aware) attention mechanisms. Results show that nuance-level models consistently outperform facet and trait-level models, reducing mean squared error by up to 74% across interaction scenarios.

</details>


### [75] [ShapeUP: Scalable Image-Conditioned 3D Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05676)
*Inbar Gat,Dana Cohen-Bar,Guy Levy,Elad Richardson,Daniel Cohen-Or*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ShapeUP is an image-conditioned 3D editing framework that translates latent representations to enable scalable, high-fidelity edits on 3D assets, using a 3D Diffusion Transformer trained on triplets (source shape, edited 2D image, edited 3D shape).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D editing struggles with trade-offs among visual controllability, geometric consistency, and scalability; existing methods are slow, drift in multi-view propagation, or limited by frozen priors.

Method: Supervised latent-to-latent translation within a native 3D representation using a 3D Diffusion Transformer (DiT). Trains on triplets (source shape, edited 2D image, edited 3D shape) to learn an image-to-edit mapping, enabling fine-grained local/global control, implicit mask-free localization, and structure-preserving edits.

Result: ShapeUP consistently outperforms both trained and training-free baselines in identity preservation and edit fidelity, demonstrating robustness and scalability for native 3D content creation.

Conclusion: The work presents a scalable, image-conditioned editing framework that leverages 3D foundation models for precise edits with strong structural consistency, enabling effective native 3D content creation.

Abstract: Recent advancements in 3D foundation models have enabled the generation of high-fidelity assets, yet precise 3D manipulation remains a significant challenge. Existing 3D editing frameworks often face a difficult trade-off between visual controllability, geometric consistency, and scalability. Specifically, optimization-based methods are prohibitively slow, multi-view 2D propagation techniques suffer from visual drift, and training-free latent manipulation methods are inherently bound by frozen priors and cannot directly benefit from scaling. In this work, we present ShapeUP, a scalable, image-conditioned 3D editing framework that formulates editing as a supervised latent-to-latent translation within a native 3D representation. This formulation allows ShapeUP to build on a pretrained 3D foundation model, leveraging its strong generative prior while adapting it to editing through supervised training. In practice, ShapeUP is trained on triplets consisting of a source 3D shape, an edited 2D image, and the corresponding edited 3D shape, and learns a direct mapping using a 3D Diffusion Transformer (DiT). This image-as-prompt approach enables fine-grained visual control over both local and global edits and achieves implicit, mask-free localization, while maintaining strict structural consistency with the original asset. Our extensive evaluations demonstrate that ShapeUP consistently outperforms current trained and training-free baselines in both identity preservation and edit fidelity, offering a robust and scalable paradigm for native 3D content creation.

</details>


### [76] [Poster: Camera Tampering Detection for Outdoor IoT Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05706)
*Shadi Attarha,Kanaga Shanmugi,Anna Förster*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two approaches for detecting tampering in still outdoor camera images are proposed: a rule-based method and a deep-learning model. Deep learning yields higher accuracy but higher computational and data demands; rule-based method is lighter-weight and better when resources or calibration time are limited. Public datasets (normal, blurred, rotated) are provided to support evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tampering of outdoor smart cameras compromises surveillance; detecting tampering in still images is particularly challenging due to the absence of frame sequences. There is a need for accurate, efficient tampering detection methods and accessible datasets.

Method: Two complementary approaches are evaluated: (1) a rule-based method (lightweight, rule-driven features) and (2) a deep-learning-based method (likely CNN/transformer-based) for classifying tampered vs. authentic images. Comparative evaluation focuses on accuracy, computational cost, and training data requirements under real-world conditions.

Result: Deep-learning model achieves higher detection accuracy; rule-based method offers lower computational load and reduced data/training requirements, making it suitable when resources or calibration time are constrained. Public datasets containing normal, blurred, and rotated images are released to facilitate method development and benchmarking.

Conclusion: There is a clear trade-off between accuracy and resource needs. Deep learning is preferable for higher accuracy when resources permit, while rule-based detection is advantageous for resource-constrained settings. The provided datasets support further development in camera tampering detection for still images.

Abstract: Recently, the use of smart cameras in outdoor settings has grown to improve surveillance and security. Nonetheless, these systems are susceptible to tampering, whether from deliberate vandalism or harsh environmental conditions, which can undermine their monitoring effectiveness. In this context, detecting camera tampering is more challenging when a camera is capturing still images rather than video as there is no sequence of continuous frames over time. In this study, we propose two approaches for detecting tampered images: a rule-based method and a deep-learning-based method. The aim is to evaluate how each method performs in terms of accuracy, computational demands, and the data required for training when applied to real-world scenarios. Our results show that the deep-learning model provides higher accuracy, while the rule-based method is more appropriate for scenarios where resources are limited and a prolonged calibration phase is impractical. We also offer publicly available datasets with normal, blurred, and rotated images to support the development and evaluation of camera tampering detection methods, addressing the need for such resources.

</details>


### [77] [Exploring the Temporal Consistency for Point-Level Weakly-Supervised Temporal Action Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05718)
*Yunchuan Ma,Laiyun Qing,Guorong Li,Yuqing Liu,Yuankai Qi,Qingming Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Multi-task self-supervised temporal understanding for point-supervised temporal action localization, introducing three tasks—Action Completion, Action Order Understanding, and Action Regularity Understanding—to model temporal consistency and improve localization; shows improvements on four benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Point-supervised (one-frame) annotation lacks explicit modeling of temporal frame relationships crucial for defining actions; leveraging temporal consistency can improve full-action localization.

Method: A multi-task learning framework that attaches three self-supervised temporal tasks to the point-supervised PTAL model: Action Completion, Action Order Understanding, Action Regularity Understanding, to enforce temporal coherence across frames; plus standard point-supervised snippet-level classification.

Result: Experiments on four benchmark datasets show the proposed method outperforms several state-of-the-art approaches, validating the effectiveness of incorporating temporal understanding tasks.

Conclusion: This work is the first to explicitly explore temporal consistency for point-supervised action localization; the proposed self-supervised tasks boost temporal understanding and localization performance, offering a new direction for PTAL research.

Abstract: Point-supervised Temporal Action Localization (PTAL) adopts a lightly frame-annotated paradigm (\textit{i.e.}, labeling only a single frame per action instance) to train a model to effectively locate action instances within untrimmed videos. Most existing approaches design the task head of models with only a point-supervised snippet-level classification, without explicit modeling of understanding temporal relationships among frames of an action. However, understanding the temporal relationships of frames is crucial because it can help a model understand how an action is defined and therefore benefits localizing the full frames of an action. To this end, in this paper, we design a multi-task learning framework that fully utilizes point supervision to boost the model's temporal understanding capability for action localization. Specifically, we design three self-supervised temporal understanding tasks: (i) Action Completion, (ii) Action Order Understanding, and (iii) Action Regularity Understanding. These tasks help a model understand the temporal consistency of actions across videos. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt to explicitly explore temporal consistency for point supervision action localization. Extensive experimental results on four benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method compared to several state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [78] [Adaptive Global and Fine-Grained Perceptual Fusion for MLLM Embeddings Compatible with Hard Negative Amplification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05729)
*Lexiang Hu,Youze Xue,Dian Li,Gang Liu,Zhouchen Lin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AGFF-Embed adapts MLLMs to produce multiple embeddings targeting different semantic dimensions, which are adaptively fused; with Explicit Gradient Amplification for hard negatives, it achieves state-of-the-art on MMEB and MMVP-VLM for both general and fine-grained understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global-only multimodal embeddings (e.g., CLIP, MLLMs) capture primarily global semantics and miss hybrid global+fine-grained patterns. A fusion mechanism across semantic dimensions is needed to handle complex, real-world scenarios.

Method: Prompt the MLLM to generate multiple embeddings focusing on diverse semantic dimensions; adaptively and smoothly fuse these embeddings. Enhance training with Explicit Gradient Amplification (EGA) to strengthen in-batch hard negatives without dataset editing.

Result: On MMEB and MMVP-VLM benchmarks, AGFF-Embed achieves state-of-the-art performance in both general and fine-grained understanding compared to other multimodal embedding models.

Conclusion: Adaptive global and fine-grained perceptual fusion with gradient-based hard negative amplification yields comprehensive state-of-the-art performance, validating multi-embedding fusion as a key strategy for robust multimodal representations.

Abstract: Multimodal embeddings serve as a bridge for aligning vision and language, with the two primary implementations -- CLIP-based and MLLM-based embedding models -- both limited to capturing only global semantic information. Although numerous studies have focused on fine-grained understanding, we observe that complex scenarios currently targeted by MLLM embeddings often involve a hybrid perceptual pattern of both global and fine-grained elements, thus necessitating a compatible fusion mechanism. In this paper, we propose Adaptive Global and Fine-grained perceptual Fusion for MLLM Embeddings (AGFF-Embed), a method that prompts the MLLM to generate multiple embeddings focusing on different dimensions of semantic information, which are then adaptively and smoothly aggregated. Furthermore, we adapt AGFF-Embed with the Explicit Gradient Amplification (EGA) technique to achieve in-batch hard negatives enhancement without requiring fine-grained editing of the dataset. Evaluation on the MMEB and MMVP-VLM benchmarks shows that AGFF-Embed comprehensively achieves state-of-the-art performance in both general and fine-grained understanding compared to other multimodal embedding models.

</details>


### [79] [Depth as Prior Knowledge for Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05730)
*Moussa Kassem Sbeyti,Nadja Klein*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DepthPrior uses depth as a non-architectural prior to improve small-object detection, via depth-aware training losses and thresholds, achieving significant gains without modifying detectors or adding sensors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Small/distant objects are hard to detect due to scale, low resolution, and clutter. Depth can help, but existing methods require complex architectural changes. The work seeks depth-informed supervision that avoids modifying detectors.

Method: Proposes DepthPrior with three components: Depth-Based Loss Weighting (DLW) to weight loss by depth, Depth-Based Loss Stratification (DLS) to stratify training losses by depth/diversity, and Depth-Aware Confidence Thresholding (DCT) during inference. Uses depth estimation as a prior rather than a feature fusion. Overhead is only depth estimation.

Result: Empirical validation on KITTI, MS COCO, VisDrone, SUN RGB-D using YOLOv11 and EfficientDet. Achieves up to +9% mAP_S and +7% mAR_S for small objects; inference recovery rates up to 95:1. Demonstrates comparable benefits without sensors, architectural changes, or additional performance costs.

Conclusion: DepthPrior shows that treating depth as prior knowledge rather than fused features can mitigate depth-induced performance degradation for small objects, offering practical gains with broad applicability and minimal overhead.

Abstract: Detecting small and distant objects remains challenging for object detectors due to scale variation, low resolution, and background clutter. Safety-critical applications require reliable detection of these objects for safe planning.
