Conclusion: Social signals in real-world HRI can be informative for error management but require strategies to handle noisiness; design implications for robust interpretation of social cues in group settings; challenges include noise, unpredictability, and data richness.

Abstract: In the real world, robots frequently make errors, yet little is known about people's social responses to errors outside of lab settings. Prior work has shown that social signals are reliable and useful for error management in constrained interactions, but it is unclear if this holds in the real world - especially with a non-social robot in repeated and group interactions with successive or propagated errors. To explore this, we built a coffee robot and conducted a public field deployment ($N = 49$). We found that participants consistently expressed varied social signals in response to errors and other stimuli, particularly during group interactions. Our findings suggest that social signals in the wild are rich (with participants volunteering information about the interaction), but "noisy." We discuss lessons, benefits, and challenges for using social signals in real-world HRI.

</details>


### [107] [Differentiable Inverse Graphics for Zero-shot Scene Reconstruction and Robot Grasping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05029)
*Octavio Arriaga,Proneet Sharma,Jichen Guo,Marc Otto,Siddhant Kadwe,Rebecca Adam*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A differentiable neuro-graphics framework combines neural foundation models with physics-based differentiable rendering to achieve zero-shot scene reconstruction and grasping from a single RGB-D image, without extra 3D data or test-time samples, outperforming model-free few-shot pose estimation methods and enabling zero-shot grasping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce data requirements and improve interpretability and generalization of robot perception and manipulation in novel environments by producing physically-consistent scene representations without heavy reliance on large 3D datasets or test-time sampling.

Method: A differentiable neuro-graphics model that fuses neural foundation models with physics-based differentiable rendering. It solves constrained optimization to jointly estimate scene parameters (meshes, lighting, material properties) and 6D object poses from a single RGB-D image and bounding boxes, without additional 3D data or test-time samples.

Result: On standard model-free few-shot benchmarks, it outperforms existing model-free few-shot pose estimation methods; zero-shot grasping experiments validate the accuracy of scene reconstructions and manipulation.

Conclusion: The approach offers a data-efficient, interpretable, and potentially generalizable pathway for robot autonomy in unseen environments by enabling physically-consistent, zero-shot scene reconstruction and grasping without extensive datasets.

Abstract: Operating effectively in novel real-world environments requires robotic systems to estimate and interact with previously unseen objects. Current state-of-the-art models address this challenge by using large amounts of training data and test-time samples to build black-box scene representations. In this work, we introduce a differentiable neuro-graphics model that combines neural foundation models with physics-based differentiable rendering to perform zero-shot scene reconstruction and robot grasping without relying on any additional 3D data or test-time samples. Our model solves a series of constrained optimization problems to estimate physically consistent scene parameters, such as meshes, lighting conditions, material properties, and 6D poses of previously unseen objects from a single RGBD image and bounding boxes. We evaluated our approach on standard model-free few-shot benchmarks and demonstrated that it outperforms existing algorithms for model-free few-shot pose estimation. Furthermore, we validated the accuracy of our scene reconstructions by applying our algorithm to a zero-shot grasping task. By enabling zero-shot, physically-consistent scene reconstruction and grasping without reliance on extensive datasets or test-time sampling, our approach offers a pathway towards more data efficient, interpretable and generalizable robot autonomy in novel environments.

</details>


### [108] [Reinforcement Learning Enhancement Using Vector Semantic Representation and Symbolic Reasoning for Human-Centered Autonomous Emergency Braking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05079)
*Vinal Asodia,Iman Sharifi,Saber Fallah*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A neuro-symbolic perception and soft-logic Reward framework improves autonomy driving DRL. It couples a neuro-symbolic feature representation (semantic, spatial, shape cues with boosted dynamic-entity features) with a Soft First-Order Logic (SFOL) reward module, enabling context-aware, value-aligned policy learning. CARLA experiments show robustness and safety gains over baselines across densities/occlusions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing camera-based DRL methods often lack high-level scene context in representations and rely on fixed, rigid reward structures, which hamper safety-critical decision-making in dynamic road environments.

Method: Propose a pipeline that builds a neuro-symbolic feature representation combining semantic, spatial, and shape information with spatially boosted features of dynamic entities; employ a Soft First-Order Logic (SFOL) reward function that uses a symbolic reasoning module to assign reward weights. Semantic and spatial predicates are extracted from segmentation maps and applied to linguistic rules to derive rewards.

Result: In CARLA experiments, the neuro-symbolic representation and SFOL reward function improved policy robustness and safety-related metrics compared to baseline representations and reward formulations across varying traffic densities and occlusion levels.

Conclusion: Integrating holistic representations with soft symbolic reasoning enables more context-aware and value-aligned decision-making for autonomous driving.

Abstract: The problem with existing camera-based Deep Reinforcement Learning approaches is twofold: they rarely integrate high-level scene context into the feature representation, and they rely on rigid, fixed reward functions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel pipeline that produces a neuro-symbolic feature representation that encompasses semantic, spatial, and shape information, as well as spatially boosted features of dynamic entities in the scene, with an emphasis on safety-critical road users. It also proposes a Soft First-Order Logic (SFOL) reward function that balances human values via a symbolic reasoning module. Here, semantic and spatial predicates are extracted from segmentation maps and applied to linguistic rules to obtain reward weights. Quantitative experiments in the CARLA simulation environment show that the proposed neuro-symbolic representation and SFOL reward function improved policy robustness and safety-related performance metrics compared to baseline representations and reward formulations across varying traffic densities and occlusion levels. The findings demonstrate that integrating holistic representations and soft reasoning into Reinforcement Learning can support more context-aware and value-aligned decision-making for autonomous driving.

</details>


### [109] [A Framework for Combining Optimization-Based and Analytic Inverse Kinematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05092)
*Thomas Cohn,Lihan Tang,Alexandre Amice,Russ Tedrake*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A new optimization IK formulation uses analytic IK as a change of variables, yielding higher success rates across challenging IK tasks (collision avoidance, grasp selection, humanoid stability) when tested on three solvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optimization-based inverse kinematics (IK) methods struggle due to the highly nonlinear joint-to-end-effector mapping and nonconvex constraints (e.g., collisions). A unified approach that leverages analytic IK could simplify optimization and improve robustness.

Method: Introduce a change-of-variables by embedding an analytic IK solution into the optimization problem. Evaluate the approach on three popular solvers representing distinct nonlinear-constrained optimization paradigms and compare with the old formulation and baseline methods across various IK problems.

Result: The proposed formulation achieves higher success rates than the old formulation and baseline methods across challenging IK tasks, including collision avoidance, grasp selection, and humanoid stability.

Conclusion: Using an analytic IK solution as a change of variables significantly improves optimization-based IK performance and offers a unified framework that benefits multiple solvers and problem classes.

Abstract: Analytic and optimization methods for solving inverse kinematics (IK) problems have been deeply studied throughout the history of robotics. The two strategies have complementary strengths and weaknesses, but developing a unified approach to take advantage of both methods has proved challenging. A key challenge faced by optimization approaches is the complicated nonlinear relationship between the joint angles and the end-effector pose. When this must be handled concurrently with additional nonconvex constraints like collision avoidance, optimization IK algorithms may suffer high failure rates. We present a new formulation for optimization IK that uses an analytic IK solution as a change of variables, and is fundamentally easier for optimizers to solve. We test our methodology on three popular solvers, representing three different paradigms for constrained nonlinear optimization. Extensive experimental comparisons demonstrate that our new formulation achieves higher success rates than the old formulation and baseline methods across various challenging IK problems, including collision avoidance, grasp selection, and humanoid stability.

</details>


### [110] [PLATO Hand: Shaping Contact Behavior with Fingernails for Precise Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05156)
*Dong Ho Kang,Aaron Kim,Mingyo Seo,Kazuto Yokoyama,Tetsuya Narita,Luis Sentis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A dexterous robotic hand (PLATO Hand) with a hybrid rigid-nail fingertip embedded in compliant pulp; supports diverse object geometries via strain-energy-guided contact, yielding improved pinching, force observability, and capability on edge-sensitive tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable precise, robust manipulation across varied geometries by shaping fingertip contact and maintaining force-motion transparency, addressing limitations of purely compliant or rigid grippers.

Method: Design of a hybrid fingertip with an internal rigid fingernail inside compliant pulp; development of a strain-energy-based bending-indentation model to guide fingertip geometry and explain contact mechanics; experimental evaluation on pinching stability, force observability, and edge-sensitive tasks (paper singulation, card picking, orange peeling).

Result: Demonstrates improved pinching stability, enhanced force observability, and successful completion of edge-sensitive manipulations, indicating the effectiveness of coupling structured contact geometry with a force-motion transparent mechanism.

Conclusion: Coupling structured contact geometry with a force-motion transparent, physically embodied mechanism provides a principled approach to precise manipulation across varied object geometries.

Abstract: We present the PLATO Hand, a dexterous robotic hand with a hybrid fingertip that embeds a rigid fingernail within a compliant pulp. This design shapes contact behavior to enable diverse interaction modes across a range of object geometries. We develop a strain-energy-based bending-indentation model to guide the fingertip design and to explain how guided contact preserves local indentation while suppressing global bending. Experimental results show that the proposed robotic hand design demonstrates improved pinching stability, enhanced force observability, and successful execution of edge-sensitive manipulation tasks, including paper singulation, card picking, and orange peeling. Together, these results show that coupling structured contact geometry with a force-motion transparent mechanism provides a principled, physically embodied approach to precise manipulation.

</details>


### [111] [Informative Path Planning with Guaranteed Estimation Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05198)
*Kalvik Jakkala,Saurav Agarwal,Jason O'Kane,Srinivas Akella*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GP-informed informative path planning with guaranteed estimation uncertainty; a three-stage pipeline (learn GP, kernel-to-coverage maps, near-shortest routing) that handles nonstationary kernels and obstacles, with approximation guarantees and real-world validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce unnecessary sampling and energy use in environmental field surveys while ensuring reconstruction quality via uncertainty guarantees under GP modeling.

Method: 1) Learn a GP from priors. 2) Transform the GP kernel into binary coverage maps per candidate sensing location indicating where uncertainty can be reduced below a target. 3) Plan a near-shortest route whose combined coverage meets a global posterior-variance constraint; extend with nonstationary kernels and obstacle handling; provide approximation guarantees for sensing-location selection and joint selection/routing under travel budget.

Result: Plans meet the uncertainty target using fewer sensing locations and shorter travel than a recent baseline on real topographic data; field experiments with bathymetry-mapping AUVs and USVs demonstrate real-world feasibility.

Conclusion: Bridges classical coverage and IPP by providing guaranteed uncertainty-aware planning; accommodates spatially varying correlations and non-convex environments; validated with real data and field deployments.

Abstract: Environmental monitoring robots often need to reconstruct spatial fields (e.g., salinity, temperature, bathymetry) under tight distance and energy constraints. Classical boustrophedon lawnmower surveys provide geometric coverage guarantees but can waste effort by oversampling predictable regions. In contrast, informative path planning (IPP) methods leverage spatial correlations to reduce oversampling, yet typically offer no guarantees on reconstruction quality. This paper bridges these approaches by addressing informative path planning with guaranteed estimation uncertainty: computing the shortest path whose measurements ensure that the Gaussian-process (GP) posterior variance -- an intrinsic uncertainty measure that lower-bounds the mean-squared prediction error under the GP model -- falls below a user-specified threshold over the monitoring region.
  We propose a three-stage approach: (i) learn a GP model from available prior information; (ii) transform the learned GP kernel into binary coverage maps for each candidate sensing location, indicating which locations' uncertainty can be reduced below a specified target; and (iii) plan a near-shortest route whose combined coverage satisfies the global uncertainty constraint. To address heterogeneous phenomena, we incorporate a nonstationary kernel that captures spatially varying correlation structure, and we accommodate non-convex environments with obstacles. Algorithmically, we present methods with provable approximation guarantees for sensing-location selection and for the joint selection-and-routing problem under a travel budget. Experiments on real-world topographic data show that our planners meet the uncertainty target using fewer sensing locations and shorter travel distances than a recent baseline, and field experiments with bathymetry-mapping autonomous surface and underwater vehicles demonstrate real-world feasibility.

</details>


### [112] [MobileManiBench: Simplifying Model Verification for Mobile Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05233)
*Wenbo Wang,Fangyun Wei,QiXiu Li,Xi Chen,Yaobo Liang,Chang Xu,Jiaolong Yang,Baining Guo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Simulation-first MobileManiBench benchmark for mobile robotic manipulation with rich annotations and 300K trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome reliance on teleoperation-heavy, static-tabletop datasets by providing a scalable, controllable, simulation-based benchmark to study vision-language-action models (VLA) in mobile manipulation.

Method: NVIDIA Isaac Sim-based pipeline that uses reinforcement learning to autonomously generate diverse manipulation trajectories with annotations: language instructions, multi-view RGB-depth-segmentation images, synchronized object/robot states and actions. Features two mobile platforms (parallel-gripper and dexterous-hand), two synchronized cameras, 630 objects in 20 categories, 5 skills (open, close, pull, push, pick), 100 tasks across 100 scenes, yielding ~300K trajectories.

Result: Benchmarking of representative VLA models conducted; provides insights into perception, reasoning, and control in complex simulated environments.

Conclusion: A simulation-first framework and large-scale benchmark enable controlled studies of robot embodiments, sensing modalities, and policy architectures, accelerating research on data efficiency and generalization in VLA-enabled manipulation.

Abstract: Vision-language-action models have advanced robotic manipulation but remain constrained by reliance on the large, teleoperation-collected datasets dominated by the static, tabletop scenes. We propose a simulation-first framework to verify VLA architectures before real-world deployment and introduce MobileManiBench, a large-scale benchmark for mobile-based robotic manipulation. Built on NVIDIA Isaac Sim and powered by reinforcement learning, our pipeline autonomously generates diverse manipulation trajectories with rich annotations (language instructions, multi-view RGB-depth-segmentation images, synchronized object/robot states and actions). MobileManiBench features 2 mobile platforms (parallel-gripper and dexterous-hand robots), 2 synchronized cameras (head and right wrist), 630 objects in 20 categories, 5 skills (open, close, pull, push, pick) with over 100 tasks performed in 100 realistic scenes, yielding 300K trajectories. This design enables controlled, scalable studies of robot embodiments, sensing modalities, and policy architectures, accelerating research on data efficiency and generalization. We benchmark representative VLA models and report insights into perception, reasoning, and control in complex simulated environments.

</details>


### [113] [Low-Cost Underwater In-Pipe Centering and Inspection Using a Minimal-Sensing Robot](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05265)
*Kalvik Jakkala,Jason O'Kane*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Minimal-sensing autonomous underwater pipe traversal using IMU, pressure, and two sonars; introduces range extraction from single-beam intensity, a closed-form pipe-centering model, and a confidence-weighted PD controller; demonstrates in-pipe traversal in a 46 cm pipe with a BlueROV2, validating lightweight, robust in-pipe navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable reliable autonomous inspection of submerged pipelines in confined, cluttered, and low-visibility environments where traditional localization signals are scarce and heavy sensors are impractical.

Method: Combine IMU, pressure sensor, downward single-beam sonar, and 360-degree rotating sonar to estimate pipe geometry. Develop an efficient method to infer wall range from single-beam sonar intensity. Use two range estimates in a closed-form geometric model to compute pipe center, and apply an adaptive confidence-weighted PD controller to keep alignment while traversing.

Result: Experimental validation in a submerged 46 cm-diameter pipe with a heavy ROV (BlueROV2). The system achieved stable centering and successful full-pipe traversal despite ambient flow and pipe deformation, without external tracking or Doppler velocity log.

Conclusion: A lightweight, computationally efficient sensing and processing architecture can enable reliable in-pipe navigation and inspection, broadening the practicality of autonomous underwater pipeline inspection in confined environments.

Abstract: Autonomous underwater inspection of submerged pipelines is challenging due to confined geometries, turbidity, and the scarcity of reliable localization cues. This paper presents a minimal-sensing strategy that enables a free-swimming underwater robot to center itself and traverse a flooded pipe of known radius using only an IMU, a pressure sensor, and two sonars: a downward-facing single-beam sonar and a rotating 360 degree sonar. We introduce a computationally efficient method for extracting range estimates from single-beam sonar intensity data, enabling reliable wall detection in noisy and reverberant conditions. A closed-form geometric model leverages the two sonar ranges to estimate the pipe center, and an adaptive, confidence-weighted proportional-derivative (PD) controller maintains alignment during traversal. The system requires no Doppler velocity log, external tracking, or complex multi-sensor arrays. Experiments in a submerged 46 cm-diameter pipe using a Blue Robotics BlueROV2 heavy remotely operated vehicle demonstrate stable centering and successful full-pipe traversal despite ambient flow and structural deformations. These results show that reliable in-pipe navigation and inspection can be achieved with a lightweight, computationally efficient sensing and processing architecture, advancing the practicality of autonomous underwater inspection in confined environments.

</details>


### [114] [Affordance-Aware Interactive Decision-Making and Execution for Ambiguous Instructions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05273)
*Hengxuan Xu,Fengbo Lan,Zhixin Zhao,Shengjie Wang,Mengqiao Liu,Jieqian Sun,Yu Cheng,Tao Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AIDE is a dual-stream framework combining interactive exploration with vision-language reasoning to handle ambiguous instructions via zero-shot affordance analysis, achieving high planning and execution performance (over 80% planning success and 95% closed-loop accuracy at 10 Hz).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots struggle to interpret vague human instructions and interact with unknown environments, leading to inefficient reasoning and poor real-time planning.

Method: Proposes Affordance-Aware Interactive Decision-Making and Execution (AIDE) with two parallel streams: Multi-Stage Inference (MSI) for decision-making and Accelerated Decision-Making (ADM) for execution; enables zero-shot affordance analysis and interprets ambiguous cues; integrates interactive exploration with Vision-Language Reasoning.

Result: In simulation and real-world tests, AIDE achieves >80% task planning success and >95% closed-loop execution accuracy at 10 Hz, outperforming existing VLM-based approaches across diverse open-world scenes.

Conclusion: AIDE demonstrates effective affordance-aware, interactive planning and execution under ambiguous instructions, enabling robust open-world robot autonomy.

Abstract: Enabling robots to explore and act in unfamiliar environments under ambiguous human instructions by interactively identifying task-relevant objects (e.g., identifying cups or beverages for "I'm thirsty") remains challenging for existing vision-language model (VLM)-based methods. This challenge stems from inefficient reasoning and the lack of environmental interaction, which hinder real-time task planning and execution. To address this, We propose Affordance-Aware Interactive Decision-Making and Execution for Ambiguous Instructions (AIDE), a dual-stream framework that integrates interactive exploration with vision-language reasoning, where Multi-Stage Inference (MSI) serves as the decision-making stream and Accelerated Decision-Making (ADM) as the execution stream, enabling zero-shot affordance analysis and interpretation of ambiguous instructions. Extensive experiments in simulation and real-world environments show that AIDE achieves the task planning success rate of over 80\% and more than 95\% accuracy in closed-loop continuous execution at 10 Hz, outperforming existing VLM-based methods in diverse open-world scenarios.

</details>


### [115] [Learning Soccer Skills for Humanoid Robots: A Progressive Perception-Action Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05310)
*Jipeng Kong,Xinzhe Liu,Yuhang Lin,Jinrui Han,Sören Schwertfeger,Chenjia Bai,Xuelong Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PAiD is a three-stage, progressive architecture for humanoid soccer that stabilizes perception-action learning, generalizes to different positions, and bridges sim-to-real gaps, achieving robust kicking on Unitree G1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humanoid soccer demands tight perception–action integration; existing modular pipelines often destabilize when components interact, while end-to-end methods can conflict in optimization objectives. A stable, scalable approach is needed to acquire embodied skills that transfer to real robots.

Method: A progressive, three-stage framework: (1) motion-skill acquisition via human motion tracking to capture high-fidelity kinematics; (2) lightweight perception–action integration to enable positional generalization without reward conflicts; (3) physics-aware sim-to-real transfer to minimize the reality gap. This divide-and-conquer design aims to establish stable foundations before advancing complexity.

Result: On Unitree G1, the method yields high-fidelity, human-like kicking with robustness across conditions such as static/rolling balls, various positions, disturbances, and indoor/outdoor scenarios, with consistent execution.

Conclusion: The PAiD framework advances robust humanoid soccer capabilities and provides a scalable, staged pathway for acquiring complex embodied skills across perception, action, and physics-based transfer. A project page is provided for details.

Abstract: Soccer presents a significant challenge for humanoid robots, demanding tightly integrated perception-action capabilities for tasks like perception-guided kicking and whole-body balance control. Existing approaches suffer from inter-module instability in modular pipelines or conflicting training objectives in end-to-end frameworks. We propose Perception-Action integrated Decision-making (PAiD), a progressive architecture that decomposes soccer skill acquisition into three stages: motion-skill acquisition via human motion tracking, lightweight perception-action integration for positional generalization, and physics-aware sim-to-real transfer. This staged decomposition establishes stable foundational skills, avoids reward conflicts during perception integration, and minimizes sim-to-real gaps. Experiments on the Unitree G1 demonstrate high-fidelity human-like kicking with robust performance under diverse conditions-including static or rolling balls, various positions, and disturbances-while maintaining consistent execution across indoor and outdoor scenarios. Our divide-and-conquer strategy advances robust humanoid soccer capabilities and offers a scalable framework for complex embodied skill acquisition. The project page is available at https://soccer-humanoid.github.io/.

</details>


### [116] [RoboPaint: From Human Demonstration to Any Robot and Any View](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05325)
*Jiacheng Fan,Zhiyue Zhao,Yiqian Zhang,Chao Chen,Peide Wang,Hengdi Zhang,Zhengxue Cheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A Real-Sim-Real data pipeline converts human demonstrations into robot-executable training data without direct teleoperation by capturing multimodal demonstrations in standardized rooms, retargeting to a dexterous robot hand with geometry/force optimization, and rendering trajectories in Isaac Sim for VLA training; results show high success in real robot tasks and strong VLA policy performance when trained on the generated data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the data bottleneck for scaling Vision-Language-Action models in dexterous manipulation by turning human demonstrations into environment-specific, robot-executable data that reduces reliance on teleoperation.

Method: 1) Collect synchronized multimodal human demonstrations (RGB-D videos, RGB videos, glove joint angles, tactile signals) in standardized rooms. 2) Apply a tactile-aware retargeting method mapping human hand states to robot dex-hand states using geometry and force-guided optimization. 3) Render retargeted trajectories in a photorealistic Isaac Sim environment to produce training data for VLA models.

Result: Retargeted dex-hand trajectories achieve 84% success across 10 diverse object manipulation tasks. VLA policies trained exclusively on the generated data achieve 80% average success on three tasks (pick-and-place, pushing, pouring).

Conclusion: The Real-Sim-Real pipeline is a scalable, cost-effective alternative to or augmentation of teleoperation, enabling efficient production of high-quality robot training data with minimal performance loss.

Abstract: Acquiring large-scale, high-fidelity robot demonstration data remains a critical bottleneck for scaling Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models in dexterous manipulation. We propose a Real-Sim-Real data collection and data editing pipeline that transforms human demonstrations into robot-executable, environment-specific training data without direct robot teleoperation. Standardized data collection rooms are built to capture multimodal human demonstrations (synchronized 3 RGB-D videos, 11 RGB videos, 29-DoF glove joint angles, and 14-channel tactile signals). Based on these human demonstrations, we introduce a tactile-aware retargeting method that maps human hand states to robot dex-hand states via geometry and force-guided optimization. Then the retargeted robot trajectories are rendered in a photorealistic Isaac Sim environment to build robot training data. Real world experiments have demonstrated: (1) The retargeted dex-hand trajectories achieve an 84\% success rate across 10 diverse object manipulation tasks. (2) VLA policies (Pi0.5) trained exclusively on our generated data achieve 80\% average success rate on three representative tasks, i.e., pick-and-place, pushing and pouring. To conclude, robot training data can be efficiently "painted" from human demonstrations using our real-sim-real data pipeline. We offer a scalable, cost-effective alternative to teleoperation with minimal performance loss for complex dexterous manipulation.

</details>


### [117] [Benchmarking Affordance Generalization with BusyBox](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05441)
*Dean Fortier,Timothy Adamson,Tess Hellebrekers,Teresa LaScala,Kofi Ennin,Michael Murray,Andrey Kolobov,Galen Mullins*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BusyBox is a physical benchmark for evaluating affordance generalization in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. It uses modular, visually diverse but functionally identical components to test generalization across variants. Strong open-weight VLAs still struggle with these variants. The authors provide open resources (CAD, BOM, dataset) to foster community benchmarking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess affordance generalization in VLA systems, a key meta-skill for robust robotic manipulation where appearance changes should not impede understanding of actionable affordances. Provide a reproducible, accessible benchmark to drive progress and fair comparisons across models.

Method: Design BusyBox with 6 modular components (switches, sliders, wires, buttons, display, dial) that can be swapped/rotated to create many variants with the same affordances. Conduct semi-automatic evaluation of VLAs on BusyBox variants using strong models (e.g., π0.5, GR00T-N1.6). Release CAD files, BOM, and a dataset of language-annotated demonstrations collected with the Mobile Aloha robot in canonical configuration.

Result: Generalization across BusyBox variants is significantly challenging even for capable open-weight VLAs, indicating a substantial gap in affordance generalization. The benchmark quantifies this limitation and provides evidence that visual variation alone can hinder manipulation instructions grounded in language.

Conclusion: BusyBox offers a practical, extensible platform for systematic affordance-generalization evaluation in VLAs. By releasing build materials and a language-annotated demonstrations dataset, it enables reproducible benchmarking and encourages future work to close the gap between visual-language understanding and robust manipulation across varied physical appearances.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have been attracting the attention of researchers and practitioners thanks to their promise of generalization. Although single-task policies still offer competitive performance, VLAs are increasingly able to handle commands and environments unseen in their training set. While generalization in vision and language space is undoubtedly important for robust versatile behaviors, a key meta-skill VLAs need to possess is affordance generalization -- the ability to manipulate new objects with familiar physical features.
  In this work, we present BusyBox, a physical benchmark for systematic semi-automatic evaluation of VLAs' affordance generalization. BusyBox consists of 6 modules with switches, sliders, wires, buttons, a display, and a dial. The modules can be swapped and rotated to create a multitude of BusyBox variations with different visual appearances but the same set of affordances. We empirically demonstrate that generalization across BusyBox variants is highly challenging even for strong open-weights VLAs such as $π_{0.5}$ and GR00T-N1.6. To encourage the research community to evaluate their own VLAs on BusyBox and to propose new affordance generalization experiments, we have designed BusyBox to be easy to build in most robotics labs. We release the full set of CAD files for 3D-printing its parts as well as a bill of materials for (optionally) assembling its electronics. We also publish a dataset of language-annotated demonstrations that we collected using the common bimanual Mobile Aloha robot on the canonical BusyBox configuration. All of the released materials are available at https://microsoft.github.io/BusyBox.

</details>


### [118] [Ontology-Driven Robotic Specification Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05456)
*Maksym Figat,Ryan M. Mackey,Michel D. Ingham*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Ontology-driven RSTM2 transforms high-level robotic goals into executable, stochastic Petri-net models with resources, enabling Monte Carlo simulations across mission/system/subsystem levels and explainable AI-assisted specification synthesis for complex multi-robot systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between high-level objectives and formal, executable specifications in safety- and mission-critical robotics; support architectural trades, resource allocation under uncertainty, and adaptive autonomous behavior; enable explainable AI-enabled assistants for autonomous specification synthesis.

Method: A hierarchical, ontology-driven approach using stochastic timed Petri nets with resources (RSTM2). It supports task-to-model transformation, Monte Carlo simulations at multiple levels (mission, system, subsystem), and autonomous specification synthesis aided by ontologies and explainable AI.

Result: A hypothetical case study demonstrates the method's ability to support architectural trades, optimize resource allocation, and analyze performance under uncertainty. The framework showcases potential applicability to complex multi-robot systems (e.g., NASA CADRE) through decentralized, resource-aware, adaptive autonomy.

Conclusion: Ontological concepts enable explainable AI-based assistants for fully autonomous specification synthesis. RSTM2 is positioned as a valuable methodology for future complex multi-robot systems that require decentralized, resource-aware, and adaptive autonomy, as exemplified by missions like NASA CADRE.

Abstract: This paper addresses robotic system engineering for safety- and mission-critical applications by bridging the gap between high-level objectives and formal, executable specifications. The proposed method, Robotic System Task to Model Transformation Methodology (RSTM2) is an ontology-driven, hierarchical approach using stochastic timed Petri nets with resources, enabling Monte Carlo simulations at mission, system, and subsystem levels. A hypothetical case study demonstrates how the RSTM2 method supports architectural trades, resource allocation, and performance analysis under uncertainty. Ontological concepts further enable explainable AI-based assistants, facilitating fully autonomous specification synthesis. The methodology offers particular benefits to complex multi-robot systems, such as the NASA CADRE mission, representing decentralized, resource-aware, and adaptive autonomous systems of the future.

</details>


### [119] [TaSA: Two-Phased Deep Predictive Learning of Tactile Sensory Attenuation for Improving In-Grasp Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05468)
*Pranav Ponnivalavan,Satoshi Funabashi,Alexander Schmitz,Tetsuya Ogata,Shigeki Sugano*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TaSA is a two-phase predictive learning framework that models self-touch dynamics and uses this model to bias manipulation policies toward external object-contact signals, yielding higher success on insertion tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans rely on self-touch perception and sensory attenuation to distinguish self-generated tactile feedback from external contacts. Robots often ignore self-contact, risking misinterpretation and damage. Incorporating predictive self-touch can improve dexterous manipulation and generalization.

Method: Two-phase deep predictive learning. Phase 1 learns self-touch dynamics by predicting tactile feedback from the robot's own actions. Phase 2 uses this learned model to guide the motion policy, emphasizing object-contact signals during manipulation.

Result: Policies trained with TaSA achieve significantly higher success rates than baselines across multiple insertion tasks (pencil lead, coins into a slot, paper clip onto paper) with varying orientations and sizes.

Conclusion: Structured tactile perception based on self-touch and sensory attenuation is critical for dexterous robotic manipulation, and TaSA provides an effective framework to realize it.

Abstract: Humans can achieve diverse in-hand manipulations, such as object pinching and tool use, which often involve simultaneous contact between the object and multiple fingers. This is still an open issue for robotic hands because such dexterous manipulation requires distinguishing between tactile sensations generated by their self-contact and those arising from external contact. Otherwise, object/robot breakage happens due to contacts/collisions. Indeed, most approaches ignore self-contact altogether, by constraining motion to avoid/ignore self-tactile information during contact. While this reduces complexity, it also limits generalization to real-world scenarios where self-contact is inevitable. Humans overcome this challenge through self-touch perception, using predictive mechanisms that anticipate the tactile consequences of their own motion, through a principle called sensory attenuation, where the nervous system differentiates predictable self-touch signals, allowing novel object stimuli to stand out as relevant. Deriving from this, we introduce TaSA, a two-phased deep predictive learning framework. In the first phase, TaSA explicitly learns self-touch dynamics, modeling how a robot's own actions generate tactile feedback. In the second phase, this learned model is incorporated into the motion learning phase, to emphasize object contact signals during manipulation. We evaluate TaSA on a set of insertion tasks, which demand fine tactile discrimination: inserting a pencil lead into a mechanical pencil, inserting coins into a slot, and fixing a paper clip onto a sheet of paper, with various orientations, positions, and sizes. Across all tasks, policies trained with TaSA achieve significantly higher success rates than baseline methods, demonstrating that structured tactile perception with self-touch based on sensory attenuation is critical for dexterous robotic manipulation.

</details>


### [120] [DECO: Decoupled Multimodal Diffusion Transformer for Bimanual Dexterous Manipulation with a Plugin Tactile Adapter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05513)
*Xukun Li,Yu Sun,Lei Zhang,Bosheng Huang,Yibo Peng,Yuan Meng,Haojun Jiang,Shaoxuan Xie,Guacai Yao,Alois Knoll,Zhenshan Bing,Xinlong Wang,Zhenguo Sun*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DECO introduces a DiT-based multimodal policy that decouples conditioning across modalities, using joint self-attention for image and action tokens, adaptive layer normalization for proprioception, cross-attention for tactile signals, and a LoRA adapter for efficient fine-tuning, complemented by the DECO-50 tactile dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable robust multimodal manipulation by decoupling conditioning across modalities and providing an efficient fine-tuning pathway, supported by a large tactile-rich dataset.

Method: A DiT-based policy where image and action tokens interact via joint self-attention. Proprioceptive states and optional conditions are injected through adaptive layer normalization. Tactile signals are injected through cross-attention. A lightweight LoRA-based adapter enables efficient fine-tuning of the pretrained policy.

Result: Introduces the DECO framework and the accompanying DECO-50 dataset. DECO-50 comprises 4 scenarios and 28 sub-tasks, with over 50 hours of data, about 5 million frames, and 8,000 successful trajectories.

Conclusion: DECO provides a modular, efficiently fine-tunable multimodal manipulation framework and a substantial tactile-enabled dataset to advance bimanual dexterous control.

Abstract: Overview of the Proposed DECO Framework.} DECO is a DiT-based policy that decouples multimodal conditioning. Image and action tokens interact via joint self attention, while proprioceptive states and optional conditions are injected through adaptive layer normalization. Tactile signals are injected via cross attention, while a lightweight LoRA-based adapter is used to efficiently fine-tune the pretrained policy. DECO is also accompanied by DECO-50, a bimanual dexterous manipulation dataset with tactile sensing, consisting of 4 scenarios and 28 sub-tasks, covering more than 50 hours of data, approximately 5 million frames, and 8,000 successful trajectories.

</details>


### [121] [Virtual-Tube-Based Cooperative Transport Control for Multi-UAV Systems in Constrained Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05516)
*Runxiao Liu,Pengda Mao,Xiangli Le,Shuang Gu,Yapeng Chen,Quan Quan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel, low-computation, dissipative-system–based control framework using virtual tubes for cooperative, obstacle-aware, cable-suspended UAV supervision, achieving tension distribution and robust multi-UAV coordination with simulations and outdoor experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable safe, scalable coordination of multiple UAVs carrying cable-suspended loads in cluttered environments, addressing the need for robust, low-overhead control in constrained spaces.

Method: Integrates virtual tube theory with dissipative systems theory to coordinate multi-UAVs and manage tension in a cable-load system. The framework dynamically reconfigures UAV roles/configurations based on obstacle layouts to facilitate efficient navigation while maintaining stability with low computational overhead.

Result: Extensive simulations demonstrate scalability to large UAV teams; outdoor experiments validate practical feasibility and robustness under real-world conditions.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a stable, scalable, and practical solution for cooperative cable-suspended load transportation in constrained environments, with potential for real-world deployment.

Abstract: This paper proposes a novel control framework for cooperative transportation of cable-suspended loads by multiple unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) operating in constrained environments. Leveraging virtual tube theory and principles from dissipative systems theory, the framework facilitates efficient multi-UAV collaboration for navigating obstacle-rich areas. The proposed framework offers several key advantages. (1) It achieves tension distribution and coordinated transportation within the UAV-cable-load system with low computational overhead, dynamically adapting UAV configurations based on obstacle layouts to facilitate efficient navigation. (2) By integrating dissipative systems theory, the framework ensures high stability and robustness, essential for complex multi-UAV operations. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated through extensive simulations, demonstrating its scalability for large-scale multi-UAV systems. Furthermore, the method is experimentally validated in outdoor scenarios, showcasing its practical feasibility and robustness under real-world conditions.

</details>


### [122] [VLN-Pilot: Large Vision-Language Model as an Autonomous Indoor Drone Operator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05552)
*Bessie Dominguez-Dager,Sergio Suescun-Ferrandiz,Felix Escalona,Francisco Gomez-Donoso,Miguel Cazorla*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A Vision-and-Language Large Model (VLLM) serves as an autonomous indoor drone pilot that grounds free-form natural language instructions in visual observations to plan and execute trajectories without GPS. Evaluated in a photorealistic indoor sim, it achieves high success on long-horizon, multi-target tasks, suggesting potential to reduce operator workload and increase safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable flexible, instruction-following UAV control in GPS-denied indoor environments by leveraging multimodal reasoning, thereby reducing engineering requirements and reliance on manual pilots.

Method: Introduce VLN-Pilot, a framework where a large VLLM reasons over language and visual input to interpret instructions, ground them in observed scenes, and generate high-level flight behaviors and plans. The system performs obstacle avoidance and dynamic reactivity without traditional rule-based path planning, validated on a custom photorealistic indoor simulation benchmark.

Result: Demonstrates high success rates on complex instruction-following tasks, including long-horizon navigation with multiple semantic targets, indicating that a VLLM-driven agent can autonomously follow instructions and potentially reduce operator workload while enhancing safety and mission flexibility.

Conclusion: VLLM-based pilots can replace remote drone pilots for scalable, human-friendly control of indoor UAVs in constrained environments, with strong implications for inspection, search-and-rescue, and facility monitoring. The approach hints at improved safety and adaptability, though real-world validation and robustness remain as future work.

Abstract: This paper introduces VLN-Pilot, a novel framework in which a large Vision-and-Language Model (VLLM) assumes the role of a human pilot for indoor drone navigation. By leveraging the multimodal reasoning abilities of VLLMs, VLN-Pilot interprets free-form natural language instructions and grounds them in visual observations to plan and execute drone trajectories in GPS-denied indoor environments. Unlike traditional rule-based or geometric path-planning approaches, our framework integrates language-driven semantic understanding with visual perception, enabling context-aware, high-level flight behaviors with minimal task-specific engineering. VLN-Pilot supports fully autonomous instruction-following for drones by reasoning about spatial relationships, obstacle avoidance, and dynamic reactivity to unforeseen events. We validate our framework on a custom photorealistic indoor simulation benchmark and demonstrate the ability of the VLLM-driven agent to achieve high success rates on complex instruction-following tasks, including long-horizon navigation with multiple semantic targets. Experimental results highlight the promise of replacing remote drone pilots with a language-guided autonomous agent, opening avenues for scalable, human-friendly control of indoor UAVs in tasks such as inspection, search-and-rescue, and facility monitoring. Our results suggest that VLLM-based pilots may dramatically reduce operator workload while improving safety and mission flexibility in constrained indoor environments.

</details>


### [123] [TOLEBI: Learning Fault-Tolerant Bipedal Locomotion via Online Status Estimation and Fallibility Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05596)
*Hokyun Lee,Woo-Jeong Baek,Junhyeok Cha,Jaeheung Park*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TOLEBI is a fault-tolerant learning framework for bipedal locomotion that injects faults in simulation (joint locking, power loss, disturbances), uses sim-to-real transfer to a real humanoid (TOCABI), and adds an online joint-status classifier for runtime fault detection, claimed as the first learning-based fault-tolerant approach in this domain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the vulnerability of bipedal locomotion to hardware faults and environmental disturbances, which can have severe real-world consequences, and fill the gap of learning-based fault tolerance in humanoid robots.

Method: Train fault-tolerant locomotion policies by injecting faults in simulation (joint locking, power loss, external disturbances); perform sim-to-real transfer to a real humanoid (TOCABI); implement an online joint-status module that classifies joint conditions from runtime observations.

Result: Validation experiments conducted on both real robot TOCABI and in simulation showing applicability of the approach; the authors claim to present the first learning-based fault-tolerant framework for bipedal locomotion.

Conclusion: TOLEBI advances learning-based fault tolerance in humanoid locomotion by enabling fault injection during training, real-world transfer, and online fault monitoring, potentially enabling more reliable robotic locomotion under faults and disturbances.

Abstract: With the growing employment of learning algorithms in robotic applications, research on reinforcement learning for bipedal locomotion has become a central topic for humanoid robotics. While recently published contributions achieve high success rates in locomotion tasks, scarce attention has been devoted to the development of methods that enable to handle hardware faults that may occur during the locomotion process. However, in real-world settings, environmental disturbances or sudden occurrences of hardware faults might yield severe consequences. To address these issues, this paper presents TOLEBI (A faulT-tOlerant Learning framEwork for Bipedal locomotIon) that handles faults on the robot during operation. Specifically, joint locking, power loss and external disturbances are injected in simulation to learn fault-tolerant locomotion strategies. In addition to transferring the learned policy to the real robot via sim-to-real transfer, an online joint status module incorporated. This module enables to classify joint conditions by referring to the actual observations at runtime under real-world conditions. The validation experiments conducted both in real-world and simulation with the humanoid robot TOCABI highlight the applicability of the proposed approach. To our knowledge, this manuscript provides the first learning-based fault-tolerant framework for bipedal locomotion, thereby fostering the development of efficient learning methods in this field.

</details>


### [124] [HiCrowd: Hierarchical Crowd Flow Alignment for Dense Human Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05608)
*Yufei Zhu,Shih-Min Yang,Martin Magnusson,Allan Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HiCrowd blends hierarchical reinforcement learning and model predictive control to navigate crowds by following compatible pedestrian groups, reducing freezing and improving efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense crowds cause the freezing robot problem. The work aims to enable safe, efficient robot navigation in crowds by leveraging human motion as guidance rather than treating humans solely as obstacles.

Method: High-level RL policy selects a follow point to align with a pedestrian group; low-level MPC tracks this guidance with short-horizon planning. The framework uses crowd motion as guidance for long-term decisions and safe short-term execution.

Result: Compared to reactive and learning baselines in offline and online settings, HiCrowd improves navigation efficiency and safety and reduces freezing behaviors on real-world and synthetic datasets.

Conclusion: Leveraging human motion as guidance is a powerful principle for safe and efficient crowd navigation, enabling better alignment with crowd flow and mitigating freeze issues.

Abstract: Navigating through dense human crowds remains a significant challenge for mobile robots. A key issue is the freezing robot problem, where the robot struggles to find safe motions and becomes stuck within the crowd. To address this, we propose HiCrowd, a hierarchical framework that integrates reinforcement learning (RL) with model predictive control (MPC). HiCrowd leverages surrounding pedestrian motion as guidance, enabling the robot to align with compatible crowd flows. A high-level RL policy generates a follow point to align the robot with a suitable pedestrian group, while a low-level MPC safely tracks this guidance with short horizon planning. The method combines long-term crowd aware decision making with safe short-term execution. We evaluate HiCrowd against reactive and learning-based baselines in offline setting (replaying recorded human trajectories) and online setting (human trajectories are updated to react to the robot in simulation). Experiments on a real-world dataset and a synthetic crowd dataset show that our method outperforms in navigation efficiency and safety, while reducing freezing behaviors. Our results suggest that leveraging human motion as guidance, rather than treating humans solely as dynamic obstacles, provides a powerful principle for safe and efficient robot navigation in crowds.

</details>


### [125] [From Vision to Decision: Neuromorphic Control for Autonomous Navigation and Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05683)
*Chuwei Wang,Eduardo Sebastián,Amanda Prorok,Anastasia Bizyaeva*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A lightweight neuromorphic control framework translating vision into action for navigation, using dynamic neuronal populations and a bifurcation-based decision mechanism to resolve indecision; validated in simulation and on a quadrotor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridges reactive, sensor-driven control with model-based planning to handle goal ambiguity and symmetry-breaking without heavy computation; inspired by mechanistic models of cognition and opinion dynamics.

Method: Encode camera pixels into dynamic neural populations that map visual target excitation to egocentric motion; employ a dynamic bifurcation to delay commitment until environmental geometry triggers a decisive state; minimal parameter set designed for real-time deployment and easy integration with image processing pipelines.

Result: Demonstrated real-time autonomy in simulation and on an experimental quadrotor; shows effective navigation/tracking with low computational load and small, interpretable parameter set.

Conclusion: The neuromorphic controller offers a parsimonious, real-time solution that unifies perception and action for vision-guided navigation, suitable for integration into existing pipelines and scalable to other perception tasks.

Abstract: Robotic navigation has historically struggled to reconcile reactive, sensor-based control with the decisive capabilities of model-based planners. This duality becomes critical when the absence of a predominant option among goals leads to indecision, challenging reactive systems to break symmetries without computationally-intense planners. We propose a parsimonious neuromorphic control framework that bridges this gap for vision-guided navigation and tracking. Image pixels from an onboard camera are encoded as inputs to dynamic neuronal populations that directly transform visual target excitation into egocentric motion commands. A dynamic bifurcation mechanism resolves indecision by delaying commitment until a critical point induced by the environmental geometry. Inspired by recently proposed mechanistic models of animal cognition and opinion dynamics, the neuromorphic controller provides real-time autonomy with a minimal computational burden, a small number of interpretable parameters, and can be seamlessly integrated with application-specific image processing pipelines. We validate our approach in simulation environments as well as on an experimental quadrotor platform.

</details>


### [126] [Task-Oriented Robot-Human Handovers on Legged Manipulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05760)
*Andreea Tulbure,Carmen Scheidemann,Elias Steiner,Marco Hutter*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AFT-Handover combines LLM-based affordance reasoning with texture-based affordance transfer for zero-shot, generalizable task-oriented handovers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing TOH methods rely on object/task-specific affordances and struggle to generalize to novel scenarios; there is a need for zero-shot generalization to support human-robot handovers.

Method: Retrieve a proxy exemplar for a novel object-task pair; establish part-level correspondences via LLM reasoning; texturize affordances for feature-based point cloud transfer; apply to robotic handovers (including legged manipulators); evaluate across diverse object-task pairs and conduct a user study.

Result: Improved handover success rates and generalization versus baselines; user study shows preference over state-of-the-art and reduced human regrasping; demonstrates feasibility on legged manipulators.

Conclusion: LLM-driven affordance reasoning combined with texture-based transfer enables zero-shot TOH generalization, advancing real-world human-robot handovers and offering broad applicability across robot platforms.

Abstract: Task-oriented handovers (TOH) are fundamental to effective human-robot collaboration, requiring robots to present objects in a way that supports the human's intended post-handover use. Existing approaches are typically based on object- or task-specific affordances, but their ability to generalize to novel scenarios is limited. To address this gap, we present AFT-Handover, a framework that integrates large language model (LLM)-driven affordance reasoning with efficient texture-based affordance transfer to achieve zero-shot, generalizable TOH. Given a novel object-task pair, the method retrieves a proxy exemplar from a database, establishes part-level correspondences via LLM reasoning, and texturizes affordances for feature-based point cloud transfer. We evaluate AFT-Handover across diverse task-object pairs, showing improved handover success rates and stronger generalization compared to baselines. In a comparative user study, our framework is significantly preferred over the current state-of-the-art, effectively reducing human regrasping before tool use. Finally, we demonstrate TOH on legged manipulators, highlighting the potential of our framework for real-world robot-human handovers.

</details>


### [127] [Scalable and General Whole-Body Control for Cross-Humanoid Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05791)
*Yufei Xue,YunFeng Lin,Wentao Dong,Yang Tang,Jingbo Wang,Jiangmiao Pang,Ming Zhou,Minghuan Liu,Weinan Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A single policy trained with cross-embodiment randomization generalizes across diverse humanoid morphologies and transfers zero-shot to unseen robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome robot-specific training and enable generalist humanoid control across varying morphologies and dynamics.

Method: XHugWBC framework with physics-consistent morphological randomization, semantically aligned observation and action spaces across robots, and policy architectures that capture morphological/dynamical properties; trained on a broad distribution of randomized embodiments.

Result: Demonstrates strong generalization and robustness across 12 simulated humanoids and 7 real robots, enabling zero-shot transfer to unseen morphologies.

Conclusion: A universal cross-embodiment humanoid controller is achievable via broad-morphology priors; this reduces per-robot training and paves the way for generalist humanoid robotics.

Abstract: Learning-based whole-body controllers have become a key driver for humanoid robots, yet most existing approaches require robot-specific training. In this paper, we study the problem of cross-embodiment humanoid control and show that a single policy can robustly generalize across a wide range of humanoid robot designs with one-time training. We introduce XHugWBC, a novel cross-embodiment training framework that enables generalist humanoid control through: (1) physics-consistent morphological randomization, (2) semantically aligned observation and action spaces across diverse humanoid robots, and (3) effective policy architectures modeling morphological and dynamical properties. XHugWBC is not tied to any specific robot. Instead, it internalizes a broad distribution of morphological and dynamical characteristics during training. By learning motion priors from diverse randomized embodiments, the policy acquires a strong structural bias that supports zero-shot transfer to previously unseen robots. Experiments on twelve simulated humanoids and seven real-world robots demonstrate the strong generalization and robustness of the resulting universal controller.

</details>


### [128] [A Hybrid Autoencoder for Robust Heightmap Generation from Fused Lidar and Depth Data for Humanoid Robot Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05855)
*Dennis Bank,Joost Cordes,Thomas Seel,Simon F. G. Ehlers*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A multimodal, robot-centric heightmap framework (EDS) combines CNN-based spatial features with GRU temporal fusion to fuse depth camera, LiDAR, and IMU data for improved terrain mapping; 3.2 s temporal context reduces drift; reported gains: ~7.2% over depth-only, ~9.9% over LiDAR-only.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable terrain perception in unstructured, human-centric environments and the limitations of single-sensor pipelines, necessitating temporally-consistent, multimodal reconstruction.

Method: Hybrid Encoder-Decoder Structure (EDS) with a CNN for spatial feature extraction and a GRU core for temporal consistency, operating on a robot-centric heightmap. Multimodal data sources: Intel RealSense depth camera, LIVOX MID-360 LiDAR processed via efficient spherical projection, and onboard IMU.

Result: Quantitative improvements in reconstruction accuracy when fusing multiple modalities: 7.2% gain over depth-only and 9.9% over LiDAR-only. Incorporating 3.2 seconds of temporal context reduces mapping drift.

Conclusion: Multimodal fusion with temporal context in a robot-centric heightmap improves terrain perception accuracy and stability for humanoid robots in unstructured environments.

Abstract: Reliable terrain perception is a critical prerequisite for the deployment of humanoid robots in unstructured, human-centric environments. While traditional systems often rely on manually engineered, single-sensor pipelines, this paper presents a learning-based framework that uses an intermediate, robot-centric heightmap representation. A hybrid Encoder-Decoder Structure (EDS) is introduced, utilizing a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for spatial feature extraction fused with a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) core for temporal consistency. The architecture integrates multimodal data from an Intel RealSense depth camera, a LIVOX MID-360 LiDAR processed via efficient spherical projection, and an onboard IMU. Quantitative results demonstrate that multimodal fusion improves reconstruction accuracy by 7.2% over depth-only and 9.9% over LiDAR-only configurations. Furthermore, the integration of a 3.2 s temporal context reduces mapping drift.

</details>


### [129] [Residual Reinforcement Learning for Waste-Container Lifting Using Large-Scale Cranes with Underactuated Tools](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05895)
*Qi Li,Karsten Berns*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Residual reinforcement learning enhances a nominal Cartesian controller for a waste-container lifting task by learning a residual policy to compensate for unmodeled dynamics, improving accuracy and robustness in simulation with domain randomization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrepancies between model-based control and real dynamics in urban waste-container lifting cause precision and swing issues; there is a need for robust performance without full end-to-end learning.

Method: Combine admittance-based Cartesian trajectory tracking and pendulum-aware swing damping with damped least-squares IK and a residual PPO policy trained in Isaac Lab; train the residual network to compensate for unmodeled dynamics, using randomized initializations and domain randomization over payload, actuator gains, and passive joint parameters to enhance generalization.

Result: In simulation, the residual policy improves tracking accuracy, reduces oscillations, and increases lifting success rate compared to the nominal controller alone.

Conclusion: The residual reinforcement learning framework provides improved robustness to unmodeled dynamics and parameter variation, with domain randomization enhancing generalization; favorable simulation results suggest potential for real-world deployment, albeit contingent on transfer to physical systems.

Abstract: This paper studies the container lifting phase of a waste-container recycling task in urban environments, performed by a hydraulic loader crane equipped with an underactuated discharge unit, and proposes a residual reinforcement learning (RRL) approach that combines a nominal Cartesian controller with a learned residual policy. All experiments are conducted in simulation, where the task is characterized by tight geometric tolerances between the discharge-unit hooks and the container rings relative to the overall crane scale, making precise trajectory tracking and swing suppression essential. The nominal controller uses admittance control for trajectory tracking and pendulum-aware swing damping, followed by damped least-squares inverse kinematics with a nullspace posture term to generate joint velocity commands. A PPO-trained residual policy in Isaac Lab compensates for unmodeled dynamics and parameter variations, improving precision and robustness without requiring end-to-end learning from scratch. We further employ randomized episode initialization and domain randomization over payload properties, actuator gains, and passive joint parameters to enhance generalization. Simulation results demonstrate improved tracking accuracy, reduced oscillations, and higher lifting success rates compared to the nominal controller alone.

</details>


### [130] [From Bench to Flight: Translating Drone Impact Tests into Operational Safety Limits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05922)
*Aziz Mohamed Mili,Louis Catar,Paul Gérard,Ilyass Tabiai,David St-Onge*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An end-to-end, open toolchain converts benchtop impact tests into deployable safety governors for indoor MAVs, including an impact rig, data-driven speed-to-impulse/contact-duration models, and online enforcement via ROS2; with datasets and code for repeatable calibration and certification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address practical, measurable human impact risk in indoor MAV operations and provide a repeatable, certified workflow to derive runtime motion limits from empirical impact data.

Method: 1) Build a compact impact rig and protocol to capture force-time profiles across drone classes and contact surfaces. 2) Develop data-driven models mapping pre-impact speed to impulse and contact duration to compute speed bounds for a target force limit. 3) Release scripts and a ROS2 node to enforce these bounds online, log compliance, and support facility-specific policies. 4) Validate workflow on multiple off-the-shelf quadrotors and indoor assets, demonstrating preservation of task throughput while meeting specified force constraints.

Result: A practical bridge from measured impacts to runtime limits; an open, repeatable process with shareable datasets and code that enables teams to certify indoor MAV operations near humans; governors maintain task performance while adhering to safety force constraints.

Conclusion: The work provides a complete, deployable pipeline from empirical impact data to runtime safety limits, enabling organizations to certify indoor MAV operations with practical tooling, documentation, and policy support.

Abstract: Indoor micro-aerial vehicles (MAVs) are increasingly used for tasks that require close proximity to people, yet practitioners lack practical methods to tune motion limits based on measured impact risk. We present an end-to-end, open toolchain that converts benchtop impact tests into deployable safety governors for drones. First, we describe a compact and replicable impact rig and protocol for capturing force-time profiles across drone classes and contact surfaces. Second, we provide data-driven models that map pre-impact speed to impulse and contact duration, enabling direct computation of speed bounds for a target force limit. Third, we release scripts and a ROS2 node that enforce these bounds online and log compliance, with support for facility-specific policies. We validate the workflow on multiple commercial off-the-shelf quadrotors and representative indoor assets, demonstrating that the derived governors preserve task throughput while meeting force constraints specified by safety stakeholders. Our contribution is a practical bridge from measured impacts to runtime limits, with shareable datasets, code, and a repeatable process that teams can adopt to certify indoor MAV operations near humans.

</details>


### [131] [Visuo-Tactile World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06001)
*Carolina Higuera,Sergio Arnaud,Byron Boots,Mustafa Mukadam,Francois Robert Hogan,Franziska Meier*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Integrating touch with vision in world models improves physics fidelity and planning for contact-rich tasks, enabling better object permanence, adherence to motion laws, and zero-shot transfer, with strong cross-task adaptability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-only models struggle with contact and occlusion in manipulation; grounding physics in touch aims to improve reliability and planning in contact-rich interactions.

Method: Train multi-task Visuo-Tactile World Models (VT-WM) that fuse visual and tactile sensing to learn contact dynamics. The model imagines future states autoregressively (imagination) and is evaluated on both planning and real-robot zero-shot tasks. It is trained across a set of contact-rich manipulation tasks to ground contact physics.

Result: VT-WM achieves ~33% improvement in object permanence and ~29% improvement in compliance with the laws of motion in autoregressive rollouts. In zero-shot real-robot experiments, it attains up to 35% higher success, especially in multi-step, contact-rich tasks. The learned contact dynamics also transfer to novel tasks, enabling reliable planning with limited demonstrations.

Conclusion: Grounding visual models with tactile contact dynamics yields better physical fidelity, planning performance, and generalization to new tasks, highlighting the benefits of a visuo-tactile world model for manipulation under contact-rich scenarios.

Abstract: We introduce multi-task Visuo-Tactile World Models (VT-WM), which capture the physics of contact through touch reasoning. By complementing vision with tactile sensing, VT-WM better understands robot-object interactions in contact-rich tasks, avoiding common failure modes of vision-only models under occlusion or ambiguous contact states, such as objects disappearing, teleporting, or moving in ways that violate basic physics. Trained across a set of contact-rich manipulation tasks, VT-WM improves physical fidelity in imagination, achieving 33% better performance at maintaining object permanence and 29% better compliance with the laws of motion in autoregressive rollouts. Moreover, experiments show that grounding in contact dynamics also translates to planning. In zero-shot real-robot experiments, VT-WM achieves up to 35% higher success rates, with the largest gains in multi-step, contact-rich tasks. Finally, VT-WM demonstrates significant downstream versatility, effectively adapting its learned contact dynamics to a novel task and achieving reliable planning success with only a limited set of demonstrations.

</details>


### [132] [CommCP: Efficient Multi-Agent Coordination via LLM-Based Communication with Conformal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06038)
*Xiaopan Zhang,Zejin Wang,Zhixu Li,Jianpeng Yao,Jiachen Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel decentralized, LLM-based communication framework (CommCP) for multi-agent multi-task Embodied Question Answering (MM-EQA) with conformal-prediction calibrated messages; introduces an MM-EQA benchmark and shows improved task success and exploration efficiency over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance information gathering in cooperative, heterogeneous robot teams performing embodied question answering, where effective non-redundant communication and coordinated exploration are crucial.

Method: Introduce CommCP, a decentralized framework where LLMs generate agent communications for MM-EQA. Conformal prediction calibrates messages to reduce distractions and improve reliability. Evaluation uses a new MM-EQA benchmark with photo-realistic household scenes.

Result: Empirical results show significant improvements in task success rate and exploration efficiency compared with baselines.

Conclusion: CommCP advances multi-agent EQA by enabling reliable, efficient inter-agent communication and cooperative exploration; the MM-EQA benchmark and resources are released for future research.

Abstract: To complete assignments provided by humans in natural language, robots must interpret commands, generate and answer relevant questions for scene understanding, and manipulate target objects. Real-world deployments often require multiple heterogeneous robots with different manipulation capabilities to handle different assignments cooperatively. Beyond the need for specialized manipulation skills, effective information gathering is important in completing these assignments. To address this component of the problem, we formalize the information-gathering process in a fully cooperative setting as an underexplored multi-agent multi-task Embodied Question Answering (MM-EQA) problem, which is a novel extension of canonical Embodied Question Answering (EQA), where effective communication is crucial for coordinating efforts without redundancy. To address this problem, we propose CommCP, a novel LLM-based decentralized communication framework designed for MM-EQA. Our framework employs conformal prediction to calibrate the generated messages, thereby minimizing receiver distractions and enhancing communication reliability. To evaluate our framework, we introduce an MM-EQA benchmark featuring diverse, photo-realistic household scenarios with embodied questions. Experimental results demonstrate that CommCP significantly enhances the task success rate and exploration efficiency over baselines. The experiment videos, code, and dataset are available on our project website: https://comm-cp.github.io.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [133] [Denoising diffusion networks for normative modeling in neuroimaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04886)
*Luke Whitbread,Lyle J. Palmer,Mark Jenkinson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DDPM-based conditional density estimation for tabular neuroimaging phenotypes enabling multivariate normative modeling; transformer backbones outperform MLP at higher dimensions, enabling scalable joint centile and deviation profiles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard normative models fit one model per imaging-derived phenotype (IDP) and miss multivariate dependencies. A unified conditional density estimator can capture inter-IDP dependence and produce calibrated centiles across many phenotypes.

Method: Use denoising diffusion probabilistic models as conditional density estimators. Compare two denoisers: (i) FiLM-conditioned MLP and (ii) SAINT-style tabular transformer with feature self-attention and intersample attention, employing covariate embeddings. Derive centiles and deviation scores via sampling. Evaluate on synthetic data with heteroscedastic/multimodal age effects and UK Biobank FreeSurfer IDPs, from 2 to 200 dimensions. Assess centile calibration (absolute centile error, empirical coverage, PIT), distributional fidelity (KS tests), multivariate dependence diagnostics, and nearest-neighbour memorisation.

Result: For low dimensions, diffusion models yield well-calibrated per-IDP outputs comparable to baselines while jointly modeling realistic dependence. At higher dimensions, the SAINT transformer backbone is substantially better calibrated than the MLP and better preserves higher-order dependence, enabling scalable joint normative models compatible with standard per-IDP pipelines.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based normative modeling provides a practical route to calibrated multivariate deviation profiles in neuroimaging.

Abstract: Normative modeling estimates reference distributions of biological measures conditional on covariates, enabling centiles and clinically interpretable deviation scores to be derived. Most neuroimaging pipelines fit one model per imaging-derived phenotype (IDP), which scales well but discards multivariate dependence that may encode coordinated patterns. We propose denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) as a unified conditional density estimator for tabular IDPs, from which univariate centiles and deviation scores are derived by sampling. We utilise two denoiser backbones: (i) a feature-wise linear modulation (FiLM) conditioned multilayer perceptron (MLP) and (ii) a tabular transformer with feature self-attention and intersample attention (SAINT), conditioning covariates through learned embeddings. We evaluate on a synthetic benchmark with heteroscedastic and multimodal age effects and on UK Biobank FreeSurfer phenotypes, scaling from dimension of 2 to 200. Our evaluation suite includes centile calibration (absolute centile error, empirical coverage, and the probability integral transform), distributional fidelity (Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests), multivariate dependence diagnostics, and nearest-neighbour memorisation analysis.
