For low dimensions, diffusion models deliver well-calibrated per-IDP outputs comparable to traditional baselines while jointly modeling realistic dependence structure. At higher dimensions, the transformer backbone remains substantially better calibrated than the MLP and better preserves higher-order dependence, enabling scalable joint normative models that remain compatible with standard per-IDP pipelines. These results support diffusion-based normative modeling as a practical route to calibrated multivariate deviation profiles in neuroimaging.

</details>


### [134] [A Causal Perspective for Enhancing Jailbreak Attack and Defense](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04893)
*Licheng Pan,Yunsheng Lu,Jiexi Liu,Jialing Tao,Haozhe Feng,Hui Xue,Zhixuan Chu,Kui Ren*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A causal-discovery framework (Causal Analyst) identifies direct prompt-feature causes of jailbreaks in LLMs by integrating LLM-based prompt encoding with GNN-based causal graphs, using a large dataset of 35k jailbreak attempts across seven LLMs and 37 features; demonstrates two applications (attack enhancement and guardrail-based defense) and claims robust causally-grounded insights over non-causal methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond correlation-based analyses of jailbreak prompts by uncovering direct causal drivers of jailbreaks, enabling both more effective attacks for testing and stronger defenses to improve LLM safety and reliability.

Method: Combine LLM-based prompt feature encoding with graph neural network (GNN) causal discovery to learn a causal graph linking 37 prompt features (from 100 attack templates and 50 queries) to jailbreak responses. Build a dataset of 35k attempts across seven LLMs. Validate via causal structure learning, baseline comparisons, and robustness checks. Demonstrate two applications: Jailbreaking Enhancer (increases attack success using the causal features) and Guardrail Advisor (extracts malicious intent from obfuscated queries).

Result: Identified direct causal drivers such as 'Positive Character' and 'Number of Task Steps' for jailbreaks; causal models outperform non-causal baselines in explaining jailbreaks and enable practical tools for offense and defense; code released for replication.

Conclusion: Causal analysis of jailbreak features provides interpretable, effective insights for improving LLM reliability, suggesting that causal perspectives are valuable for safety research and defense design.

Abstract: Uncovering the mechanisms behind "jailbreaks" in large language models (LLMs) is crucial for enhancing their safety and reliability, yet these mechanisms remain poorly understood. Existing studies predominantly analyze jailbreak prompts by probing latent representations, often overlooking the causal relationships between interpretable prompt features and jailbreak occurrences. In this work, we propose Causal Analyst, a framework that integrates LLMs into data-driven causal discovery to identify the direct causes of jailbreaks and leverage them for both attack and defense. We introduce a comprehensive dataset comprising 35k jailbreak attempts across seven LLMs, systematically constructed from 100 attack templates and 50 harmful queries, annotated with 37 meticulously designed human-readable prompt features. By jointly training LLM-based prompt encoding and GNN-based causal graph learning, we reconstruct causal pathways linking prompt features to jailbreak responses. Our analysis reveals that specific features, such as "Positive Character" and "Number of Task Steps", act as direct causal drivers of jailbreaks. We demonstrate the practical utility of these insights through two applications: (1) a Jailbreaking Enhancer that targets identified causal features to significantly boost attack success rates on public benchmarks, and (2) a Guardrail Advisor that utilizes the learned causal graph to extract true malicious intent from obfuscated queries. Extensive experiments, including baseline comparisons and causal structure validation, confirm the robustness of our causal analysis and its superiority over non-causal approaches. Our results suggest that analyzing jailbreak features from a causal perspective is an effective and interpretable approach for improving LLM reliability. Our code is available at https://github.com/Master-PLC/Causal-Analyst.

</details>


### [135] [Momentum Attention: The Physics of In-Context Learning and Spectral Forensics for Mechanistic Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04902)
*Kingsuk Maitra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Momentum Attention: a symplectic, momentum-augmented Transformer that acts as a physical circuit with a high-pass duality, enabling single-layer induction and spectral analysis; validated by extensive experiments and scaling laws.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address topological depth constraints in induction heads by injecting velocity/kinematic priors, enabling new capabilities (single-layer induction, spectral forensics) and offering a physics-inspired interpretability framework.

Method: Introduce velocity p_t = q_t - q_{t-1} and a symplectic shear ? over queries/keys, formalize a Symplectic-Filter Duality equating the physical shear to a high-pass filter, prove an Orthogonality Theorem for DC/AC signal separation when Low-Pass RoPE interacts with High-Pass Momentum, and validate via 5,100+ controlled experiments using a 125M Momentum model against a 350M baseline.

Result: Demonstrated single-layer induction and spectral forensics; momentum-depth fungibility with gamma* = 4.17 × N^{-0.74}; achieved strong induction performance on tasks and ~2.9% validation loss relative to a 350M baseline; extensive empirical validation documented in supplementary materials.

Conclusion: Provides a physics-inspired analytical toolkit linking Generative AI, Hamiltonian dynamics, and signal processing, introducing a cornerstone Symplectic-Filter Duality and Orthogonality Theorem to analyze and extend Transformer capabilities.

Abstract: The Mechanistic Interpretability (MI) program has mapped the Transformer as a precise computational graph. We extend this graph with a conservation law and time-varying AC dynamics, viewing it as a physical circuit. We introduce Momentum Attention, a symplectic augmentation embedding physical priors via the kinematic difference operator $p_t = q_t - q_{t-1}$, implementing the symplectic shear $\hat{q}_t = q_t + γp_t$ on queries and keys. We identify a fundamental Symplectic-Filter Duality: the physical shear is mathematically equivalent to a High-Pass Filter. This duality is our cornerstone contribution -- by injecting kinematic momentum, we sidestep the topological depth constraint ($L \geq 2$) for induction head formation. While standard architectures require two layers for induction from static positions, our extension grants direct access to velocity, enabling Single-Layer Induction and Spectral Forensics via Bode Plots. We formalize an Orthogonality Theorem proving that DC (semantic) and AC (mechanistic) signals segregate into orthogonal frequency bands when Low-Pass RoPE interacts with High-Pass Momentum. Validated through 5,100+ controlled experiments (documented in Supplementary Appendices A--R and 27 Jupyter notebooks), our 125M Momentum model exceeds expectations on induction-heavy tasks while tracking a 350M baseline within $\sim$2.9% validation loss. Dedicated associative recall experiments reveal a scaling law $γ^* = 4.17 \times N^{-0.74}$ establishing momentum-depth fungibility. We offer this framework as a complementary analytical toolkit connecting Generative AI, Hamiltonian Physics, and Signal Processing.

</details>


### [136] [Mind the Performance Gap: Capability-Behavior Trade-offs in Feature Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04903)
*Eitan Sprejer,Oscar Agustín Stanchi,María Victoria Carro,Denise Alejandra Mester,Iván Arcuschin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Feature steering can alter targeted behaviors but at a substantial cost to overall task performance and coherence; simple prompting provides a better balance; mechanistic control methods entail a general capability-behavior trade-off.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate the practical effectiveness and trade-offs of feature steering (Auto Steer) versus prompt-based baselines in large language models across real tasks.

Method: Empirical comparison of Goodfire's Auto Steer against prompting baselines across 14 steering queries (innocuous and safety-related) on 171 MMLU questions using Llama-8B and Llama-70B; metrics include accuracy, coherence, and behavioral control; report target-behavior scores and task performance.

Result: Auto Steer achieves higher target-behavior scores (Llama-8B: 3.33 vs 2.98; Llama-70B: 3.57 vs 3.10) but markedly degrades task performance (accuracy: 66%→46% on Llama-8B; 87%→73% on Llama-70B) and coherence (4.62→2.24 on Llama-8B; 4.94→3.89 on Llama-70B). Simple prompting yields best overall balance.

Conclusion: Current feature-steering methods reveal a fundamental capability-behavior trade-off; not ideal for deployment requiring high task performance; empirical characterization is necessary for mechanistic control approaches.

Abstract: Feature steering has emerged as a promising approach for controlling LLM behavior through direct manipulation of internal representations, offering advantages over prompt engineering. However, its practical effectiveness in real-world applications remains poorly understood, particularly regarding potential trade-offs with output quality. We show that feature steering methods substantially degrade model performance even when successfully controlling target behaviors, a critical trade-off. Specifically, we evaluate Goodfire's Auto Steer against prompt engineering baselines across 14 steering queries (covering innocuous and safety-relevant behaviors) on 171 Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) questions using Llama-8B and Llama-70B, measuring accuracy, coherence, and behavioral control. Our findings show that Auto Steer successfully modifies target behaviors (achieving scores of 3.33 vs. 2.98 for prompting on Llama-8B and 3.57 vs. 3.10 on Llama-70B), but causes dramatic performance degradation: accuracy on the MMLU questions drops from 66% to 46% on Llama-8B and 87% to 73% on Llama-70B, with coherence falling from 4.62 to 2.24 and 4.94 to 3.89 respectively. Simple prompting achieves the best overall balance. These findings highlight limitations of current feature steering methods for practical deployment where task performance cannot be sacrificed. More broadly, our work demonstrates that mechanistic control methods face fundamental capability-behavior trade-offs that must be empirically characterized before deployment.

</details>


### [137] [DCER: Dual-Stage Compression and Energy-Based Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04904)
*Yiwen Wang,Jiahao Qin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DCER uses dual-stage compression and energy-based reconstruction to robustly fuse multimodal data under noise and missing modalities, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust multimodal fusion faces two key challenges: noisy inputs degrade representation quality and missing modalities cause prediction failures.

Method: Two-stage compression: (1) within-modality frequency transforms (audio: wavelet, video: DCT) to denoise while preserving task-relevant patterns; (2) cross-modality bottleneck tokens to force genuine cross-modal integration. For missing modalities, energy-based reconstruction recovers representations via gradient descent on a learned energy function, with the final energy providing intrinsic uncertainty quantification.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, and CH-SIMS; a U-shaped robustness pattern indicating fusion benefits at both complete and high-missing conditions; the energy correlates with prediction error (rho > 0.72).

Conclusion: DCER offers robust multimodal fusion with intrinsic uncertainty quantification; code will be released on GitHub.

Abstract: Multimodal fusion faces two robustness challenges: noisy inputs degrade representation quality, and missing modalities cause prediction failures. We propose DCER, a
  unified framework addressing both challenges through dual-stage compression and energy-based reconstruction. The compression stage operates at two levels:
  within-modality frequency transforms (wavelet for audio, DCT for video) remove noise while preserving task-relevant patterns, and cross-modality bottleneck tokens
  force genuine integration rather than modality-specific shortcuts. For missing modalities, energy-based reconstruction recovers representations via gradient descent
  on a learned energy function, with the final energy providing intrinsic uncertainty quantification (\r{ho} > 0.72 correlation with prediction error). Experiments on
  CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, and CH-SIMS demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across all benchmarks, with a U-shaped robustness pattern favoring multimodal fusion at
  both complete and high-missing conditions. The code will be available on Github.

</details>


### [138] [LISA: Laplacian In-context Spectral Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04906)
*Julio Candanedo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LISA is an inference-time adaptation framework for Laplacian-based time-series models that uses prefix observations to produce diffusion-coordinate representations and lightweight latent adapters, improving forecasts especially under nonstationary dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address nonstationarity and distributional shift in time-series by enabling fast, inference-time adaptation without full retraining, leveraging spectral methods and in-context information.

Method: Construct delay-coordinate embeddings to derive diffusion coordinates via Laplacian spectral learning, use a frozen nonlinear decoder for one-step predictions, and attach lightweight latent-space adapters implemented as Gaussian-process regression or an attention-like Markov operator over context windows; adaptation occurs entirely at inference using only the observed prefix.

Result: Empirical results across forecasting and autoregressive rollout tasks show improvement over a frozen baseline; gains are pronounced when dynamics change, illustrating the benefit of in-context adaptation.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a link between in-context adaptation and nonparametric spectral methods for dynamical systems and presents a viable, scalable approach to adapt Laplacian-based models at inference time.

Abstract: We propose Laplacian In-context Spectral Analysis (LISA), a method for inference-time adaptation of Laplacian-based time-series models using only an observed prefix. LISA combines delay-coordinate embeddings and Laplacian spectral learning to produce diffusion-coordinate state representations, together with a frozen nonlinear decoder for one-step prediction. We introduce lightweight latent-space residual adapters based on either Gaussian-process regression or an attention-like Markov operator over context windows. Across forecasting and autoregressive rollout experiments, LISA improves over the frozen baseline and is often most beneficial under changing dynamics. This work links in-context adaptation to nonparametric spectral methods for dynamical systems.

</details>


### [139] [Physics as the Inductive Bias for Causal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04907)
*Jianhong Chen,Naichen Shi,Xubo Yue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Integrating partial physical knowledge via an SDE framework: drift encodes known ODE dynamics, diffusion captures unknown causal couplings; scalable sparsity-based MLE with graph-structure exploiting estimation; provides causal graph recovery guarantees and improved, physically consistent results over data-driven baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address identifiability, stability, and robustness in causal discovery for dynamical systems by combining physics-based models with data-driven causal inference, handling feedback, cycles, and non-stationarity beyond acyclic assumptions.

Method: Model system evolution as a stochastic differential equation where the drift term encodes predefined ODE dynamics and the diffusion term captures unknown causal interactions. Develop a sparsity-inducing maximum likelihood estimator that leverages causal graph structure for scalable parameter estimation and provide theoretical conditions for recovering the causal graph.

Result: Experiments on dynamical systems with diverse causal structures show improved causal graph recovery and more stable, physically consistent estimates compared to purely data-driven state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: Partial physical knowledge can be effectively integrated into causal discovery for dynamical systems via an SDE framework, yielding better identifiability, stability, and robustness in non-acyclic, non-stationary settings.

Abstract: Causal discovery is often a data-driven paradigm to analyze complex real-world systems. In parallel, physics-based models such as ordinary differential equations (ODEs) provide mechanistic structure for many dynamical processes. Integrating these paradigms potentially allows physical knowledge to act as an inductive bias, improving identifiability, stability, and robustness of causal discovery in dynamical systems. However, such integration remains challenging: real dynamical systems often exhibit feedback, cyclic interactions, and non-stationary data trend, while many widely used causal discovery methods are formulated under acyclicity or equilibrium-based assumptions. In this work, we propose an integrative causal discovery framework for dynamical systems that leverages partial physical knowledge as an inductive bias. Specifically, we model system evolution as a stochastic differential equation (SDE), where the drift term encodes known ODE dynamics and the diffusion term corresponds to unknown causal couplings beyond the prescribed physics. We develop a scalable sparsity-inducing MLE algorithm that exploits causal graph structure for efficient parameter estimation. Under mild conditions, we establish guarantees to recover the causal graph. Experiments on dynamical systems with diverse causal structures show that our approach improves causal graph recovery and produces more stable, physically consistent estimates than purely data-driven state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [140] [Temporal Pair Consistency for Variance-Reduced Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04908)
*Chika Maduabuchi,Jindong Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Temporal Pair Consistency (TPC) reduces gradient variance in continuous-time generative models by coupling velocity predictions at paired timesteps along the same probability path, improving sample quality and efficiency without changing model architecture or solvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous-time generative models (diffusion, flow matching, rectified flow) suffer high estimator variance due to timestep-wise independence; variance reduction without altering models/paths/solvers is desirable.

Method: Introduce Temporal Pair Consistency (TPC): a lightweight, estimator-level regularization that couples velocity predictions at paired timesteps along the same probability path, yielding a quadratic, trajectory-coupled regularization; preserves the original flow-matching objective while reducing variance; compatible with modern pipelines.

Result: Empirically improves sample quality and efficiency on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet across resolutions; achieves lower FID at equal or lower computational cost than prior methods; extends to state-of-the-art pipelines with noise-augmented training, score-based denoising, and rectified flow.

Conclusion: TPC provides a principled variance-reduction mechanism that preserves the base objective, reduces gradient variance via trajectory coupling, and scales to current high-performance pipelines without architectural or solver changes.

Abstract: Continuous-time generative models, such as diffusion models, flow matching, and rectified flow, learn time-dependent vector fields but are typically trained with objectives that treat timesteps independently, leading to high estimator variance and inefficient sampling. Prior approaches mitigate this via explicit smoothness penalties, trajectory regularization, or modified probability paths and solvers. We introduce Temporal Pair Consistency (TPC), a lightweight variance-reduction principle that couples velocity predictions at paired timesteps along the same probability path, operating entirely at the estimator level without modifying the model architecture, probability path, or solver. We provide a theoretical analysis showing that TPC induces a quadratic, trajectory-coupled regularization that provably reduces gradient variance while preserving the underlying flow-matching objective. Instantiated within flow matching, TPC improves sample quality and efficiency across CIFAR-10 and ImageNet at multiple resolutions, achieving lower FID at identical or lower computational cost than prior methods, and extends seamlessly to modern SOTA-style pipelines with noise-augmented training, score-based denoising, and rectified flow.

</details>


### [141] [Learning Where It Matters: Geometric Anchoring for Robust Preference Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04909)
*Youngjae Cho,Jongsuk Kim,Ji-Hoon Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GAPO replaces a fixed reference policy with a dynamic, geometry-aware adversarial anchor to reweight pairwise preferences, improving robustness to noisy supervision while preserving or improving standard LLM alignment and reasoning performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed-reference regularization in DPO miscalibrates as the policy drifts, causing distributional mismatch and spurious signals under noise. Reference-free methods avoid mismatch but risk unbounded reward drift. A geometry-aware anchor aims to balance robustness and signal fidelity.

Method: Introduce an adversarial local perturbation of the current policy within a small radius as a pessimistic dynamic anchor. Use this anchor to adaptively reweight each preference pair by local sensitivity. Define Anchor Gap as the reward difference between the policy and its anchor, and under smoothness, show it approximates worst-case local margin degradation. Optimize a logistic objective weighted by the Anchor Gap to downweight brittle instances and emphasize robust signals.

Result: GAPO yields improved robustness across diverse noise settings, while matching or improving performance on standard LLM alignment and reasoning benchmarks.

Conclusion: Dynamic geometry-aware anchoring provides robustness to label noise and drift without sacrificing alignment performance, by downweighting brittle preferences and emphasizing robust signals through a theoretically motivated Anchor Gap.

Abstract: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and related methods align large language models from pairwise preferences by regularizing updates against a fixed reference policy. As the policy drifts, a static reference, however, can become increasingly miscalibrated, leading to distributional mismatch and amplifying spurious preference signals under noisy supervision. Conversely, reference-free variants avoid mismatch but often suffer from unconstrained reward drift. We propose Geometric Anchor Preference Optimization (GAPO), which replaces the fixed reference with a dynamic, geometry-aware anchor: an adversarial local perturbation of the current policy within a small radius that serves as a pessimistic baseline. This anchor enables an adaptive reweighting mechanism, modulating the importance of each preference pair based on its local sensitivity. We further introduce the Anchor Gap, the reward discrepancy between the policy and its anchor, and show under smoothness conditions that it approximates worst-case local margin degradation. Optimizing a logistic objective weighted by this gap downweights geometrically brittle instances while emphasizing robust preference signals. Across diverse noise settings, GAPO consistently improves robustness while matching or improving performance on standard LLM alignment and reasoning benchmarks.

</details>


### [142] [A logical re-conception of neural networks: Hamiltonian bitwise part-whole architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04911)
*E Bowen,R Granger,A Rodriguez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Relational graph-encoded system with a graph-Hamiltonian energy to enforce relation constraints; low-precision, linear-scaling; combines symbolic-like relational reasoning with ANN compatibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond standard ANN representations by directly encoding relations (part-whole, adjacency) as intrinsic components of the architecture, enabling symbolic-like reasoning and abductive inference.

Method: Graph-encoded data using a fixed set of elemental relation codes; a graph-Hamiltonian operator computes energies among encodings; ground states satisfy all relation constraints; low-precision arithmetic; shows equivalence to a subset of ANN operations; hierarchical relational representations emerge.

Result: Produces symbolic-like relational structures (part-of, next-to), abductive inference, relational position-based encodings; can process standard ANN tasks; linear in edge count; potentially simpler architectures.

Conclusion: An initial system offering a bridge between symbolic relational representations and neural-like computation, with room for tooling and extensions.

Abstract: We introduce a simple initial working system in which relations (such as part-whole) are directly represented via an architecture with operating and learning rules fundamentally distinct from standard artificial neural network methods. Arbitrary data are straightforwardly encoded as graphs whose edges correspond to codes from a small fixed primitive set of elemental pairwise relations, such that simple relational encoding is not an add-on, but occurs intrinsically within the most basic components of the system. A novel graph-Hamiltonian operator calculates energies among these encodings, with ground states denoting simultaneous satisfaction of all relation constraints among graph vertices. The method solely uses radically low-precision arithmetic; computational cost is correspondingly low, and scales linearly with the number of edges in the data. The resulting unconventional architecture can process standard ANN examples, but also produces representations that exhibit characteristics of symbolic computation. Specifically, the method identifies simple logical relational structures in these data (part-of; next-to), building hierarchical representations that enable abductive inferential steps generating relational position-based encodings, rather than solely statistical representations. Notably, an equivalent set of ANN operations are derived, identifying a special case of embedded vector encodings that may constitute a useful approach to current work in higher-level semantic representation. The very simple current state of the implemented system invites additional tools and improvements.

</details>


### [143] [A$^2$-LLM: An End-to-end Conversational Audio Avatar Large Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04913)
*Xiaolin Hu,Hang Yuan,Xinzhu Sang,Binbin Yan,Zhou Yu,Cong Huang,Kai Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An end-to-end conversational audio avatar LLM (A^2-LLM) jointly reasons over language, audio prosody, and 3D facial motion to produce emotionally expressive avatars, addressing latency and error accumulation in cascaded pipelines. It introduces FLAME-QA, a multimodal dataset for aligning semantic intent with facial dynamics in QA tasks, and demonstrates real-time performance (≈500 ms latency, 0.7 RTF).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cascaded dialogue systems suffer from error propagation, high latency, and limited real-time emotional expressiveness due to lack of access to full conversational context and rigid lip-sync. An end-to-end model that jointly reasons over language, prosody, and facial motion can improve expressiveness and responsiveness.

Method: Proposes A^2-LLM, an end-to-end model that jointly reasons about language, audio prosody, and 3D facial motion within a unified framework. Introduces FLAME-QA, a high-quality multimodal dataset designed to align semantic intent with expressive facial dynamics in a QA format.

Result: The system achieves superior emotional expressiveness while maintaining real-time efficiency, with ~500 ms latency and 0.7× real-time factor (RTF).

Conclusion: An end-to-end conversational audio avatar LLM can enhance emotional expressiveness and responsiveness by integrating language, prosody, and facial motion, offering potential improvements over traditional cascaded pipelines and enabling real-time, emotionally rich HCI.

Abstract: Developing expressive and responsive conversational digital humans is a cornerstone of next-generation human-computer interaction. While large language models (LLMs) have significantly enhanced dialogue capabilities, most current systems still rely on cascaded architectures that connect independent modules. These pipelines are often plagued by accumulated errors, high latency, and poor real-time performance. Lacking access to the underlying conversational context, these pipelines inherently prioritize rigid lip-sync over emotional depth. To address these challenges, we propose A$^2$-LLM, an end-to-end conversational audio avatar large language model that jointly reasons about language, audio prosody, and 3D facial motion within a unified framework. To facilitate training, we introduce FLAME-QA, a high-quality multimodal dataset designed to align semantic intent with expressive facial dynamics within a QA format. By leveraging deep semantic understanding, A$^2$-LLM generates emotionally rich facial movements beyond simple lip-synchronization. Experimental results demonstrate that our system achieves superior emotional expressiveness while maintaining real-time efficiency (500 ms latency, 0.7 RTF).

</details>


### [144] [SLAY: Geometry-Aware Spherical Linearized Attention with Yat-Kernel](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04915)
*Jose Miguel Luna,Taha Bouhsine,Krzysztof Choromanski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SLAY is a linear-time attention mechanism on unit-sphere queries/keys using Yat-kernel interactions. It uses Bernstein's theorem to express a spherical Yat-kernel as a nonnegative mixture of polynomial-exponential kernels, enabling a positive random-feature approximation that yields O(L) attention while preserving near-softmax performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for scalable Transformers with softmax-like attention without quadratic cost. Existing linear-time alternatives (e.g., Performers, Cosformers) trade off accuracy or performance. The work seeks a geometry-aware, efficient alternative by leveraging inverse-square-inspired interactions on the sphere.

Method: Constrain queries and keys to the unit sphere so attention depends on angular alignment. Represent the spherical Yat-kernel as a positive mixture of polynomial-exponential kernels via Bernstein's theorem. Derive a strictly positive random-feature approximation to enable linear-time attention. Prove positive definiteness and boundedness on the sphere, ensuring well-defined, nonnegative attention scores.

Result: Empirically, SLAY closely matches standard softmax attention in performance while maintaining linear time and memory. It consistently outperforms prior linear-time methods like Performers and Cosformers, offering the best-known linear-time approximation to softmax to date.

Conclusion: SLAY provides a near-perfect linear-time surrogate to softmax attention with robust theoretical guarantees (positivity, boundedness) and strong empirical performance, enabling scalable Transformers without the typical trade-offs of attention linearization.

Abstract: We propose a new class of linear-time attention mechanisms based on a relaxed and computationally efficient formulation of the recently introduced E-Product, often referred to as the Yat-kernel (Bouhsine, 2025). The resulting interactions are geometry-aware and inspired by inverse-square interactions in physics. Our method, Spherical Linearized Attention with Yat Kernels (SLAY), constrains queries and keys to the unit sphere so that attention depends only on angular alignment. Using Bernstein's theorem, we express the spherical Yat-kernel as a nonnegative mixture of polynomial-exponential product kernels and derive a strictly positive random-feature approximation enabling linear-time O(L) attention. We establish positive definiteness and boundedness on the sphere and show that the estimator yields well-defined, nonnegative attention scores. Empirically, SLAY achieves performance that is nearly indistinguishable from standard softmax attention while retaining linear time and memory scaling, and consistently outperforms prior linear-time attention mechanisms such as Performers and Cosformers. To the best of our knowledge, SLAY represents the closest linear-time approximation to softmax attention reported to date, enabling scalable Transformers without the typical performance trade-offs of attention linearization.

</details>


### [145] [Multi-Aspect Mining and Anomaly Detection for Heterogeneous Tensor Streams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04917)
*Soshi Kakio,Yasuko Matsubara,Ren Fujiwara,Yasushi Sakurai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes HeteroComp for continuous, heterogeneous tensor streams to detect group anomalies using Gaussian-process-based modeling of continuous attributes and temporal dynamics; claims superior accuracy and data-length independent runtime vs. state-of-the-art.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current tensor methods fail for heterogeneous data and temporal dynamics: discretization or inappropriate distribution assumptions degrade performance, and group anomalies (e.g., DoS) are hard to detect.

Method: HeteroComp summarizes streams into components representing latent groups per attribute and their temporal dynamics; uses Gaussian process priors to model unknown distributions and evolution; directly estimates densities from data; provides concise summaries.

Result: Empirical experiments on real datasets show improved group anomaly detection accuracy over state-of-the-art; computational time does not grow with stream length.

Conclusion: HeteroComp effectively handles heterogeneous tensor streams and temporal dynamics, enabling robust, scalable group anomaly detection.

Abstract: Analysis and anomaly detection in event tensor streams consisting of timestamps and multiple attributes - such as communication logs(time, IP address, packet length)- are essential tasks in data mining. While existing tensor decomposition and anomaly detection methods provide useful insights, they face the following two limitations. (i) They cannot handle heterogeneous tensor streams, which comprises both categorical attributes(e.g., IP address) and continuous attributes(e.g., packet length). They typically require either discretizing continuous attributes or treating categorical attributes as continuous, both of which distort the underlying statistical properties of the data.Furthermore, incorrect assumptions about the distribution family of continuous attributes often degrade the model's performance. (ii) They discretize timestamps, failing to track the temporal dynamics of streams(e.g., trends, abnormal events), which makes them ineffective for detecting anomalies at the group level, referred to as 'group anomalies' (e.g, DoS attacks). To address these challenges, we propose HeteroComp, a method for continuously summarizing heterogeneous tensor streams into 'components' representing latent groups in each attribute and their temporal dynamics, and detecting group anomalies. Our method employs Gaussian process priors to model unknown distributions of continuous attributes, and temporal dynamics, which directly estimate probability densities from data. Extracted components give concise but effective summarization, enabling accurate group anomaly detection. Extensive experiments on real datasets demonstrate that HeteroComp outperforms the state-of-the-art algorithms for group anomaly detection accuracy, and its computational time does not depend on the data stream length.

</details>


### [146] [Simulated Adoption: Decoupling Magnitude and Direction in LLM In-Context Conflict Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04918)
*Long Zhang,Fangwei Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Across three LLMs, conflicting in-context information is not universally explained by magnitude dilution; instead, compliance arises from orthogonal interference in the residual stream, rotating hidden state via a steering vector that is quasi-orthogonal to the true direction, challenging scalar confidence metrics and implying the need for vectorial monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uncover the mechanistic basis of sycophancy/compliance in LLMs by distinguishing magnitude-based suppression from directional, geometric changes in the residual stream during counterfactual contexts.

Method: Layer-wise geometric analysis of residual updates induced by counterfactual contexts across Qwen-4B, Llama-3.1-8B, and GLM-4-9B. Decompose changes into radial (norm-based) and angular (cosine-based) components; compare effects on factual vs. counterfactual queries.

Result: The Manifold Dilution hypothesis is not universal: two of the three architectures maintain stable residual norms even as factual performance degrades. Compliance is characterized by Orthogonal Interference, where the conflicting context injects a steering vector quasi-orthogonal to the ground-truth direction, effectively rotating the hidden state representation without reducing its magnitude.

Conclusion: Effective detection of hallucinations requires vectorial (directional) analysis of internal representations, not scalar confidence measures. Models may simulate adoption of in-context info via geometric displacement, underscoring the need for monitoring residual directions to distinguish genuine knowledge integration from mimicry.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) frequently prioritize conflicting in-context information over pre-existing parametric memory, a phenomenon often termed sycophancy or compliance. However, the mechanistic realization of this behavior remains obscure, specifically how the model resolves these knowledge conflicts through compliance, and whether this suppression arises from signal magnitude dilution or directional geometric alteration within the residual stream. To resolve this, we conducted a layer-wise geometric analysis across Qwen-4B, Llama-3.1-8B, and GLM-4-9B, decomposing the residual stream updates induced by counter-factual contexts into radial (norm-based) and angular (cosine-based) components. Our empirical results reject the universality of the "Manifold Dilution" hypothesis, as two of the three architectures maintained stable residual norms despite exhibiting significant performance degradation on factual queries. Instead, we observed that compliance is consistently characterized by "Orthogonal Interference," where the conflicting context injects a steering vector that is quasi-orthogonal to the ground-truth direction, effectively rotating the hidden state representation. This suggests that models do not "unlearn" or suppress the magnitude of internal truths but rather employ a mechanism of geometric displacement to bypass the correct unembedding vector, effectively simulating adoption while preserving the original structural magnitude. These findings challenge scalar confidence metrics for detecting hallucinations and underscore the necessity of vectorial monitoring to distinguish between genuine knowledge integration and superficial in-context mimicry.

</details>


### [147] [Gradually Compacting Large Language Models for Reasoning Like a Boiling Frog](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04919)
*Yiran Zhao,Shengyang Zhou,Zijian Wu,Tongyan Hu,Yuhui Xu,Rengan Dou,Kenji Kawaguchi,Shafiq Joty,Junnan Li,Michael Qizhe Shieh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A gradual pruning-and-finetuning loop (PTL) enables iterative, fine-grained compression of LLMs, achieving about 50% parameter reduction with minimal performance loss and broad applicability across pruning strategies and tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the substantial resource demands of large language models by removing redundant parameters without causing steep declines in reasoning or generalization performance, avoiding abrupt degradation typical of aggressive pruning.

Method: Partition the compression into multiple small iterations, applying a Prune-Tune Loop (PTL) at each stage to progressively shrink the model and recover performance via fine-tuning. PTL is compatible with various pruning strategies (neuron, layer pruning) and post-training approaches (continual pre-training, reinforcement learning). The method is evaluated on reasoning tasks and code generation.

Result: PTL enables compression to roughly half the original size with only lightweight post-training and preserves performance comparable to the original model on reasoning tasks. It demonstrates flexibility across pruning strategies and tasks, including code generation, indicating broad applicability.

Conclusion: PTL offers a flexible, effective framework for resource-efficient LLM deployment by gradually pruning and retuning, mitigating abrupt performance drops and extending applicability across pruning methods and post-training techniques.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive reasoning capabilities, but their substantial size often demands significant computational resources. To reduce resource consumption and accelerate inference, it is essential to eliminate redundant parameters without compromising performance. However, conventional pruning methods that directly remove such parameters often lead to a dramatic drop in model performance in reasoning tasks, and require extensive post-training to recover the lost capabilities. In this work, we propose a gradual compacting method that divides the compression process into multiple fine-grained iterations, applying a Prune-Tune Loop (PTL) at each stage to incrementally reduce model size while restoring performance with finetuning. This iterative approach-reminiscent of the "boiling frog" effect-enables the model to be progressively compressed without abrupt performance loss. Experimental results show that PTL can compress LLMs to nearly half their original size with only lightweight post-training, while maintaining performance comparable to the original model on reasoning tasks. Moreover, PTL is flexible and can be applied to various pruning strategies, such as neuron pruning and layer pruning, as well as different post-training methods, including continual pre-training and reinforcement learning. Additionally, experimental results confirm the effectiveness of PTL on a variety of tasks beyond mathematical reasoning, such as code generation, demonstrating its broad applicability.

</details>


### [148] [CyIN: Cyclic Informative Latent Space for Bridging Complete and Incomplete Multimodal Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04920)
*Ronghao Lin,Qiaolin He,Sijie Mai,Ying Zeng,Aolin Xiong,Li Huang,Yap-Peng Tan,Haifeng Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CyIN proposes a cyclic Information Bottleneck framework for robust multimodal learning with incomplete inputs, using latent purification and cross-modal translation to jointly train on complete and incomplete data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world multimodal data often have missing modalities, causing degradation of pre-trained models. A unified framework that bridges complete and incomplete multimodal learning via informative latent spaces and cross-modal reconstruction is needed.

Method: Introduce token- and label-level Information Bottleneck cyclically across modalities; use variational approximation to obtain task-relevant latent variables; purify latent representations; perform cross-modal cyclic translation to reconstruct missing modalities through forward and reverse propagation; jointly optimize complete and incomplete learning in a single model.

Result: Extensive experiments on 4 multimodal datasets show superior performance in both complete and various incomplete scenarios.

Conclusion: CyIN effectively bridges complete and incomplete multimodal learning via cyclic IB and cross-modal reconstruction, enabling robust multimodal fusion under missing modalities.

Abstract: Multimodal machine learning, mimicking the human brain's ability to integrate various modalities has seen rapid growth. Most previous multimodal models are trained on perfectly paired multimodal input to reach optimal performance. In real-world deployments, however, the presence of modality is highly variable and unpredictable, causing the pre-trained models in suffering significant performance drops and fail to remain robust with dynamic missing modalities circumstances. In this paper, we present a novel Cyclic INformative Learning framework (CyIN) to bridge the gap between complete and incomplete multimodal learning. Specifically, we firstly build an informative latent space by adopting token- and label-level Information Bottleneck (IB) cyclically among various modalities. Capturing task-related features with variational approximation, the informative bottleneck latents are purified for more efficient cross-modal interaction and multimodal fusion. Moreover, to supplement the missing information caused by incomplete multimodal input, we propose cross-modal cyclic translation by reconstruct the missing modalities with the remained ones through forward and reverse propagation process. With the help of the extracted and reconstructed informative latents, CyIN succeeds in jointly optimizing complete and incomplete multimodal learning in one unified model. Extensive experiments on 4 multimodal datasets demonstrate the superior performance of our method in both complete and diverse incomplete scenarios.

</details>


### [149] [Imposing Boundary Conditions on Neural Operators via Learned Function Extensions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04923)
*Sepehr Mousavi,Siddhartha Mishra,Laura De Lorenzis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes boundary-to-domain latent extensions to condition neural operators on complex non-homogeneous boundary conditions, enabling standard operator architectures to exploit boundary data; validated on 18 PDE datasets (Poisson, linear elasticity, hyperelasticity) with state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural operators often struggle with highly variable boundary conditions. A general, architecture-agnostic conditioning mechanism is needed to inject boundary information into the operator.

Method: Introduce boundary-to-domain extensions: map boundary data into latent pseudo-extensions defined over the entire spatial domain. These extensions can be consumed by any standard neural operator architecture, enabling joint learning of boundary effects and domain inputs. The approach is evaluated by coupling with a domain-to-domain operator across 18 datasets spanning Poisson, linear elasticity, and hyperelasticity with highly variable, mixed-type, component-wise, and multi-segment BCs on diverse geometries.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy, outperforming baselines by large margins, with robust performance across datasets; no hyperparameter tuning required across the 18 datasets.

Conclusion: Learning boundary-to-domain extensions is an effective and practical strategy for imposing complex BCs within neural operator frameworks, expanding their applicability to a broader class of PDE-governed problems.

Abstract: Neural operators have emerged as powerful surrogates for the solution of partial differential equations (PDEs), yet their ability to handle general, highly variable boundary conditions (BCs) remains limited. Existing approaches often fail when the solution operator exhibits strong sensitivity to boundary forcings. We propose a general framework for conditioning neural operators on complex non-homogeneous BCs through function extensions. Our key idea is to map boundary data to latent pseudo-extensions defined over the entire spatial domain, enabling any standard operator learning architecture to consume boundary information. The resulting operator, coupled with an arbitrary domain-to-domain neural operator, can learn rich dependencies on complex BCs and input domain functions at the same time. To benchmark this setting, we construct 18 challenging datasets spanning Poisson, linear elasticity, and hyperelasticity problems, with highly variable, mixed-type, component-wise, and multi-segment BCs on diverse geometries. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art accuracy, outperforming baselines by large margins, while requiring no hyperparameter tuning across datasets. Overall, our results demonstrate that learning boundary-to-domain extensions is an effective and practical strategy for imposing complex BCs in existing neural operator frameworks, enabling accurate and robust scientific machine learning models for a broader range of PDE-governed problems.

</details>


### [150] [Knowing When to Answer: Adaptive Confidence Refinement for Reliable Audio-Visual Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04924)
*Dinh Phu Tran,Jihoon Jeong,Saad Wazir,Seongah Kim,Thao Do,Cem Subakan,Daeyoung Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Adaptive Confidence Refinement (ACR) for Reliable Audio-Visual Question Answering, enhancing abstention by refining MSP with a Residual Risk Head and a Confidence Gating Head; achieves consistent gains across in-/out-of-distribution and bias scenarios on multiple AVQA models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AVQA models excel at accuracy but lack reliable uncertainty estimates to abstain when likely wrong. MSP is not Bayes-optimal without strong calibration, which deep multimodal models rarely have, motivating a method that preserves MSP while correcting its failures.

Method: ACR keeps MSP as the primary confidence signal and adds two learned heads: (i) Residual Risk Head predicting low-magnitude correctness residuals missed by MSP, and (ii) Confidence Gating Head assessing MSP trustworthiness. The approach applies input-adaptive residual corrections when MSP is unreliable, enabling adaptive abstention without discarding MSP.

Result: Theoretical analysis and experiments show ACR outperforms existing methods on in-distribution, out-of-distribution, and data bias settings across three AVQA architectures, indicating robust improvement for R-AVQA.

Conclusion: ACR provides a solid foundation for Reliable AVQA by leveraging MSP with learnable corrections and gating, offering improved abstention behavior and robustness; code will be released upon acceptance.

Abstract: We present a formal problem formulation for \textit{Reliable} Audio-Visual Question Answering ($\mathcal{R}$-AVQA), where we prefer abstention over answering incorrectly. While recent AVQA models have high accuracy, their ability to identify when they are likely wrong and their consequent abstention from answering remain underexplored areas of research. To fill this gap, we explore several approaches and then propose Adaptive Confidence Refinement (ACR), a lightweight method to further enhance the performance of $\mathcal{R}$-AVQA. Our key insight is that the Maximum Softmax Probability (MSP) is Bayes-optimal only under strong calibration, a condition usually not met in deep neural networks, particularly in multimodal models. Instead of replacing MSP, our ACR maintains it as a primary confidence signal and applies input-adaptive residual corrections when MSP is deemed unreliable. ACR introduces two learned heads: i) a Residual Risk Head that predicts low-magnitude correctness residuals that MSP does not capture, and ii) a Confidence Gating Head to determine MSP trustworthiness. Our experiments and theoretical analysis show that ACR consistently outperforms existing methods on in- and out-of-disrtibution, and data bias settings across three different AVQA architectures, establishing a solid foundation for $\mathcal{R}$-AVQA task. The code and checkpoints will be available upon acceptance \href{https://github.com/PhuTran1005/R-AVQA}{at here}

</details>


### [151] [Internalizing LLM Reasoning via Discovery and Replay of Latent Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04925)
*Zhenning Shi,Yijia Zhu,Junhan Shi,Xun Zhang,Lei Wang,Congcong Miao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STIR uses dynamic latent trajectory control to internalize reasoning, combining action induction, sparse tool basis, and value-modulated interventions to improve accuracy and reduce tokens, outperforming static activation steering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static activation steering vectors fail to adapt to non-stationary, evolving reasoning tasks; there is a need for adaptive, efficient mechanisms that internalize reasoning without explicit chain-of-thought generation.

Method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) differential intrinsic action induction to harvest latent reasoning successes and crystallize steering primitives; (2) sparse control basis construction to build a compact, geometrically diverse tool library; (3) value-modulated trajectory intervention that injects context-specific impulses via anchor-based gating; internalizes reasoning to bypass explicit generation.

Result: On six arithmetic and logical benchmarks across four models, STIR yields average accuracy gains of 1.9 to 7.5 percentage points and reduces average token consumption by up to 35% compared to vanilla decoding.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that explicit chain-of-thought benefits can be realized through dynamic latent trajectory control by internalizing the reasoning process; code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: The internalization of chain-of-thought processes into hidden states has emerged as a highly efficient paradigm for scaling test-time compute. However, existing activation steering methods rely on static control vectors that fail to adapt to the non-stationary evolution of complex reasoning tasks. To address this limitation, we propose STIR (Self-Distilled Tools for Internal Reasoning), a framework that reformulates reasoning enhancement as a dynamic latent trajectory control problem. STIR introduces a synergistic three-stage pipeline: (1) differential intrinsic action induction harvests latent reasoning successes to crystallize steering primitives; (2) sparse control basis construction curates a compact, geometrically diverse tool library; and (3) value-modulated trajectory intervention dynamically injects context-specific impulses via anchor-based gating. Extensive experiments on six arithmetic and logical benchmarks across four representative models demonstrate that STIR improves average accuracy by 1.9% to 7.5% while reducing average token consumption by up to 35% compared to vanilla decoding. These findings demonstrate that the benefits of explicit chain-of-thought can be realized through dynamic latent trajectory control, internalizing the reasoning process to bypass the explicit generation while achieving superior fidelity. Our code is available at https://github.com/sznnzs/LLM-Latent-Action.

</details>


### [152] [Euphonium: Steering Video Flow Matching via Process Reward Gradient Guided Stochastic Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04928)
*Ruizhe Zhong,Jiesong Lian,Xiaoyue Mi,Zixiang Zhou,Yuan Zhou,Qinglin Lu,Junchi Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Euphonium reframes sampling for online RL alignment as a gradient-guided SDE, yielding dense reward-directed exploration, a distillation objective to embed guidance into the model, and a Dual-Reward optimization that blends latent process rewards with final pixel rewards; shows improved alignment and faster convergence in text-to-video generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current online RL with undirected exploration and sparse rewards leads to data-inefficient learning for flow-matching alignment models. A principled, gradient-guided sampling mechanism can make exploration denser and more targeted toward high-reward regions.

Method: Formulate the sampling process as a stochastic differential equation whose drift term incorporates the gradient of a Process Reward Model. Demonstrate that prior methods (e.g., Flow-GRPO, DanceGRPO) are special cases. Derive a distillation objective to internalize guidance into the flow network, removing inference-time dependence on the reward model. Instantiate with Dual-Reward Group Relative Policy Optimization, combining latent process rewards for credit assignment with pixel-level outcome rewards for visual fidelity.

Result: Empirical evaluation on text-to-video generation shows Euphonium yields better alignment than existing methods and accelerates training convergence by approximately 1.66×.

Conclusion: A principled, reward-gradient-guided sampling framework that unifies and extends prior approaches, reduces reliance on external reward models at inference, and delivers faster, better-aligned generation in practice.

Abstract: While online Reinforcement Learning has emerged as a crucial technique for aligning flow matching models with human preferences, current approaches are hindered by inefficient exploration during training rollouts. Relying on undirected stochasticity and sparse outcome rewards, these methods struggle to discover high-reward samples, resulting in data-inefficient and slow optimization. To address these limitations, we propose Euphonium, a novel framework that steers generation via process reward gradient guided dynamics. Our key insight is to formulate the sampling process as a theoretically principled Stochastic Differential Equation that explicitly incorporates the gradient of a Process Reward Model into the flow drift. This design enables dense, step-by-step steering toward high-reward regions, advancing beyond the unguided exploration in prior works, and theoretically encompasses existing sampling methods (e.g., Flow-GRPO, DanceGRPO) as special cases. We further derive a distillation objective that internalizes the guidance signal into the flow network, eliminating inference-time dependency on the reward model. We instantiate this framework with a Dual-Reward Group Relative Policy Optimization algorithm, combining latent process rewards for efficient credit assignment with pixel-level outcome rewards for final visual fidelity. Experiments on text-to-video generation show that Euphonium achieves better alignment compared to existing methods while accelerating training convergence by 1.66x.

</details>


### [153] [TurboBoA: Faster and Exact Attention-aware Quantization without Backpropagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04929)
*Junhan Kim,Yeo Jeong Park,Seungwoo Son,Chungman Lee,Ho-young Kim,Joonyoung Kim,Yongkweon Jeon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TurboBoA is a backpropagation-free post-training quantization (PTQ) method that speeds up BoA by jointly quantizing multiple out-channels with a closed-form error compensation, adding error-propagation correction, and using adaptive grid with coordinate descent. It yields more than 3x speedup over BoA while improving accuracy, achieving state-of-the-art results with outlier suppression for both weight-only and weight-activation quantization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce memory and compute in large language models via PTQ. GPTQ’s layer-wise independence hurts accuracy in low-bit regimes, while BoA accounts for inter-layer dependencies but suffers from sequential, channel-wise bottlenecks. A faster, backpropagation-free PTQ that preserves inter-layer benefits is needed.

Method: Three core innovations: (i) joint quantization of multiple out-channels with a closed-form error compensation rule to reduce sequential bottlenecks; (ii) a correction mechanism for errors propagated from preceding quantized layers; (iii) adaptive grid computation with coordinate descent refinement to maintain alignment during iterative updates. The approach is backpropagation-free.

Result: Extensive experiments show substantial acceleration over BoA and improved accuracy; when combined with outlier suppression, TurboBoA achieves state-of-the-art results in both weight-only and weight-activation quantization.

Conclusion: TurboBoA delivers a fast, accurate PTQ solution for LLMs, enabling quantization with significant speedups and improved performance; code will be released at the provided GitHub URL.

Abstract: The rapid growth of large language models (LLMs) has heightened the importance of post-training quantization (PTQ) for reducing memory and computation costs. Among PTQ methods, GPTQ has gained significant attention for its efficiency, enabling billion-scale LLMs to be quantized within a few GPU hours. However, GPTQ's assumption of layer-wise independence leads to severe accuracy drops in low-bit regimes. Recently, BoA improved upon GPTQ by incorporating inter-layer dependencies within attention modules, but its reliance on sequential quantization across all out-channels makes it substantially less efficient. In this paper, we propose TurboBoA, a new backpropagation-free PTQ algorithm that preserves the accuracy benefits of BoA while significantly accelerating the process. The proposed TurboBoA introduces three key innovations: (i) joint quantization of multiple out-channels with a closed-form error compensation rule, which reduces sequential bottlenecks and yields more than a three-fold speedup; (ii) a correction mechanism for errors propagated from preceding quantized layers; and (iii) adaptive grid computation with coordinate descent refinement to maintain alignment during iterative updates. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TurboBoA delivers substantial acceleration over BoA while consistently improving accuracy. When combined with outlier suppression techniques, it achieves state-of-the-art results in both weight-only and weight-activation quantization. The code will be available at https://github.com/SamsungLabs/TurboBoA.

</details>


### [154] [Depth-Wise Emergence of Prediction-Centric Geometry in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04931)
*Shahar Haim,Daniel C McNamee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decoder-only LLMs show a depth-wise shift from context-processing to prediction-forming, underpinned by a geometrically structured late-layer code that enables selective causal control of predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uncover the mechanistic-geometric basis by which context is transformed into predictions in decoder-only LLMs, integrating geometric analysis with causal interventions.

Method: Geometric analysis of layer-wise representations combined with mechanistic interventions; analysis of angular organization and vector norms; assessment of how geometry relates to prediction distributions and causal control.

Result: Late-layer representations exhibit angular organization that parameterizes prediction similarity; representation norms encode context-specific information that does not determine predictions; together they provide a mechanistic account of context-to-prediction transformation and enable selective causal control over token prediction.

Conclusion: A mechanistic-geometric framework clarifies how context is transformed into predictions in decoder-only LLMs, highlighting the distinct roles of angular geometry (prediction similarity) and norms (context content) in late layers.

Abstract: We show that decoder-only large language models exhibit a depth-wise transition from context-processing to prediction-forming phases of computation accompanied by a reorganization of representational geometry. Using a unified framework combining geometric analysis with mechanistic intervention, we demonstrate that late-layer representations implement a structured geometric code that enables selective causal control over token prediction. Specifically, angular organization of the representation geometry parametrizes prediction distributional similarity, while representation norms encode context-specific information that does not determine prediction. Together, these results provide a mechanistic-geometric account of the dynamics of transforming context into predictions in LLMs.

</details>


### [155] [Comparing Euclidean and Hyperbolic K-Means for Generalized Category Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04932)
*Mohamad Dalal,Thomas B. Moeslund,Joakim Bruslund Haurum*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HC-GCD embeds data on the Lorentz hyperboloid and clusters with hyperbolic K-Means, evaluating on Semantic Shift Benchmark datasets. It matches state-of-the-art hyperbolic GCD and outperforms Euclidean clustering, with ablations showing norm clipping can harm unseen-class clustering while helping seen-class accuracy; results vary by dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation that prior hyperbolic GCD methods only use hyperbolic geometry for representation learning and convert to Euclidean space for clustering. Propose end-to-end hyperbolic clustering to better leverage hierarchical structure.

Method: Represent data in the Lorentz hyperboloid model, apply a hyperbolic K-Means clustering algorithm directly in hyperbolic space, and evaluate on Semantic Shift Benchmark datasets. Conduct ablations on Euclidean embedding norm clipping and compare clustering consistency across label granularity.

Result: HC-GCD achieves performance on par with the previous state-of-the-art hyperbolic GCD method. Hyperbolic K-Means yields higher clustering accuracy than Euclidean K-Means. Ablations show norm clipping of Euclidean embeddings decreases unseen-class clustering accuracy but increases seen-class accuracy; overall accuracy is dataset-dependent. Hyperbolic K-Means yields more consistent clusters across varying label granularity.

Conclusion: End-to-end hyperbolic clustering in the Lorentz model is viable for GCD and can match or exceed Euclidean-based clustering and traditional hyperbolic representations. The approach enhances clustering stability across label granularities and highlights the sensitivity of unseen/seen-cluster performance to Euclidean norm clipping.

Abstract: Hyperbolic representation learning has been widely used to extract implicit hierarchies within data, and recently it has found its way to the open-world classification task of Generalized Category Discovery (GCD). However, prior hyperbolic GCD methods only use hyperbolic geometry for representation learning and transform back to Euclidean geometry when clustering. We hypothesize this is suboptimal. Therefore, we present Hyperbolic Clustered GCD (HC-GCD), which learns embeddings in the Lorentz Hyperboloid model of hyperbolic geometry, and clusters these embeddings directly in hyperbolic space using a hyperbolic K-Means algorithm. We test our model on the Semantic Shift Benchmark datasets, and demonstrate that HC-GCD is on par with the previous state-of-the-art hyperbolic GCD method. Furthermore, we show that using hyperbolic K-Means leads to better accuracy than Euclidean K-Means. We carry out ablation studies showing that clipping the norm of the Euclidean embeddings leads to decreased accuracy in clustering unseen classes, and increased accuracy for seen classes, while the overall accuracy is dataset dependent. We also show that using hyperbolic K-Means leads to more consistent clusters when varying the label granularity.

</details>


### [156] [Linear Model Merging Unlocks Simple and Scalable Multimodal Data Mixture Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04937)
*Davide Berasi,Matteo Farina,Massimiliano Mancini,Elisa Ricci*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A data-mixing optimization problem for SFT of multimodal LLMs is addressed by using model merging as a proxy to rapidly estimate the performance of different data mixtures; domain-specific experts are merged to simulate mixture weights, achieving high correlation with actually trained mixtures across 14 benchmarks, enabling scalable search. Code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DMO is combinatorial and expensive; existing methods are costly; model merging can cheaply generate multiple proxy models to approximate performance of mixtures, enabling efficient exploration.

Method: Train domain-specific multimodal experts; construct weighted-parameter interpolations across expert models to form proxy models representing potential data mixtures; evaluate their performance to estimate corresponding mixture efficacy; perform experiments on 14 multimodal benchmarks; compare ranks with true mixtures.

Result: Proxy models via merging show high rank correlation with models trained on actual data mixtures; decouples mixture search from heavy training; demonstrates scalability; empirical evidence across 14 benchmarks; code available.

Conclusion: Using merged proxies is an effective, scalable strategy for Data Mixture Optimization in SFT of multimodal LLMs, enabling rapid identification of near-optimal data mixtures without full retraining.

Abstract: Selecting the best data mixture is critical for successful Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) of Multimodal Large Language Models. However, determining the optimal mixture weights across multiple domain-specific datasets remains a significant bottleneck due to the combinatorial search space and the high cost associated with even a single training run. This is the so-called Data Mixture Optimization (DMO) problem. On the other hand, model merging unifies domain-specific experts through parameter interpolation. This strategy is efficient, as it only requires a single training run per domain, yet oftentimes leads to suboptimal models. In this work, we take the best of both worlds, studying model merging as an efficient strategy for estimating the performance of different data mixtures. We train domain-specific multimodal experts and evaluate their weighted parameter-space combinations to estimate the efficacy of corresponding data mixtures. We conduct extensive experiments on 14 multimodal benchmarks, and empirically demonstrate that the merged proxy models exhibit a high rank correlation with models trained on actual data mixtures. This decouples the search for optimal mixtures from the resource-intensive training process, thereby providing a scalable and efficient strategy for navigating the complex landscape of mixture weights. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/BerasiDavide/mLLMs_merging_4_DMO.

</details>


### [157] [Transolver-3: Scaling Up Transformer Solvers to Industrial-Scale Geometries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04940)
*Hang Zhou,Haixu Wu,Haonan Shangguan,Yuezhou Ma,Huikun Weng,Jianmin Wang,Mingsheng Long*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transolver-3 extends neural PDE solvers to industrial-scale meshes by integrating two architectural optimizations (slice by associativity, geometry tiling), an amortized training regime on random high-res subsets, and a state caching strategy at inference, enabling simulation on meshes >160M cells and achieving strong performance across aerospace/auto benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The key motivation is to bridge the gap between limited GPU memory and the high resolution required for industrial PDE simulations, enabling high-fidelity surrogate modeling on meshes with hundreds of millions of cells.

Method: Introduce two optimizations: (1) faster slice and deslice leveraging matrix-multiplication associativity; (2) geometry slice tiling to partition computation across geometry. Pair with amortized training on random submeshes and a physical state caching mechanism during inference to manage memory and throughput.

Result: Demonstrates capability to handle meshes with over 160 million cells and strong performance across three challenging benchmarks (aircraft and automotive design tasks).

Conclusion: Transolver-3 provides a scalable, memory-efficient neural PDE solver framework suitable for industrial-scale simulations, closing the gap between high-fidelity requirements and GPU memory limits; potential for deployment in aerospace/automotive design workflows.

Abstract: Deep learning has emerged as a transformative tool for the neural surrogate modeling of partial differential equations (PDEs), known as neural PDE solvers. However, scaling these solvers to industrial-scale geometries with over $10^8$ cells remains a fundamental challenge due to the prohibitive memory complexity of processing high-resolution meshes. We present Transolver-3, a new member of the Transolver family as a highly scalable framework designed for high-fidelity physics simulations. To bridge the gap between limited GPU capacity and the resolution requirements of complex engineering tasks, we introduce two key architectural optimizations: faster slice and deslice by exploiting matrix multiplication associative property and geometry slice tiling to partition the computation of physical states. Combined with an amortized training strategy by learning on random subsets of original high-resolution meshes and a physical state caching technique during inference, Transolver-3 enables high-fidelity field prediction on industrial-scale meshes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Transolver-3 is capable of handling meshes with over 160 million cells, achieving impressive performance across three challenging simulation benchmarks, including aircraft and automotive design tasks.

</details>


### [158] [Improving Set Function Approximation with Quasi-Arithmetic Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04941)
*Tomas Tokar,Scott Sanner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Neuralized Kolmogorov Mean (NKM) and quasi-arithmetic neural networks (QUANNs) as trainable aggregation mechanisms for set-structured data, with universal approximation guarantees and improved transferability, achieving strong empirical results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Set-structured data often rely on fixed, non-learnable pooling (e.g., sum/max) which limits expressivity and transferability. A flexible, learnable aggregation can capture complex set statistics and improve generalization; invertible components may yield better latent representations.

Method: Propose NKM as a trainable, invertible neural-based central-tendency measure. Build QUANNs that incorporate NKM as the aggregation function within a set-function decomposition framework. Provide theoretical results showing universal approximation for a broad class of set-decompositions; leverage invertible components to obtain structured latent representations.

Result: Empirically, QUANNs outperform state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks and produce embeddings that transfer well to tasks not involving sets.

Conclusion: QUANNs with NKM offer a powerful, generalizable aggregation mechanism for sets, combining universal approximation with enhanced transferability and structured representations via invertible components; potential applicability spans multiple domains beyond traditional set tasks.

Abstract: Sets represent a fundamental abstraction across many types of data. To handle the unordered nature of set-structured data, models such as DeepSets and PointNet rely on fixed, non-learnable pooling operations (e.g., sum or max) -- a design choice that can hinder the transferability of learned embeddings and limits model expressivity. More recently, learnable aggregation functions have been proposed as more expressive alternatives. In this work, we advance this line of research by introducing the Neuralized Kolmogorov Mean (NKM) -- a novel, trainable framework for learning a generalized measure of central tendency through an invertible neural function. We further propose quasi-arithmetic neural networks (QUANNs), which incorporate the NKM as a learnable aggregation function. We provide a theoretical analysis showing that, QUANNs are universal approximators for a broad class of common set-function decompositions and, thanks to their invertible neural components, learn more structured latent representations. Empirically, QUANNs outperform state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks, while learning embeddings that transfer effectively even to tasks that do not involve sets.

</details>


### [159] [Privileged Information Distillation for Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04942)
*Emiliano Penaloza,Dheeraj Vattikonda,Nicolas Gontier,Alexandre Lacoste,Laurent Charlin,Massimo Caccia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: π-Distill enables distilling PI-conditioned frontier models into action-only policies via a joint teacher-student objective; OPSD offers an RL-based alternative with reverse KL penalty; both methods outperform standard supervised finetuning + RL in several benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: bridge the gap between training-time privileged information and inference-time policies when internal reasoning is hidden in frontier models, enabling effective distillation using action trajectories.

Method: π-Distill trains a PI-conditioned teacher and an unconditioned student within a single model, sharing weights and using PI as conditioning; OPSD trains with reinforcement learning using a reverse KL penalty to align the student with the PI-conditioned teacher.

Result: both approaches successfully distill frontier agents using action-only PI; π-Distill generally outperforms, with OPSD competitive in some settings; outperforms standard supervised finetuning + RL across multiple benchmarks, models, PI forms.

Conclusion: PI-based distillation is viable for transferring abilities learned with PI to inference-time policies; π-Distill is robust and OPSD is competitive under certain conditions; results highlight factors enabling learning with PI and guide when to use OPSD.

Abstract: Training-time privileged information (PI) can enable language models to succeed on tasks they would otherwise fail, making it a powerful tool for reinforcement learning in hard, long-horizon settings. However, transferring capabilities learned with PI to policies that must act without it at inference time remains a fundamental challenge. We study this problem in the context of distilling frontier models for multi-turn agentic environments, where closed-source systems typically hide their internal reasoning and expose only action trajectories. This breaks standard distillation pipelines, since successful behavior is observable but the reasoning process is not. For this, we introduce π-Distill, a joint teacher-student objective that trains a PI-conditioned teacher and an unconditioned student simultaneously using the same model. Additionally, we also introduce On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD), an alternative approach that trains using Reinforcement Learning (RL) with a reverse KL-penalty between the student and the PI-conditioned teacher. We show that both of these algorithms effectively distill frontier agents using action-only PI.
