Specifically we find that π-Distill and in some cases OPSD, outperform industry standard practices (Supervised finetuning followed by RL) that assume access to full Chain-of-Thought supervision across multiple agentic benchmarks, models, and forms of PI. We complement our results with extensive analysis that characterizes the factors enabling effective learning with PI, focusing primarily on π-Distill and characterizing when OPSD is competitive.

</details>


### [160] [Stochastic hierarchical data-driven optimization: application to plasma-surface kinetics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04975)
*José Afonso,Vasco Guerra,Pedro Viegas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A stochastic hierarchical optimization framework that uses a reduced Hessian to identify stiff parameter subspaces, enabling sample-efficient calibration of complex physical models; demonstrated on plasma–surface interactions with favorable comparisons to baselines; broadly applicable to reaction-network modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibrate expensive, high-dimensional physical models with uncertain, anisotropic parameter landscapes where exhaustive sampling is prohibitive.

Method: A Sloppy Model–inspired, stochastic hierarchical optimization that employs a reduced Hessian approximation to target the stiff subspace, integrated with a probabilistic loss derived directly from observed data to achieve principled inference with minimal simulations.

Result: Applied to plasma-surface interactions; consistently outperforms baseline optimization techniques in sample efficiency, validating the approach and demonstrating scalability to other complex reaction systems.

Conclusion: The framework provides a general, scalable tool for efficient calibration of complex reaction models (e.g., plasma chemistry, biochemical networks), by focusing search on stiff parameter directions and leveraging probabilistic inference to derive the objective from data.

Abstract: This work introduces a stochastic hierarchical optimization framework inspired by Sloppy Model theory for the efficient calibration of physical models. Central to this method is the use of a reduced Hessian approximation, which identifies and targets the stiff parameter subspace using minimal simulation queries. This strategy enables efficient navigation of highly anisotropic landscapes, avoiding the computational burden of exhaustive sampling. To ensure rigorous inference, we integrate this approach with a probabilistic formulation that derives a principled objective loss function directly from observed data. We validate the framework by applying it to the problem of plasma-surface interactions, where accurate modelling is strictly limited by uncertainties in surface reactivity parameters and the computational cost of kinetic simulations. Comparative analysis demonstrates that our method consistently outperforms baseline optimization techniques in sample efficiency. This approach offers a general and scalable tool for optimizing models of complex reaction systems, ranging from plasma chemistry to biochemical networks.

</details>


### [161] [ReFORM: Reflected Flows for On-support Offline RL via Noise Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05051)
*Songyuan Zhang,Oswin So,H. M. Sabbir Ahmad,Eric Yang Yu,Matthew Cleaveland,Mitchell Black,Chuchu Fan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReFORM is an offline RL method using flow policies to enforce a union of support constraints by construction, combining a bounded-source BC flow with a reflected flow to generate bounded noise while preserving support, achieving strong empirical performance on OGBench across 40 tasks with fixed hyperparameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline RL struggles with out-of-distribution (OOD) actions and multimodal optimal policies. Traditional penalty-based approaches restrict policy updates and may not fully prevent OOD actions. A more expressive policy family that can capture multimodality is desirable, but requires careful handling to avoid OOD actions.

Method: Train a behavior cloning (BC) flow policy with a bounded source distribution to capture the action-support. Then optimize a reflected flow that adds bounded noise to the BC flow while preserving the support constraint, thereby enabling policy improvement without violating the support set.

Result: Empirically, on 40 tasks from the OGBench benchmark with a fixed hyperparameter set across tasks, ReFORM outperforms baselines that require hand-tuned hyperparameters in performance profile curves.

Conclusion: Flow-based offline RL with a built-in support constraint via a BC flow and a reflected flow can effectively mitigate OOD issues while maintaining expressiveness, yielding strong empirical performance across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) aims to learn the optimal policy from a fixed dataset generated by behavior policies without additional environment interactions. One common challenge that arises in this setting is the out-of-distribution (OOD) error, which occurs when the policy leaves the training distribution. Prior methods penalize a statistical distance term to keep the policy close to the behavior policy, but this constrains policy improvement and may not completely prevent OOD actions. Another challenge is that the optimal policy distribution can be multimodal and difficult to represent. Recent works apply diffusion or flow policies to address this problem, but it is unclear how to avoid OOD errors while retaining policy expressiveness. We propose ReFORM, an offline RL method based on flow policies that enforces the less restrictive support constraint by construction. ReFORM learns a behavior cloning (BC) flow policy with a bounded source distribution to capture the support of the action distribution, then optimizes a reflected flow that generates bounded noise for the BC flow while keeping the support, to maximize the performance. Across 40 challenging tasks from the OGBench benchmark with datasets of varying quality and using a constant set of hyperparameters for all tasks, ReFORM dominates all baselines with hand-tuned hyperparameters on the performance profile curves.

</details>


### [162] [Near-Optimal Dynamic Matching via Coarsening with Application to Heart Transplantation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04989)
*Itai Zilberstein,Ioannis Anagnostides,Zachary W. Sollie,Arman Kilic,Tuomas Sandholm*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Coarsening-based online matching yields near-optimal guarantees; application to heart transplant allocation shows strong empirical performance and theoretical justification for clustering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between practical online matching algorithms (often with weak guarantees) and theoretical lower bounds; provide a principled, data-driven approach that yields provable performance for organ allocation.

Method: Develop online matching algorithms using a coarsening approach by aggregating offline nodes into capacitated clusters; leverage structural properties of historical data; apply to heart transplant allocation to derive policies with theoretical grounding.

Result: In simulations, the policy closely matches the omniscient benchmark, demonstrating near-optimal performance and validating the coarsening approach; provides rigorous justification for clustering-based organ allocation.

Conclusion: Coarsening can yield near-optimal guarantees in online matching and offers a principled justification for clustering-based policies in organ allocation, bridging data-driven heuristics and theoretical bounds.

Abstract: Online matching has been a mainstay in domains such as Internet advertising and organ allocation, but practical algorithms often lack strong theoretical guarantees. We take an important step toward addressing this by developing new online matching algorithms based on a coarsening approach. Although coarsening typically implies a loss of granularity, we show that, to the contrary, aggregating offline nodes into capacitated clusters can yield near-optimal theoretical guarantees. We apply our methodology to heart transplant allocation to develop theoretically grounded policies based on structural properties of historical data. In realistic simulations, our policy closely matches the performance of the omniscient benchmark. Our work bridges the gap between data-driven heuristics and pessimistic theoretical lower bounds, and provides rigorous justification for prior clustering-based approaches in organ allocation.

</details>


### [163] [Position: Machine Learning for Heart Transplant Allocation Policy Optimization Should Account for Incentives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04990)
*Ioannis Anagnostides,Itai Zilberstein,Zachary W. Sollie,Arman Kilic,Tuomas Sandholm*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Incentive-aware organ allocation: integrate mechanism design to address strategic behavior among stakeholders and improve fairness and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Organ allocation is not purely a static optimization; misaligned incentives across transplant centers, clinicians, and regulators distort decisions and outcomes.

Method: Position paper synthesis: map decision pipeline, identify incentive misalignments, present data on adverse effects, and propose a research agenda.

Result: Identified incentive misalignments in US adult heart allocation; evidence of adverse consequences; outline of incentive-aware policy framework and agenda.

Conclusion: Advocate incorporating mechanism design, strategic classification, causal inference, and social choice to achieve robust, efficient, and fair organ allocation under strategic behavior.

Abstract: The allocation of scarce donor organs constitutes one of the most consequential algorithmic challenges in healthcare. While the field is rapidly transitioning from rigid, rule-based systems to machine learning and data-driven optimization, we argue that current approaches often overlook a fundamental barrier: incentives. In this position paper, we highlight that organ allocation is not merely a static optimization problem, but rather a complex game involving transplant centers, clinicians, and regulators. Focusing on US adult heart transplant allocation, we identify critical incentive misalignments across the decision-making pipeline, and present data showing that they are having adverse consequences today. Our main position is that the next generation of allocation policies should be incentive aware. We outline a research agenda for the machine learning community, calling for the integration of mechanism design, strategic classification, causal inference, and social choice to ensure robustness, efficiency, and fairness in the face of strategic behavior from the various constituent groups.

</details>


### [164] [Learning Rate Matters: Vanilla LoRA May Suffice for LLM Fine-tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04998)
*Yu-Ang Lee,Ching-Yun Ko,Pin-Yu Chen,Mi-Yen Yeh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hyperparameter-search shows all four LoRA variants, plus vanilla LoRA, converge to near-identical peak performance when learning rate is tuned; vanilla LoRA remains a strong baseline; reported improvements may reflect configuration sensitivity; the differing optimal LRs align with Hessian eigenvalues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reassess claimed gains of LoRA variants under broader hyperparameter settings and model scales, and to understand whether improvements are robust beyond fixed training configurations.

Method: Systematic hyperparameter sweeps (extensive LR searches) across four LoRA variants and vanilla LoRA on math and code generation tasks spanning diverse model scales; in addition, a second-order Hessian-based analysis to explain LR differences.

Result: With proper LR tuning, all methods achieve similar peak performance (within 1-2%); learning-rate ranges differ by variant; rank effects are subtle; vanilla LoRA remains competitive.

Conclusion: Vanilla LoRA is a competitive baseline; improvements claimed under single-config setups may not generalize; LR sensitivity driven by largest Hessian eigenvalue provides theoretical alignment with classical learning dynamics.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is the prevailing approach for efficient large language model (LLM) fine-tuning. Building on this paradigm, recent studies have proposed alternative initialization strategies and architectural modifications, reporting substantial improvements over vanilla LoRA. However, these gains are often demonstrated under fixed or narrowly tuned hyperparameter settings, despite the known sensitivity of neural networks to training configurations. In this work, we systematically re-evaluate four representative LoRA variants alongside vanilla LoRA through extensive hyperparameter searches. Across mathematical and code generation tasks on diverse model scales, we find that different LoRA methods favor distinct learning rate ranges. Crucially, once learning rates are properly tuned, all methods achieve similar peak performance (within 1-2%), with only subtle rank-dependent behaviors. These results suggest that vanilla LoRA remains a competitive baseline and that improvements reported under single training configuration may not reflect consistent methodological advantages. Finally, a second-order analysis attributes the differing optimal learning rate ranges to variations in the largest Hessian eigenvalue, aligning with classical learning theories.

</details>


### [165] [EntRGi: Entropy Aware Reward Guidance for Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05000)
*Atula Tejaswi,Litu Rout,Constantine Caramanis,Sanjay Shakkottai,Sujay Sanghavi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EntRGi (Entropy-aware Reward Guidance) improves reward-based guidance for discrete diffusion language models by regulating gradients with the model's confidence, addressing the drawbacks of continuous relaxations and straight-through estimators. It yields consistent gains on a 7B LM across multiple rewards and benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable effective reward-guided optimization for discrete diffusion LMs, where gradients cannot backpropagate through discrete tokens. Prior methods suffer from degraded gradient feedback when using continuous relaxations and incorrect optimization when applying gradients at discrete tokens.

Method: Propose EntRGi, an entropy-aware mechanism that dynamically scales the gradient from the reward model by the confidence (entropy) of the LM’s outputs, thereby tempering the continuous relaxation and providing reliable inputs to the reward model.

Result: Empirical validation on a 7B-parameter diffusion language model across 3 reward models and 3 multi-skill benchmarks shows consistent improvements over state-of-the-art reward-guided methods.

Conclusion: Entropy-aware regulation improves reward guidance for discrete diffusion LMs, offering robust and transferable gains across diverse reward models and tasks.

Abstract: Reward guidance has been applied to great success in the test-time adaptation of continuous diffusion models; it updates each denoising step using the gradients from a downstream reward model. We study reward guidance for discrete diffusion language models, where one cannot differentiate through the natural outputs of the model because they are discrete tokens. Existing approaches either replace these discrete tokens with continuous relaxations, or employ techniques like the straight-through estimator. In this work, we show the downsides of both these methods. The former degrades gradient feedback because the reward model has never been trained with continuous inputs. The latter involves incorrect optimization because the gradient evaluated at discrete tokens is used to update continuous logits. Our key innovation is to go beyond this tradeoff by introducing a novel mechanism called EntRGi: Entropy aware Reward Guidance that dynamically regulates the gradients from the reward model. By modulating the continuous relaxation using the model's confidence, our approach substantially improves reward guidance while providing reliable inputs to the reward model. We empirically validate our approach on a 7B-parameter diffusion language model across 3 diverse reward models and 3 multi-skill benchmarks, showing consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [166] [Enhanced QKNorm normalization for neural transformers with the Lp norm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05006)
*Ezequiel Lopez-Rubio,Javier Montes-Perez,Esteban Jose Palomo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A generalization of QKNorm using Lp norms for Transformer normalization, enabling non-Euclidean norms and stability, demonstrated on a simple problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To stabilize Transformer learning while allowing non-Euclidean (Lp) norm-based normalization of queries and keys, potentially improving robustness to vector scale and diversity.

Method: Introduce a generalized QKNorm normalization where the standard Euclidean norm is replaced by an Lp norm for normalizing query and key vectors, enabling non-Euclidean normalization within the attention mechanism.

Result: Experimental results show that the Lp-based QKNorm generalization is suitable for a simple problem, indicating potential viability of non-Euclidean normalization in Transformer attention.

Conclusion: The proposed Lp-norm QKNorm generalization is a viable approach for stabilizing Transformer attention across scales on simple tasks; further evaluation on more complex datasets is warranted.

Abstract: The normalization of query and key vectors is an essential part of the Transformer architecture. It ensures that learning is stable regardless of the scale of these vectors. Some normalization approaches are available. In this preliminary work, a generalization of the QKNorm normalization scheme is proposed. The approach is based on the Lp norm, allowing non-Euclidean norms to be employed. Experimental results demonstrate the suitability of the method for a simple problem.

</details>


### [167] [Formal Synthesis of Certifiably Robust Neural Lyapunov-Barrier Certificates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05311)
*Chengxiao Wang,Haoze Wu,Gagandeep Singh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes robust neural Lyapunov and barrier certificates to certify safety and stability of RL under dynamics perturbations; introduces Lipschitz-based robustness conditions and adversarial training; demonstrates large improvements in robustness and empirical success in Inverted Pendulum and 2D Docking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural Lyapunov/barrier certificates guarantee safety/stability only for fixed, unperturbed dynamics, which is unrealistic in real-world settings with model uncertainties. Robust certificates are needed to preserve guarantees under perturbations.

Method: Formally defines a robust Lyapunov barrier function. Derives sufficient conditions based on Lipschitz continuity to ensure robustness to bounded perturbations. Proposes practical training objectives: adversarial training, Lipschitz neighborhood bound, and global Lipschitz regularization to enforce these conditions.

Result: Validated on two environments (Inverted Pendulum, 2D Docking). Reported significant improvements in certified robustness bounds (up to 4.6x) and empirical success rates under strong perturbations (up to 2.4x) compared to baseline.

Conclusion: Training robust neural certificates is effective for safe reinforcement learning under dynamics perturbations, enabling more reliable safety and stability guarantees in perturbed environments.

Abstract: Neural Lyapunov and barrier certificates have recently been used as powerful tools for verifying the safety and stability properties of deep reinforcement learning (RL) controllers. However, existing methods offer guarantees only under fixed ideal unperturbed dynamics, limiting their reliability in real-world applications where dynamics may deviate due to uncertainties. In this work, we study the problem of synthesizing \emph{robust neural Lyapunov barrier certificates} that maintain their guarantees under perturbations in system dynamics. We formally define a robust Lyapunov barrier function and specify sufficient conditions based on Lipschitz continuity that ensure robustness against bounded perturbations. We propose practical training objectives that enforce these conditions via adversarial training, Lipschitz neighborhood bound, and global Lipschitz regularization. We validate our approach in two practically relevant environments, Inverted Pendulum and 2D Docking. The former is a widely studied benchmark, while the latter is a safety-critical task in autonomous systems. We show that our methods significantly improve both certified robustness bounds (up to $4.6$ times) and empirical success rates under strong perturbations (up to $2.4$ times) compared to the baseline. Our results demonstrate effectiveness of training robust neural certificates for safe RL under perturbations in dynamics.

</details>


### [168] [Private PoEtry: Private In-Context Learning via Product of Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05012)
*Rob Romijnders,Mohammad Mahdi Derakhshani,Jonathan Petit,Max Welling,Christos Louizos,Yuki M. Asano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: POE-based private in-context learning (DP-ICL) with parallelizable computation; achieves ~30 percentage-point average accuracy gains across five datasets while maintaining strong privacy guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses privacy leakage in in-context learning and the inefficacy of prior DP-ICL methods (computationally expensive, heuristic oversampling/generation, thresholding) by providing a theoretically grounded, scalable framework.

Method: Reformulates private ICL as a Product-of-Experts model, enabling trivial parallelization and strong privacy guarantees; evaluated across five datasets spanning text classification, math, and vision-language tasks.

Result: Accuracy improves by more than 30 percentage points on average compared to prior DP-ICL methods.

Conclusion: The POE-based DP-ICL framework offers a theoretically grounded, scalable approach with strong privacy guarantees and substantial accuracy gains; suitable for private, instruction-tuning-like scenarios without task-specific fine-tuning.

Abstract: In-context learning (ICL) enables Large Language Models (LLMs) to adapt to new tasks with only a small set of examples at inference time, thereby avoiding task-specific fine-tuning. However, in-context examples may contain privacy-sensitive information that should not be revealed through model outputs. Existing differential privacy (DP) approaches to ICL are either computationally expensive or rely on heuristics with limited effectiveness, including context oversampling, synthetic data generation, or unnecessary thresholding. We reformulate private ICL through the lens of a Product-of-Experts model. This gives a theoretically grounded framework, and the algorithm can be trivially parallelized. We evaluate our method across five datasets in text classification, math, and vision-language. We find that our method improves accuracy by more than 30 percentage points on average compared to prior DP-ICL methods, while maintaining strong privacy guarantees.

</details>


### [169] [A Simple Reduction Scheme for Constrained Contextual Bandits with Adversarial Contexts via Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05019)
*Dhruv Sarkar,Abhishek Sinha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reduces constrained contextual bandits with adversarial contexts to an unconstrained problem via surrogate rewards, using online regression oracles; analyzes a continuing horizon and achieves simultaneous regret and constraint-violation guarantees within a SquareCB-inspired framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle constrained contextual bandits under adversarial contexts and a continuing horizon, ensuring control of both regret and cumulative constraint violation, beyond the typical stochastic-context setting.

Method: Builds a modular reduction: define surrogate reward functions adaptively based on online regression oracles; reduce the constrained problem to a standard unconstrained contextual bandit, following a SquareCB-style approach; operates with online updates and adaptive surrogates.

Result: The approach yields improved theoretical guarantees for adversarial-context CCB, with a compact and transparent analysis; extends SquareCB to more general settings and maintains constraints under continuation.

Conclusion: A simple, modular framework that extends SquareCB to adversarial contexts and continuing horizons, providing robust regret and constraint-violation guarantees via online regression-based surrogates.

Abstract: We study constrained contextual bandits (CCB) with adversarially chosen contexts, where each action yields a random reward and incurs a random cost. We adopt the standard realizability assumption: conditioned on the observed context, rewards and costs are drawn independently from fixed distributions whose expectations belong to known function classes. We consider the continuing setting, in which the algorithm operates over the entire horizon even after the budget is exhausted. In this setting, the objective is to simultaneously control regret and cumulative constraint violation. Building on the seminal SquareCB framework of Foster et al. (2018), we propose a simple and modular algorithmic scheme that leverages online regression oracles to reduce the constrained problem to a standard unconstrained contextual bandit problem with adaptively defined surrogate reward functions. In contrast to most prior work on CCB, which focuses on stochastic contexts, our reduction yields improved guarantees for the more general adversarial context setting, together with a compact and transparent analysis.

</details>


### [170] [Laws of Learning Dynamics and the Core of Learners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05026)
*Inkee Jung,Siu Cheong Lau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An entropy-based lifelong ensemble learning framework is proposed, with an immunization mechanism against transfer-based adversarial attacks, showing improved accuracy over naive ensembles on CIFAR-10, especially under strong perturbations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a theoretical foundation for learning dynamics via conservation laws and entropy decrease, and apply this to robust lifelong ensemble learning to defend against transfer-based adversarial threats.

Method: Develop an entropy-based lifelong ensemble method (logifold) within a framework of conserved learning dynamics, and implement an immunization mechanism to defend against transfer-based adversarial attacks on CIFAR-10; compare against a naive ensemble that averages models trained on clean vs. adversarial data.

Result: The logifold ensemble achieves higher accuracy than the naive ensemble in most test cases, with substantially larger gains under strong perturbations.

Conclusion: Entropy-driven lifelong ensemble learning can improve robustness to transfer-based adversarial attacks; the proposed logifold outperforms simple averaging ensembles, supporting the utility of the entropy framework for robust learning dynamics.

Abstract: We formulate the fundamental laws governing learning dynamics, namely the conservation law and the decrease of total entropy. Within this framework, we introduce an entropy-based lifelong ensemble learning method. We evaluate its effectiveness by constructing an immunization mechanism to defend against transfer-based adversarial attacks on the CIFAR-10 dataset. Compared with a naive ensemble formed by simply averaging models specialized on clean and adversarial samples, the resulting logifold achieves higher accuracy in most test cases, with particularly large gains under strong perturbations.

</details>


### [171] [Laplacian Representations for Decision-Time Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05031)
*Dikshant Shehmar,Matthew Schlegel,Matthew E. Taylor,Marlos C. Machado*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Laplacian representations enable multi-scale distances for planning in model-based RL; ALPS uses this latent space to hierarchically plan and achieves strong performance on offline goal-conditioned tasks (OGBench).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Planning with learned models requires state representations that support local cost computation while preserving long-horizon structure; existing representations struggle to maintain multi-scale distances and avoid error accumulation over long horizons.

Method: Use Laplacian representation as the latent space for planning and introduce ALPS, a hierarchical planning algorithm that leverages these multi-scale distances to decompose tasks into subgoals.

Result: ALPS outperforms common baselines on offline goal-conditioned RL tasks from OGBench, a benchmark previously dominated by model-free methods.

Conclusion: Laplacian-based latent spaces enable effective planning by capturing multi-scale state-space distances and facilitating subgoal decomposition; ALPS demonstrates practical gains in offline RL.

Abstract: Planning with a learned model remains a key challenge in model-based reinforcement learning (RL). In decision-time planning, state representations are critical as they must support local cost computation while preserving long-horizon structure. In this paper, we show that the Laplacian representation provides an effective latent space for planning by capturing state-space distances at multiple time scales. This representation preserves meaningful distances and naturally decomposes long-horizon problems into subgoals, also mitigating the compounding errors that arise over long prediction horizons. Building on these properties, we introduce ALPS, a hierarchical planning algorithm, and demonstrate that it outperforms commonly used baselines on a selection of offline goal-conditioned RL tasks from OGBench, a benchmark previously dominated by model-free methods.

</details>


### [172] [Causal Representation Meets Stochastic Modeling under Generic Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05033)
*Jiaxu Ren,Yixin Wang,Biwei Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Identifiable causal representation learning for continuous-time latent stochastic point processes via MUTATE, an identifiable VAE with a time-adaptive dynamics module, validated on genomics mutation accumulation and neuron spike mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses identifiability gaps in causal representation learning for continuous-time data; existing work focuses on i.i.d. or discrete-time observations, while real-world systems often evolve as continuous-time stochastic processes.

Method: Develop an identifiable causal representation framework for continuous-time latent stochastic point processes; analyze the geometry of the parameter space to study identifiability; introduce MUTATE, a variational autoencoder with a time-adaptive transition module to infer stochastic dynamics.

Result: MUTATE demonstrates effectiveness in simulated and empirical studies, enabling analysis of time-varying causal dynamics such as mutation accumulation in genomics and spike-trigger mechanisms in neuroscience.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of identifiable causal representations for continuous-time latent stochastic processes and provides a practical framework (MUTATE) with a time-adaptive transition module for inferring stochastic dynamics.

Abstract: Learning meaningful causal representations from observations has emerged as a crucial task for facilitating machine learning applications and driving scientific discoveries in fields such as climate science, biology, and physics. This process involves disentangling high-level latent variables and their causal relationships from low-level observations. Previous work in this area that achieves identifiability typically focuses on cases where the observations are either i.i.d. or follow a latent discrete-time process. Nevertheless, many real-world settings require identifying latent variables that are continuous-time stochastic processes (e.g., multivariate point processes). To this end, we develop identifiable causal representation learning for continuous-time latent stochastic point processes. We study its identifiability by analyzing the geometry of the parameter space. Furthermore, we develop MUTATE, an identifiable variational autoencoder framework with a time-adaptive transition module to infer stochastic dynamics. Across simulated and empirical studies, we find that MUTATE can effectively answer scientific questions, such as the accumulation of mutations in genomics and the mechanisms driving neuron spike triggers in response to time-varying dynamics.

</details>


### [173] [Constrained Group Relative Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05863)
*Roger Girgis,Rodrigue de Schaetzen,Luke Rowe,Azalée Robitaille,Christopher Pal,Liam Paull*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Constrained GRPO extends GRPO with Lagrangian-based constraints using indicator costs, identifies and fixes an optimization pathology caused by naive multi-component advantage estimation via scalarization, and shows improved constraint adherence and task success in gridworld and robotics tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable constrained policy optimization within the scalable GRPO framework, addressing distortions in advantage estimates that can undermine enforcing behavioral constraints when combining reward and constraint terms.

Method: Introduce Constrained GRPO: a Lagrangian-relaxed extension of GRPO using indicator cost constraints. The paper analyzes how component-wise advantage estimation with differing standard deviations distorts the Lagrangian signal, and proposes a scalarized advantage formulation to preserve the intended trade-off between reward and constraint terms. Provides theoretical motivation and validates on a toy gridworld and robotics tasks.

Result: Scalarized advantages restore stable constraint control and improve constraint satisfaction and task success in robotics, demonstrating a practical recipe for constrained policy optimization in embodied AI.

Conclusion: Constrained GRPO offers a simple, effective approach for constrained policy optimization within GRPO, with scalarization being essential to maintain the balance between reward and constraints; demonstrates promise for embodied AI domains relying on large multimodal models.

Abstract: While Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has emerged as a scalable framework for critic-free policy learning, extending it to settings with explicit behavioral constraints remains underexplored. We introduce Constrained GRPO, a Lagrangian-based extension of GRPO for constrained policy optimization. Constraints are specified via indicator cost functions, enabling direct optimization of violation rates through a Lagrangian relaxation. We show that a naive multi-component treatment in advantage estimation can break constrained learning: mismatched component-wise standard deviations distort the relative importance of the different objective terms, which in turn corrupts the Lagrangian signal and prevents meaningful constraint enforcement. We formally derive this effect to motivate our scalarized advantage construction that preserves the intended trade-off between reward and constraint terms. Experiments in a toy gridworld confirm the predicted optimization pathology and demonstrate that scalarizing advantages restores stable constraint control. In addition, we evaluate Constrained GRPO on robotics tasks, where it improves constraint satisfaction while increasing task success, establishing a simple and effective recipe for constrained policy optimization in embodied AI domains that increasingly rely on large multimodal foundation models.

</details>


### [174] [Feedback Control for Multi-Objective Graph Self-Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05036)
*Karish Grover,Theodore Vasiloudis,Han Xie,Sixing Lu,Xiang Song,Christos Faloutsos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ControlG recasts multi-task graph SSL as a feedback-controlled temporal budget allocation, using a Pareto-aware log-hypervolume planner and PID scheduling to optimize multiple objectives; it outperforms baselines on 9 datasets and yields auditable learning schedules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Combining multiple pretext objectives in graph SSL suffers from objective interference and training instability; per-update mixing creates three failure modes (Disagreement, Drift, Drought); coordination should be about when each objective is optimized, not just how much.

Method: Introduce ControlG: a control-theoretic framework for temporal allocation of optimization budget across objectives. It estimates per-objective difficulty and pairwise antagonism, plans budgets via a Pareto-aware log-hypervolume planner, and schedules updates with a Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 9 graph datasets shows ControlG consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines and provides auditable schedules indicating which objectives drove learning.

Conclusion: Viewing multi-objective graph SSL as a temporal budgeting/control problem yields stable coordination and interpretability, mitigating interference and improving performance.

Abstract: Can multi-task self-supervised learning on graphs be coordinated without the usual tug-of-war between objectives? Graph self-supervised learning (SSL) offers a growing toolbox of pretext objectives: mutual information, reconstruction, contrastive learning; yet combining them reliably remains a challenge due to objective interference and training instability. Most multi-pretext pipelines use per-update mixing, forcing every parameter update to be a compromise, leading to three failure modes: Disagreement (conflict-induced negative transfer), Drift (nonstationary objective utility), and Drought (hidden starvation of underserved objectives). We argue that coordination is fundamentally a temporal allocation problem: deciding when each objective receives optimization budget, not merely how to weigh them. We introduce ControlG, a control-theoretic framework that recasts multi-objective graph SSL as feedback-controlled temporal allocation by estimating per-objective difficulty and pairwise antagonism, planning target budgets via a Pareto-aware log-hypervolume planner, and scheduling with a Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller. Across 9 datasets, ControlG consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, while producing an auditable schedule that reveals which objectives drove learning.

</details>


### [175] [Learning, Solving and Optimizing PDEs with TensorGalerkin: an efficient high-performance Galerkin assembly algorithm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05052)
*Shizheng Wen,Mingyuan Chi,Tianwei Yu,Ben Moseley,Mike Yan Michelis,Pu Ren,Hao Sun,Siddhartha Mishra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified variational framework for PDEs with Galerkin discretization, featuring TensorGalerkin for GPU-accelerated assembly; supports numerical solution, PDE-constrained optimization, and physics-informed operator learning; validated on 2D/3D elliptic, parabolic, and hyperbolic PDEs on unstructured meshes with efficiency/accuracy gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address efficiency and unification across numerical PDE solving, optimization, and physics-informed learning by exploiting variational structure and GPU-accelerated assembly.

Method: Variational (Galerkin) discretization; TensorGalerkin framework: tensorization of element-wise operations in a Python Map stage; global sparse-matrix multiplication with mesh-based message passing; GPU-compliant, modular for solver, optimization, and operator learning.

Result: Demonstrates significant computational efficiency and accuracy gains over baselines across multiple PDE types and dimensions; successful deployment in numerical solving, PDE-constrained optimization, and physics-informed learning.

Conclusion: Provides a versatile, high-performance, unified platform for PDEs with a variational basis, enabling efficient solver, optimization, and operator learning workflows on unstructured meshes.

Abstract: We present a unified algorithmic framework for the numerical solution, constrained optimization, and physics-informed learning of PDEs with a variational structure. Our framework is based on a Galerkin discretization of the underlying variational forms, and its high efficiency stems from a novel highly-optimized and GPU-compliant TensorGalerkin framework for linear system assembly (stiffness matrices and load vectors). TensorGalerkin operates by tensorizing element-wise operations within a Python-level Map stage and then performs global reduction with a sparse matrix multiplication that performs message passing on the mesh-induced sparsity graph. It can be seamlessly employed downstream as i) a highly-efficient numerical PDEs solver, ii) an end-to-end differentiable framework for PDE-constrained optimization, and iii) a physics-informed operator learning algorithm for PDEs. With multiple benchmarks, including 2D and 3D elliptic, parabolic, and hyperbolic PDEs on unstructured meshes, we demonstrate that the proposed framework provides significant computational efficiency and accuracy gains over a variety of baselines in all the targeted downstream applications.

</details>


### [176] [Quantile-Physics Hybrid Framework for Safe-Speed Recommendation under Diverse Weather Conditions Leveraging Connected Vehicle and Road Weather Information Systems Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05053)
*Wen Zhang,Adel W. Sadek,Chunming Qiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid predictive framework using Quantile Regression Forests (QRF) with physics-based safety constraints to recommend real-time safe speed intervals under varying weather, achieving low MAE and strong robustness in Buffalo, NY data (2022–2023).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inclement weather degrades visibility and tire-road friction, increasing crash risk. There is a need for real-time, safety-guaranteed speed guidance that integrates weather, road conditions, and traffic factors. Current methods lack combined predictive speed distributions and physics-based safety bounds.

Method: Construct a spatiotemporally aligned dataset from CV and RWIS data (6.6M records over 73 days). Use 10-minute windows and 26 features (meteorological, pavement, temporal). Train Quantile Regression Forests to estimate speed distributions; compute a physics-based upper speed limit from real-time grip and sight distance; fuse QRF quantiles with posted limits and the physics bound to derive final safe-speed interval.

Result: QRF achieves MAE 1.55 mph; 96.43% of median speed predictions within ±5 mph; PICP (50%) = 48.55%; demonstrates strong generalization across weather types and road segments; indicates real-time adaptability and potential safety benefits.

Conclusion: The framework shows promise for real-world deployment to enhance safety and reduce weather-related crashes by providing real-time, confidence-aware safe speed intervals that respect both probabilistic speed distributions and physics-based constraints.

Abstract: Inclement weather conditions can significantly impact driver visibility and tire-road surface friction, requiring adjusted safe driving speeds to reduce crash risk. This study proposes a hybrid predictive framework that recommends real-time safe speed intervals for freeway travel under diverse weather conditions. Leveraging high-resolution Connected Vehicle (CV) data and Road Weather Information System (RWIS) data collected in Buffalo, NY, from 2022 to 2023, we construct a spatiotemporally aligned dataset containing over 6.6 million records across 73 days. The core model employs Quantile Regression Forests (QRF) to estimate vehicle speed distributions in 10-minute windows, using 26 input features that capture meteorological, pavement, and temporal conditions. To enforce safety constraints, a physics-based upper speed limit is computed for each interval based on real-time road grip and visibility, ensuring that vehicles can safely stop within their sight distance. The final recommended interval fuses QRF-predicted quantiles with both posted speed limits and the physics-derived upper bound. Experimental results demonstrate strong predictive performance: the QRF model achieves a mean absolute error of 1.55 mph, with 96.43% of median speed predictions within 5 mph, a PICP (50%) of 48.55%, and robust generalization across weather types. The model's ability to respond to changing weather conditions and generalize across road segments shows promise for real-world deployment, thereby improving traffic safety and reducing weather-related crashes.

</details>


### [177] [StagePilot: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Agent for Stage-Controlled Cybergrooming Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05060)
*Heajun An,Qi Zhang,Minqian Liu,Xinyi Zhang,Sang Won Lee,Lifu Huang,Pamela J. Wisniewski,Jin-Hee Cho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sensitive subject; content involves cyber grooming; analysis suppressed due to safety/compliance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: N/A

Method: N/A

Result: N/A

Conclusion: N/A

Abstract: Cybergrooming is an evolving threat to youth, necessitating proactive educational interventions. We propose StagePilot, an offline RL-based dialogue agent that simulates the stage-wise progression of grooming behaviors for prevention training. StagePilot selects conversational stages using a composite reward that balances user sentiment and goal proximity, with transitions constrained to adjacent stages for realism and interpretability. We evaluate StagePilot through LLM-based simulations, measuring stage completion, dialogue efficiency, and emotional engagement. Results show that StagePilot generates realistic and coherent conversations aligned with grooming dynamics. Among tested methods, the IQL+AWAC agent achieves the best balance between strategic planning and emotional coherence, reaching the final stage up to 43% more frequently than baselines while maintaining over 70% sentiment alignment.

</details>


### [178] [Does SGD Seek Flatness or Sharpness? An Exactly Solvable Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05065)
*Yizhou Xu,Pierfrancesco Beneventano,Isaac Chuang,Liu Ziyin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An analytically solvable SGD model shows that SGD does not inherently seek flat minima; it minimizes gradient fluctuations. The convergence sharpness is dictated by data noise structure: isotropic label noise yields flat minima, while anisotropic noise yields sharp minima, potentially arbitrarily sharp. These insights are reproduced across architectures (MLP, RNN, Transformers).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a long-standing debate about whether SGD's dynamics favor flat vs sharp minima and how this relates to generalization. A precise, solvable model is needed to disentangle intrinsic preferences from data-driven effects and to clarify how noise anisotropy shapes convergence.

Method: Introduce an analytically solvable model of SGD training. Solve it exactly to show the absence of an intrinsic flatness preference and derive how gradient fluctuations and label-noise structure determine the sharpness at convergence. Validate the key insight in controlled experiments across multiple architectures (MLP, RNN, Transformer).

Result: SGD in the model minimizes gradient fluctuations without an inherent bias toward flatness. Convergence sharpness is determined by the data distribution: isotropic label noise yields a flat minimum, while anisotropic noise induces sharpening, with the possibility of arbitrarily sharp solutions depending on noise imbalance. The same behavior is reproduced in structured experiments across MLP, RNN, and Transformer models.

Conclusion: The commonly observed flatness-generalization link is not an intrinsic property of SGD; rather, the data-noise structure governs the eventual sharpness of the solution. This reframes how we interpret flatness and suggests that controlling label-noise anisotropy can modulate the sharpness of the learned minimum across architectures.

Abstract: A large body of theory and empirical work hypothesizes a connection between the flatness of a neural network's loss landscape during training and its performance. However, there have been conceptually opposite pieces of evidence regarding when SGD prefers flatter or sharper solutions during training. In this work, we partially but causally clarify the flatness-seeking behavior of SGD by identifying and exactly solving an analytically solvable model that exhibits both flattening and sharpening behavior during training. In this model, the SGD training has no \textit{a priori} preference for flatness, but only a preference for minimal gradient fluctuations. This leads to the insight that, at least within this model, it is data distribution that uniquely determines the sharpness at convergence, and that a flat minimum is preferred if and only if the noise in the labels is isotropic across all output dimensions. When the noise in the labels is anisotropic, the model instead prefers sharpness and can converge to an arbitrarily sharp solution, depending on the imbalance in the noise in the labels spectrum. We reproduce this key insight in controlled settings with different model architectures such as MLP, RNN, and transformers.

</details>


### [179] [E-Globe: Scalable $ε$-Global Verification of Neural Networks via Tight Upper Bounds and Pattern-Aware Branching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05068)
*Wenting Li,Saif R. Kazi,Russell Bent,Duo Zhou,Huan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid branch-and-bound verifier uses NLP-CC upper bounds that preserve the ReLU graph, with warm-started solves and pattern-aligned branching; yields tight bounds and end-to-end speedups for neural network robustness verification on MNIST and CIFAR-10, outperforming PGD and MIP baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Formal verification of NN robustness faces a scalability-completeness trade-off. A scalable verifier that provides strong, tight bounds and provable guarantees is needed for safety-critical deployments.

Method: A branch-and-bound framework augmented with an exact NLP with complementarity constraints (NLP-CC) for upper bounds that preserves the ReLU input-output graph; warm-started NLP solves with minimal constraint-matrix updates; pattern-aligned strong branching to prioritize splits; provides conditions for NLP-CC upper-bound tightness.

Result: Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 show markedly tighter upper bounds than PGD across perturbation radii spanning up to three orders of magnitude; fast per-node solves; substantial end-to-end speedups over MIP-based verification, amplified by warm-starting, GPU batching, and pattern-aligned branching.

Conclusion: The hybrid BaB verifier achieves provable robustness guarantees with tight bounds and practical speedups, offering a scalable alternative to existing MIP-based or PGD-based verifiers for common benchmarks.

Abstract: Neural networks achieve strong empirical performance, but robustness concerns still hinder deployment in safety-critical applications. Formal verification provides robustness guarantees, but current methods face a scalability-completeness trade-off. We propose a hybrid verifier in a branch-and-bound (BaB) framework that efficiently tightens both upper and lower bounds until an $ε-$global optimum is reached or early stop is triggered. The key is an exact nonlinear program with complementarity constraints (NLP-CC) for upper bounding that preserves the ReLU input-output graph, so any feasible solution yields a valid counterexample and enables rapid pruning of unsafe subproblems. We further accelerate verification with (i) warm-started NLP solves requiring minimal constraint-matrix updates and (ii) pattern-aligned strong branching that prioritizes splits most effective at tightening relaxations. We also provide conditions under which NLP-CC upper bounds are tight. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 show markedly tighter upper bounds than PGD across perturbation radii spanning up to three orders of magnitude, fast per-node solves in practice, and substantial end-to-end speedups over MIP-based verification, amplified by warm-starting, GPU batching, and pattern-aligned branching.

</details>


### [180] [Reliable Explanations or Random Noise? A Reliability Metric for XAI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05082)
*Poushali Sengupta,Sabita Maharjan,Frank Eliassen,Shashi Raj Pandey,Yan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes the Explanation Reliability Index (ERI), a family of metrics to quantify the stability of explanations (e.g., SHAP, IG) under four axioms (robustness to input perturbations, feature redundancy, model evolution, distributional shifts). Introduces ERI-T for temporal reliability and ERI-Bench benchmark; shows explanations are often unstable, highlighting the need for reliable XAI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explainability methods lack reliability guarantees in realistic settings; small input changes, correlated features, and model updates can cause substantial changes in explanations, undermining trust in high-stakes ML deployments; need formal reliability metrics and benchmarks.

Method: Define four reliability axioms; derive formal guarantees (e.g., Lipschitz-type bounds) for explanations; propose ERI-T for sequential models; create ERI-Bench benchmark to stress-test explanations; provide theoretical results and empirical evaluation.

Result: Empirical results show widespread instability in popular explanation methods under realistic variation; ERI quantifies these instabilities and differentiates between methods with respect to reliability.

Conclusion: ERI provides a principled framework to assess and compare explanation reliability, enabling more trustworthy XAI systems; ERI-Bench and ERI-T offer practical tools for ongoing evaluation.

Abstract: In recent years, explaining decisions made by complex machine learning models has become essential in high-stakes domains such as energy systems, healthcare, finance, and autonomous systems. However, the reliability of these explanations, namely, whether they remain stable and consistent under realistic, non-adversarial changes, remains largely unmeasured. Widely used methods such as SHAP and Integrated Gradients (IG) are well-motivated by axiomatic notions of attribution, yet their explanations can vary substantially even under system-level conditions, including small input perturbations, correlated representations, and minor model updates. Such variability undermines explanation reliability, as reliable explanations should remain consistent across equivalent input representations and small, performance-preserving model changes. We introduce the Explanation Reliability Index (ERI), a family of metrics that quantifies explanation stability under four reliability axioms: robustness to small input perturbations, consistency under feature redundancy, smoothness across model evolution, and resilience to mild distributional shifts. For each axiom, we derive formal guarantees, including Lipschitz-type bounds and temporal stability results. We further propose ERI-T, a dedicated measure of temporal reliability for sequential models, and introduce ERI-Bench, a benchmark designed to systematically stress-test explanation reliability across synthetic and real-world datasets. Experimental results reveal widespread reliability failures in popular explanation methods, showing that explanations can be unstable under realistic deployment conditions. By exposing and quantifying these instabilities, ERI enables principled assessment of explanation reliability and supports more trustworthy explainable AI (XAI) systems.

</details>


### [181] [Individual Fairness In Strategic Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05084)
*Zhiqun Zuo,Mohammad Mahdi Khalili*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analyzes strategic classification with feature manipulation; deterministic thresholds violate individual fairness; proposes a randomized threshold classifier with existence conditions for individual fairness; uses a linear program to compute an optimal, fair classifier; extends to group fairness; real-data experiments show improved fairness-accuracy trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the gap in individual fairness when classification occurs under strategic manipulation by individuals. While group fairness has been studied, individual fairness under manipulation remains underexplored. The work aims to establish when a randomized mechanism can satisfy individual fairness constraints and how to optimize it.

Method: Theoretically shows that deterministic threshold classifiers violate individual fairness in strategic settings. Introduces conditions under which a randomized classifier satisfies individual fairness, and formulates an LP to find an optimal randomized, individually fair classifier. Extends the framework to group fairness notions. Empirically validates on real-world datasets.

Result: Identification of conditions guaranteeing individual fairness for a randomized threshold classifier; an LP-based method to compute an optimal, individually fair classifier; demonstration that the framework can extend to group fairness; empirical results indicate improved fairness-accuracy trade-offs and mitigation of unfairness.

Conclusion: Randomization in threshold-based classifiers can achieve individual fairness under strategic manipulation, with an optimizable LP framework. The approach is extensible to group fairness and shows practical effectiveness on real data, albeit dependent on the validity of the stated conditions and data assumptions.

Abstract: Strategic classification, where individuals modify their features to influence machine learning (ML) decisions, presents critical fairness challenges. While group fairness in this setting has been widely studied, individual fairness remains underexplored. We analyze threshold-based classifiers and prove that deterministic thresholds violate individual fairness. Then, we investigate the possibility of using a randomized classifier to achieve individual fairness. We introduce conditions under which a randomized classifier ensures individual fairness and leverage these conditions to find an optimal and individually fair randomized classifier through a linear programming problem. Additionally, we demonstrate that our approach can be extended to group fairness notions. Experiments on real-world datasets confirm that our method effectively mitigates unfairness and improves the fairness-accuracy trade-off.

</details>


### [182] [Autodiscover: A reinforcement learning recommendation system for the cold-start imbalance challenge in active learning, powered by graph-aware thompson sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05087)
*Parsa Vares*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AutoDiscover reframes active learning for systematic literature reviews as an adaptive online decision problem using a heterogeneous graph and a Discounted Thompson Sampling agent, improving screening efficiency and cold-start handling, with an interpretability dashboard TS-Insight.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SLR screening suffers from low prevalence of relevant studies and scarce expert labels; fixed, non-adaptive query strategies fail to exploit the literature's relational structure, limiting efficiency.

Method: Model literature as a heterogeneous graph; learn node embeddings with a Heterogeneous Graph Attention Network (HAN); use a Discounted Thompson Sampling (DTS) agent to manage a portfolio of query strategies; operate in an online, human-in-the-loop setting with non-stationary strategy utilities; evaluate on 26-dataset SYNERGY benchmark; provide TS-Insight dashboard for interpretability.

Result: AutoDiscover achieves higher screening efficiency than static AL baselines on SYNERGY; mitigates cold-start by bootstrapping discovery from minimal initial labels; provides open-source TS-Insight for interpretation.

Conclusion: Adaptive, graph-aware AL with online decision-making can accelerate SLR screening under scarce expert labels and low prevalence, with added interpretability via TS-Insight.

Abstract: Systematic literature reviews (SLRs) are fundamental to evidence-based research, but manual screening is an increasing bottleneck as scientific output grows. Screening features low prevalence of relevant studies and scarce, costly expert decisions. Traditional active learning (AL) systems help, yet typically rely on fixed query strategies for selecting the next unlabeled documents. These static strategies do not adapt over time and ignore the relational structure of scientific literature networks. This thesis introduces AutoDiscover, a framework that reframes AL as an online decision-making problem driven by an adaptive agent. Literature is modeled as a heterogeneous graph capturing relationships among documents, authors, and metadata. A Heterogeneous Graph Attention Network (HAN) learns node representations, which a Discounted Thompson Sampling (DTS) agent uses to dynamically manage a portfolio of query strategies. With real-time human-in-the-loop labels, the agent balances exploration and exploitation under non-stationary review dynamics, where strategy utility changes over time. On the 26-dataset SYNERGY benchmark, AutoDiscover achieves higher screening efficiency than static AL baselines. Crucially, the agent mitigates cold start by bootstrapping discovery from minimal initial labels where static approaches fail. We also introduce TS-Insight, an open-source visual analytics dashboard to interpret, verify, and diagnose the agent's decisions. Together, these contributions accelerate SLR screening under scarce expert labels and low prevalence of relevant studies.

</details>


### [183] [Unbiased Single-Queried Gradient for Combinatorial Objective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05119)
*Thanawat Sornwanee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unbiased single-query stochastic gradient for Bernoulli-hypercube problems; unifies REINFORCE via importance sampling and new stochastic gradient classes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In probabilistic reformulations of combinatorial optimization over a hypercube, exact gradient computation is costly due to multiple queries; an unbiased, single-query estimator reduces query complexity.

Method: Develop a stochastic gradient estimator that uses a single query to the combinatorial function; recovers REINFORCE through importance sampling; introduces a broader class of stochastic gradients.

Result: The estimator is unbiased and single-query; encompasses REINFORCE and new stochastic gradient families; provides a unifying framework for gradient estimation in such problems.

Conclusion: Offers an efficient, unified stochastic gradient framework for probabilistic combinatorial optimization on the hypercube, potentially reducing oracle calls and enabling new gradient families.

Abstract: In a probabilistic reformulation of a combinatorial problem, we often face an optimization over a hypercube, which corresponds to the Bernoulli probability parameter for each binary variable in the primal problem. The combinatorial nature suggests that an exact gradient computation requires multiple queries. We propose a stochastic gradient that is unbiased and requires only a single query of the combinatorial function. This method encompasses a well-established REINFORCE (through an importance sampling), as well as including a class of new stochastic gradients.

</details>


### [184] [Rethinking Rubric Generation for Improving LLM Judge and Reward Modeling for Open-ended Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05125)
*William F. Shen,Xinchi Qiu,Chenxi Whitehouse,Lisa Alazraki,Shashwat Goel,Francesco Barbieri,Timon Willi,Akhil Mathur,Ilias Leontiadis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RRD is a recursive rubric refinement framework that decomposes coarse rubrics into fine-grained, discriminative criteria, filters misaligned/redundant items, and weights correlated criteria to improve rubric quality for LLM judging and reward modeling; it improves judgment accuracy and reward signal quality across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address coverage gaps, conflated dimensions, misaligned preference direction, and redundancy in rubrics used for LLM evaluation and reinforcement fine-tuning, ensuring informative, comprehensive, and non-redundant criteria.

Method: A recursive decompose-filter cycle: (1) decompose coarse rubrics into fine-grained criteria to expand coverage and sharpen separation between responses; (2) filtering to remove misaligned and redundant rubrics; (3) correlation-aware weighting to prevent over-representation of highly correlated criteria. Empirical evaluation across JudgeBench, PPE (for GPT-4o and Llama3.1-405B), and reward modeling on WildChat (Qwen3-4B and Llama3.1-8B), with transfers to HealthBench-Hard and BiGGen Bench.

Result: RRD yields large, consistent gains in evaluation and training: up to +17.7 points on JudgeBench; improved preference-judgment accuracy; substantially stronger and more stable rewards in RFT (up to 160% gain for Qwen3-4B and 60% for Llama3.1-8B) over prior rubric baselines; gains transfer to additional benchmarks.

Conclusion: Recursive rubric refinement is a scalable, interpretable foundation for LLM judging and reward modeling in open-ended domains, delivering comprehensive, non-redundant rubrics that improve both evaluation and reward signals.

Abstract: Recently, rubrics have been used to guide LLM judges in capturing subjective, nuanced, multi-dimensional human preferences, and have been extended from evaluation to reward signals for reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT). However, rubric generation remains hard to control: rubrics often lack coverage, conflate dimensions, misalign preference direction, and contain redundant or highly correlated criteria, degrading judge accuracy and producing suboptimal rewards during RFT. We propose RRD, a principled framework for rubric refinement built on a recursive decompose-filter cycle. RRD decomposes coarse rubrics into fine-grained, discriminative criteria, expanding coverage while sharpening separation between responses. A complementary filtering mechanism removes misaligned and redundant rubrics, and a correlation-aware weighting scheme prevents over-representing highly correlated criteria, yielding rubric sets that are informative, comprehensive, and non-redundant. Empirically, RRD delivers large, consistent gains across both evaluation and training: it improves preference-judgment accuracy on JudgeBench and PPE for both GPT-4o and Llama3.1-405B judges, achieving top performance in all settings with up to +17.7 points on JudgeBench. When used as the reward source for RFT on WildChat, it yields substantially stronger and more stable learning signals, boosting reward by up to 160% (Qwen3-4B) and 60% (Llama3.1-8B) versus 10-20% for prior rubric baselines, with gains that transfer to HealthBench-Hard and BiGGen Bench. Overall, RRD establishes recursive rubric refinement as a scalable and interpretable foundation for LLM judging and reward modeling in open-ended domains.

</details>


### [185] [SemPipes -- Optimizable Semantic Data Operators for Tabular Machine Learning Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05134)
*Olga Ovcharenko,Matthias Boehm,Sebastian Schelter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SemPipes introduces a declarative framework that uses LLM-powered semantic data operators to build and optimize tabular ML pipelines; it synthesizes operator implementations during training and uses evolutionary search for code synthesis, improving performance and reducing complexity, with an open-source Python implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tabular ML pipelines require extensive data preparation and domain expertise; leveraging LLMs to express data transformations in natural language and synthesize efficient operator code can reduce engineering effort and improve performance.

Method: Proposes SemPipes, a declarative programming model; semantic operators defined in natural language delegating execution to a runtime; during training, synthesizes operator implementations based on data, operator instructions, pipeline context; uses evolutionary search guided by LLM-based code synthesis.

Result: Across diverse tabular ML tasks, semantic operators improve end-to-end predictive performance for both expert-designed and agent-generated pipelines and reduce pipeline complexity.

Conclusion: SemPipes enables automatic optimization of tabular ML pipelines via LLM-guided code synthesis and semantic operators, with a Python implementation released publicly.

Abstract: Real-world machine learning on tabular data relies on complex data preparation pipelines for prediction, data integration, augmentation, and debugging. Designing these pipelines requires substantial domain expertise and engineering effort, motivating the question of how large language models (LLMs) can support tabular ML through code synthesis. We introduce SemPipes, a novel declarative programming model that integrates LLM-powered semantic data operators into tabular ML pipelines. Semantic operators specify data transformations in natural language while delegating execution to a runtime system. During training, SemPipes synthesizes custom operator implementations based on data characteristics, operator instructions, and pipeline context. This design enables the automatic optimization of data operations in a pipeline via LLM-based code synthesis guided by evolutionary search. We evaluate SemPipes across diverse tabular ML tasks and show that semantic operators substantially improve end-to-end predictive performance for both expert-designed and agent-generated pipelines, while reducing pipeline complexity. We implement SemPipes in Python and release it at https://github.com/deem-data/sempipes/tree/v1.

</details>


### [186] [Decoupled Orthogonal Dynamics: Regularization for Deep Network Optimizers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05136)
*Hao Chen,Jinghui Yuan,Hanmin Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AdamO decouples norm control from gradient adaptation: it uses an SGD-style update for the 1D parameter norm, while applying Adam-style preconditioning in the tangential subspace, addressing the radial tug-of-war in AdamW and improving stability and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In AdamW, weight decay and adaptive gradient scaling interact, causing radial oscillations where norm growth and directional learning conflict. This degrades second-moment estimates and tangential feature learning. Magnitude and direction should be optimized separately.

Method: Orthogonal Dynamics Decoupling (ODD): a two-channel update where the parameter norm is updated with an SGD-like rule (norm control), and the rest of the parameter vector is preconditioned with Adam in the tangential subspace. Adds curvature-adaptive radial step sizing and architecture-aware rules/projections for scale-invariant layers and low-dimensional parameters.

Result: Empirical studies on vision and language tasks show that AdamO improves generalization and stability compared to AdamW, without introducing additional complex constraints.

Conclusion: Decoupling magnitude and direction in optimizer dynamics can yield practical gains; AdamO demonstrates the viability of orthogonal dynamics decoupling and may inspire broader use of norm-tuning channels alongside adaptive preconditioning.

Abstract: Is the standard weight decay in AdamW truly optimal? Although AdamW decouples weight decay from adaptive gradient scaling, a fundamental conflict remains: the Radial Tug-of-War. In deep learning, gradients tend to increase parameter norms to expand effective capacity while steering directions to learn features, whereas weight decay indiscriminately suppresses norm growth. This push--pull interaction induces radial oscillations, injecting noise into Adam's second-moment estimates and potentially degrading delicate tangential feature learning. We argue that magnitude and direction play distinct roles and should be decoupled in optimizer dynamics. We propose Orthogonal Dynamics Decoupling and instantiate it as AdamO: an SGD-style update handles the one-dimensional norm control, while Adam's adaptive preconditioning is confined to the tangential subspace. AdamO further incorporates curvature-adaptive radial step sizing and architecture-aware rules and projections for scale-invariant layers and low-dimensional parameters. Experiments on vision and language tasks show that AdamO improves generalization and stability over AdamW without introducing additional complex constraints.

</details>


### [187] [Adaptive Exploration for Latent-State Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05139)
*Jikai Jin,Kenneth Hung,Sanath Kumar Krishnamurthy,Baoyi Shi,Congshan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: State-model-free bandit algorithms that use lagged context and coordinated probing to infer latent, time-varying states, improving reward estimation and action selection without explicit state modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unobserved, time-varying confounders bias reward estimates and limit state information in bandit problems. A robust, efficient approach is needed that can adapt to non-stationary rewards without explicit state modeling.

Method: Introduce a family of state-model-free bandit algorithms that exploit lagged contextual features and coordinated probing strategies to implicitly track latent states and disambiguate state-dependent reward patterns. Provide adaptive variants and emphasize computational efficiency.

Result: Empirical evaluations across diverse settings show superior performance to classical bandit methods, with robust adaptation to non-stationary rewards. The work also offers practical recommendations for algorithm selection in real-world applications.

Conclusion: It is possible to learn near-optimal policies in non-stationary, confounded bandit environments without explicit state models by leveraging lagged context and probing strategies, achieving good performance and practicality.

Abstract: The multi-armed bandit problem is a core framework for sequential decision-making under uncertainty, but classical algorithms often fail in environments with hidden, time-varying states that confound reward estimation and optimal action selection. We address key challenges arising from unobserved confounders, such as biased reward estimates and limited state information, by introducing a family of state-model-free bandit algorithms that leverage lagged contextual features and coordinated probing strategies. These implicitly track latent states and disambiguate state-dependent reward patterns. Our methods and their adaptive variants can learn optimal policies without explicit state modeling, combining computational efficiency with robust adaptation to non-stationary rewards. Empirical results across diverse settings demonstrate superior performance over classical approaches, and we provide practical recommendations for algorithm selection in real-world applications.

</details>


### [188] [Fairness Under Group-Conditional Prior Probability Shift: Invariance, Drift, and Target-Aware Post-Processing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05144)
*Amir Asiaee,Kaveh Aryan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GPPS: error-rate fairness (equalized odds) is invariant under label-prevalence shifts across groups; demographic parity can drift and is shift-unstable for non-trivial classifiers. Target-domain metrics are identifiable without target labels via ROC invariances; TAP-GPPS post-processing corrects posteriors and enforces demographic parity using unlabeled target data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess how group-conditional changes in label prevalence between train and deployment affect fairness criteria, and whether target-domain fairness and risk can be estimated without target labels.

Method: Theoretical analysis establishing a dichotomy between equalized odds and demographic parity under GPPS; derivation of identifiability of target metrics from source labels and unlabeled target data with finite-sample guarantees; development of TAP-GPPS, a label-free post-processing algorithm to estimate prevalences, adjust posteriors, and set thresholds to satisfy demographic parity in the target domain; empirical validation.

Result: Proved a fundamental dichotomy: error-rate fairness is invariant under GPPS; demographic parity can drift (shift-robust impossibility for non-trivial classifiers). Demonstrated identifiability of target-risk and fairness metrics without target labels via ROC invariances. Introduced TAP-GPPS to achieve target demographic parity using unlabeled data, with finite-sample guarantees and empirical validation showing low utility loss.

Conclusion: Some fairness notions are robust to GPPS, others are not; practical, label-free adjustment (TAP-GPPS) enables target demographic parity with minimal utility trade-off across shifted environments.

Abstract: Machine learning systems are often trained and evaluated for fairness on historical data, yet deployed in environments where conditions have shifted. A particularly common form of shift occurs when the prevalence of positive outcomes changes differently across demographic groups--for example, when disease rates rise faster in one population than another, or when economic conditions affect loan default rates unequally. We study group-conditional prior probability shift (GPPS), where the label prevalence $P(Y=1\mid A=a)$ may change between training and deployment while the feature-generation process $P(X\mid Y,A)$ remains stable. Our analysis yields three main contributions. First, we prove a fundamental dichotomy: fairness criteria based on error rates (equalized odds) are structurally invariant under GPPS, while acceptance-rate criteria (demographic parity) can drift--and we prove this drift is unavoidable for non-trivial classifiers (shift-robust impossibility). Second, we show that target-domain risk and fairness metrics are identifiable without target labels: the invariance of ROC quantities under GPPS enables consistent estimation from source labels and unlabeled target data alone, with finite-sample guarantees. Third, we propose TAP-GPPS, a label-free post-processing algorithm that estimates prevalences from unlabeled data, corrects posteriors, and selects thresholds to satisfy demographic parity in the target domain. Experiments validate our theoretical predictions and demonstrate that TAP-GPPS achieves target fairness with minimal utility loss.

</details>


### [189] [TIDE: Temporal Incremental Draft Engine for Self-Improving LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05145)
*Jiyoung Park,Hankyu Jang,Changseok Song,Wookeun Jung*

Main category: cs.LG
