TL;DR: TIDE is a serving-engine-native framework that enables online draft adaptation for speculative decoding in LLM inference by reusing hidden states as training signals, enabling zero-overhead adaptation and adaptive control, achieving up to 1.15x throughput improvement and 1.67x faster training signal generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speculative decoding can accelerate LLM inference but is hard to realize in practice due to evolving workloads and system-level constraints. Integrating draft adaptation into serving engines and leveraging existing inference signals can reduce overhead and adapt to runtime conditions.

Method: TIDE integrates online draft adaptation directly into high-performance inference systems, reuses target model hidden states as training signals to enable zero-overhead draft adaptation without reloading the model, and uses adaptive runtime control to enable speculation and training only when beneficial. It also maps decoupled inference and training to appropriate GPU classes across heterogeneous clusters.

Result: Across diverse real-world workloads, TIDE achieves up to 1.15x throughput improvement over static speculative decoding and reduces draft training time by 1.67x compared to approaches that recompute training signals.

Conclusion: TIDE demonstrates that practical, hardware-aware, serving-engine-integrated speculative decoding can yield meaningful throughput gains and faster adaptation by reusing inference-time signals and adapting dynamically to workload conditions.

Abstract: Speculative decoding can substantially accelerate LLM inference, but realizing its benefits in practice is challenging due to evolving workloads and system-level constraints. We present TIDE (Temporal Incremental Draft Engine), a serving-engine-native framework that integrates online draft adaptation directly into high-performance LLM inference systems. TIDE reuses target model hidden states generated during inference as training signals, enabling zero-overhead draft adaptation without reloading the target model, and employs adaptive runtime control to activate speculation and training only when beneficial. TIDE exploits heterogeneous clusters by mapping decoupled inference and training to appropriate GPU classes. Across diverse real-world workloads, TIDE achieves up to 1.15x throughput improvement over static speculative decoding while reducing draft training time by 1.67x compared to approaches that recompute training signals.

</details>


### [190] [Cross-talk based multi-task learning for fault classification of physically coupled machine system](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05146)
*Wonjun Yi,Rismaya Kumar Mishra,Yong-Hwa Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MTL with cross-talk and a residual neural dimension reductor improves fault classification by exploiting physical coupling; outperforms single-task, naive multi-task, and shared trunk models on drone and motor fault benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Signals contain physically coupled information; leveraging this via cross-talk MTL can enhance fault recognition and avoid negative transfer.

Method: Extend residual neural dimension reductor to a cross-talk MTL framework; apply to drone fault dataset (machine type and maneuvering direction affect frequency content) and motor compound fault dataset (inner/outer race faults, misalignment, unbalance); compare single-channel vs multi-channel inputs.

Result: Proposed approach consistently outperforms baselines across both benchmarks, including single-task, multi-class naive MTL, and shared trunk MTL.

Conclusion: Cross-talk MTL with RND effectively exploits physical couplings to improve fault diagnosis; robust across input configurations and demonstrates a practical advantage over traditional MTL designs.

Abstract: Machine systems inherently generate signals in which fault conditions and various physical variables are physically coupled. Although many existing fault classification studies rely solely on direct fault labels, the aforementioned signals naturally embed additional information shaped by other physically coupled information. Herein, we leverage this coupling through a multi-task learning (MTL) framework that jointly learns fault conditions and the related physical variables. Among MTL architectures, crosstalk structures have distinct advantages because they allow for controlled information exchange between tasks through the cross-talk layer while preventing negative transfer, in contrast to shared trunk architectures that often mix incompatible features. We build on our previously introduced residual neural dimension reductor model, and extend its application to two benchmarks where physical coupling is prominent. The first benchmark is a drone fault dataset, in which machine type and maneuvering direction significantly alter the frequency components of measured signals even under the same nominal condition. By learning fault classification together with these physical attributes, the cross-talk architecture can better classify faults. The second benchmark dataset is the motor compound fault dataset. In this system, each fault component, inner race fault, outer race fault, misalignment, and unbalance is coupled to the other. For motor compound fault, we also test classification performance when we use single-channel data or multi-channel data as input to the classifier. Across both benchmarks, our residual neural dimension reductor, consistently outperformed single-task models, multi-class models that merge all label combinations, and shared trunk multi-task models.

</details>


### [191] [CoSA: Compressed Sensing-Based Adaptation of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05148)
*Songtao Wei,Yi Li,Bohan Zhang,Zhichun Guo,Ying Huang,Yuede Ji,Miao Yin,Guanpeng Li,Bingzhe Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoSA is a compressed-sensing-based PEFT method that uses fixed random projections and a compact learnable core to encode and reconstruct weight updates, offering greater expressivity than low-rank methods while remaining efficient; it matches or surpasses state-of-the-art PEFT across 10 tasks and 5 model families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the expressivity limitation of low-rank weight-update subspaces in PEFT (e.g., LoRA, PiSSA) when task adaptation requires more flexible updates.

Method: Express weight updates as a synthesis via fixed random projection matrices and a compact learnable core. Ground the approach in compressed sensing theory, showing weight updates can be encoded into a low-dimensional space and mapped back through random projections.

Result: Empirical evaluation over 10 diverse tasks (NLP understanding and generation) and 5 model scales from RoBERTa, Llama, and Qwen families shows CoSA consistently matches or outperforms state-of-the-art PEFT methods.

Conclusion: CoSA provides a principled, efficient, and expressive framework for multi-scale model adaptation, broadening the applicability of PEFT beyond traditional low-rank constraints.

Abstract: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) has emerged as a practical paradigm for adapting large language models (LLMs) without updating all parameters. Most existing approaches, such as LoRA and PiSSA, rely on low-rank decompositions of weight updates. However, the low-rank assumption may restrict expressivity, particularly in task-specific adaptation scenarios where singular values are distributed relatively uniformly. To address this limitation, we propose CoSA (Compressed Sensing-Based Adaptation), a new PEFT method extended from compressed sensing theory. Instead of constraining weight updates to a low-rank subspace, CoSA expresses them through fixed random projection matrices and a compact learnable core. We provide a formal theoretical analysis of CoSA as a synthesis process, proving that weight updates can be compactly encoded into a low-dimensional space and mapped back through random projections. Extensive experimental results show that CoSA provides a principled perspective for efficient and expressive multi-scale model adaptation. Specifically, we evaluate CoSA on 10 diverse tasks, including natural language understanding and generation, employing 5 models of different scales from RoBERTa, Llama, and Qwen families. Across these settings, CoSA consistently matches or outperforms state-of-the-art PEFT methods.

</details>


### [192] [Position: Capability Control Should be a Separate Goal From Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05164)
*Shoaib Ahmed Siddiqui,Eleni Triantafillou,David Krueger,Adrian Weller*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Advocates treating capability control as distinct from alignment, proposing a three-layer defense-in-depth framework (data-, learning-, system-based controls) to impose hard operational limits on foundation model behavior, with attention to dual-use and compositional generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models' broad training distributions yield powerful but potentially misusable behaviors; explicit capability restrictions are needed in addition to alignment.

Method: Organize capability controls into a tripartite framework across the model lifecycle: (i) data-based control of the training distribution, (ii) learning-based interventions at weight or representation level, and (iii) system-based guardrails on inputs, outputs, and actions; argue for defense-in-depth by combining complementary controls.

Result: A structured framework identifying three control layers, mapping their failure modes when used alone, and outlining a defense-in-depth approach; highlights open challenges such as dual-use knowledge and compositional generalization.

Conclusion: Capability control is a distinct, operational necessity that complements alignment; implementing layered defenses across data, learning, and system interfaces is essential to reduce misuse and failures, though substantial open research challenges remain.

Abstract: Foundation models are trained on broad data distributions, yielding generalist capabilities that enable many downstream applications but also expand the space of potential misuse and failures. This position paper argues that capability control -- imposing restrictions on permissible model behavior -- should be treated as a distinct goal from alignment. While alignment is often context and preference-driven, capability control aims to impose hard operational limits on permissible behaviors, including under adversarial elicitation. We organize capability control mechanisms across the model lifecycle into three layers: (i) data-based control of the training distribution, (ii) learning-based control via weight- or representation-level interventions, and (iii) system-based control via post-deployment guardrails over inputs, outputs, and actions. Because each layer has characteristic failure modes when used in isolation, we advocate for a defense-in-depth approach that composes complementary controls across the full stack. We further outline key open challenges in achieving such control, including the dual-use nature of knowledge and compositional generalization.

</details>


### [193] [EBPO: Empirical Bayes Shrinkage for Stabilizing Group-Relative Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05165)
*Kevin Han,Yuhang Zhou,Mingze Gao,Gedi Zhou,Serena Li,Abhishek Kumar,Xiangjun Fan,Weiwei Li,Lizhu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EBPO improves RLVR training by addressing GRPO's estimator variance and vanishing gradient issues through empirical Bayes shrinkage of baselines. It blends local group statistics with a global prior updated online via Welford's method, yielding lower MSE, non-vanishing penalties, and better stability, especially with small groups and when using curriculum learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO suffers from high estimator variance with small group sizes and vanishing gradients in saturated failure regimes (identical zero rewards). A robust baseline estimation that borrows strength across groups is needed to stabilize learning and improve sample efficiency in RLVR for LLMs.

Method: Introduce Empirical Bayes Policy Optimization (EBPO): a shrinkage-based regularization of local group baselines using a global prior updated online via Welford's algorithm. EBPO balances local statistics with global information to reduce MSE and maintain informative penalties; provides theoretical guarantees on MSE, entropy decay, and non-vanishing penalties; uses difficulty-stratified curriculum to enhance training.

Result: Empirically, EBPO outperforms GRPO and baselines on AIME and OlympiadBench, delivering improved training stability, better gains with small group sizes, and stronger benefits from curriculum learning.

Conclusion: EBPO provides a principled, stable, and effective alternative to GRPO for RLVR in LLMs, offering lower estimator variance, maintained penalty signals, and improved performance, especially under computational constraints and with curriculum-based training.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has proven effective for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, dominant approaches like Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) face critical stability challenges: they suffer from high estimator variance under computational constraints (small group sizes) and vanishing gradient signals in saturated failure regimes where all responses yield identical zero rewards. To address this, we propose Empirical Bayes Policy Optimization (EBPO), a novel framework that regularizes local group-based baselines by borrowing strength from the policy's accumulated global statistics. Instead of estimating baselines in isolation, EBPO employs a shrinkage estimator that dynamically balances local group statistics with a global prior updated via Welford's online algorithm. Theoretically, we demonstrate that EBPO guarantees strictly lower Mean Squared Error (MSE), bounded entropy decay, and non-vanishing penalty signals in failure scenarios compared to GRPO. Empirically, EBPO consistently outperforms GRPO and other established baselines across diverse benchmarks, including AIME and OlympiadBench. Notably, EBPO exhibits superior training stability, achieving high-performance gains even with small group sizes, and benefits significantly from difficulty-stratified curriculum learning.

</details>


### [194] [Benchmarking Artificial Intelligence Models for Daily Coastal Hypoxia Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05178)
*Magesh Rajasekaran,Md Saiful Sajol,Chris Alvin,Supratik Mukhopadhyay,Yanda Ou,Z. George Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ST-Transformer outperforms BiLSTM, Medformer, and TCN in daily coastal hypoxia classification using 12 years of hindcast data for training and 4 years for testing; achieves AUC-ROC 0.982–0.992; provides a reproducible real-time prediction framework with code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Daily hypoxia forecasts are needed for responsive ecosystem management beyond coarse seasonal models.

Method: Four architectures evaluated on identical data preprocessing: BiLSTM, Medformer, ST-Transformer, TCN; training 2009-2020 hindcast data; testing 2020-2024; features include stratification, sediment oxygen consumption, temperature-dependent decomposition; evaluation includes AUC-ROC and McNemar test; same data pipelines; reproducible framework; code at GitHub.

Result: ST-Transformer achieved highest performance across metrics and test periods; AUC-ROC between 0.982 and 0.992; McNemar tests show significant differences; robust across periods.

Conclusion: Provides a reproducible, operational framework for real-time hypoxia prediction in the Gulf of Mexico region; potential to support ocean modeling and ecosystem resilience; code available.

Abstract: Coastal hypoxia, especially in the northern part of Gulf of Mexico, presents a persistent ecological and economic concern. Seasonal models offer coarse forecasts that miss the fine-scale variability needed for daily, responsive ecosystem management. We present study that compares four deep learning architectures for daily hypoxia classification: Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM), Medformer (Medical Transformer), Spatio-Temporal Transformer (ST-Transformer), and Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN). We trained our models with twelve years of daily hindcast data from 2009-2020 Our training data consists of 2009-2020 hindcast data from a coupled hydrodynamic-biogeochemical model. Similarly, we use hindcast data from 2020 through 2024 as a test data. We constructed classification models incorporating water column stratification, sediment oxygen consumption, and temperature-dependent decomposition rates. We evaluated each architectures using the same data preprocessing, input/output formulation, and validation protocols. Each model achieved high classification accuracy and strong discriminative ability with ST-Transformer achieving the highest performance across all metrics and tests periods (AUC-ROC: 0.982-0.992). We also employed McNemar's method to identify statistically significant differences in model predictions. Our contribution is a reproducible framework for operational real-time hypoxia prediction that can support broader efforts in the environmental and ocean modeling systems community and in ecosystem resilience. The source code is available https://github.com/rmagesh148/hypoxia-ai/

</details>


### [195] [Data-Centric Interpretability for LLM-based Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05183)
*John Yan,Michael Yu,Yuqi Sun,Alexander Duffy,Tyler Marques,Matthew Lyle Olson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Meta-Autointerp groups SAE features to interpret RL training dynamics in a large-scale Diplomacy setting, revealing fine-grained and strategic behaviors. Most SAE features are significant; human usefulness is mixed, but some SAE-derived hypotheses are predictive and beneficial for downstream tasks. Demonstrated prompt-based improvement (+14.2%). SAEs and LLM-summaries offer complementary interpretability for trustworthy LLM behavior during training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how agent behavior evolves during complex, multi-agent RL training with LLMs, and to establish data-centric interpretability using Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) and LLM-based summarization.

Method: Apply pretrained Sparse Autoencoders to large-scale RL runs in Full-Press Diplomacy; introduce Meta-Autointerp to cluster SAE features into interpretable hypotheses about training dynamics; evaluate feature significance auto-ly; run two human studies to assess usefulness; test downstream utility by prompting an untrained agent; compare complementary insights from SAEs and LLM summaries.

Result: Automated evaluation finds 90% of discovered SAE Meta-Features significant; identifies fine-grained behaviors (role-playing, degenerate outputs, language switching) and high-level strategic behaviors plus environment-specific bugs; discovery of reward hacking behavior; human studies show most SAE- and LLM-generated hypotheses are not helpful, though some SAE-derived hypotheses are predictive for downstream tasks; augmenting an untrained agent's system prompt yields +14.2% score; SAEs and LLM-summaries provide complementary views.

Conclusion: SAEs and LLM-summarizers together offer a practical starting point for data-centric interpretability of complex RL training and LLM behavior, enabling trustworthy analysis; while many hypotheses may be of limited utility to humans, a subset is predictive and actionable for downstream tasks, suggesting a productive direction for future research.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly trained in complex Reinforcement Learning, multi-agent environments, making it difficult to understand how behavior changes over training. Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) have recently shown to be useful for data-centric interpretability. In this work, we analyze large-scale reinforcement learning training runs from the sophisticated environment of Full-Press Diplomacy by applying pretrained SAEs, alongside LLM-summarizer methods. We introduce Meta-Autointerp, a method for grouping SAE features into interpretable hypotheses about training dynamics. We discover fine-grained behaviors including role-playing patterns, degenerate outputs, language switching, alongside high-level strategic behaviors and environment-specific bugs. Through automated evaluation, we validate that 90% of discovered SAE Meta-Features are significant, and find a surprising reward hacking behavior. However, through two user studies, we find that even subjectively interesting and seemingly helpful SAE features may be worse than useless to humans, along with most LLM generated hypotheses. However, a subset of SAE-derived hypotheses are predictively useful for downstream tasks. We further provide validation by augmenting an untrained agent's system prompt, improving the score by +14.2%. Overall, we show that SAEs and LLM-summarizer provide complementary views into agent behavior, and together our framework forms a practical starting point for future data-centric interpretability work on ensuring trustworthy LLM behavior throughout training.

</details>


### [196] [SpectraKAN: Conditioning Spectral Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05187)
*Chun-Wun Cheng,Carola-Bibiane Schönlieb,Angelica I. Aviles-Rivero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SpectraKAN introduces an input-conditioned spectral operator by modulating a multi-scale Fourier trunk with a compact global representation via single-query cross-attention, achieving state-of-the-art PDE forecasting with strong theoretical guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static Fourier kernels in Fourier Neural Operators (FNO) limit the ability to capture multi-scale, regime-dependent, and anisotropic PDE dynamics that depend on the global state; an input-conditioned spectral operator is needed.

Method: Extract a compact global representation from spatio-temporal history and use it to modulate a multi-scale Fourier trunk through single-query cross-attention, turning static spectral convolution into an input-conditioned integral operator. Theoretical results show convergence to a resolution-independent continuous operator under mesh refinement and Lipschitz-controlled global modulation.

Result: State-of-the-art performance across diverse PDE benchmarks; RMSE reduced by up to 49% compared to strong baselines, with particularly large gains on challenging spatio-temporal prediction tasks.

Conclusion: SpectraKAN merges the efficiency of spectral methods with adaptive, globally conditioned modulation, providing robust PDE operator learning supported by convergence and Lipschitz-continuity guarantees.

Abstract: Spectral neural operators, particularly Fourier Neural Operators (FNO), are a powerful framework for learning solution operators of partial differential equations (PDEs) due to their efficient global mixing in the frequency domain. However, existing spectral operators rely on static Fourier kernels applied uniformly across inputs, limiting their ability to capture multi-scale, regime-dependent, and anisotropic dynamics governed by the global state of the system. We introduce SpectraKAN, a neural operator that conditions the spectral operator on the input itself, turning static spectral convolution into an input-conditioned integral operator. This is achieved by extracting a compact global representation from spatio-temporal history and using it to modulate a multi-scale Fourier trunk via single-query cross-attention, enabling the operator to adapt its behaviour while retaining the efficiency of spectral mixing. We provide theoretical justification showing that this modulation converges to a resolution-independent continuous operator under mesh refinement and KAN gives smooth, Lipschitz-controlled global modulation. Across diverse PDE benchmarks, SpectraKAN achieves state-of-the-art performance, reducing RMSE by up to 49% over strong baselines, with particularly large gains on challenging spatio-temporal prediction tasks.

</details>


### [197] [Double-P: Hierarchical Top-P Sparse Attention for Long-Context LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05191)
*Wentao Ni,Kangqi Zhang,Zhongming Yu,Oren Nelson,Mingu Lee,Hong Cai,Fatih Porikli,Jongryool Kim,Zhijian Liu,Jishen Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Double-P is a two-stage hierarchical top-p sparse attention framework that reduces long-context attention cost with minimal accuracy loss by combining coarse cluster-level estimation with selective token-level refinement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context inference in LLMs creates a dominant decoding bottleneck from attention over large key-value caches. Fixed-budget top-k sparse attention is inflexible to heterogeneous attention patterns, while top-p methods must balance accuracy, selection overhead, and sparse-cost. A method that jointly optimizes these three aspects is needed for scalable, accurate long-context decoding.

Method: Double-P performs a two-stage top-p sparsification. First, a coarse-grained top-p estimation is done at the cluster level using size-weighted centroids to filter attention. Then a second, token-level top-p stage refines computation adaptively, allocating attention only where needed. This hierarchical design optimizes accuracy, selection overhead, and sparse attention cost.

Result: Across long-context benchmarks, Double-P achieves near-zero accuracy drop, reduces attention computation overhead by up to 1.8x, and delivers up to 1.3x end-to-end decoding speedup over state-of-the-art fixed-budget sparse attention methods.

Conclusion: A hierarchical, two-stage top-p sparse attention framework can jointly optimize accuracy, selection overhead, and sparse cost, delivering substantial efficiency gains for long-context inference without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: As long-context inference becomes central to large language models (LLMs), attention over growing key-value caches emerges as a dominant decoding bottleneck, motivating sparse attention for scalable inference. Fixed-budget top-k sparse attention cannot adapt to heterogeneous attention distributions across heads and layers, whereas top-p sparse attention directly preserves attention mass and provides stronger accuracy guarantees. Existing top-p methods, however, fail to jointly optimize top-p accuracy, selection overhead, and sparse attention cost, which limits their overall efficiency. We present Double-P, a hierarchical sparse attention framework that optimizes all three stages. Double-P first performs coarse-grained top-p estimation at the cluster level using size-weighted centroids, then adaptively refines computation through a second top-p stage that allocates token-level attention only when needed. Across long-context benchmarks, Double-P consistently achieves near-zero accuracy drop, reducing attention computation overhead by up to 1.8x and delivers up to 1.3x end-to-end decoding speedup over state-of-the-art fixed-budget sparse attention methods.

</details>


### [198] [Extreme Weather Nowcasting via Local Precipitation Pattern Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05204)
*Changhoon Song,Teng Yuan Chang,Youngjoon Hong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Efficient deterministic nowcasting with exPreCast and a balanced radar dataset, achieving state-of-the-art performance in normal and extreme rainfall while addressing computational bottlenecks and dataset biases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for accurate, fast, and generalizable precipitation nowcasting that captures fine-scale structures and extreme events, overcoming limitations of diffusion-based ensembles (computationally heavy) and biased deterministic models, as well as skewed benchmark datasets.

Method: Proposes exPreCast, a deterministic framework incorporating local spatiotemporal attention, a texture-preserving cubic dual upsampling decoder, and a temporal extractor to flexibly adjust forecasting horizons. Evaluated on SEVIR, MeteoNet, and a newly constructed balanced KMA radar dataset containing ordinary and extreme rainfall events.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on established benchmarks and the balanced KMA dataset, delivering accurate and reliable nowcasts across normal and extreme rainfall regimes.

Conclusion: ExPreCast offers an efficient alternative to diffusion-based ensembles, combining high-fidelity texture preservation with flexible horizon control, while the balanced dataset enhances generalization across rainfall regimes; suitable for real-time deployment if computational constraints are met.

Abstract: Accurate forecasting of extreme weather events such as heavy rainfall or storms is critical for risk management and disaster mitigation. Although high-resolution radar observations have spurred extensive research on nowcasting models, precipitation nowcasting remains particularly challenging due to pronounced spatial locality, intricate fine-scale rainfall structures, and variability in forecasting horizons. While recent diffusion-based generative ensembles show promising results, they are computationally expensive and unsuitable for real-time applications. In contrast, deterministic models are computationally efficient but remain biased toward normal rainfall. Furthermore, the benchmark datasets commonly used in prior studies are themselves skewed--either dominated by ordinary rainfall events or restricted to extreme rainfall episodes--thereby hindering general applicability in real-world settings. In this paper, we propose exPreCast, an efficient deterministic framework for generating finely detailed radar forecasts, and introduce a newly constructed balanced radar dataset from the Korea Meteorological Administration (KMA), which encompasses both ordinary precipitation and extreme events. Our model integrates local spatiotemporal attention, a texture-preserving cubic dual upsampling decoder, and a temporal extractor to flexibly adjust forecasting horizons. Experiments on established benchmarks (SEVIR and MeteoNet) as well as on the balanced KMA dataset demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, delivering accurate and reliable nowcasts across both normal and extreme rainfall regimes.

</details>


### [199] [Disentangled Representation Learning via Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05214)
*Jinjin Chi,Taoping Liu,Mengtao Yin,Ximing Li,Yongcheng Jing,Dacheng Tao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Flow-matching framework for disentangled representations with an orthogonality regularizer improves disentanglement, controllability, and sample fidelity over diffusion-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based disentanglement methods often rely on inductive biases for factor independence but lack strong semantic alignment and suffer from cross-factor interference. A method with explicit factor-conditioned modeling and regularization is needed to improve semantic alignment and factor separation.

Method: A flow matching-based framework that learns factor-conditioned flows in a compact latent space to achieve disentanglement, augmented by a non-overlap (orthogonality) regularizer that suppresses cross-factor interference and reduces information leakage between factors.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple datasets show consistent improvements over representative baselines, achieving higher disentanglement scores and improved controllability and sample fidelity.

Conclusion: Factor-conditioned flows with an orthogonality regularizer effectively enhance semantic alignment and disentanglement, demonstrating the efficacy of the proposed flow-based framework.

Abstract: Disentangled representation learning aims to capture the underlying explanatory factors of observed data, enabling a principled understanding of the data-generating process. Recent advances in generative modeling have introduced new paradigms for learning such representations. However, existing diffusion-based methods encourage factor independence via inductive biases, yet frequently lack strong semantic alignment. In this work, we propose a flow matching-based framework for disentangled representation learning, which casts disentanglement as learning factor-conditioned flows in a compact latent space. To enforce explicit semantic alignment, we introduce a non-overlap (orthogonality) regularizer that suppresses cross-factor interference and reduces information leakage between factors. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over representative baselines, yielding higher disentanglement scores as well as improved controllability and sample fidelity.

</details>


### [200] [Private Prediction via Shrinkage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05219)
*Chao Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Private streaming prediction can achieve polylogarithmic dependence on the number of queries T, improving over the standard sqrt(T) composition bound. The labeled sample complexity scales with VC-dimension or dimension for adaptive adversaries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dwork and Feldman introduced differentially private prediction in a streaming context. The goal is to answer many unlabeled queries while keeping the transcript private, with minimal labeled data.

Method: For an oblivious online adversary and any concept class C, construct a private predictor that answers T queries using ~O(VC(C)^{3.5} log^{3.5} T) labeled examples. For an adaptive online adversary with halfspaces in R^d, achieve ~O(d^{5.5} log T) labeled examples. The results rely on private prediction mechanisms in streaming settings and appropriate privacy composition analyses.

Result: Oblivious adversary: |S| = ~O(VC(C)^{3.5} log^{3.5} T) labeled examples. Adaptive adversary (halfspaces in R^d): |S| = ~O(d^{5.5} log T).

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that in streaming differential privacy, the dependence on the number of queries can be reduced to polylogarithmic in T, with labeled-sample complexities that scale with VC-dimension or dimension, depending on the adversary model.

Abstract: We study differentially private prediction introduced by Dwork and Feldman (COLT 2018): an algorithm receives one labeled sample set $S$ and then answers a stream of unlabeled queries while the output transcript remains $(\varepsilon,δ)$-differentially private with respect to $S$. Standard composition yields a $\sqrt{T}$ dependence for $T$ queries.
  We show that this dependence can be reduced to polylogarithmic in $T$ in streaming settings. For an oblivious online adversary and any concept class $\mathcal{C}$, we give a private predictor that answers $T$ queries with $|S|= \tilde{O}(VC(\mathcal{C})^{3.5}\log^{3.5}T)$ labeled examples. For an adaptive online adversary and halfspaces over $\mathbb{R}^d$, we obtain $|S|=\tilde{O}\left(d^{5.5}\log T\right)$.

</details>


### [201] [ZeroS: Zero-Sum Linear Attention for Efficient Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05230)
*Jiecheng Lu,Xu Han,Yan Sun,Viresh Pati,Yubin Kim,Siddhartha Somani,Shihao Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ZeroS is a linear-attention variant that removes the zero-order term and reweights zero-sum softmax residuals to enable stable, potentially negative attention weights while keeping O(N) complexity; it expands expressivity beyond convex combinations and matches or surpasses softmax attention empirically.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two core limitations of existing linear attention: (1) restricted to convex combinations, blending only additive information, and (2) uniform accumulated weight bias (1/t) that dilutes attention in long contexts, limiting long-range modeling.

Method: Remove the constant 1/t term; reweight the remaining zero-sum softmax residuals to produce stable weights that can be positive or negative; enable a single attention layer to perform contrastive operations; maintain O(N) time/space complexity; provide theoretical arguments that the representation set is expanded beyond convex combinations.

Result: Empirical evaluations show ZeroS matches or exceeds standard softmax attention across diverse sequence modeling benchmarks.

Conclusion: Zero-Sum Linear Attention achieves stable, more expressive attention with the same linear-time complexity, enabling contrastive weighting and broader representational capacity.

Abstract: Linear attention methods offer Transformers $O(N)$ complexity but typically underperform standard softmax attention. We identify two fundamental limitations affecting these approaches: the restriction to convex combinations that only permits additive information blending, and uniform accumulated weight bias that dilutes attention in long contexts. We propose Zero-Sum Linear Attention (ZeroS), which addresses these limitations by removing the constant zero-order term $1/t$ and reweighting the remaining zero-sum softmax residuals. This modification creates mathematically stable weights, enabling both positive and negative values and allowing a single attention layer to perform contrastive operations. While maintaining $O(N)$ complexity, ZeroS theoretically expands the set of representable functions compared to convex combinations. Empirically, it matches or exceeds standard softmax attention across various sequence modeling benchmarks.

</details>


### [202] [Balanced Anomaly-guided Ego-graph Diffusion Model for Inductive Graph Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05232)
*Chunyu Wei,Siyuan He,Yu Wang,Yueguo Chen,Yunhai Wang,Bing Bai,Yidong Zhang,Yong Xie,Shunming Zhang,Fei Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A data-centric framework combining discrete ego-graph diffusion for anomaly-specific graph generation with curriculum-based augmentation to address dynamic graphs and severe class imbalance in graph anomaly detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GAD faces two intertwined challenges: (1) the predominance of transductive, static graph assumptions hindering applicability to evolving networks, and (2) extreme class imbalance causing biased models that fail to generalize to unseen anomalies. A data-centric approach that jointly models dynamics and augments underrepresented anomalies is needed to improve generalization and detection.

Method: 1) A discrete ego-graph diffusion model that captures the local topology around anomalies to generate ego-graphs aligned with the anomalous structural distribution. 2) A curriculum anomaly augmentation mechanism that dynamically adjusts synthetic data generation during training to emphasize underrepresented anomaly patterns, improving detection and generalization in dynamic and imbalanced settings.

Result: Experiments on five datasets show that the proposed framework improves detection performance over baselines, demonstrating better generalization to unseen anomalies in dynamic graphs.

Conclusion: A data-centric framework that unifies dynamic graph modeling with balanced synthetic anomaly data effectively addresses both the dynamic and class-imbalance challenges in graph anomaly detection.

Abstract: Graph anomaly detection (GAD) is crucial in applications like fraud detection and cybersecurity. Despite recent advancements using graph neural networks (GNNs), two major challenges persist. At the model level, most methods adopt a transductive learning paradigm, which assumes static graph structures, making them unsuitable for dynamic, evolving networks. At the data level, the extreme class imbalance, where anomalous nodes are rare, leads to biased models that fail to generalize to unseen anomalies. These challenges are interdependent: static transductive frameworks limit effective data augmentation, while imbalance exacerbates model distortion in inductive learning settings. To address these challenges, we propose a novel data-centric framework that integrates dynamic graph modeling with balanced anomaly synthesis. Our framework features: (1) a discrete ego-graph diffusion model, which captures the local topology of anomalies to generate ego-graphs aligned with anomalous structural distribution, and (2) a curriculum anomaly augmentation mechanism, which dynamically adjusts synthetic data generation during training, focusing on underrepresented anomaly patterns to improve detection and generalization. Experiments on five datasets demonstrate that the effectiveness of our framework.

</details>


### [203] [Faithful Bi-Directional Model Steering via Distribution Matching and Distributed Interchange Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05234)
*Yuntai Bao,Xuhong Zhang,Jintao Chen,Ge Su,Yuxiang Cai,Hao Peng,Bing Sun,Haiqin Weng,Liu Yan,Jianwei Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CDAS is a novel steering method built on DAS, using weakly supervised distribution matching with distributed interchange interventions (DIIs) to enable data-driven, bi-directional steering. It is complementary to preference-based methods and shows robustness in safety tasks, with performance benefits amplified by larger model scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Intervention-based model steering is lightweight and interpretable but prone to overfitting when optimized with fine-tuning objectives. Effective steering likely requires identifying internal model mechanisms rather than enforcing external preferences.

Method: Extend distributed alignment search (DAS) with Concept DAS (CDAS). Retain DAS's distributed interchange intervention (DII) but replace probability-maximization with a weakly supervised distribution-matching objective that aligns intervened output distributions with counterfactuals. DIIs enable bi-directional steering and allow steering factors to be data-derived, reducing hyperparameter tuning. This yields more faithful and stable control.

Result: On AxBench, CDAS does not always outperform preference-optimization methods but gains more with larger models. In two safety-related case studies, CDAS achieves systematic steering (overriding safety refusals and neutralizing a chain-of-thought backdoor) while maintaining general model utility. The approach is complementary to preference-optimization and can be a robust intervention-based steering method.

Conclusion: CDAS offers a complementary, robust approach to intervention-based model steering, leveraging data-derived, bidirectional interventions with weak supervision. It reduces hyperparameter tuning and scales favorably with model size in some settings, particularly safety-related tasks. Code is available at the provided GitHub URL.

Abstract: Intervention-based model steering offers a lightweight and interpretable alternative to prompting and fine-tuning. However, by adapting strong optimization objectives from fine-tuning, current methods are susceptible to overfitting and often underperform, sometimes generating unnatural outputs. We hypothesize that this is because effective steering requires the faithful identification of internal model mechanisms, not the enforcement of external preferences. To this end, we build on the principles of distributed alignment search (DAS), the standard for causal variable localization, to propose a new steering method: Concept DAS (CDAS). While we adopt the core mechanism of DAS, distributed interchange intervention (DII), we introduce a novel distribution matching objective tailored for the steering task by aligning intervened output distributions with counterfactual distributions. CDAS differs from prior work in two main ways: first, it learns interventions via weak-supervised distribution matching rather than probability maximization; second, it uses DIIs that naturally enable bi-directional steering and allow steering factors to be derived from data, reducing the effort required for hyperparameter tuning and resulting in more faithful and stable control. On AxBench, a large-scale model steering benchmark, we show that CDAS does not always outperform preference-optimization methods but may benefit more from increased model scale. In two safety-related case studies, overriding refusal behaviors of safety-aligned models and neutralizing a chain-of-thought backdoor, CDAS achieves systematic steering while maintaining general model utility. These results indicate that CDAS is complementary to preference-optimization approaches and conditionally constitutes a robust approach to intervention-based model steering. Our code is available at https://github.com/colored-dye/concept_das.

</details>


### [204] [CORP: Closed-Form One-shot Representation-Preserving Structured Pruning for Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05243)
*Boxiang Zhang,Baijian Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CORP is a one-shot, closed-form structured pruning method for Vision Transformers that removes MLP hidden dimensions and attention substructures without labels, gradients, or fine-tuning, using a calibration set to recover representations via ridge regression and fold compensation into weights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce inference cost of Vision Transformers post-training by aggressively pruning structure without retraining or labeled data, exploiting redundancy in MLP and attention components.

Method: Formulate pruning as a representation recovery problem. Treat removed activations and attention logits as affine functions of retained components; derive closed-form ridge regression solutions that integrate compensation into the model weights; operate with only a small unlabeled calibration set, no gradients or fine-tuning.

Result: Experiments on ImageNet with DeiT models reveal redundancy in MLP and attention representations. Without compensation, pruning degrades accuracy; with CORP, accuracy is preserved under aggressive sparsity. On DeiT-Huge, 50% pruning of both MLP and attention retains 82.8% Top-1. Pruning completes in under 20 minutes on a single GPU, yielding substantial efficiency gains.

Conclusion: CORP enables effective, fast, one-shot structured pruning for Vision Transformers under strict post-training constraints, demonstrating practical viability and highlighting the captured redundancy in MLP and attention pathways.

Abstract: Vision Transformers achieve strong accuracy but incur high compute and memory cost. Structured pruning can reduce inference cost, but most methods rely on retraining or multi-stage optimization. These requirements limit post-training deployment. We propose \textbf{CORP}, a closed-form one-shot structured pruning framework for Vision Transformers. CORP removes entire MLP hidden dimensions and attention substructures without labels, gradients, or fine-tuning. It operates under strict post-training constraints using only a small unlabeled calibration set. CORP formulates structured pruning as a representation recovery problem. It models removed activations and attention logits as affine functions of retained components and derives closed-form ridge regression solutions that fold compensation into model weights. This minimizes expected representation error under the calibration distribution. Experiments on ImageNet with DeiT models show strong redundancy in MLP and attention representations. Without compensation, one-shot structured pruning causes severe accuracy degradation. With CORP, models preserve accuracy under aggressive sparsity. On DeiT-Huge, CORP retains 82.8\% Top-1 accuracy after pruning 50\% of both MLP and attention structures. CORP completes pruning in under 20 minutes on a single GPU and delivers substantial real-world efficiency gains.

</details>


### [205] [TADS: Task-Aware Data Selection for Multi-Task Multimodal Pre-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05251)
*Guanjie Cheng,Boyi Li,Lingyu Sun,Mengying Zhu,Yangyang Wu,Xinkui Zhao,Shuiguang Deng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A task-aware, data-efficient framework (TADS) for multi-task multimodal pre-training that selects high-utility data via a learnable value function incorporating quality, relevance, and diversity, plus meta-learning feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address noise, misalignment, and redundancy in web-scale multimodal data; overcome biases of heuristic methods and multi-task gaps of task-agnostic approaches.

Method: Define TADS with intrinsic quality from unimodal/cross-modal operators; task relevance via interpretable similarity vectors; distributional diversity via cluster-based weighting; and a meta-learning loop using proxy models to adapt the data selection policy across multiple tasks.

Result: On CC12M, TADS achieves higher zero-shot performance on ImageNet, CIFAR-100, MS-COCO, Flickr30K using 36% data, outperforming baselines by about 1.0% on average.

Conclusion: TADS improves data efficiency for multi-task multimodal pre-training by curating a high-utility subset that yields a higher performance ceiling under the same computational budget.

Abstract: Large-scale multimodal pre-trained models like CLIP rely heavily on high-quality training data, yet raw web-crawled datasets are often noisy, misaligned, and redundant, leading to inefficient training and suboptimal generalization. Existing data selection methods are either heuristic-based, suffering from bias and limited diversity, or data-driven but task-agnostic, failing to optimize for multi-task scenarios. To address these gaps, we introduce TADS (Task-Aware Data Selection), a novel framework for multi-task multimodal pre-training that integrates Intrinsic Quality, Task Relevance, and Distributional Diversity into a learnable value function. TADS employs a comprehensive quality assessment system with unimodal and cross-modal operators, quantifies task relevance via interpretable similarity vectors, and optimizes diversity through cluster-based weighting. A feedback-driven meta-learning mechanism adaptively refines the selection strategy based on proxy model performance across multiple downstream tasks. Experiments on CC12M demonstrate that TADS achieves superior zero-shot performance on benchmarks like ImageNet, CIFAR-100, MS-COCO, and Flickr30K, using only 36% of the data while outperforming baselines by an average of 1.0%. This highlights that TADS significantly enhances data efficiency by curating a high-utility subset that yields a much higher performance ceiling within the same computational constraints.

</details>


### [206] [Hybrid Gated Flow (HGF): Stabilizing 1.58-bit LLMs via Selective Low-Rank Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05269)
*David Alejandro Trejo Pizzo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid Gated Flow (HGF) combines a 1.58-bit ternary backbone with a learnable FP16 correction path via adaptive gates to recover accuracy lost to extreme quantization, achieving meaningful quality gains with modest memory overhead and demonstrating stability that scales to 1.2B–3B models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate the memory bottleneck of edge-deployed LLMs by aggressively quantizing weights to 1.58-bit while addressing accuracy loss. Prior 1.58-bit methods incur substantial perplexity increases; HGF aims to recover a substantial portion of FP16 performance with controlled memory overhead.

Method: Introduce Hybrid Gated Flow, a dual-stream architecture: a ternary 1.58-bit backbone and a low-rank FP16 correction path, governed by adaptive gates. Train/test on TinyStories with 2500 and 3500 steps, compare against BitNet. Extend experiments to 1.2B and 3B models on SlimPajama and FineWeb-Edu to assess scalability.

Result: HGF-5.4 achieves validation loss 0.9306 vs BitNet 1.0294, recovering ~55% of the quality gap between ternary quantization and FP16 (0.8490). Memory overhead is ~12–15% beyond the ternary backbone. Emergent behavior: quantization acts as structural regularization; Diff_Only baseline shows instability while HGF remains robust. Preliminary large-scale results suggest linear scaling of stability and quality recovery to production-scale models (1.2B–3B).

Conclusion: HGF provides stable, quality-aware responses under aggressive 1.58-bit quantization with moderate memory overhead, enabling more feasible edge deployment of LLMs and suggesting quantization as a regularizer; larger-scale validation supports scalability, with future work on broader datasets and deployment considerations.

Abstract: The deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) on edge devices is fundamentally constrained by the "Memory Wall" -- a hardware limitation where memory bandwidth, not compute, becomes the bottleneck. Recent 1.58-bit quantization techniques (e.g., BitNet b1.58) dramatically reduce memory footprint but typically incur a perplexity degradation of 20-25% compared to FP16 baselines. In this work, we introduce Hybrid Gated Flow (HGF), a dual-stream architecture that couples a 1.58-bit ternary backbone with a learnable, low-rank FP16 correction path controlled by adaptive gates.
  Through extensive experiments on the TinyStories dataset across two training regimes (2500 and 3500 steps), we demonstrate that HGF 5.4 achieves a validation loss of 0.9306 compared to BitNet's 1.0294, recovering approximately 55% of the quality gap between pure ternary quantization and the FP16 baseline (0.8490). This recovery is achieved with only ~12-15% memory overhead beyond the ternary backbone.
  Furthermore, we provide empirical evidence for an emergent phenomenon: quantization as structural regularization. While a full-precision differential attention baseline (Diff_Only) exhibited training instability with validation loss exceeding 1.68, the ternary-anchored HGF maintained robust convergence throughout training. Finally, we report preliminary results extending this architecture to 1.2B and 3B parameter models trained on SlimPajama and FineWeb-Edu. These larger-scale experiments confirm that the architectural stability and quality recovery observed in small-scale proxies scale linearly to production-grade language modeling regimes.

</details>


### [207] [Back to Basics: Revisiting Exploration in Reinforcement Learning for LLM Reasoning via Generative Probabilities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05281)
*Pengyi Li,Elizaveta Goncharova,Andrey Kuznetsov,Ivan Oseledets*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProGRPO with Advantage Re-weighting Mechanism (ARM) mitigates entropy collapse in GRPO-based RL for LLM reasoning by reweighting advantages with prompt perplexity and answer confidence, improving diversity while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) tends to converge to low-entropy policies, causing mode collapse and reduced output diversity in LLM reasoning tasks.

Method: Introduce Advantage Re-weighting Mechanism (ARM) that integrates Prompt Perplexity and Answer Confidence into the advantage estimation, dynamically reshaping the reward signal to downweight over-confident reasoning paths and reallocate probability mass toward under-explored correct solutions; analyze sampling probability dynamics to justify the approach.

Result: Empirical evaluations on Qwen2.5 and DeepSeek show improved diversity and reduced entropy collapse; on Qwen2.5-7B, ProGRPO outperforms GRPO by 5.7% in Pass@1 and 13.9% in Pass@32, indicating enhanced capability to generate diverse correct reasoning paths.

Conclusion: ARM yields a better exploration–exploitation trade-off, increasing output diversity and response entropy with competitive accuracy for reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as an indispensable paradigm for enhancing reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs). However, standard policy optimization methods, such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), often converge to low-entropy policies, leading to severe mode collapse and limited output diversity. We analyze this issue from the perspective of sampling probability dynamics, identifying that the standard objective disproportionately reinforces the highest-likelihood paths, thereby suppressing valid alternative reasoning chains. To address this, we propose a novel Advantage Re-weighting Mechanism (ARM) designed to equilibrate the confidence levels across all correct responses. By incorporating Prompt Perplexity and Answer Confidence into the advantage estimation, our method dynamically reshapes the reward signal to attenuate the gradient updates of over-confident reasoning paths, while redistributing probability mass toward under-explored correct solutions. Empirical results demonstrate that our approach significantly enhances generative diversity and response entropy while maintaining competitive accuracy, effectively achieving a superior trade-off between exploration and exploitation in reasoning tasks. Empirical results on Qwen2.5 and DeepSeek models across mathematical and coding benchmarks show that ProGRPO significantly mitigates entropy collapse. Specifically, on Qwen2.5-7B, our method outperforms GRPO by 5.7% in Pass@1 and, notably, by 13.9% in Pass@32, highlighting its superior capability in generating diverse correct reasoning paths.

</details>


### [208] [Robust Inference-Time Steering of Protein Diffusion Models via Embedding Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05285)
*Minhuan Li,Jiequn Han,Pilar Cossio,Luhuan Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EmbedOpt offers inference-time optimization in a conditional embedding space to steer diffusion models toward experimental likelihoods, yielding improved map fitting and competitive distance-constraint performance with greater robustness and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Posterior sampling for diffusion-based biomolecular structure generation struggles when targets lie in low-density regions of the prior, requiring brittle likelihood weighting. There is a need for a robust, inference-time mechanism that leverages rich sequence/coevolution signals to satisfy experimental data.

Method: Introduce EmbedOpt, an inference-time approach that optimizes over a conditional embedding space encoding sequence and coevolution information to align the diffusion prior with experimental constraints. This avoids coordinate-based likelihood weighting and uses smooth optimization. Evaluated on two benchmarks: cryo-EM map fitting and experimental distance constraints.

Result: EmbedOpt outperforms coordinate-based posterior sampling in map-fitting tasks, matches performance on distance-constraint tasks, and shows superior robustness across hyperparameters spanning two orders of magnitude. It also reduces the required number of diffusion steps, achieving better efficiency.

Conclusion: Embedding-space conditioned inference is a promising strategy for drift-diffusion-based biomolecular modeling, mitigating brittleness of likelihood weighting and improving efficiency; future work could extend to additional experimental constraints and larger biomolecular systems.

Abstract: In many biophysical inverse problems, the goal is to generate biomolecular conformations that are both physically plausible and consistent with experimental measurements. As recent sequence-to-structure diffusion models provide powerful data-driven priors, posterior sampling has emerged as a popular framework by guiding atomic coordinates to target conformations using experimental likelihoods. However, when the target lies in a low-density region of the prior, posterior sampling requires aggressive and brittle weighting of the likelihood guidance. Motivated by this limitation, we propose EmbedOpt, an alternative inference-time approach for steering diffusion models to optimize experimental likelihoods in the conditional embedding space. As this space encodes rich sequence and coevolutionary signals, optimizing over it effectively shifts the diffusion prior to align with experimental constraints. We validate EmbedOpt on two benchmarks simulating cryo-electron microscopy map fitting and experimental distance constraints. We show that EmbedOpt outperforms the coordinate-based posterior sampling method in map fitting tasks, matches performance on distance constraint tasks, and exhibits superior engineering robustness across hyperparameters spanning two orders of magnitude. Moreover, its smooth optimization behavior enables a significant reduction in the number of diffusion steps required for inference, leading to better efficiency.

</details>


### [209] [HealthMamba: An Uncertainty-aware Spatiotemporal Graph State Space Model for Effective and Reliable Healthcare Facility Visit Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05286)
*Dahai Yu,Lin Jiang,Rongchao Xu,Guang Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HealthMamba is an uncertainty-aware spatiotemporal framework for predicting healthcare facility visits, combining a Unified Spatiotemporal Context Encoder, GraphMamba for hierarchical spatiotemporal modeling, and a three-mechanism uncertainty quantification module; validated on four large real-world state datasets with about 6.0% accuracy improvement and 3.5% better uncertainty quantification over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work largely treats healthcare visit prediction as simple time-series forecasting, neglecting intrinsic spatial dependencies across facility types and lacking reliability under abnormal conditions (e.g., emergencies). This motivates a unified spatiotemporal, graph-based, and uncertainty-aware approach for accurate and reliable predictions.

Method: Three components: (i) Unified Spatiotemporal Context Encoder that integrates heterogeneous static (e.g., facility attributes) and dynamic information; (ii) GraphMamba, a Graph State Space Model for hierarchical spatiotemporal modeling to capture dependencies across facility types and locations; (iii) a comprehensive uncertainty quantification (UQ) module combining three UQ mechanisms to provide reliable predictions under uncertainty.

Result: Empirical evaluation on four large-scale real-world datasets from California, New York, Texas, and Florida shows HealthMamba achieves ~6.0% improvement in prediction accuracy and ~3.5% improvement in uncertainty quantification over state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: HealthMamba advances accurate and reliable healthcare facility visit prediction by integrating heterogeneous context, hierarchical spatiotemporal modeling, and robust uncertainty quantification, demonstrated across multiple states/datasets.

Abstract: Healthcare facility visit prediction is essential for optimizing healthcare resource allocation and informing public health policy. Despite advanced machine learning methods being employed for better prediction performance, existing works usually formulate this task as a time-series forecasting problem without considering the intrinsic spatial dependencies of different types of healthcare facilities, and they also fail to provide reliable predictions under abnormal situations such as public emergencies. To advance existing research, we propose HealthMamba, an uncertainty-aware spatiotemporal framework for accurate and reliable healthcare facility visit prediction. HealthMamba comprises three key components: (i) a Unified Spatiotemporal Context Encoder that fuses heterogeneous static and dynamic information, (ii) a novel Graph State Space Model called GraphMamba for hierarchical spatiotemporal modeling, and (iii) a comprehensive uncertainty quantification module integrating three uncertainty quantification mechanisms for reliable prediction. We evaluate HealthMamba on four large-scale real-world datasets from California, New York, Texas, and Florida. Results show HealthMamba achieves around 6.0% improvement in prediction accuracy and 3.5% improvement in uncertainty quantification over state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [210] [A Short and Unified Convergence Analysis of the SAG, SAGA, and IAG Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05304)
*Feng Zhu,Robert W. Heath,Aritra Mitra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified, modular convergence analysis for SAG, SAGA, and IAG on finite-sum, smooth strongly convex objectives, yielding high-probability bounds for SAG/SAGA and improved rates for IAG; two-step framework with delayed-sub-sampling concentration and a Lyapunov function.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reconcile disparate analyses of stochastic variance-reduction algorithms (SAG, SAGA) and their deterministic counterpart (IAG), provide a single framework, and obtain high-probability bounds that extend to non-convex objectives and Markov sampling, addressing the long and intricate original SAG proof.

Method: (i) bound the algorithmic delays caused by stochastic sub-sampling using simple concentration tools; (ii) construct a novel Lyapunov function that captures the impact of these delays, enabling a unified convergence analysis across SAG, SAGA, and IAG.

Result: A short, modular, and unified convergence proof establishing high-probability bounds for SAG and SAGA and improved (best-known) rates for IAG; the framework is extendable to non-convex objectives and Markov sampling.

Conclusion: The work provides a single, versatile analysis framework that unifies previously disparate proofs, streamlines SAG proofs, and delivers tighter rates for IAG, with potential for broader applicability to non-convex settings and dependent sampling.

Abstract: Stochastic variance-reduced algorithms such as Stochastic Average Gradient (SAG) and SAGA, and their deterministic counterparts like the Incremental Aggregated Gradient (IAG) method, have been extensively studied in large-scale machine learning. Despite their popularity, existing analyses for these algorithms are disparate, relying on different proof techniques tailored to each method. Furthermore, the original proof of SAG is known to be notoriously involved, requiring computer-aided analysis. Focusing on finite-sum optimization with smooth and strongly convex objective functions, our main contribution is to develop a single unified convergence analysis that applies to all three algorithms: SAG, SAGA, and IAG. Our analysis features two key steps: (i) establishing a bound on delays due to stochastic sub-sampling using simple concentration tools, and (ii) carefully designing a novel Lyapunov function that accounts for such delays. The resulting proof is short and modular, providing the first high-probability bounds for SAG and SAGA that can be seamlessly extended to non-convex objectives and Markov sampling. As an immediate byproduct of our new analysis technique, we obtain the best known rates for the IAG algorithm, significantly improving upon prior bounds.

</details>


### [211] [Accelerated Sequential Flow Matching: A Bayesian Filtering Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05319)
*Yinan Huang,Hans Hao-Hsun Hsu,Junran Wang,Bo Dai,Pan Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sequential Flow Matching uses Bayesian filtering to propagate predictive posteriors in time, enabling fast, few-step sampling for streaming, multi-modal predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce inference latency and backlog in real-time streaming with flow-based models by leveraging information from the previous posterior, addressing the inefficiency of re-sampling from a non-informative initial distribution.

Method: Treat sequential inference as learning a probability flow that transports the predictive distribution from time t to t+1, aligning with Bayesian belief updates. Initialize generation from the previous posterior to provide a principled warm start, and provide theoretical justification for faster sampling over naïve re-sampling.

Result: Empirically competitive with full-step diffusion while requiring only one or a few sampling steps, achieving faster sampling across forecasting, decision-making, and state-estimation tasks.

Conclusion: Framing sequential inference via Bayesian filtering offers a principled, efficient approach for real-time deployment of flow-based models.

Abstract: Sequential prediction from streaming observations is a fundamental problem in stochastic dynamical systems, where inherent uncertainty often leads to multiple plausible futures. While diffusion and flow-matching models are capable of modeling complex, multi-modal trajectories, their deployment in real-time streaming environments typically relies on repeated sampling from a non-informative initial distribution, incurring substantial inference latency and potential system backlogs. In this work, we introduce Sequential Flow Matching, a principled framework grounded in Bayesian filtering. By treating streaming inference as learning a probability flow that transports the predictive distribution from one time step to the next, our approach naturally aligns with the recursive structure of Bayesian belief updates. We provide theoretical justification that initializing generation from the previous posterior offers a principled warm start that can accelerate sampling compared to naïve re-sampling. Across a wide range of forecasting, decision-making and state estimation tasks, our method achieves performance competitive with full-step diffusion while requiring only one or very few sampling steps, therefore with faster sampling. It suggests that framing sequential inference via Bayesian filtering provides a new and principled perspective towards efficient real-time deployment of flow-based models.

</details>


### [212] [GAS: Enhancing Reward-Cost Balance of Generative Model-assisted Offline Safe RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05323)
*Zifan Liu,Xinran Li,Shibo Chen,Jun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GAS improves offline safe RL by enabling stitching of suboptimal trajectories and balancing reward vs cost through data augmentation, relabeling, and expectile-regression-based goal estimation to guide policy training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OSRL requires high performance under constraints using only pre-collected data. Generative-model-based approaches struggle with stitching suboptimal trajectories and balancing conflicting reward and constraint goals.

Method: (1) Transition-level data augmentation and relabeling to construct higher-quality trajectories. (2) Learn goal functions via expectile regression on relabeled data to estimate achievable reward and cost goals for a broader set of return pairs. (3) Use goals to guide policy training and reshape dataset for a more uniform reward-cost distribution, improving stability.

Result: Empirical results show GAS achieves superior tradeoffs between reward maximization and constraint satisfaction compared to existing methods, demonstrating robust constrained performance.

Conclusion: GAS effectively enhances stitching and constraint balance in OSRL by data augmentation, relabeling, and expectile-goal estimation, enabling more robust and efficient policy learning under safety constraints.

Abstract: Offline Safe Reinforcement Learning (OSRL) aims to learn a policy to achieve high performance in sequential decision-making while satisfying constraints, using only pre-collected datasets. Recent works, inspired by the strong capabilities of Generative Models (GMs), reformulate decision-making in OSRL as a conditional generative process, where GMs generate desirable actions conditioned on predefined reward and cost values. However, GM-assisted methods face two major challenges in OSRL: (1) lacking the ability to "stitch" optimal transitions from suboptimal trajectories within the dataset, and (2) struggling to balance reward targets with cost targets, particularly when they are conflict. To address these issues, we propose Goal-Assisted Stitching (GAS), a novel algorithm designed to enhance stitching capabilities while effectively balancing reward maximization and constraint satisfaction. To enhance the stitching ability, GAS first augments and relabels the dataset at the transition level, enabling the construction of high-quality trajectories from suboptimal ones. GAS also introduces novel goal functions, which estimate the optimal achievable reward and cost goals from the dataset. These goal functions, trained using expectile regression on the relabeled and augmented dataset, allow GAS to accommodate a broader range of reward-cost return pairs and achieve a better tradeoff between reward maximization and constraint satisfaction compared to human-specified values. The estimated goals then guide policy training, ensuring robust performance under constrained settings. Furthermore, to improve training stability and efficiency, we reshape the dataset to achieve a more uniform reward-cost return distribution. Empirical results validate the effectiveness of GAS, demonstrating superior performance in balancing reward maximization and constraint satisfaction compared to existing methods.

</details>


### [213] [Pool-based Active Learning as Noisy Lossy Compression: Characterizing Label Complexity via Finite Blocklength Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05333)
*Kosuke Sugiyama,Masato Uchida*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reframes pool-based active learning as a noisy lossy compression problem and derives information-theoretic lower bounds on label complexity and generalization error via finite blocklength analysis, highlighting overfitting and inductive-bias mismatch.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish fundamental limits and a unified information-theoretic framework for data selection and learning in pool-based AL, clarifying how data selection strategy and learning algorithm affect performance.

Method: Map pool observations to noisy symbols; treat data selection as data compression; model learning as decoding; apply finite blocklength analysis of noisy lossy compression to obtain lower bounds; relate results to stability theory.

Result: Lower bounds on label complexity and generalization error for a given algorithm under optimal data selection; bounds include terms for overfitting and inductive-bias mismatch; align with existing IT bounds and stability theory; not previously applied to pool-based AL.

Conclusion: Offers a new theoretical perspective on pool-based AL and a path to algorithm design with information-theoretic guarantees, by integrating data selection and learning into a single framework.

Abstract: This paper proposes an information-theoretic framework for analyzing the theoretical limits of pool-based active learning (AL), in which a subset of instances is selectively labeled. The proposed framework reformulates pool-based AL as a noisy lossy compression problem by mapping pool observations to noisy symbol observations, data selection to compression, and learning to decoding. This correspondence enables a unified information-theoretic analysis of data selection and learning in pool-based AL. Applying finite blocklength analysis of noisy lossy compression, we derive information-theoretic lower bounds on label complexity and generalization error that serve as theoretical limits for a given learning algorithm under its associated optimal data selection strategy. Specifically, our bounds include terms that reflect overfitting induced by the learning algorithm and the discrepancy between its inductive bias and the target task, and are closely related to established information-theoretic bounds and stability theory, which have not been previously applied to the analysis of pool-based AL. These properties yield a new theoretical perspective on pool-based AL.

</details>


### [214] [Smoothness Errors in Dynamics Models and How to Avoid Them](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05352)
*Edward Berman,Luisa Li,Jung Yeon Park,Robin Walters*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduce relaxed unitary convolutions for graph/mesh neural networks to balance smoothing in dynamics modeling; demonstrate improved performance over baselines on diffusion-like PDEs and weather forecasting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Over-smoothing in graph neural networks degrades dynamic modeling. Unitary convolutions preserve smoothness but can be overly constraining for processes that naturally smooth over time (e.g., diffusion). A controlled relaxation may retain structure while allowing necessary smoothing.

Method: Propose relaxed unitary convolutions that interpolate between unitary and non-unitary operators; generalize the approach from graphs to meshes; analyze smoothing effects; conduct experiments on heat/wave PDEs on complex meshes and on weather forecasting; compare against mesh-aware transformers and equivariant neural networks.

Result: Relaxed unitary convolutions outperform strong baselines across tasks; unitary constraints can hurt diffusion-like dynamics; the mesh-generalized version maintains gains.

Conclusion: Relaxed unitary convolutions provide a better balance between preserving smoothness and allowing natural smoothing in physical systems; applicable to graphs and meshes; leads to improved PDE solvers and forecasting with GNNs and related architectures.

Abstract: Modern neural networks have shown promise for solving partial differential equations over surfaces, often by discretizing the surface as a mesh and learning with a mesh-aware graph neural network. However, graph neural networks suffer from oversmoothing, where a node's features become increasingly similar to those of its neighbors. Unitary graph convolutions, which are mathematically constrained to preserve smoothness, have been proposed to address this issue. Despite this, in many physical systems, such as diffusion processes, smoothness naturally increases and unitarity may be overconstraining. In this paper, we systematically study the smoothing effects of different GNNs for dynamics modeling and prove that unitary convolutions hurt performance for such tasks. We propose relaxed unitary convolutions that balance smoothness preservation with the natural smoothing required for physical systems. We also generalize unitary and relaxed unitary convolutions from graphs to meshes. In experiments on PDEs such as the heat and wave equations over complex meshes and on weather forecasting, we find that our method outperforms several strong baselines, including mesh-aware transformers and equivariant neural networks.

</details>


### [215] [Bayesian Neighborhood Adaptation for Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05358)
*Paribesh Regmi,Rui Li,Kishan K C*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Bayesian framework that adaptively infers the effective neighborhood scope (number of hops) for GNNs via a beta-process prior, enabling simultaneous optimization of GNN parameters. It improves expressivity and achieves competitive or superior node classification performance on both homophilic and heterophilic graphs, with well-calibrated predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed or pre-searched neighborhood scopes in GNNs are inefficient and may bias results. There is a need for a unified, data-driven method to determine appropriate hop-based aggregation scopes that works across both homophily regimes.

Method: Model the message-passing in GNNs as a stochastic process and place a beta-process prior on the number of hops used in aggregation. Use Bayesian inference to infer the plausible neighborhood scope jointly with learning GNN parameters.

Result: Theoretical analysis indicates scope inference enhances GNN expressivity.
