Empirical results on benchmark homophilic and heterophilic datasets show compatibility with state-of-the-art GNN variants and achieve competitive or superior node classification accuracy, with well-calibrated predictions.

Conclusion: Adaptive neighborhood-scope inference via a beta-process Bayesian framework enhances GNN performance across graph types and complements existing architectures, offering a principled, efficient way to determine aggregation scopes.

Abstract: The neighborhood scope (i.e., number of hops) where graph neural networks (GNNs) aggregate information to characterize a node's statistical property is critical to GNNs' performance. Two-stage approaches, training and validating GNNs for every pre-specified neighborhood scope to search for the best setting, is a time-consuming task and tends to be biased due to the search space design. How to adaptively determine proper neighborhood scopes for the aggregation process for both homophilic and heterophilic graphs remains largely unexplored. We thus propose to model the GNNs' message-passing behavior on a graph as a stochastic process by treating the number of hops as a beta process. This Bayesian framework allows us to infer the most plausible neighborhood scope for message aggregation simultaneously with the optimization of GNN parameters. Our theoretical analysis shows that the scope inference improves the expressivity of a GNN. Experiments on benchmark homophilic and heterophilic datasets show that the proposed method is compatible with state-of-the-art GNN variants, achieving competitive or superior performance on the node classification task, and providing well-calibrated predictions.

</details>


### [216] [Hinge Regression Tree: A Newton Method for Oblique Regression Tree Splitting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05371)
*Hongyi Li,Han Lin,Jun Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces the Hinge Regression Tree (HRT) for oblique splits by reframing each split as a nonlinear least-squares problem over two linear predictors whose hinge envelope yields ReLU-like power. The alternating fitting is equivalent to a damped Gauss-Newton method within fixed partitions. The paper proves monotone convergence for a backtracking line-search variant, shows fast and stable convergence with optional ridge regularization, and establishes universal approximation with an explicit O(δ^2) rate. Empirically, HRT matches or outperforms single-tree baselines with more compact structures on synthetic and real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Oblique decision trees offer powerful decision boundaries but learning high-quality oblique splits is NP-hard and existing methods rely on slow search or theory-free heuristics. There is a need for a principled, efficient optimization framework with convergence guarantees and compact, accurate trees.

Method: Formulates each split using two linear predictors and their max/min envelope to create a ReLU-like nonlinearity. The fitting procedure is alternating between the two predictors and is mathematically equivalent to a damped Gauss-Newton (Newton-like) method within fixed partitions. An optional ridge penalty is considered. A backtracking line search is used to guarantee monotone decrease of the local objective and convergence.

Result: The node-level optimization converges, with both fixed and adaptively damped variants yielding fast, stable convergence. The backtracking variant ensures monotone decreases of the objective. The method is a universal approximator with explicit O(δ^2) approximation rate. Empirical results on synthetic and real-world data show HRT matches or outperforms single-tree baselines while producing more compact models.

Conclusion: HRT provides a principled, convergent optimization-based approach to learning oblique decision trees, delivering strong theoretical guarantees (convergence, universality) and practical performance improvements (compact trees, competitive accuracy) over traditional single-tree baselines.

Abstract: Oblique decision trees combine the transparency of trees with the power of multivariate decision boundaries, but learning high-quality oblique splits is NP-hard, and practical methods still rely on slow search or theory-free heuristics. We present the Hinge Regression Tree (HRT), which reframes each split as a non-linear least-squares problem over two linear predictors whose max/min envelope induces ReLU-like expressive power. The resulting alternating fitting procedure is exactly equivalent to a damped Newton (Gauss-Newton) method within fixed partitions. We analyze this node-level optimization and, for a backtracking line-search variant, prove that the local objective decreases monotonically and converges; in practice, both fixed and adaptive damping yield fast, stable convergence and can be combined with optional ridge regularization. We further prove that HRT's model class is a universal approximator with an explicit $O(δ^2)$ approximation rate, and show on synthetic and real-world benchmarks that it matches or outperforms single-tree baselines with more compact structures.

</details>


### [217] [Erase at the Core: Representation Unlearning for Machine Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05375)
*Jaewon Lee,Yongwoo Kim,Donghyun Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Erase at the Core (EC), a plug-in framework that enforces forgetting across the network hierarchy via multi-layer contrastive unlearning and deeply supervised losses, reducing representational similarity while preserving retain-set performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Logit-level forgetting is common in unlearning methods, but intermediate representations often retain information, causing superficial forgetting. Deeper, hierarchical forgetting is needed.

Method: Attach auxiliary modules to intermediate layers; perform multi-layer contrastive unlearning on the forget set and deeply supervised learning on the retain set. Apply both contrastive unlearning and cross-entropy losses at multiple supervision points with layer-wise weighting. EC is model-agnostic and can be plugged into existing methods.

Result: EC achieves effective logit-level forgetting and substantially lowers the similarity of intermediate representations to the original model. It also preserves retain-set performance and can enhance other unlearning methods by improving representation-level forgetting.

Conclusion: EC demonstrates how hierarchical, multi-layer supervision can realize comprehensive forgetting across a network, is model-agnostic, and serves as a practical plug-in to strengthen existing unlearning approaches.

Abstract: Many approximate machine unlearning methods demonstrate strong logit-level forgetting -- such as near-zero accuracy on the forget set -- yet continue to preserve substantial information within their internal feature representations. We refer to this discrepancy as superficial forgetting. Recent studies indicate that most existing unlearning approaches primarily alter the final classifier, leaving intermediate representations largely unchanged and highly similar to those of the original model. To address this limitation, we introduce the Erase at the Core (EC), a framework designed to enforce forgetting throughout the entire network hierarchy. EC integrates multi-layer contrastive unlearning on the forget set with retain set preservation through deeply supervised learning. Concretely, EC attaches auxiliary modules to intermediate layers and applies both contrastive unlearning and cross-entropy losses at each supervision point, with layer-wise weighted losses. Experimental results show that EC not only achieves effective logit-level forgetting, but also substantially reduces representational similarity to the original model across intermediate layers. Furthermore, EC is model-agnostic and can be incorporated as a plug-in module into existing unlearning methods, improving representation-level forgetting while maintaining performance on the retain set.

</details>


### [218] [Assessing Electricity Demand Forecasting with Exogenous Data in Time Series Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05390)
*Wei Soon Cheong,Lian Lian Jiang,Jamie Ng Suat Ling*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Foundation models for time-series forecasting in electricity demand show mixed results; Chronos-2 leads among FM in zero-shot, but simple baselines can outperform FM in stable climates (Singapore) for short horizons; architecture and geography are crucial; universal FM superiority is not supported; domain-specific models are favored.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess cross-channel exogenous feature usage by foundation models vs a strong LSTM baseline across Singapore and Australia at hourly and daily granularity; compare multiple FM architectures (MOIRAI, MOMENT, TinyTimeMixers, ChronosX, Chronos-2) under three feature configurations.

Method: Empirical study evaluating MOIRAI, MOMENT, TinyTimeMIXers, ChronosX, Chronos-2 on hourly and daily electricity demand data for Singapore and Australia. Three feature configurations: all features, selected features, target-only. Baseline: reversible instance-normalized LSTM. Analyze performance in zero-shot settings across horizons.

Result: Found highly variable effectiveness. Chronos-2 achieves the best performance among foundation models in zero-shot settings; however, a simple baseline frequently outperforms all foundation models in Singapore’s stable climate, particularly for short-term horizons. Architectural choices matter: TTMs channel-mixing and Chronos-2’s grouped attention consistently enable exploitation of exogenous features, while other approaches show inconsistent benefits. Geographic context is important: foundation models show advantages mainly in variable climates. Overall, foundation models are not universally superior; domain-specific models are needed in energy forecasting.

Conclusion: Foundation-model-based forecasting in energy is context-dependent; climate variability and architectural design determine gains from exogenous features. Do not assume universal FM superiority; prioritize domain-tuned architectures and region-specific modeling.

Abstract: Time-series foundation models have emerged as a new paradigm for forecasting, yet their ability to effectively leverage exogenous features -- critical for electricity demand forecasting -- remains unclear. This paper empirically evaluates foundation models capable of modeling cross-channel correlations against a baseline LSTM with reversible instance normalization across Singaporean and Australian electricity markets at hourly and daily granularities. We systematically assess MOIRAI, MOMENT, TinyTimeMixers, ChronosX, and Chronos-2 under three feature configurations: all features, selected features, and target-only. Our findings reveal highly variable effectiveness: while Chronos-2 achieves the best performance among foundation models (in zero-shot settings), the simple baseline frequently outperforms all foundation models in Singapore's stable climate, particularly for short-term horizons. Model architecture proves critical, with synergistic architectural implementations (TTM's channel-mixing, Chronos-2's grouped attention) consistently leveraging exogenous features, while other approaches show inconsistent benefits. Geographic context emerges as equally important, with foundation models demonstrating advantages primarily in variable climates. These results challenge assumptions about universal foundation model superiority and highlight the need for domain-specific models, specifically in the energy domain.

</details>


### [219] [Robust Federated Learning via Byzantine Filtering over Encrypted Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05410)
*Adda Akram Bendoukha,Aymen Boudguiga,Nesrine Kaaniche,Renaud Sirdey,Didem Demirag,Sébastien Gambs*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified FL framework that merges CKKS homomorphic encryption-based secure aggregation with property-inference-inspired meta-classifiers for Byzantine filtering, trained on shadow updates to detect various Byzantine attacks and reweight updates; includes an automated method to select CKKS kernel and dimensionality; validated on FEMNIST, CIFAR10, GTSRB, and acsincome with 90-94% Byzantine-detection accuracy and modest utility loss, with encrypted inference times of 6-26s.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To jointly address privacy-preserving aggregation and Byzantine resilience in Federated Learning, which are typically tackled separately; achieve secure, robust aggregation without sacrificing performance.

Method: 1) Train a set of filtering meta-classifiers on labeled shadow updates to reproduce a spectrum of Byzantine attacks (backdoor, gradient-inversion, label-flipping, shuffling). Use their outputs to reweight and cancel Byzantine encrypted updates during aggregation. 2) Develop an automated CKKS parameter selection process (kernel choice and dimensionality) to optimize homomorphic inference, aggregation constraints, and efficiency. 3) Empirically evaluate on FEMNIST, CIFAR10, GTSRB, and acsincome; report Byzantine-detection accuracy and impact on model utility and encrypted runtimes.

Result: SVM-based filtering achieves 90-94% accuracy in identifying Byzantine updates; achieves only marginal drops in model utility; encrypted inference runtimes range from 6-24 seconds and 9-26 seconds for total aggregation across experiments.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of a unified privacy-preserving and Byzantine-robust FL approach by integrating HE-based secure aggregation with meta-classifier filtering; shows promising results across multiple benchmarks, though details on adaptability to novel attacks and scalability require further exploration.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) aims to train a collaborative model while preserving data privacy. However, the distributed nature of this approach still raises privacy and security issues, such as the exposure of sensitive data due to inference attacks and the influence of Byzantine behaviors on the trained model. In particular, achieving both secure aggregation and Byzantine resilience remains challenging, as existing solutions often address these aspects independently. In this work, we propose to address these challenges through a novel approach that combines homomorphic encryption for privacy-preserving aggregation with property-inference-inspired meta-classifiers for Byzantine filtering. First, following the property-inference attacks blueprint, we train a set of filtering meta-classifiers on labeled shadow updates, reproducing a diverse ensemble of Byzantine misbehaviors in FL, including backdoor, gradient-inversion, label-flipping and shuffling attacks. The outputs of these meta-classifiers are then used to cancel the Byzantine encrypted updates by reweighting. Second, we propose an automated method for selecting the optimal kernel and the dimensionality hyperparameters with respect to homomorphic inference, aggregation constraints and efficiency over the CKKS cryptosystem. Finally, we demonstrate through extensive experiments the effectiveness of our approach against Byzantine participants on the FEMNIST, CIFAR10, GTSRB, and acsincome benchmarks. More precisely, our SVM filtering achieves accuracies between $90$% and $94$% for identifying Byzantine updates at the cost of marginal losses in model utility and encrypted inference runtimes ranging from $6$ to $24$ seconds and from $9$ to $26$ seconds for an overall aggregation.

</details>


### [220] [BLITZRANK: Principled Zero-shot Ranking Agents with Tournament Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05448)
*Sheshansh Agrawal,Thien Hang Nguyen,Douwe Kiela*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A tournament-graph based framework for k-wise LLM reranking that converts k-document comparisons into a global preference graph, uses transitive closure to infer additional orderings, handles non-transitive judgments by forming equivalence tiers, and uses a greedy information-gain query schedule to identify top-m items with fewer model calls; empirically superior across 14 benchmarks/5 LLMs, with ~25-40% token reduction vs baselines and ~7x savings vs pairwise methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome inefficiencies and underutilization of information in existing LLM reranking approaches by exploiting the full information from k-wise comparisons and providing certifiable rankings with fewer model calls.

Method: Propose a tournament-graph framework where each k-document comparison yields a complete tournament of binom(k,2) pairwise preferences; aggregate into a global preference graph; compute transitive closure to obtain additional rankings; determine when a candidate's rank is certifiably determined; design a greedy query schedule that maximizes information gain for top-m identification; handle non-transitivity by collapsing cycles into equivalence classes to form tiered rankings.

Result: Across 14 benchmarks and 5 LLMs, the method achieves Pareto dominance over existing reranking approaches, matching or exceeding accuracy while using 25-40% fewer tokens than comparable methods and 7x fewer tokens than pairwise approaches, with near-identical quality.

Conclusion: The framework provides a principled, efficient, and robust reranking strategy that leverages complete pairwise information from k-wise comparisons, yields certifiable rankings, and scales well across models and benchmarks.

Abstract: Large language models have emerged as powerful zero-shot rerankers for retrieval-augmented generation, offering strong generalization without task-specific training. However, existing LLM reranking methods either rely on heuristics that fail to fully exploit the information revealed by each ranking decision or are inefficient when they do. We introduce a tournament graph framework that provides a principled foundation for $k$-wise reranking. Our key observation is that each $k$-document comparison reveals a complete tournament of $\binom{k}{2}$ pairwise preferences. These tournaments are aggregated into a global preference graph, whose transitive closure yields many additional orderings without further model invocations. We formalize when a candidate's rank is certifiably determined and design a query schedule that greedily maximizes information gain towards identifying the top-$m$ items. Our framework also gracefully handles non-transitive preferences - cycles induced by LLM judgments - by collapsing them into equivalence classes that yield principled tiered rankings. Empirically, across 14 benchmarks and 5 LLMs, our method achieves Pareto dominance over existing methods: matching or exceeding accuracy while requiring 25-40% fewer tokens than comparable approaches, and 7$\times$ fewer than pairwise methods at near-identical quality.

</details>


### [221] [When Are RL Hyperparameters Benign? A Study in Offline Goal-Conditioned RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05459)
*Jan Malte Töpperwien,Aditya Mohan,Marius Lindauer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Offline goal-conditioned RL can show robust hyperparameter behavior even under non-stationarity; bootstrapping amplifies sensitivity via destructive gradient interference, with QRL proving more robust than HIQL when modest expert data is present.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Disentangle whether hyperparameter sensitivity in RL is intrinsic or driven by training mechanisms by studying offline data with controllable non-stationarity and comparing two representative algorithms.

Method: Evaluate HIQL (bootstrapped TD-learning) and QRL (quasimetric representation learning) in offline goal-conditioned RL with fixed data distributions and scheduled data-quality shifts, across stationary and non-stationary regimes; introduce an inter-goal gradient alignment diagnostic to explain sensitivity.

Result: QRL maintains broad near-optimal regions once ~20% expert data is available, while HIQL develops sharp optima that drift across training phases; bootstrapped objectives exhibit stronger destructive gradient interference, aligning with higher hyperparameter sensitivity.

Conclusion: Hyperparameter sensitivity in RL is not inevitabile; bootstrapping dynamics amplify it. Designing more robust objectives (e.g., reducing destructive gradient interference) can yield greater hyperparameter robustness, especially in offline settings.

Abstract: Hyperparameter sensitivity in Deep Reinforcement Learning (RL) is often accepted as unavoidable. However, it remains unclear whether it is intrinsic to the RL problem or exacerbated by specific training mechanisms. We investigate this question in offline goal-conditioned RL, where data distributions are fixed, and non-stationarity can be explicitly controlled via scheduled shifts in data quality. Additionally, we study varying data qualities under both stationary and non-stationary regimes, and cover two representative algorithms: HIQL (bootstrapped TD-learning) and QRL (quasimetric representation learning). Overall, we observe substantially greater robustness to changes in hyperparameter configurations than commonly reported for online RL, even under controlled non-stationarity. Once modest expert data is present ($\approx$ 20\%), QRL maintains broad, stable near-optimal regions, while HIQL exhibits sharp optima that drift significantly across training phases. To explain this divergence, we introduce an inter-goal gradient alignment diagnostic. We find that bootstrapped objectives exhibit stronger destructive gradient interference, which coincides directly with hyperparameter sensitivity. These results suggest that high sensitivity to changes in hyperparameter configurations during training is not inevitable in RL, but is amplified by the dynamics of bootstrapping, offering a pathway toward more robust algorithmic objective design.

</details>


### [222] [Thermodynamic Limits of Physical Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05463)
*Koichi Takahashi,Yusuke Hayashi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two bits-per-joule metrics are proposed: Thermodynamic Epiplexity per Joule and Empowerment per Joule, anchored in stochastic thermodynamics and Landauer bounds. The work links information acquisition to energy under explicit boundary assumptions, proposes MDL surrogates for latent structures, and offers a unified reporting framework with guidelines to enable consistent comparisons across AI systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge AI capability with physical efficiency by defining measurable, transfer-agnostic energy-information metrics that capture recognition (model-building) and control (action influence) axes, while clarifying how energy costs constrain information gains.

Method: Theoretical framework grounded in stochastic thermodynamics. Derives a Landauer-scale closed-cycle benchmark for epiplexity under a thermodynamic-learning inequality with explicit subsystem/boundary assumptions. Analyzes how reset/reuse and boundary closure affect the link between information gain and dissipation. Presents a decoupling construction showing information gain and in-boundary dissipation can be uncoupled without those assumptions. Proposes MDL-based surrogates for settings with latent structure unobserved. Proposes a unified efficiency framework with a reporting checklist (boundary/energy accounting, coarse-graining/noise, horizon/reset, cost conventions) and connects to energy-adjusted scaling analyses.

Result: Establishes two complementary metrics: Thermodynamic Epiplexity per Joule (environment-encoded structure in internal state per unit energy) and Empowerment per Joule (sensorimotor channel capacity per energy over a horizon). Clarifies when Landauer costs act as closed-cycle benchmarks and when they do not, depending on boundary conditions; links operational epiplexity to MDL epiplexity when latent variables are unavailable; provides a practical reporting framework with guidelines to enable consistent comparisons.

Conclusion: The proposed dual-metric framework defines two axes of physical intelligence (recognition and control), grounded in stochastic thermodynamics and Landauer limits. It offers a practical, transparent reporting protocol to reduce ambiguity in bits-per-joule comparisons and suggests MDL surrogates for empirical settings, with potential extensions to energy-aware scaling analyses.

Abstract: Modern AI systems achieve remarkable capabilities at the cost of substantial energy consumption. To connect intelligence to physical efficiency, we propose two complementary bits-per-joule metrics under explicit accounting conventions: (1) Thermodynamic Epiplexity per Joule -- bits of structural information about a theoretical environment-instance variable newly encoded in an agent's internal state per unit measured energy within a stated boundary -- and (2) Empowerment per Joule -- the embodied sensorimotor channel capacity (control information) per expected energetic cost over a fixed horizon. These provide two axes of physical intelligence: recognition (model-building) vs.control (action influence). Drawing on stochastic thermodynamics, we show how a Landauer-scale closed-cycle benchmark for epiplexity acquisition follows as a corollary of a standard thermodynamic-learning inequality under explicit subsystem assumptions, and we clarify how Landauer-scaled costs act as closed-cycle benchmarks under explicit reset/reuse and boundary-closure assumptions; conversely, we give a simple decoupling construction showing that without such assumptions -- and without charging for externally prepared low-entropy resources (e.g.fresh memory) crossing the boundary -- information gain and in-boundary dissipation need not be tightly linked. For empirical settings where the latent structure variable is unavailable, we align the operational notion of epiplexity with compute-bounded MDL epiplexity and recommend reporting MDL-epiplexity / compression-gain surrogates as companions. Finally, we propose a unified efficiency framework that reports both metrics together with a minimal checklist of boundary/energy accounting, coarse-graining/noise, horizon/reset, and cost conventions to reduce ambiguity and support consistent bits-per-joule comparisons, and we sketch connections to energy-adjusted scaling analyses.

</details>


### [223] [A Unified Framework for Rethinking Policy Divergence Measures in GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05494)
*Qingyuan Wu,Yuhui Wang,Simon Sinong Zhan,Yanning Dai,Shilong Deng,Sarra Habchi,Qi Zhu,Matthias Gallé,Chao Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified clipping framework for RL in LLMs that reframes policy divergence; introduces KL3-based constraint, equivalent to asymmetric clipping, boosting exploration while maintaining GRPO-style simplicity; improved stability and math reasoning performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RLVR methods rely on clipping likelihood ratios (GRPO family). They can be unstable and may not optimally balance exploration. A principled framework over general policy divergences could yield better stability and performance, and a variance-reduced KL estimator (KL3) may offer practical benefits.

Method: Proposes a unified framework that characterizes policy divergence measures (likelihood ratios, KL, etc.). Identifies KL3 as a variance-reduced Monte Carlo estimator of KL divergence. Proves that KL3-based constraint is mathematically equivalent to an asymmetric ratio-based clipping that reallocates probability mass toward high-confidence actions.

Result: The KL3-based constraint yields stronger exploration while preserving simplicity of GRPO-style methods. Theoretical equivalence is established. Empirical results on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show improved training stability and final performance when KL3 is integrated into GRPO.

Conclusion: Policy divergence constraints are crucial in RLVR for LLMs. The KL3-based constraint offers a principled and effective approach, combining stability with improved exploration, and the unified framework enables systematic analysis across divergence measures.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verified Reward (RLVR) has emerged as a critical paradigm for advancing the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). Most existing RLVR methods, such as GRPO and its variants, ensure stable updates by constraining policy divergence through clipping likelihood ratios. This paper introduces a unified clipping framework that characterizes existing methods via a general notion of policy divergence, encompassing both likelihood ratios and Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergences and extending to alternative measures. The framework provides a principled foundation for systematically analyzing how different policy divergence measures affect exploration and performance. We further identify the KL3 estimator, a variance-reduced Monte Carlo estimator of the KL divergence, as a key policy divergence constraint. We theoretically demonstrate that the KL3-based constraint is mathematically equivalent to an asymmetric ratio-based clipping that reallocates probability mass toward high-confidence actions, promoting stronger exploration while retaining the simplicity of GRPO-style methods. Empirical results on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that incorporating the KL3 estimator into GRPO improves both training stability and final performance, highlighting the importance of principled policy divergence constraints in policy optimization.

</details>


### [224] [Detecting Misbehaviors of Large Vision-Language Models by Evidential Uncertainty Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05535)
*Tao Huang,Rui Wang,Xiaofei Liu,Yi Qin,Li Duan,Liping Jing*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EUQ introduces fine-grained evidential uncertainty quantification for LVLMs, capturing information conflict and ignorance to detect misbehaviors (hallucinations, jailbreaks, adversarial vulnerabilities, OOD) in a single forward pass; it outperforms baselines and provides layer-wise interpretability; code available on GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LVLMs exhibit misbehaviors under incompetent or adversarial inputs due to epistemic uncertainty. Existing methods measure overall uncertainty but fail to detect when knowledge conflicts or when information is absent. A fine-grained, efficient approach is needed for reliable deployment in critical settings.

Method: Interpret model output head features as supporting (positive) or opposing (negative) evidence. Use Evidence Theory to model and aggregate evidence, producing metrics for internal conflict and ignorance in a single forward pass. Evaluate across four misbehavior categories (hallucinations, jailbreaks, adversarial vulnerabilities, OOD). Analyze layer-wise evidential uncertainty dynamics for interpretability.

Result: EUQ outperforms strong baselines across four misbehavior categories, linking hallucinations to high internal conflict and OOD failures to high ignorance. Provides a unified, forward-pass capable uncertainty framework with interpretable layer-wise dynamics. Source code released at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: EUQ offers a practical, effective approach to uncertainty quantification in LVLMs, enabling fine-grained detection of misbehaviors through internal evidence conflict and ignorance, with added interpretability via layer-wise analysis and accessible implementation.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have shown substantial advances in multimodal understanding and generation. However, when presented with incompetent or adversarial inputs, they frequently produce unreliable or even harmful content, such as fact hallucinations or dangerous instructions. This misalignment with human expectations, referred to as \emph{misbehaviors} of LVLMs, raises serious concerns for deployment in critical applications. These misbehaviors are found to stem from epistemic uncertainty, specifically either conflicting internal knowledge or the absence of supporting information. However, existing uncertainty quantification methods, which typically capture only overall epistemic uncertainty, have shown limited effectiveness in identifying such issues. To address this gap, we propose Evidential Uncertainty Quantification (EUQ), a fine-grained method that captures both information conflict and ignorance for effective detection of LVLM misbehaviors. In particular, we interpret features from the model output head as either supporting (positive) or opposing (negative) evidence. Leveraging Evidence Theory, we model and aggregate this evidence to quantify internal conflict and knowledge gaps within a single forward pass. We extensively evaluate our method across four categories of misbehavior, including hallucinations, jailbreaks, adversarial vulnerabilities, and out-of-distribution (OOD) failures, using state-of-the-art LVLMs, and find that EUQ consistently outperforms strong baselines, showing that hallucinations correspond to high internal conflict and OOD failures to high ignorance. Furthermore, layer-wise evidential uncertainty dynamics analysis helps interpret the evolution of internal representations from a new perspective. The source code is available at https://github.com/HT86159/EUQ.

</details>


### [225] [When Shared Knowledge Hurts: Spectral Over-Accumulation in Model Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05536)
*Yayuan Li,Ze Peng,Jian Zhang,Jintao Guo,Yue Duan,Yinghuan Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SVC is a training-free, data-free post-processing that calibrates singular values during model merging to prevent inflation from overlapping task subspaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: When merging fine-tuned models, aligned spectral directions cause repeated accumulation of shared knowledge, inflating singular values and biasing the merged model.

Method: SVC quantifies subspace overlap and rescales inflated singular values to restore a balanced spectrum without retraining or data.

Result: Across vision and language benchmarks, SVC improves merging baselines, achieves state-of-the-art performance, and boosts Task Arithmetic by 13% by modifying only singular values.

Conclusion: SVC provides a simple, effective, and modality-agnostic post-processing to stabilize spectrum during model merging without additional data or training.

Abstract: Model merging combines multiple fine-tuned models into a single model by adding their weight updates, providing a lightweight alternative to retraining. Existing methods primarily target resolving conflicts between task updates, leaving the failure mode of over-counting shared knowledge unaddressed. We show that when tasks share aligned spectral directions (i.e., overlapping singular vectors), a simple linear combination repeatedly accumulates these directions, inflating the singular values and biasing the merged model toward shared subspaces. To mitigate this issue, we propose Singular Value Calibration (SVC), a training-free and data-free post-processing method that quantifies subspace overlap and rescales inflated singular values to restore a balanced spectrum. Across vision and language benchmarks, SVC consistently improves strong merging baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance. Furthermore, by modifying only the singular values, SVC improves the performance of Task Arithmetic by 13.0%. Code is available at: https://github.com/lyymuwu/SVC.

</details>


### [226] [Steering Large Reasoning Models towards Concise Reasoning via Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05539)
*Yawei Li,Benjamin Bergner,Yinghan Zhao,Vihang Prakash Patil,Bei Chen,Cheng Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlowSteer uses nonlinear, distribution-level transport (Flow Matching) to map verbose reasoning activations to concise reasoning, enabling input-dependent, complete distribution shifts; it improves token efficiency and maintains or improves task performance versus linear steering baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global linear shifts are insufficient to capture the complex, high-variance distributional changes required to compress reasoning; a full distributional transport approach can more effectively reduce verbosity while preserving reasoning quality.

Method: Learn a velocity field via Flow Matching to transform the distribution of verbose-reasoning activations into the distribution of concise-reasoning activations at inference time, enabling input-dependent, nonlinear steering of LRMs; compare against leading inference-time baselines across diverse reasoning benchmarks.

Result: FlowSteer achieves more compact (concise) reasoning with improved token efficiency and competitive or superior task performance compared to strong inference-time baselines.

Conclusion: Modeling full distributional transport with generative techniques provides a principled, effective framework for controlling LRMs, enabling concise reasoning without sacrificing capability.

Abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) excel at complex reasoning tasks, but their efficiency is often hampered by overly verbose outputs. Prior steering methods attempt to address this issue by applying a single, global vector to hidden representations -- an approach grounded in the restrictive linear representation hypothesis. In this work, we introduce FlowSteer, a nonlinear steering method that goes beyond uniform linear shifts by learning a complete transformation between the distributions associated with verbose and concise reasoning. This transformation is learned via Flow Matching as a velocity field, enabling precise, input-dependent control over the model's reasoning process. By aligning steered representations with the distribution of concise-reasoning activations, FlowSteer yields more compact reasoning than the linear shifts. Across diverse reasoning benchmarks, FlowSteer demonstrates strong task performance and token efficiency compared to leading inference-time baselines. Our work demonstrates that modeling the full distributional transport with generative techniques offers a more effective and principled foundation for controlling LRMs.

</details>


### [227] [Unveiling Implicit Advantage Symmetry: Why GRPO Struggles with Exploration and Difficulty Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05548)
*Zhiqi Yu,Zhangquan Chen,Mengting Liu,Heye Zhang,Liangqiong Qu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Asymmetric GRAE (A-GRAE) replaces symmetric advantage weighting in Group Relative Advantage Estimation with dynamic, asymmetric incentives and a curriculum-style sampling focus, yielding improved exploration and learning efficiency in RL-based LLM/MLLM reasoning across seven benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address inefficiencies in exploration and difficulty adaptation caused by the implicit symmetry in GRAE, which hampers discovering novel correct solutions and underemphasizes changing sample difficulty.

Method: Introduce A-GRAE, which modulates the relative advantages asymmetrically and shifts emphasis from simpler to harder samples over time (curriculum-like transition). Include dynamic exploration incentives and non-stationary difficulty focus to improve exploration and sample efficiency.

Result: Empirical evaluation across seven benchmarks shows A-GRAE consistently improves GRPO and its variants on both LLMs and MLLMs.

Conclusion: Asymmetric modification of GRAE mitigates symmetry-induced limitations, enhancing exploration and curriculum-driven learning, yielding robust gains across diverse models and tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), particularly GRPO, has become the standard for eliciting LLM reasoning. However, its efficiency in exploration and difficulty adaptation remains an open challenge. In this work, we argue that these bottlenecks stem from an implicit advantage symmetry inherent in Group Relative Advantage Estimation (GRAE). This symmetry induces two critical limitations: (i) at the group level, strict symmetry in weights between correct and incorrect trajectories leaves unsampled action logits unchanged, thereby hindering exploration of novel correct solution. (ii) at the sample level, the algorithm implicitly prioritizes medium-difficulty samples, remaining agnostic to the non-stationary demands of difficulty focus. Through controlled experiments, we reveal that this symmetric property is sub-optimal, yielding two pivotal insights: (i) asymmetrically suppressing the advantages of correct trajectories encourages essential exploration. (ii) learning efficiency is maximized by a curriculum-like transition-prioritizing simpler samples initially before gradually shifting to complex ones. Motivated by these findings, we propose Asymmetric GRAE (A-GRAE), which dynamically modulates exploration incentives and sample-difficulty focus. Experiments across seven benchmarks demonstrate that A-GRAE consistently improves GRPO and its variants across both LLMs and MLLMs.

</details>


### [228] [Logical Guidance for the Exact Composition of Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05549)
*Francesco Alesiani,Jonathan Warrell,Tanja Bien,Henrik Christiansen,Matheus Ferraz,Mathias Niepert*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LOGDIFF enables exact and hybrid logical guidance for diffusion models by modeling constraints as logical circuits. It derives a Boolean calculus that allows exact guidance from atomic scores given conditional independence/disjointness, and compiles common distributions into such circuits. A hybrid approach combining atomic scores with posterior probabilities bridges classifier-guidance and classifier-free guidance, with demonstrated effectiveness on image and protein structure generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide principled, compositional, and exact logical conditioning for diffusion models at inference time, enabling complex logical expressions beyond simple conditioning and addressing the gap between logical constraints and score-based guidance.

Method: 1) Derive an exact Boolean calculus: show that if a logical formula can be represented as a circuit where conjunctions combine conditionally independent subformulas and disjunctions combine subformulas that are either conditionally independent or mutually exclusive, then exact logical guidance can be computed from atomic scores and posterior probabilities via a recursive algorithm. 2) Prove that for common distribution classes any Boolean formula can be compiled into such a circuit. 3) Propose a hybrid guidance approach that combines atomic guidance scores with posterior probability estimates to bridge classifier-guidance and classifier-free guidance, applicable to both compositional logical guidance and standard conditional generation.

Result: The framework enables exact logical guidance from atomic scores and posteriors for formulas that admit the specified circuit representation; any Boolean formula can be compiled into such a circuit for many common distributions; a hybrid guidance method is presented to integrate atomic and posterior information. Empirical validation on multiple image and protein structure generation tasks demonstrates effectiveness of the proposed approach.

Conclusion: LOGDIFF offers a principled, scalable approach to enforce complex logical constraints in diffusion models, providing exact guidance where the circuit conditions hold and a practical hybrid method otherwise, with demonstrated utility in image and protein structure generation.

Abstract: We propose LOGDIFF (Logical Guidance for the Exact Composition of Diffusion Models), a guidance framework for diffusion models that enables principled constrained generation with complex logical expressions at inference time.
  We study when exact score-based guidance for complex logical formulas can be obtained from guidance signals associated with atomic properties.
  First, we derive an exact Boolean calculus that provides a sufficient condition for exact logical guidance.
  Specifically, if a formula admits a circuit representation in which conjunctions combine conditionally independent subformulas and disjunctions combine subformulas that are either conditionally independent or mutually exclusive, exact logical guidance is achievable.
  In this case, the guidance signal can be computed exactly from atomic scores and posterior probabilities using an efficient recursive algorithm.
  Moreover, we show that, for commonly encountered classes of distributions, any desired Boolean formula is compilable into such a circuit representation.
  Second, by combining atomic guidance scores with posterior probability estimates, we introduce a hybrid guidance approach that bridges classifierguidance and classifier-free guidance, applicable to both compositional logical guidance and standard conditional generation.
  We demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework on multiple image and protein structure generation tasks.

</details>


### [229] [MAGPrompt: Message-Adaptive Graph Prompt Tuning for Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05567)
*Long D. Nguyen,Binh P. Nguyen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces message-adaptive graph prompt tuning that injects prompts into the GNN message passing steps, reweighting neighbor messages and adding task-specific prompts during aggregation while keeping the backbone frozen; yields improved few-shot gains and competitive full-shot performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the mismatch between pre-training objectives and downstream tasks and the inefficiency of existing graph prompts that only modify inputs or representations without altering neighborhood interactions; aims for parameter-efficient adaptation.

Method: Injects learnable prompts into the message passing phase to reweight incoming neighbor messages and incorporate task-specific prompt vectors during aggregation, with the backbone GNN frozen. Compatible with common GNN backbones and pre-training strategies, applicable across node- and graph-level downstream tasks.

Result: Experiments on diverse node- and graph-level datasets show consistent gains over prior graph prompting methods in few-shot settings, while achieving performance competitive with fine-tuning in full-shot regimes.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective, parameter-efficient adaptation of frozen GNNs by modulating neighborhood interactions through message-passing prompts, with broad applicability across downstream tasks.

Abstract: Pre-trained graph neural networks (GNNs) transfer well, but adapting them to downstream tasks remains challenging due to mismatches between pre-training objectives and task requirements. Graph prompt tuning offers a parameter-efficient alternative to fine-tuning, yet most methods only modify inputs or representations and leave message passing unchanged, limiting their ability to adapt neighborhood interactions. We propose message-adaptive graph prompt tuning, which injects learnable prompts into the message passing step to reweight incoming neighbor messages and add task-specific prompt vectors during message aggregation, while keeping the backbone GNN frozen. The approach is compatible with common GNN backbones and pre-training strategies, and applicable across downstream settings. Experiments on diverse node- and graph-level datasets show consistent gains over prior graph prompting methods in few-shot settings, while achieving performance competitive with fine-tuning in full-shot regimes.

</details>


### [230] [EdgeMask-DG*: Learning Domain-Invariant Graph Structures via Adversarial Edge Masking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05571)
*Rishabh Bhattacharya,Naresh Manwani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes EdgeMask-DG and EdgeMask-DG* that use adversarial edge masking and feature-enriched graphs to identify domain-invariant structural information for graph domain generalization, achieving state-of-the-art results on diverse benchmarks (Cora OOD: 78.0% worst-case).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Structural shifts cause graphs to vary across domains; existing DG methods rely on fixed augmentations or global perturbations that miss which edges carry domain-invariant signals. Domain-invariant structure is hypothesized to lie in consensus across multiple topologies derived from topology and feature similarity.

Method: EdgeMask-DG uses a min–max adversarial edge masker that learns worst-case continuous masks under a sparsity constraint, forcing a GNN to perform well under perturbations. EdgeMask-DG* extends this to a second enriched graph that combines the original topology with feature-derived edges, enabling discovery of invariances under noisy/domain-specific topologies. This constitutes adaptive adversarial topology search on feature-enriched graphs, with a formal robust optimization justification.

Result: Achieves new state-of-the-art in graph DG benchmarks across citation, social, and temporal networks. On Cora OOD, EdgeMask-DG* attains 78.0% worst-case domain accuracy, +3.8 percentage points over the previous best (74.2%).

Conclusion: Provides a robust optimization justification for learning domain-invariant structure via adaptive adversarial topology search over enriched graphs; first to systematically combine adversarial topology masking with feature-enriched graphs; code available.

Abstract: Structural shifts pose a significant challenge for graph neural networks, as graph topology acts as a covariate that can vary across domains. Existing domain generalization methods rely on fixed structural augmentations or training on globally perturbed graphs, mechanisms that do not pinpoint which specific edges encode domain-invariant information. We argue that domain-invariant structural information is not rigidly tied to a single topology but resides in the consensus across multiple graph structures derived from topology and feature similarity. To capture this, we first propose EdgeMask-DG, a novel min-max algorithm where an edge masker learns to find worst-case continuous masks subject to a sparsity constraint, compelling a task GNN to perform effectively under these adversarial structural perturbations. Building upon this, we introduce EdgeMask-DG*, an extension that applies this adversarial masking principle to an enriched graph. This enriched graph combines the original topology with feature-derived edges, allowing the model to discover invariances even when the original topology is noisy or domain-specific. EdgeMask-DG* is the first to systematically combine adaptive adversarial topology search with feature-enriched graphs. We provide a formal justification for our approach from a robust optimization perspective. We demonstrate that EdgeMask-DG* achieves new state-of-the-art performance on diverse graph domain generalization benchmarks, including citation networks, social networks, and temporal graphs. Notably, on the Cora OOD benchmark, EdgeMask-DG* lifts the worst-case domain accuracy to 78.0\%, a +3.8 pp improvement over the prior state of the art (74.2\%). The source code for our experiments can be found here: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TMLR-EAEF/

</details>


### [231] [OpenMAG: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Multimodal-Attributed Graph](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05576)
*Chenxi Wan,Xunkai Li,Yilong Zuo,Haokun Deng,Sihan Li,Bowen Fan,Hongchao Qin,Ronghua Li,Guoren Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OpenMAG offers a unified, large-scale MAG benchmark with 19 datasets, 6 domains, 16 encoders, 24 models, and 8 tasks to enable fair evaluation and systematic insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MAG benchmarks lack comprehensive domain coverage, encoder flexibility, model diversity, and broad task scope, hindering fair and thorough evaluation of MAG methods.

Method: Assemble OpenMAG by collecting 19 datasets across 6 domains; include 16 encoders (static and trainable feature encoding); standardize a library of 24 SOTA models; support 8 downstream tasks; evaluate along axes of necessity, data quality, effectiveness, robustness, efficiency; release code.

Result: Derived 14 fundamental insights into MAG learning from systematic evaluation; OpenMAG enables fair, unified comparisons.

Conclusion: OpenMAG fills critical evaluation gaps in MAG research and provides tooling and benchmarks to guide future development.

Abstract: Multimodal-Attributed Graph (MAG) learning has achieved remarkable success in modeling complex real-world systems by integrating graph topology with rich attributes from multiple modalities. With the rapid proliferation of novel MAG models capable of handling intricate cross-modal semantics and structural dependencies, establishing a rigorous and unified evaluation standard has become imperative. Although existing benchmarks have facilitated initial progress, they exhibit critical limitations in domain coverage, encoder flexibility, model diversity, and task scope, presenting significant challenges to fair evaluation. To bridge this gap, we present OpenMAG, a comprehensive benchmark that integrates 19 datasets across 6 domains and incorporates 16 encoders to support both static and trainable feature encoding. OpenMAG further implements a standardized library of 24 state-of-the-art models and supports 8 downstream tasks, enabling fair comparisons within a unified framework. Through systematic assessment of necessity, data quality, effectiveness, robustness, and efficiency, we derive 14 fundamental insights into MAG learning to guide future advancements. Our code is available at https://github.com/YUKI-N810/OpenMAG.

</details>


### [232] [On the Superlinear Relationship between SGD Noise Covariance and Loss Landscape Curvature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05600)
*Yikuan Zhang,Ning Yang,Yuhai Tu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SGD noise covariance C is not simply proportional to the Hessian H; instead C ∝ E_p[h_p^2] (per-sample Hessians), leading to approximate commutativity with H and a diagonal C_ii ∝ H_ii^γ with 1 ≤ γ ≤ 2. This bound is set by the per-sample Hessian spectra and holds across datasets, architectures, and loss functions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify the relationship between SGD-induced noise and curvature in deep nets, challenging the common Fisher-Hessian equivalence for negative log-likelihood losses and establishing a loss-agnostic, spectrum-driven link between C and H.

Method: Leverage Activity–Weight Duality to derive C ∝ E_p[h_p^2] with H = E_p[h_p], independent of the specific loss formulation. Analyze commutativity and diagonal power-law relation; bound exponent γ by per-sample Hessian spectra. Validate theoretically and empirically across diverse tasks.

Result: Demonstrates that C and H commute approximately (not exactly) and that diagonal terms satisfy C_ii ∝ H_ii^γ with 1 ≤ γ ≤ 2. The exponent bound is determined by the distribution of per-sample Hessian eigenvalues. Empirical results across datasets, architectures, and losses confirm these bounds.

Conclusion: Provides a unified, loss-agnostic characterization of the SGD noise–curvature relationship in deep learning, explaining anisotropic noise patterns and their bias toward flatter directions. Highlights when Fisher–Hessian equivalence fails and underscores the role of per-sample curvature in shaping SGD dynamics.

Abstract: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) introduces anisotropic noise that is correlated with the local curvature of the loss landscape, thereby biasing optimization toward flat minima. Prior work often assumes an equivalence between the Fisher Information Matrix and the Hessian for negative log-likelihood losses, leading to the claim that the SGD noise covariance $\mathbf{C}$ is proportional to the Hessian $\mathbf{H}$. We show that this assumption holds only under restrictive conditions that are typically violated in deep neural networks. Using the recently discovered Activity--Weight Duality, we find a more general relationship agnostic to the specific loss formulation, showing that $\mathbf{C} \propto \mathbb{E}_p[\mathbf{h}_p^2]$, where $\mathbf{h}_p$ denotes the per-sample Hessian with $\mathbf{H} = \mathbb{E}_p[\mathbf{h}_p]$. As a consequence, $\mathbf{C}$ and $\mathbf{H}$ commute approximately rather than coincide exactly, and their diagonal elements follow an approximate power-law relation $C_{ii} \propto H_{ii}^γ$ with a theoretically bounded exponent $1 \leq γ\leq 2$, determined by per-sample Hessian spectra. Experiments across datasets, architectures, and loss functions validate these bounds, providing a unified characterization of the noise-curvature relationship in deep learning.

</details>


### [233] [Shiva-DiT: Residual-Based Differentiable Top-$k$ Selection for Efficient Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05605)
*Jiaji Zhang,Hailiang Zhao,Guoxuan Zhu,Ruichao Sun,Jiaju Wu,Xinkui Zhao,Hanlin Tang,Weiyi Lu,Kan Liu,Tao Lan,Lin Qu,Shuiguang Deng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Shiva-DiT introduces a differentiable, static-budget-friendly token pruning method for diffusion transformers using residual-based differentiable top-k selection, achieving faster inference with preserved fidelity and reduced ragged tensor overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quadratic self-attention in Diffusion Transformers leads to high computational costs. Existing pruning methods struggle to be differentiable, efficient, and compatible with strict static hardware budgets. A technique is needed that enables end-to-end learning while yielding deterministic token counts suitable for static compilation.

Method: Residual-based differentiable top-k selection with a residual-aware straight-through estimator to enforce deterministic token counts; Context-Aware Router and Adaptive Ratio Policy to autonomously learn an adaptive pruning schedule; end-to-end learnability preserved via residual gradient estimation.

Result: On mainstream models including SD3.5, Shiva-DiT achieves a new Pareto frontier: 1.54x wall-clock speedup with superior fidelity relative to baselines and effectively eliminates ragged tensor overheads.

Conclusion: Shiva-DiT successfully reconciles differentiability, efficiency, and static hardware budgets for diffusion transformers, delivering faster inference without sacrificing accuracy and removing ragged-tensor overheads.

Abstract: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) incur prohibitive computational costs due to the quadratic scaling of self-attention. Existing pruning methods fail to simultaneously satisfy differentiability, efficiency, and the strict static budgets required for hardware overhead. To address this, we propose Shiva-DiT, which effectively reconciles these conflicting requirements via Residual-Based Differentiable Top-$k$ Selection. By leveraging a residual-aware straight-through estimator, our method enforces deterministic token counts for static compilation while preserving end-to-end learnability through residual gradient estimation. Furthermore, we introduce a Context-Aware Router and Adaptive Ratio Policy to autonomously learn an adaptive pruning schedule. Experiments on mainstream models, including SD3.5, demonstrate that Shiva-DiT establishes a new Pareto frontier, achieving a 1.54$\times$ wall-clock speedup with superior fidelity compared to existing baselines, effectively eliminating ragged tensor overheads.

</details>


### [234] [Path-Guided Flow Matching for Dataset Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05616)
*Xuhui Li,Zhengquan Luo,Xiwei Liu,Yongqiang Yu,Zhiqiang Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Path-Guided Flow Matching (PGFM), a flow-matching-based generative distillation framework operating in a frozen VAE latent space to produce fast, deterministic synthesis via a small-step ODE, achieving competitive or superior performance with markedly improved efficiency over diffusion-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based dataset distillation methods face sampling time overhead, trajectory instability, and degraded generalization under strong control or low hardware throughput; a fast, stable, deterministic alternative is needed.

Method: Train a flow-matching model in the latent space of a frozen variational autoencoder to perform class-conditional transport from Gaussian noise to data; introduce a continuous path-to-prototype guidance algorithm for ODE-consistent path control, ensuring trajectories land on assigned prototypes while maintaining diversity and efficiency.

Result: Empirical evaluation on high-resolution benchmarks shows PGFM matches or surpasses diffusion-based distillation in performance while requiring fewer sampling steps; achieves up to 7.6x efficiency gains and 78% mode coverage, demonstrating improved efficiency with competitive accuracy.

Conclusion: PGFM is the first flow-matching-based framework for generative distillation, enabling fast, deterministic synthesis and improved efficiency, with strong performance on high-resolution datasets and potential to generalize across distillation settings.

Abstract: Dataset distillation compresses large datasets into compact synthetic sets with comparable performance in training models. Despite recent progress on diffusion-based distillation, this type of method typically depends on heuristic guidance or prototype assignment, which comes with time-consuming sampling and trajectory instability and thus hurts downstream generalization especially under strong control or low IPC. We propose \emph{Path-Guided Flow Matching (PGFM)}, the first flow matching-based framework for generative distillation, which enables fast deterministic synthesis by solving an ODE in a few steps. PGFM conducts flow matching in the latent space of a frozen VAE to learn class-conditional transport from Gaussian noise to data distribution. Particularly, we develop a continuous path-to-prototype guidance algorithm for ODE-consistent path control, which allows trajectories to reliably land on assigned prototypes while preserving diversity and efficiency. Extensive experiments across high-resolution benchmarks demonstrate that PGFM matches or surpasses prior diffusion-based distillation approaches with fewer steps of sampling while delivering competitive performance with remarkably improved efficiency, e.g., 7.6$\times$ more efficient than the diffusion-based counterparts with 78\% mode coverage.

</details>


### [235] [Mode-Dependent Rectification for Stable PPO Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05619)
*Mohamad Mohamad,Francesco Ponzio,Xavier Descombes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mode-Dependent Rectification (MDR) stabilizes PPO when using mode-dependent layers (e.g., BatchNorm, dropout) by a lightweight dual-phase training procedure, avoiding architectural changes and improving stability and performance across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mode-dependent components cause training-evaluation discrepancies in on-policy RL, leading to policy mismatch, distributional drift, and reward collapse, particularly in PPO.

Method: Introduce MDR, a dual-phase training procedure that rectifies mode-dependent behavior during training vs. evaluation without altering the network architecture.

Result: MDR consistently improves stability and performance across procedurally generated games and real-world patch-localization tasks and generalizes to other mode-dependent layers.

Conclusion: MDR provides a simple, effective, architecture-free remedy for mode-dependent layer instability in PPO, with broad applicability to other mode-dependent components.

Abstract: Mode-dependent architectural components (layers that behave differently during training and evaluation, such as Batch Normalization or dropout) are commonly used in visual reinforcement learning but can destabilize on-policy optimization. We show that in Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), discrepancies between training and evaluation behavior induced by Batch Normalization lead to policy mismatch, distributional drift, and reward collapse. We propose Mode-Dependent Rectification (MDR), a lightweight dual-phase training procedure that stabilizes PPO under mode-dependent layers without architectural changes. Experiments across procedurally generated games and real-world patch-localization tasks demonstrate that MDR consistently improves stability and performance, and extends naturally to other mode-dependent layers.

</details>


### [236] [Rewards as Labels: Revisiting RLVR from a Classification Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05630)
*Zepeng Zhai,Meilin Chen,Jiaxuan Zhao,Junlang Qian,Lei Shen,Yuan Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: REAL reframes verifiable rewards as categorical labels, turning policy optimization into a classification problem and using anchor logits to stabilize learning, achieving consistent gains over GRPO, DAPO, and GSPO across 1.5B and 7B LMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To fix gradient misassignment for positives and gradient domination for negatives observed in RLVR methods (e.g., GRPO), which impairs efficient and balanced policy updates in LLM reasoning tasks.

Method: Introduce Rewards as Labels (REAL) to treat verifiable rewards as categorical labels rather than scalar weights, reformulating policy optimization as a classification task. Augment with anchor logits to stabilize learning and ensure monotonic, bounded gradient weighting; even a vanilla binary cross-entropy can be effective.

Result: REAL improves Pass@1 on mathematical reasoning benchmarks: +6.7% over DAPO at 1.5B; at 7B, +6.2% vs DAPO and +1.7% vs GSPO. Realizable gains with vanilla BCE: +4.5% over DAPO. Overall, REAL enhances training stability and outperforms strong RLVR variants across scales.

Conclusion: REAL provides a safer and more effective gradient mechanism by framing verifiable rewards as labels, achieving consistent performance gains and stability in RLVR for LLMs, with anchor logits contributing to learning; further evaluation on broader domains and ablations could solidify its generality.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards has recently advanced the capabilities of Large Language Models in complex reasoning tasks by providing explicit rule-based supervision. Among RLVR methods, GRPO and its variants have achieved strong empirical performance. Despite their success, we identify that they suffer from Gradient Misassignment in Positives and Gradient Domination in Negatives, which lead to inefficient and suboptimal policy updates. To address these issues, we propose Rewards as Labels (REAL), a novel framework that revisits verifiable rewards as categorical labels rather than scalar weights, thereby reformulating policy optimization as a classification problem. Building on this, we further introduce anchor logits to enhance policy learning. Our analysis reveals that REAL induces a monotonic and bounded gradient weighting, enabling balanced gradient allocation across rollouts and effectively mitigating the identified mismatches. Extensive experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that REAL improves training stability and consistently outperforms GRPO and strong variants such as DAPO. On the 1.5B model, REAL improves average Pass@1 over DAPO by 6.7%. These gains further scale to 7B model, REAL continues to outperform DAPO and GSPO by 6.2% and 1.7%, respectively. Notably, even with a vanilla binary cross-entropy, REAL remains stable and exceeds DAPO by 4.5% on average.

</details>


### [237] [Structural Disentanglement in Bilinear MLPs via Architectural Inductive Bias](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05635)
*Ojasva Nema,Kaustubh Sharma,Aditya Chauhan,Parikshit Pareek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bilinear (multiplicative) MLPs induce a non-mixing, orthogonal-subspace representation under gradient flow, enabling reliable structural disentanglement, unlearning, and long-horizon extrapolation; theory supported by experiments across algebraic tasks, showing multiplicative architectures better recover true operators than pointwise nonlinear networks, highlighting architecture as a key factor in model editability and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Selective unlearning and long-horizon extrapolation fail in modern networks, possibly due to how representations are learned. The paper investigates whether an architectural inductive bias—explicit multiplicative interactions via bilinear parameterizations—aids structural disentanglement and reliable model editing.

Method: 1) Analytical proof that bilinear parameterizations exhibit a non-mixing property under gradient flow, leading to orthogonal decomposition of functional components. 2) Controlled experiments on tasks with underlying algebraic structure (modular arithmetic, cyclic reasoning, Lie group dynamics) and unlearning benchmarks. 3) Comparison with pointwise nonlinear networks to assess ability to recover true operators aligned with the algebraic structure.

Result: The bilinear parameterization yields a non-mixing dynamic that separates components into orthogonal subspaces; multiplicative architectures more accurately recover operators consistent with the underlying algebraic structure; these methods enable surgical model modifications and improve reliability of unlearning; experiments across modular arithmetic, cyclic reasoning, Lie group dynamics, and unlearning benchmarks support the claims.

Conclusion: Architectural inductive bias, via multiplicative (bilinear) interactions, shapes representational structure in a way that enhances editability and generalization. This provides a mathematical foundation for surgical interventions and suggests that preserving/learning true algebraic operators is more feasible with bilinear architectures than with standard pointwise nonlinear networks.

Abstract: Selective unlearning and long-horizon extrapolation remain fragile in modern neural networks, even when tasks have underlying algebraic structure. In this work, we argue that these failures arise not solely from optimization or unlearning algorithms, but from how models structure their internal representations during training. We explore if having explicit multiplicative interactions as an architectural inductive bias helps in structural disentanglement, through Bilinear MLPs. We show analytically that bilinear parameterizations possess a `non-mixing' property under gradient flow conditions, where functional components separate into orthogonal subspace representations. This provides a mathematical foundation for surgical model modification. We validate this hypothesis through a series of controlled experiments spanning modular arithmetic, cyclic reasoning, Lie group dynamics, and targeted unlearning benchmarks. Unlike pointwise nonlinear networks, multiplicative architectures are able to recover true operators aligned with the underlying algebraic structure. Our results suggest that model editability and generalization are constrained by representational structure, and that architectural inductive bias plays a central role in enabling reliable unlearning.

</details>


### [238] [Joint Embedding Variational Bayes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05639)
*Amin Oji,Paul Fieguth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Variational Joint Embedding (VJE): a self-supervised probabilistic representation learning framework that combines joint embedding with variational inference, using a heavy-tailed likelihood and polar decomposition to stabilize training, achieving competitive results on standard benchmarks and improved anomaly detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill a gap in self-supervised learning by embedding probabilistic semantics into representations without reconstruction or contrastive losses, addressing norm-induced instabilities and enabling uncertainty quantification through a variational posterior.

Method: Define a latent-variable model on encoder embeddings and maximize a symmetric conditional ELBO. Use a heavy-tailed Student-t likelihood with polar decomposition to decouple direction and magnitude. Employ an amortized inference network producing a diagonal Gaussian variational posterior whose variances are tied to the likelihood scale, enabling anisotropic uncertainty without projection heads.

Result: On ImageNet-1K, CIFAR-10/100, and STL-10, VJE matches standard non-contrastive baselines under linear and k-NN evaluation. In one-class CIFAR-10 anomaly detection, likelihood-based scoring with VJE outperforms comparable self-supervised baselines.

Conclusion: VJE provides a probabilistic framing for self-supervised embeddings, offering training stability via polar decomposition, explicit uncertainty modeling, and competitive performance across classification and anomaly detection benchmarks.

Abstract: We introduce Variational Joint Embedding (VJE), a framework that synthesizes joint embedding and variational inference to enable self-supervised learning of probabilistic representations in a reconstruction-free, non-contrastive setting. Compared to energy-based predictive objectives that optimize pointwise discrepancies, VJE maximizes a symmetric conditional evidence lower bound (ELBO) for a latent-variable model defined directly on encoder embeddings. We instantiate the conditional likelihood with a heavy-tailed Student-$t$ model using a polar decomposition that explicitly decouples directional and radial factors to prevent norm-induced instabilities during training. VJE employs an amortized inference network to parameterize a diagonal Gaussian variational posterior whose feature-wise variances are shared with the likelihood scale to capture anisotropic uncertainty without auxiliary projection heads. Across ImageNet-1K, CIFAR-10/100, and STL-10, VJE achieves performance comparable to standard non-contrastive baselines under linear and k-NN evaluation. We further validate these probabilistic semantics through one-class CIFAR-10 anomaly detection, where likelihood-based scoring under the proposed model outperforms comparable self-supervised baselines.

</details>


### [239] [Empowering Time Series Analysis with Large-Scale Multimodal Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05646)
*Peng Chen,Siyuan Wang,Shiyan Hu,Xingjian Wu,Yang Shu,Zhongwen Rao,Meng Wang,Yijie Li,Bin Yang,Chenjuan Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes multimodal pretraining for time series using endogenous modalities (images/text) and exogenous knowledge (news); introduces MM-TS, a large-scale multimodal time series dataset (up to 1B points), and HORAI, a frequency-enhanced multimodal foundation model with a Cross-Modality Encoder and a Time-Frequency Decoder; reports state-of-the-art zero-shot performance in forecasting and anomaly detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of a unified multimodal pretraining paradigm for time series and to improve generalization by integrating heterogeneous modalities.

Method: Automated data construction of MM-TS (six domains, up to 1B points). HORAI architecture with a Frequency-enhanced Cross-Modality Encoder and a Time-Frequency Decoder for fusing multimodal features and enhancing generalization; pretraining on MM-TS followed by zero-shot evaluation.

Result: HORAI achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on time series forecasting and anomaly detection, demonstrating strong cross-modal and cross-domain generalization.

Conclusion: The work establishes a feasible multimodal foundation-model paradigm for time series, introduces a large-scale dataset, and presents a model that generalizes across modalities and domains, enabling broader multimodal time-series understanding.

Abstract: While existing time series foundation models primarily rely on large-scale unimodal pretraining, they lack complementary modalities to enhance time series understanding. Building multimodal foundation models is a natural next step, but it faces key challenges: 1) lack of a unified multimodal pretraining paradigm and large-scale multimodal corpora for time series analysis; 2) how to effectively integrate heterogeneous modalities and enhance model generalization. To address these challenges, we take an early step toward multimodal foundation models for time series analysis. We first propose a multimodal pretraining paradigm that leverages time series with endogenous modalities (derived images and text) and exogenous knowledge (real-world news), providing a comprehensive multi-view perspective for time series analysis. To support this, we develop an automated data construction pipeline to curate MM-TS, the first large-scale multimodal time series dataset spanning six domains, with up to one billion points. Then we propose HORAI, a frequency-enhanced multimodal foundation model. It integrates two core components: the Frequency-enhanced Cross-Modality Encoder and the Time-Frequency Decoder, designed to effectively fuse multimodal features and enhance model generalization across modalities and domains. After pretraining on MM-TS, HORAI achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on time series forecasting and anomaly detection tasks, demonstrating strong generalization.

</details>


### [240] [End-to-End Compression for Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05649)
*Guri Zabërgja,Rafiq Kamel,Arlind Kadra,Christian M. M. Frey,Josif Grabocka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TACO: an end-to-end tabular compression model that compresses training data into a latent space to enable fast, memory-efficient in-context predictions; achieves up to 94x faster inference and up to 97% memory reduction on TabArena with little to no accuracy loss, outperforming tabular transformers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In-context learning for tabular data faces quadratic-scale attention costs in transformers; gradient-boosted trees still strong, but there's a need for scalable, efficient tabular foundation models that can handle large datasets.

Method: Proposes TACO, an end-to-end tabular compression model that encodes the training dataset into a latent representation, enabling forward-pass inference using the compressed data; tested on TabArena; reports speedups and memory reductions with maintained accuracy.

Result: On TabArena, up to 94x faster inference and up to 97% less memory usage compared to state-of-the-art tabular transformer; maintains performance with no significant degradation; scales better with larger datasets and surpasses baselines.

Conclusion: Latent-space compression for tabular data offers substantial efficiency gains and competitive accuracy, suggesting a viable path for scalable tabular foundation models beyond conventional transformers.

Abstract: The long-standing dominance of gradient-boosted decision trees for tabular data has recently been challenged by in-context learning tabular foundation models. In-context learning methods fit and predict in one forward pass without parameter updates by leveraging the training data as context for predicting on query test points. While recent tabular foundation models achieve state-of-the-art performance, their transformer architecture based on the attention mechanism has quadratic complexity regarding dataset size, which in turn increases the overhead on training and inference time, and limits the capacity of the models to handle large-scale datasets. In this work, we propose TACO, an end-to-end tabular compression model that compresses the training dataset in a latent space.
