We test our method on the TabArena benchmark, where our proposed method is up to 94x faster in inference time, while consuming up to 97\% less memory compared to the state-of-the-art tabular transformer architecture, all while retaining performance without significant degradation. Lastly, our method not only scales better with increased dataset sizes, but it also achieves better performance compared to other baselines.

</details>


### [241] [Alignment Verifiability in Large Language Models: Normative Indistinguishability under Behavioral Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05656)
*Igor Santos-Grueiro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Framing LLM alignment evaluation as an identifiability problem under partial observability; shows finite behavioral tests cannot uniquely identify latent alignment; benchmarks provide bounds, not certainties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridges the gap between observable behavior and latent alignment by treating evaluation as an inference problem under limited, regime-influenced signals; clarifies why current benchmarks may mislead about true alignment.

Method: Formalizes the Alignment Verifiability Problem and Normative Indistinguishability; proves a negative yet sharply delimited identifiability result under finite evaluation and evaluation-aware agents.

Result: Observed compliance does not uniquely identify latent alignment; alignment tests are estimators of indistinguishability classes rather than verifiers of alignment; passing stricter tests narrows the compatible hypothesis space but cannot yield a single correct latent alignment.

Conclusion: Benchmarks should be interpreted as upper bounds on observable compliance within a regime and as estimators of equivalence classes of latent alignment, rather than definitive verifications; this reframes evaluation as bounding the space of plausible alignments.

Abstract: Behavioral evaluation is the dominant paradigm for assessing alignment in large language models (LLMs). In practice, alignment is inferred from performance under finite evaluation protocols - benchmarks, red-teaming suites, or automated pipelines - and observed compliance is often treated as evidence of underlying alignment. This inference step, from behavioral evidence to claims about latent alignment properties, is typically implicit and rarely analyzed as an inference problem in its own right.
  We study this problem formally. We frame alignment evaluation as an identifiability question under partial observability and allow agent behavior to depend on information correlated with the evaluation regime. Within this setting, we introduce the Alignment Verifiability Problem and the notion of Normative Indistinguishability, capturing when distinct latent alignment hypotheses induce identical distributions over all evaluator-accessible signals.
  Our main result is a negative but sharply delimited identifiability theorem. Under finite behavioral evaluation and evaluation-aware agents, observed behavioral compliance does not uniquely identify latent alignment. That is, even idealized behavioral evaluation cannot, in general, certify alignment as a latent property.
  We further show that behavioral alignment tests should be interpreted as estimators of indistinguishability classes rather than verifiers of alignment. Passing increasingly stringent tests may reduce the space of compatible hypotheses, but cannot collapse it to a singleton under the stated conditions. This reframes alignment benchmarks as providing upper bounds on observable compliance within a regime, rather than guarantees of underlying alignment.

</details>


### [242] [Tight Long-Term Tail Decay of (Clipped) SGD in Non-Convex Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05657)
*Aleksandar Armacki,Dragana Bajović,Dušan Jakovetić,Soummya Kar,Ali H. Sayed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies long-term tail decay of SGD- and clipped SGD-induced processes using large deviations, establishing sharp exponential tail bounds for the gradient norm of the best iterate in non-convex settings under bounded noise and heavy-tailed noise, with rates e^{-t/log t} and e^{-t^{β_p}/log t} respectively, and matching lower bounds e^{-t} up to polylog factors, improving over prior finite-time bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in tail-risk analysis for SGD: quantify probability of failure for a fixed error threshold and capture true long-term tail behavior over millions of iterations, enabling stronger per-run guarantees in modern ML training.

Method: Apply large deviations theory to SGD dynamics; derive asymptotic tail bounds for the gradient norm-squared of the best iterate under two noise regimes (bounded noise and clipped SGD with heavy-tailed noise p in (1,2]); compute decay exponents and prove lower bounds showing tightness up to polylog factors.

Result: Upper tail bounds with decay rates e^{-t/log t} for SGD with bounded noise; e^{-t^{β_p}/log t} for c-SGD with heavy-tailed noise (β_p as defined; p in (1,2]; and e^{-t/log^2 t} for p=2); matching lower bound e^{-t} (up to polylog); shows significant improvement over finite-time tail bounds (e^{-√t}, e^{-t^{β_p/2}}).

Conclusion: Long-term tail behavior of SGD-based methods is governed by large deviations, yielding much faster tail decay than previously known, thus providing stronger long-run guarantees for individual runs; results delineate regimes where tails decay rapidly and indicate tightness.

Abstract: The study of tail behaviour of SGD-induced processes has been attracting a lot of interest, due to offering strong guarantees with respect to individual runs of an algorithm. While many works provide high-probability guarantees, quantifying the error rate for a fixed probability threshold, there is a lack of work directly studying the probability of failure, i.e., quantifying the tail decay rate for a fixed error threshold. Moreover, existing results are of finite-time nature, limiting their ability to capture the true long-term tail decay which is more informative for modern learning models, typically trained for millions of iterations. Our work closes these gaps, by studying the long-term tail decay of SGD-based methods through the lens of large deviations theory, establishing several strong results in the process. First, we provide an upper bound on the tails of the gradient norm-squared of the best iterate produced by (vanilla) SGD, for non-convex costs and bounded noise, with long-term decay at rate $e^{-t/\log(t)}$. Next, we relax the noise assumption by considering clipped SGD (c-SGD) under heavy-tailed noise with bounded moment of order $p \in (1,2]$, showing an upper bound with long-term decay at rate $e^{-t^{β_p}/\log(t)}$, where $β_p = \frac{4(p-1)}{3p-2}$ for $p \in (1,2)$ and $e^{-t/\log^2(t)}$ for $p = 2$. Finally, we provide lower bounds on the tail decay, at rate $e^{-t}$, showing that our rates for both SGD and c-SGD are tight, up to poly-logarithmic factors. Notably, our results demonstrate an order of magnitude faster long-term tail decay compared to existing work based on finite-time bounds, which show rates $e^{-\sqrt{t}}$ and $e^{-t^{β_p/2}}$, $p \in (1,2]$, for SGD and c-SGD, respectively. As such, we uncover regimes where the tails decay much faster than previously known, providing stronger long-term guarantees for individual runs.

</details>


### [243] [Probabilistic Multi-Regional Solar Power Forecasting with Any-Quantile Recurrent Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05660)
*Slawek Smyl,Paweł Pełka,Grzegorz Dudek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces AQ-RNN for any-quantile probabilistic forecasting of multi-regional PV; achieves calibrated quantiles across probability levels within a single model and leverages spatial-temporal cross-region information for robust system-level forecasts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PV penetration increases uncertainty in power systems; deterministic point forecasts are insufficient. A probabilistic, cross-regional forecasting approach with calibrated intervals can improve decision-making and reliability.

Method: Any-Quantile Recurrent Neural Network (AQ-RNN) with a dual-track architecture processing series-specific and cross-regional context, enhanced by dilated recurrent cells, patch-based temporal modeling, and a dynamic ensemble mechanism. Outputs calibrated conditional quantiles at arbitrary probability levels from a single trained model.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 30 years of hourly PV data from 259 European regions; benchmarked against statistical and neural probabilistic baselines; the framework achieves consistent improvements in forecast accuracy, calibration, and prediction interval quality, supporting uncertainty-aware energy management and operations.

Conclusion: AQ-RNN is well-suited for uncertainty-aware, multi-regional PV forecasting, enabling coherent probabilistic forecasts of varying quantiles within one model and leveraging spatial dependencies to improve system-level robustness.

Abstract: The increasing penetration of photovoltaic (PV) generation introduces significant uncertainty into power system operation, necessitating forecasting approaches that extend beyond deterministic point predictions. This paper proposes an any-quantile probabilistic forecasting framework for multi-regional PV power generation based on the Any-Quantile Recurrent Neural Network (AQ-RNN). The model integrates an any-quantile forecasting paradigm with a dual-track recurrent architecture that jointly processes series-specific and cross-regional contextual information, supported by dilated recurrent cells, patch-based temporal modeling, and a dynamic ensemble mechanism.
  The proposed framework enables the estimation of calibrated conditional quantiles at arbitrary probability levels within a single trained model and effectively exploits spatial dependencies to enhance robustness at the system level. The approach is evaluated using 30 years of hourly PV generation data from 259 European regions and compared against established statistical and neural probabilistic baselines. The results demonstrate consistent improvements in forecast accuracy, calibration, and prediction interval quality, underscoring the suitability of the proposed method for uncertainty-aware energy management and operational decision-making in renewable-dominated power systems.

</details>


### [244] [Accelerating Benchmarking of Functional Connectivity Modeling via Structure-aware Core-set Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05667)
*Ling Zhan,Zhen Li,Junjie Huang,Tao Jia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes SCLCS to select a small, ranking-preserving core-set for FC benchmarking; achieves 10% data with preserved ranking on REST-meta-MDD, outperforming SOTA by up to 23.2% in nDCG@k.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmark hundreds of FC modeling methods on large-scale fMRI datasets is computationally prohibitive; need a core-set that preserves relative performance rankings to enable scalable benchmarking.

Method: Adaptive Transformer-based representation learning of FC structure per sample; Structural Perturbation Score (SPS) to quantify stability; top-k ranking to identify stable samples; density-balanced sampling to ensure diversity; evaluation on REST-meta-MDD dataset with 10% core-set; code available.

Result: The core-set selected with only 10% of data preserves the ground-truth model ranking; achieves up to 23.2% improvement in ranking consistency (nDCG@k) over SOTA core-set methods; first formalization of core-set selection for FC operator benchmarking.

Conclusion: SCLCS enables scalable, robust large-scale FC operator benchmarking by producing structurally robust and diverse core-sets; facilitates routine benchmarking in computational neuroscience; code released.

Abstract: Benchmarking the hundreds of functional connectivity (FC) modeling methods on large-scale fMRI datasets is critical for reproducible neuroscience. However, the combinatorial explosion of model-data pairings makes exhaustive evaluation computationally prohibitive, preventing such assessments from becoming a routine pre-analysis step. To break this bottleneck, we reframe the challenge of FC benchmarking by selecting a small, representative core-set whose sole purpose is to preserve the relative performance ranking of FC operators. We formalize this as a ranking-preserving subset selection problem and propose Structure-aware Contrastive Learning for Core-set Selection (SCLCS), a self-supervised framework to select these core-sets. SCLCS first uses an adaptive Transformer to learn each sample's unique FC structure. It then introduces a novel Structural Perturbation Score (SPS) to quantify the stability of these learned structures during training, identifying samples that represent foundational connectivity archetypes. Finally, while SCLCS identifies stable samples via a top-k ranking, we further introduce a density-balanced sampling strategy as a necessary correction to promote diversity, ensuring the final core-set is both structurally robust and distributionally representative. On the large-scale REST-meta-MDD dataset, SCLCS preserves the ground-truth model ranking with just 10% of the data, outperforming state-of-the-art (SOTA) core-set selection methods by up to 23.2% in ranking consistency (nDCG@k). To our knowledge, this is the first work to formalize core-set selection for FC operator benchmarking, thereby making large-scale operators comparisons a feasible and integral part of computational neuroscience. Code is publicly available on https://github.com/lzhan94swu/SCLCS

</details>


### [245] [Stable but Wrong: When More Data Degrades Scientific Conclusions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05668)
*Zhipeng Zhang,Kai Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: More data can mislead, not help, when observation reliability degrades in unobservable ways; convergence and calibration do not guarantee correctness; require explicit constraints on the data-generating process.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Expose intrinsic limit of data-driven inference and diagnostics; show that data abundance can amplify bias under unobservable degradation; argue for integrity constraints on observations.

Method: Use minimal synthetic experiments to demonstrate a regime where inference procedures converge and appear well-calibrated yet converge to incorrect conclusions; analyze how increasing data amplifies error and how residual-based diagnostics fail.

Result: Identifies a structural regime where data growth worsens bias; standard diagnostics remain misleadingly normal; convergence is not epistemically valid; data availability alone cannot ensure validity.

Conclusion: Inference must be governed by explicit constraints on observational integrity; otherwise, data abundance can produce irreversible, systemic errors.

Abstract: Modern science increasingly relies on ever-growing observational datasets and automated inference pipelines, under the implicit belief that accumulating more data makes scientific conclusions more reliable. Here we show that this belief can fail in a fundamental and irreversible way. We identify a structural regime in which standard inference procedures converge smoothly, remain well calibrated, and pass conventional diagnostic checks, yet systematically converge to incorrect conclusions. This failure arises when the reliability of observations degrades in a manner that is intrinsically unobservable to the inference process itself. Using minimal synthetic experiments, we demonstrate that in this regime additional data do not correct error but instead amplify it, while residual-based and goodness-of-fit diagnostics remain misleadingly normal. These results reveal an intrinsic limit of data-driven science: stability, convergence, and confidence are not sufficient indicators of epistemic validity. We argue that inference cannot be treated as an unconditional consequence of data availability, but must instead be governed by explicit constraints on the integrity of the observational process.

</details>


### [246] [Perception-Based Beliefs for POMDPs with Visual Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05679)
*Miriam Schäfers,Merlijn Krale,Thiago D. Simão,Nils Jansen,Maximilian Weininger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A perception-based belief update framework for POMDPs (PBP) integrates a perception model that maps high-dimensional observations to state distributions, enabling standard belief updates with uncertain perceptions. It uses uncertainty quantification to adjust updates, implemented with two traditional POMDP solvers, and empirically outperforms end-to-end deep RL while improving robustness to visual corruption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-dimensional observations (e.g., camera images) render belief- and filtering-based POMDP solvers intractable. By delegating perception to a classifier that outputs p(s|ob), the planner can operate on beliefs over states rather than raw observations.

Method: Introduce PBP: a perception-based layer that provides p(s|ob) from images. If the classifier is exact, the PBP belief update coincides with the standard POMDP update. To handle classifier misspecification, incorporate uncertainty quantification and propose two methods to adjust the belief update accordingly. The framework is instantiated with two traditional (non-deep) POMDP solvers and evaluated on tasks with visual inputs.

Result: Empirically, PBP outperforms existing end-to-end deep reinforcement learning methods. Additionally, incorporating uncertainty quantification improves robustness of the belief updates against visual corruption.

Conclusion: Integrating learned perception with principled belief updates yields robust, tractable planning in high-dimensional observation spaces. Uncertainty quantification about the perception model is valuable for maintaining reliability under perception noise and corruption; future work could explore richer perception models and scalability.

Abstract: Partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) are a principled planning model for sequential decision-making under uncertainty. Yet, real-world problems with high-dimensional observations, such as camera images, remain intractable for traditional belief- and filtering-based solvers. To tackle this problem, we introduce the Perception-based Beliefs for POMDPs framework (PBP), which complements such solvers with a perception model. This model takes the form of an image classifier which maps visual observations to probability distributions over states. PBP incorporates these distributions directly into belief updates, so the underlying solver does not need to reason explicitly over high-dimensional observation spaces. We show that the belief update of PBP coincides with the standard belief update if the image classifier is exact. Moreover, to handle classifier imprecision, we incorporate uncertainty quantification and introduce two methods to adjust the belief update accordingly. We implement PBP using two traditional POMDP solvers and empirically show that (1) it outperforms existing end-to-end deep RL methods and (2) uncertainty quantification improves robustness of PBP against visual corruption.

</details>


### [247] [Pseudo-Invertible Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06042)
*Yamit Ehrlich,Nimrod Berman,Assaf Shocher*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Generalizes Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse to nonlinear regimes via SPNNs, enabling a tractable nonlinear PInv and non-linear back-projection, enabling zero-shot inversion of nonlinear degradations and semantic control without retraining diffusion priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for a nonlinear counterpart to PInv to enforce consistency in nonlinear inverse problems and to broaden zero-shot recovery capabilities in neural network-based systems.

Method: Define Surjective Pseudo-invertible Neural Networks that admit a tractable nonlinear PInv; formalize Non-Linear Back-Projection (NLBP) that enforces f(x)=y via the PInv; generalize linear back-projection x' = x + A^†(y-Ax); extend diffusion-based null-space projection to nonlinear degradations.

Result: SPNNs provide geometric guarantees (including back-projection) and enable zero-shot inversion under nonlinear degradations; enable semantic control in generation without retraining diffusion priors.

Conclusion: Nonlinear PInv via SPNNs expands the scope of inverse problems, providing a practical nonlinear back-projection framework and enabling zero-shot inversion and semantic control in nonlinear settings.

Abstract: The Moore-Penrose Pseudo-inverse (PInv) serves as the fundamental solution for linear systems. In this paper, we propose a natural generalization of PInv to the nonlinear regime in general and to neural networks in particular. We introduce Surjective Pseudo-invertible Neural Networks (SPNN), a class of architectures explicitly designed to admit a tractable non-linear PInv. The proposed non-linear PInv and its implementation in SPNN satisfy fundamental geometric properties. One such property is null-space projection or "Back-Projection", $x' = x + A^\dagger(y-Ax)$, which moves a sample $x$ to its closest consistent state $x'$ satisfying $Ax=y$. We formalize Non-Linear Back-Projection (NLBP), a method that guarantees the same consistency constraint for non-linear mappings $f(x)=y$ via our defined PInv. We leverage SPNNs to expand the scope of zero-shot inverse problems. Diffusion-based null-space projection has revolutionized zero-shot solving for linear inverse problems by exploiting closed-form back-projection. We extend this method to non-linear degradations. Here, "degradation" is broadly generalized to include any non-linear loss of information, spanning from optical distortions to semantic abstractions like classification. This approach enables zero-shot inversion of complex degradations and allows precise semantic control over generative outputs without retraining the diffusion prior.

</details>


### [248] [Mining Generalizable Activation Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05688)
*Alex Vitvitskyi,Michael Boratko,Matej Grcic,Razvan Pascanu,Deep Shah,Petar Veličković*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Evolutionary search with AlphaEvolve and frontier LLM mutators broadens activation-function discovery; it can encode inductive biases via out-of-distribution fitness, with meaningful activations found on small synthetic datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Activation functions shape optimization, expressivity, and inductive bias; flexible, knowledge-informed search methods are needed beyond hand-designed spaces.

Method: Use AlphaEvolve with frontier LLMs as mutators to explore a wide space of Python functions within a FLOP budget, biasing search toward meaningful activations; incorporate out-of-distribution performance asfitness to encode inductive biases; empirical exploration on small synthetic datasets.

Result: Demonstrates that meaningful activation functions can be discovered within a broad search space; LLM-based mutators bias the search toward known useful forms; small synthetic datasets suffice to reveal useful activations.

Conclusion: LLM-guided evolutionary search is a viable framework for activation-function discovery and encoding of target inductive biases, enabling wider search spaces and flexible biasing objectives; promising for further research at scale.

Abstract: The choice of activation function is an active area of research, with different proposals aimed at improving optimization, while maintaining expressivity. Additionally, the activation function can significantly alter the implicit inductive bias of the architecture, controlling its non-linear behavior. In this paper, in line with previous work, we argue that evolutionary search provides a useful framework for finding new activation functions, while we also make two novel observations. The first is that modern pipelines, such as AlphaEvolve, which relies on frontier LLMs as a mutator operator, allows for a much wider and flexible search space; e.g., over all possible python functions within a certain FLOP budget, eliminating the need for manually constructed search spaces. In addition, these pipelines will be biased towards meaningful activation functions, given their ability to represent common knowledge, leading to a potentially more efficient search of the space. The second observation is that, through this framework, one can target not only performance improvements but also activation functions that encode particular inductive biases. This can be done by using performance on out-of-distribution data as a fitness function, reflecting the degree to which the architecture respects the inherent structure in the data in a manner independent of distribution shifts. We carry an empirical exploration of this proposal and show that relatively small scale synthetic datasets can be sufficient for AlphaEvolve to discover meaningful activations.

</details>


### [249] [Shared LoRA Subspaces for almost Strict Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06043)
*Prakhar Kaushik,Ankit Vaidya,Shravan Chaudhari,Rama Chellappa,Alan Yuille*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Share is a parameter-efficient continual fine-tuning method that learns a single, shared low-rank subspace to incrementally integrate knowledge from new tasks, enabling forward transfer with reduced parameters and memory, and without data replay or multiple adapters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable scalable, efficient lifelong learning for large pretrained models by addressing catastrophic forgetting and high retraining costs, while avoiding replay buffers and multiple adapters.

Method: Construct a dynamic, shared low-rank subspace that captures core knowledge from past tasks and incrementally incorporates new task directions. Update the subspace as new tasks arrive, enabling forward transfer and reducing interference. A single Share model replaces many task-specific LoRA adapters across modalities (vision, language, 3D, text-to-image).

Result: Achieves up to 100x parameter reduction and 281x memory savings compared with traditional LoRA, while maintaining performance comparable to jointly trained models. Demonstrated across image classification, natural language understanding, 3D pose estimation, and text-to-image generation.

Conclusion: Share offers a practical, scalable approach for lifelong learning in large-scale AI systems by providing a single, continually updated shared subspace that supports asynchronous continual learning with minimal forgetting.

Abstract: Adapting large pretrained models to new tasks efficiently and continually is crucial for real-world deployment but remains challenging due to catastrophic forgetting and the high cost of retraining. While parameter-efficient tuning methods like low rank adaptation (LoRA) reduce computational demands, they lack mechanisms for strict continual learning and knowledge integration, without relying on data replay, or multiple adapters. We propose Share, a novel approach to parameter efficient continual finetuning that learns and dynamically updates a single, shared low-rank subspace, enabling seamless adaptation across multiple tasks and modalities. Share constructs a foundational subspace that extracts core knowledge from past tasks and incrementally integrates new information by identifying essential subspace directions. Knowledge from each new task is incorporated into this evolving subspace, facilitating forward knowledge transfer, while minimizing catastrophic interference. This approach achieves up to 100x parameter reduction and 281x memory savings over traditional LoRA methods, maintaining performance comparable to jointly trained models. A single Share model can replace hundreds of task-specific LoRA adapters, supporting scalable, asynchronous continual learning. Experiments across image classification, natural language understanding, 3D pose estimation, and text-to-image generation validate its effectiveness, making Share a practical and scalable solution for lifelong learning in large-scale AI systems.

</details>


### [250] [Almost Asymptotically Optimal Active Clustering Through Pairwise Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05690)
*Rachel S. Y. Teo,P. N. Karthik,Ramya Korlakai Vinayak,Vincent Y. F. Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: New analysis framework for clustering M items into an unknown K with noisy, actively collected pairwise bandit feedback. Derives a fundamental lower bound on required queries via change-of-measure (sup-inf). Proposes an asymptotically optimal algorithm using a GLR-based stopping rule; provides a computationally feasible GLR variant. Empirically shows the gap to the lower bound is within a constant factor of optimal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Characterize sample (query) complexity and design efficient active clustering under noisy binary feedback; enable reliable recovery of clusters with confidence guarantees.

Method: 1) Establish a sup-inf lower bound on the expected number of queries using change-of-measure; 2) Design an asymptotically optimal stopping rule based on the GLR statistic; 3) Develop a computationally feasible GLR variant; 4) Empirically compare performance to the lower bound.

Result: Fundamental lower bound on query complexity; an asymptotically optimal active clustering algorithm with a GLR-based stopping rule; a tractable GLR surrogate whose performance gap to the bound is empirically within a constant factor.

Conclusion: Links theoretical limits with a practical algorithm, providing confidence-aware clustering under noisy pairwise feedback and suggesting near-optimal performance with computable estimates of optimality gaps; future work on finite-sample guarantees and extensions.

Abstract: We propose a new analysis framework for clustering $M$ items into an unknown number of $K$ distinct groups using noisy and actively collected responses. At each time step, an agent is allowed to query pairs of items and observe bandit binary feedback. If the pair of items belongs to the same (resp.\ different) cluster, the observed feedback is $1$ with probability $p>1/2$ (resp.\ $q<1/2$). Leveraging the ubiquitous change-of-measure technique, we establish a fundamental lower bound on the expected number of queries needed to achieve a desired confidence in the clustering accuracy, formulated as a sup-inf optimization problem. Building on this theoretical foundation, we design an asymptotically optimal algorithm in which the stopping criterion involves an empirical version of the inner infimum -- the Generalized Likelihood Ratio (GLR) statistic -- being compared to a threshold. We develop a computationally feasible variant of the GLR statistic and show that its performance gap to the lower bound can be accurately empirically estimated and remains within a constant multiple of the lower bound.

</details>


### [251] [FedRandom: Sampling Consistent and Accurate Contribution Values in Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05693)
*Arno Geimer,Beltran Fiz Pontiveros,Radu State*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedRandom mitigates contribution instability in federated learning by treating instability as a statistical estimation problem and generating additional samples to stabilize contribution evaluation; evaluated on CIFAR-10, MNIST, CIFAR-100, and FMNIST, it reduces distance to ground truth by >33% in 50% of scenarios and improves stability in >90% of cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In FL with a limited number of data-rich participants, fair contribution assessment is essential for compensation and security (detecting free-riders and malicious actors). Existing aggregation-strategy-induced instability harms participation; a robust contribution estimation is needed.

Method: FedRandom reframes contribution instability as a statistical estimation problem and generates more samples than standard FL to obtain more reliable contribution evaluations. This sampling-based approach aims to reduce estimator variance and improve convergence of contribution metrics across data distributions.

Result: Quantitative results show FedRandom reduces the distance to ground truth by more than a third in half of evaluated scenarios and increases stability in more than 90% of cases. Experiments cover CIFAR-10, MNIST, CIFAR-100, and FMNIST.

Conclusion: FedRandom effectively mitigates contribution estimation instability in federated learning, enabling fairer compensation, better detection of free-riders and malicious actors, and more robust participation across heterogeneous data distributions.

Abstract: Federated Learning is a privacy-preserving decentralized approach for Machine Learning tasks. In industry deployments characterized by a limited number of entities possessing abundant data, the significance of a participant's role in shaping the global model becomes pivotal given that participation in a federation incurs costs, and participants may expect compensation for their involvement. Additionally, the contributions of participants serve as a crucial means to identify and address potential malicious actors and free-riders. However, fairly assessing individual contributions remains a significant hurdle. Recent works have demonstrated a considerable inherent instability in contribution estimations across aggregation strategies. While employing a different strategy may offer convergence benefits, this instability can have potentially harming effects on the willingness of participants in engaging in the federation. In this work, we introduce FedRandom, a novel mitigation technique to the contribution instability problem. Tackling the instability as a statistical estimation problem, FedRandom allows us to generate more samples than when using regular FL strategies. We show that these additional samples provide a more consistent and reliable evaluation of participant contributions. We demonstrate our approach using different data distributions across CIFAR-10, MNIST, CIFAR-100 and FMNIST and show that FedRandom reduces the overall distance to the ground truth by more than a third in half of all evaluated scenarios, and improves stability in more than 90% of cases.

</details>


### [252] [Limitations of SGD for Multi-Index Models Beyond Statistical Queries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05704)
*Daniel Barzilai,Ohad Shamir*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a non-SQ framework to study vanilla SGD limits for single-index and multi-index models, avoiding SQ-related issues and enabling broad applicability to deep networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SQ-based analyses of SGD often rely on adversarial or specially structured gradient noise and may misrepresent standard SGD; existing results also depend on nontrivial algorithmic tweaks, prompting a need for a framework that reflects vanilla SGD.

Method: Develops a non-SQ analytical framework to analyze vanilla SGD for target functions depending on low-dimensional projections of inputs (single-index and multi-index models), with applicability to broad settings and architectures including deep networks.

Result: The framework yields results that apply to a broad class of settings and architectures, demonstrating limitations of standard vanilla SGD for the considered model families; avoids the non-reflective gradient-noise assumptions of SQ analyses.

Conclusion: A non-SQ framework provides a faithful tool to study the limitations of vanilla SGD across a wide range of models, including potentially deep neural networks, improving alignment with standard SGD practice.

Abstract: Understanding the limitations of gradient methods, and stochastic gradient descent (SGD) in particular, is a central challenge in learning theory. To that end, a commonly used tool is the Statistical Queries (SQ) framework, which studies performance limits of algorithms based on noisy interaction with the data. However, it is known that the formal connection between the SQ framework and SGD is tenuous: Existing results typically rely on adversarial or specially-structured gradient noise that does not reflect the noise in standard SGD, and (as we point out here) can sometimes lead to incorrect predictions. Moreover, many analyses of SGD for challenging problems rely on non-trivial algorithmic modifications, such as restricting the SGD trajectory to the sphere or using very small learning rates. To address these shortcomings, we develop a new, non-SQ framework to study the limitations of standard vanilla SGD, for single-index and multi-index models (namely, when the target function depends on a low-dimensional projection of the inputs). Our results apply to a broad class of settings and architectures, including (potentially deep) neural networks.

</details>


### [253] [Fix Representation (Optimally) Before Fairness: Finite-Sample Shrinkage Population Correction and the True Price of Fairness Under Subpopulation Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05707)
*Amir Asiaee,Kaveh Aryan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Subpopulation shift explains apparent fairness-accuracy tradeoffs;  full importance weighting is asymptotically unbiased but suboptimal in finite samples; optimal finite-sample correction is a shrinkage reweighting between target and training mixtures; a proper evaluation protocol fixes representation before fairness and uses shrinkage-corrected baselines to reveal the true fairness-utility frontier.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forecasts when training data misrepresent subgroup proportions can create spurious tension between predictive accuracy and group fairness. Under stable within-group distributions but shifted group proportions, standard fairness comparisons can mislead about the cost of fairness.

Method: Theoretically analyze importance-weighting under subpopulation shift; derive (i) asymptotic unbiasedness of full correction and (ii) finite-sample optimal shrinkage reweighting; propose an evaluation protocol: fix representation, compare fairness methods against a shrinkage-corrected baseline; validate with experiments on synthetic data, Adult, and COMPAS.

Result: Showed that apparent “fairness helps accuracy” can stem from improper weighting; shrinkage-corrected baselines align fairness with true utility cost; experiments confirm predictions and reveal the genuine fairness-utility frontier under the proposed protocol.

Conclusion: Misrepresentation of subgroup proportions can masquerade as fairness-utility tradeoffs. A shrinkage-based finite-sample correction coupled with a representation-controlled evaluation protocol reveals the true cost of fairness and eliminates spurious tradeoffs.

Abstract: Machine learning practitioners frequently observe tension between predictive accuracy and group fairness constraints -- yet sometimes fairness interventions appear to improve accuracy. We show that both phenomena can be artifacts of training data that misrepresents subgroup proportions. Under subpopulation shift (stable within-group distributions, shifted group proportions), we establish: (i) full importance-weighted correction is asymptotically unbiased but finite-sample suboptimal; (ii) the optimal finite-sample correction is a shrinkage reweighting that interpolates between target and training mixtures; (iii) apparent "fairness helps accuracy" can arise from comparing fairness methods to an improperly-weighted baseline. We provide an actionable evaluation protocol: fix representation (optimally) before fairness -- compare fairness interventions against a shrinkage-corrected baseline to isolate the true, irreducible price of fairness. Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks (Adult, COMPAS) validate our theoretical predictions and demonstrate that this protocol eliminates spurious tradeoffs, revealing the genuine fairness-utility frontier.

</details>


### [254] [Projected Boosting with Fairness Constraints: Quantifying the Cost of Fair Training Distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05713)
*Amir Asiaee,Kaveh Aryan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FairBoost enforces group fairness in boosting by projecting the ensemble's exponential-weights distribution onto a convex fairness set, yielding a tunable tradeoff between accuracy and fairness captured by a KL-based fairness cost δ_t in the exponential-loss bound.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To integrate group fairness constraints into boosting while retaining analyzable training dynamics and to explicitly quantify the accuracy–fairness tradeoff.

Method: Compute the ensemble-induced exponential-weights distribution w^t, project onto a convex set of fair distributions q^t, and train weak learners on the projected (fair) distribution. The projection reduces the effective edge by δ_t = sqrt(KL(w^t || q^t)/2). Prove an exponential-loss bound with rate depending on (edge − δ_t).

Result: Derived exponential-loss bound where convergence rate is governed by the adjusted edge (weak-learner edge minus the fairness cost). Empirical results on standard benchmarks show competitive fairness–accuracy tradeoffs and stable training curves.

Conclusion: The framework provides a principled link between fairness projections and boosting dynamics, quantifies the accuracy–fairness tradeoff via KL-based cost, and is empirically validated on benchmarks.

Abstract: Boosting algorithms enjoy strong theoretical guarantees: when weak learners maintain positive edge, AdaBoost achieves geometric decrease of exponential loss. We study how to incorporate group fairness constraints into boosting while preserving analyzable training dynamics. Our approach, FairBoost, projects the ensemble-induced exponential-weights distribution onto a convex set of distributions satisfying fairness constraints (as a reweighting surrogate), then trains weak learners on this fair distribution. The key theoretical insight is that projecting the training distribution reduces the effective edge of weak learners by a quantity controlled by the KL-divergence of the projection. We prove an exponential-loss bound where the convergence rate depends on weak learner edge minus a "fairness cost" term $δ_t = \sqrt{\mathrm{KL}(w^t \| q^t)/2}$. This directly quantifies the accuracy-fairness tradeoff in boosting dynamics. Experiments on standard benchmarks validate the theoretical predictions and demonstrate competitive fairness-accuracy tradeoffs with stable training curves.

</details>


### [255] [Muon in Associative Memory Learning: Training Dynamics and Scaling Laws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05725)
*Binghui Li,Kaifei Wang,Han Zhong,Pinyan Lu,Liwei Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Muon optimizer yields exponential speedup over gradient descent in noiseless linear associative memory with softmax retrieval and hierarchical frequency spectrum; under noisy, power-decay spectrum, Muon achieves superior scaling and acts as an implicit matrix preconditioner; theory and experiments (synthetic long-tail tasks and LLaMA-style pretraining) support the claims.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand the dynamics and scaling of Muon, a matrix-sign-based optimizer, in a linear associative memory setting with softmax retrieval and label noise. Compare to gradient descent, derive convergence rates, and interpret Muon as an adaptive preconditioner arising from task alignment and a block-symmetric gradient structure.

Method: Theoretical analysis of GD vs Muon in a linear associative memory with softmax retrieval across noiseless and noisy regimes with hierarchical frequency spectra. Derivation of convergence behavior, speedups, and scaling laws. Interpretation of Muon as an implicit preconditioner; comparison to a sign-based preconditioner under oracle task representations. Empirical validation on synthetic long-tail classification and LLaMA-style pre-training.

Result: GD exhibits imbalanced learning across frequency components, slow convergence dominated by low frequencies. Muon mitigates this imbalance, yielding faster and more uniform progress. In the noiseless case, Muon achieves exponential speedup over GD. Under a noisy, power-decay spectrum, Muon has a derived optimization scaling law and superior scaling efficiency versus GD. Muon can be viewed as an implicit matrix preconditioner from adaptive task alignment and block-symmetric gradient structure; a coordinate-wise sign operator preconditioner can match Muon only with oracle access to unknown task representations, which is infeasible for SignGD in practice. Experiments on synthetic long-tail classification and LLaMA-style pre-training corroborate the theory.

Conclusion: Muon provides robust, accelerated optimization in structured, long-tailed and noisy settings by acting as an adaptive preconditioner that regularizes gradient dynamics. The results suggest practical benefits for large-scale training where task heterogeneity and frequency imbalance are prominent.

Abstract: Muon updates matrix parameters via the matrix sign of the gradient and has shown strong empirical gains, yet its dynamics and scaling behavior remain unclear in theory. We study Muon in a linear associative memory model with softmax retrieval and a hierarchical frequency spectrum over query-answer pairs, with and without label noise. In this setting, we show that Gradient Descent (GD) learns frequency components at highly imbalanced rates, leading to slow convergence bottlenecked by low-frequency components. In contrast, the Muon optimizer mitigates this imbalance, leading to faster and more uniform progress. Specifically, in the noiseless case, Muon achieves an exponential speedup over GD; in the noisy case with a power-decay frequency spectrum, we derive Muon's optimization scaling law and demonstrate its superior scaling efficiency over GD. Furthermore, we show that Muon can be interpreted as an implicit matrix preconditioner arising from adaptive task alignment and block-symmetric gradient structure. In contrast, the preconditioner with coordinate-wise sign operator could match Muon under oracle access to unknown task representations, which is infeasible for SignGD in practice. Experiments on synthetic long-tail classification and LLaMA-style pre-training corroborate the theory.

</details>


### [256] [CSRv2: Unlocking Ultra-Sparse Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05735)
*Lixuan Guo,Yifei Wang,Tiansheng Wen,Yifan Wang,Aosong Feng,Bo Chen,Stefanie Jegelka,Chenyu You*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CSRv2 enables practical ultra-sparse embeddings by stabilizing sparsity learning and enhancing representations, achieving large efficiency gains while maintaining or improving accuracy, even with only two active features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense embeddings are costly; CSR's ultra-sparse regime underperforms; need a training recipe to maintain quality at extreme sparsity for real-time/edge deployments.

Method: Progressive k-annealing to stabilize sparsity; supervised contrastive objectives to improve representation; full backbone finetuning for end-to-end adaptability.

Result: Reduces dead neurons from 80% to 20%; 14% accuracy gain at k=2; matches CSR at k=8 and MRL at 32 dims with two active features; 7x speedup vs MRL; up to 300x compute/memory efficiency vs dense; improvements in text/vision; CSRv2 outperforms CSR by 7%/4% at k=4 and 14%/6% at k=2.

Conclusion: Ultra-sparse embeddings become viable and practically deployable; CSRv2 expands design space for real-time and edge AI without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: In the era of large foundation models, the quality of embeddings has become a central determinant of downstream task performance and overall system capability. Yet widely used dense embeddings are often extremely high-dimensional, incurring substantial costs in storage, memory, and inference latency. To address these, Contrastive Sparse Representation (CSR) is recently proposed as a promising direction, mapping dense embeddings into high-dimensional but k-sparse vectors, in contrast to compact dense embeddings such as Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL). Despite its promise, CSR suffers severe degradation in the ultra-sparse regime, where over 80% of neurons remain inactive, leaving much of its efficiency potential unrealized. In this paper, we introduce CSRv2, a principled training approach designed to make ultra-sparse embeddings viable. CSRv2 stabilizes sparsity learning through progressive k-annealing, enhances representational quality via supervised contrastive objectives, and ensures end-to-end adaptability with full backbone finetuning. CSRv2 reduces dead neurons from 80% to 20% and delivers a 14% accuracy gain at k=2, bringing ultra-sparse embeddings on par with CSR at k=8 and MRL at 32 dimensions, all with only two active features. While maintaining comparable performance, CSRv2 delivers a 7x speedup over MRL, and yields up to 300x improvements in compute and memory efficiency relative to dense embeddings in text representation. Extensive experiments across text and vision demonstrate that CSRv2 makes ultra-sparse embeddings practical without compromising performance, where CSRv2 achieves 7%/4% improvement over CSR when k=4 and further increases this gap to 14%/6% when k=2 in text/vision representation. By making extreme sparsity viable, CSRv2 broadens the design space for real-time and edge-deployable AI systems where both embedding quality and efficiency are critical.

</details>


### [257] [Learning to Inject: Automated Prompt Injection via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05746)
*Xin Chen,Jie Zhang,Florian Tramer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AutoInject uses a 1.5B adversarial suffix generator with reinforcement learning to craft universal, transferable prompt-injection attacks in black-box LLM agents, achieving high attack success while preserving benign task performance across frontier models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prompt injection poses a critical, hard-to-detect vulnerability in LLM agents. Current attacks rely on human-crafted prompts and red-teaming, limiting scalability and adaptability. An automated, transferable method would enable scalable vulnerability assessment and stronger baselines for defense research.

Method: A reinforcement learning framework (AutoInject) that optimizes for attack success and utility preservation. It generates universal adversarial suffixes, operates in a black-box setting with query-based optimization, and supports transfer attacks to unseen models and tasks. Uses a 1.5B parameter suffix generator. Evaluated on frontier systems and the AgentDojo benchmark.

Result: The approach successfully compromised frontier systems including GPT 5 Nano, Claude Sonnet 3.5, and Gemini 2.5 Flash on AgentDojo, establishing a stronger automated baseline for prompt-injection attacks.

Conclusion: Automating adversarial suffix generation enhances scalability and transferability of prompt-injection attacks, enabling stronger benchmarks for defense research. Limitations include reliance on black-box optimization, potential overfitting to benchmark tasks, and the need for defenses against automated, transferable attacks across diverse models.

Abstract: Prompt injection is one of the most critical vulnerabilities in LLM agents; yet, effective automated attacks remain largely unexplored from an optimization perspective. Existing methods heavily depend on human red-teamers and hand-crafted prompts, limiting their scalability and adaptability. We propose AutoInject, a reinforcement learning framework that generates universal, transferable adversarial suffixes while jointly optimizing for attack success and utility preservation on benign tasks. Our black-box method supports both query-based optimization and transfer attacks to unseen models and tasks. Using only a 1.5B parameter adversarial suffix generator, we successfully compromise frontier systems including GPT 5 Nano, Claude Sonnet 3.5, and Gemini 2.5 Flash on the AgentDojo benchmark, establishing a stronger baseline for automated prompt injection research.

</details>


### [258] [How to Achieve the Intended Aim of Deep Clustering Now, without Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05749)
*Kai Ming Ting,Wei-Jie Xu,Hang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DEC does not inherently overcome k-means limitations; a non-deep method that leverages cluster distribution can address arbitrary shapes, sizes, and densities better than DEC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate whether deep clustering methods (e.g., DEC) truly overcome fundamental k-means limitations and whether relying on learned representations suffices without exploiting data distribution.

Method: Review DEC's end-to-end training with a k-means–like objective; contrast with a non-deep baseline that uses distributional information about clusters; analyze implications for deep clustering’s ability to handle arbitrary cluster structures.

Result: Findings suggest that DEC does not inherently solve k-means’ issues; a non-deep approach exploiting cluster distribution can achieve the intended clustering improvements.

Conclusion: Deep clustering may not inherently overcome k-means limitations; incorporating data-distribution information is crucial. Claims about DEC’s superiority should be re-evaluated in light of distribution-aware baselines.

Abstract: Deep clustering (DC) is often quoted to have a key advantage over $k$-means clustering. Yet, this advantage is often demonstrated using image datasets only, and it is unclear whether it addresses the fundamental limitations of $k$-means clustering. Deep Embedded Clustering (DEC) learns a latent representation via an autoencoder and performs clustering based on a $k$-means-like procedure, while the optimization is conducted in an end-to-end manner. This paper investigates whether the deep-learned representation has enabled DEC to overcome the known fundamental limitations of $k$-means clustering, i.e., its inability to discover clusters of arbitrary shapes, varied sizes and densities. Our investigations on DEC have a wider implication on deep clustering methods in general. Notably, none of these methods exploit the underlying data distribution. We uncover that a non-deep learning approach achieves the intended aim of deep clustering by making use of distributional information of clusters in a dataset to effectively address these fundamental limitations.

</details>


### [259] [Variational Speculative Decoding: Rethinking Draft Training from Token Likelihood to Sequence Acceptance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05774)
*Xiandong Zou,Jianshu Li,Jing Huang,Pan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Variational Speculative Decoding (VSD) to close the training-decoding gap in speculative decoding by treating draft paths as latent variables and optimizing a variational objective for higher-quality proposals and faster acceptance; uses EM with MCMC, ARW, and CAR, achieving notable speedups over prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training optimizes single greedy trajectories, but decoding involves evaluating and ranking multiple sampled drafts. This mismatch motivates a variational framework that aligns training with decoding dynamics to improve inference efficiency.

Method: Formulate draft training as variational inference over latent draft paths. Derive an ELBO that maximizes target-model acceptance. Introduce path-level utility and optimize via Expectation-Maximization: E-step samples from an oracle-filtered posterior using MCMC; M-step maximizes a weighted likelihood using Adaptive Rejection Weighting (ARW) and Confidence-Aware Regularization (CAR). Theoretical analysis shows increased expected acceptance length and speedup.

Result: Empirical evaluation across LLMs and MLLMs demonstrates speedups of up to 9.6% versus EAGLE-3 and 7.9% versus ViSpec, indicating substantial decoding efficiency gains while maintaining or improving draft quality.

Conclusion: VSD closes the training-decoding gap by treating drafts as latent variables and optimizing a robust variational objective with EM to raise acceptance probability and reduce variance, yielding consistent, model-agnostic speedups in speculative decoding.

Abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates inference for (M)LLMs, yet a training-decoding discrepancy persists: while existing methods optimize single greedy trajectories, decoding involves verifying and ranking multiple sampled draft paths. We propose Variational Speculative Decoding (VSD), formulating draft training as variational inference over latent proposals (draft paths). VSD maximizes the marginal probability of target-model acceptance, yielding an ELBO that promotes high-quality latent proposals while minimizing divergence from the target distribution. To enhance quality and reduce variance, we incorporate a path-level utility and optimize via an Expectation-Maximization procedure. The E-step draws MCMC samples from an oracle-filtered posterior, while the M-step maximizes weighted likelihood using Adaptive Rejection Weighting (ARW) and Confidence-Aware Regularization (CAR). Theoretical analysis confirms that VSD increases expected acceptance length and speedup. Extensive experiments across LLMs and MLLMs show that VSD achieves up to a 9.6% speedup over EAGLE-3 and 7.9% over ViSpec, significantly improving decoding efficiency.

</details>


### [260] [Cross-Domain Offline Policy Adaptation via Selective Transition Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05776)
*Mengbei Yan,Jiafei Lyu,Shengjie Sun,Zhongjian Qiao,Jingwen Yang,Zichuan Lin,Deheng Ye,Xiu Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STC selectively corrects source-domain transitions to match target dynamics in cross-domain offline RL, using inverse policy and reward models to adjust actions/rewards and a forward dynamics model to filter corrected samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-domain offline RL faces dynamics mismatch when combining offline data from a source domain with a target domain dataset. Direct merging can hurt performance; existing mitigation (transition filtering, reward modification) may underutilize useful source data.

Method: Apply inverse policy to correct source actions, a reward model to adjust rewards, aligning transitions with target dynamics. Use a forward dynamics model to retain corrected samples that better match target dynamics, forming the Selective Transition Correction (STC) algorithm.

Result: Experiments across diverse environments with dynamic shifts show STC achieving superior performance over baselines by enabling more reliable use of source data for policy adaptation.

Conclusion: Selective Transition Correction enables effective use of cross-domain offline data by aligning source transitions to target dynamics and selecting high-fidelity corrections, improving policy adaptation.

Abstract: It remains a critical challenge to adapt policies across domains with mismatched dynamics in reinforcement learning (RL). In this paper, we study cross-domain offline RL, where an offline dataset from another similar source domain can be accessed to enhance policy learning upon a target domain dataset. Directly merging the two datasets may lead to suboptimal performance due to potential dynamics mismatches. Existing approaches typically mitigate this issue through source domain transition filtering or reward modification, which, however, may lead to insufficient exploitation of the valuable source domain data. Instead, we propose to modify the source domain data into the target domain data. To that end, we leverage an inverse policy model and a reward model to correct the actions and rewards of source transitions, explicitly achieving alignment with the target dynamics. Since limited data may result in inaccurate model training, we further employ a forward dynamics model to retain corrected samples that better match the target dynamics than the original transitions. Consequently, we propose the Selective Transition Correction (STC) algorithm, which enables reliable usage of source domain data for policy adaptation. Experiments on various environments with dynamics shifts demonstrate that STC achieves superior performance against existing baselines.

</details>


### [261] [How Controlling the Variance can Improve Training Stability of Sparsely Activated DNNs and CNNs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05779)
*Emily Dent,Jared Tanner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Higher fixed Gaussian process variance at Edge-of-Chaos initialization improves expressivity and training stability for networks with sparse, activation-shifting/clipping (CReLU), enabling very high sparsity (~90%) with near/full accuracy and suggesting energy-efficiency benefits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explore how the often-underutilized variance in EoC Gaussian-process initializations affects learning when using sparsity-inducing activations. Determine whether larger fixed GP variance improves expressivity and stability under high sparsity.

Method: Theoretically examine the EoC fixed-point covariance with a shifted/clipped ReLU (CReLUτ,m). Empirically train DNNs/CNNs with varying initialization variances and the CReLUτ,m activation, evaluating expressivity, training stability, accuracy, and sparsity levels (up to ~90%).

Result: Larger fixed GP variances at initialization enhance expressivity under heavy activation sparsity, improve training stability, and enable near-full accuracy even with very high hidden-layer sparsity. This suggests potential energy savings in fully connected layers and supports a design principle for initialization in sparse regimes.

Conclusion: Under-utilized Gaussian-process variance in EoC initializations is consequential for sparse networks. Tuning toward larger initial variance can maintain performance while enabling extreme sparsity, offering a path to energy-efficient deep learning models.

Abstract: The intermediate layers of deep networks can be characterised as a Gaussian process, in particular the Edge-of-Chaos (EoC) initialisation strategy prescribes the limiting covariance matrix of the Gaussian process. Here we show that the under-utilised chosen variance of the Gaussian process is important in the training of deep networks with sparsity inducing activation, such as a shifted and clipped ReLU, $\text{CReLU}_{τ,m}(x)=\min(\max(x-τ,0),m)$. Specifically, initialisations leading to larger fixed Gaussian process variances, allow for improved expressivity with activation sparsity as large as 90% in DNNs and CNNs, and generally improve the stability of the training process. Enabling full, or near full, accuracy at such high levels of sparsity in the hidden layers suggests a promising mechanism to reduce the energy consumption of machine learning models involving fully connected layers.

</details>


### [262] [Distributional Reinforcement Learning with Diffusion Bridge Critics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05783)
*Shutong Ding,Yimiao Zhou,Ke Hu,Mokai Pan,Shan Zhong,Yanwei Fu,Jingya Wang,Ye Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Diffusion Bridge Critics (DBC), a diffusion-based distributional critic modeling the inverse CDF of Q-values via a diffusion bridge, with an analytic integral to reduce discretization error; claims state-of-the-art results on MuJoCo and easy plug-and-play integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate value estimation is critical in RL, making the critic more important than policy expressiveness. In stochastic tasks, a distributional critic better captures uncertainty. While diffusion-based policies have been explored, diffusion-based critics have not, and a distributional, expressive critic can improve learning.

Method: Model the inverse CDF of the Q-value using a diffusion bridge. This captures the full value distribution and avoids collapsing to a trivial Gaussian due to the diffusion bridge's distribution-matching properties. Derive an analytic integral to address discretization errors in DBC. Treat DBC as a plug-and-play component that can be integrated into existing RL frameworks.

Result: Empirical experiments on MuJoCo benchmarks show DBC outperforms prior distributional critic models.

Conclusion: DBC is the first diffusion-bridge-based critic, offering improved value distribution estimation and easy integration into existing RL pipelines; it advances diffusion-based RL by focusing on robust, distributional criticism.

Abstract: Recent advances in diffusion-based reinforcement learning (RL) methods have demonstrated promising results in a wide range of continuous control tasks. However, existing works in this field focus on the application of diffusion policies while leaving the diffusion critics unexplored. In fact, since policy optimization fundamentally relies on the critic, accurate value estimation is far more important than policy expressiveness. Furthermore, given the stochasticity of most reinforcement learning tasks, it has been confirmed that the critic is more appropriately depicted with a distributional model. Motivated by these points, we propose a novel distributional RL method with Diffusion Bridge Critics (DBC). DBC directly models the inverse cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the Q value. This allows us to accurately capture the value distribution and prevents it from collapsing into a trivial Gaussian distribution owing to the strong distribution-matching capability of the diffusion bridge. Moreover, we further derive an analytic integral formula to address discretization errors in DBC, which is essential in value estimation. To our knowledge, DBC is the first work to employ the diffusion bridge model as the critic. Notably, DBC is also a plug-and-play component and can be integrated into most existing RL frameworks. Experimental results on MuJoCo robot control benchmarks demonstrate the superiority of DBC compared with previous distributional critic models.

</details>


### [263] [Classification Under Local Differential Privacy with Model Reversal and Model Averaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05797)
*Caihong Qin,Yang Bai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reframe local differential privacy learning as a transfer problem: noisy data are the source, clean data the target. Introduce (i) a noised binary feedback evaluation to gauge utility, (ii) model reversal to repair weak classifiers by inverting decision boundaries, and (iii) model averaging to weight reversed classifiers by estimated utility. Provide theoretical excess risk bounds under LDP and empirical evidence of accuracy gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LDP perturbs data at the source, harming utility. Recasting private learning as transfer learning seeks to exploit the noisy source more effectively to improve performance while preserving privacy.

Method: 1) Noised binary feedback-based evaluation to estimate dataset utility under LDP. 2) Model reversal to salvage underperforming classifiers by inverting decision boundaries. 3) Model averaging to combine multiple reversed classifiers with weights based on estimated utility. Theoretical excess risk bounds are derived to quantify improvement under LDP.

Result: The approach yields theoretical excess risk bounds under LDP and empirical demonstrations on simulated and real-world data showing substantial improvements in classification accuracy.

Conclusion: A transfer-learning perspective with noised-feedback evaluation, model reversal, and model averaging can effectively reduce the utility gap induced by LDP, improving classification performance while maintaining privacy.

Abstract: Local differential privacy (LDP) has become a central topic in data privacy research, offering strong privacy guarantees by perturbing user data at the source and removing the need for a trusted curator. However, the noise introduced by LDP often significantly reduces data utility. To address this issue, we reinterpret private learning under LDP as a transfer learning problem, where the noisy data serve as the source domain and the unobserved clean data as the target. We propose novel techniques specifically designed for LDP to improve classification performance without compromising privacy: (1) a noised binary feedback-based evaluation mechanism for estimating dataset utility; (2) model reversal, which salvages underperforming classifiers by inverting their decision boundaries; and (3) model averaging, which assigns weights to multiple reversed classifiers based on their estimated utility. We provide theoretical excess risk bounds under LDP and demonstrate how our methods reduce this risk. Empirical results on both simulated and real-world datasets show substantial improvements in classification accuracy.

</details>


### [264] [Bifrost: Steering Strategic Trajectories to Bridge Contextual Gaps for Self-Improving Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05810)
*Quan M. Tran,Zhuo Huang,Wenbin Zhang,Bo Han,Koji Yatani,Masashi Sugiyama,Tongliang Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bifrost trains without additional training to adapt previously solved trajectories to new tasks by aligning context and trajectory at the representation (hidden-state) level, addressing context mismatch in trajectory reuse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Context shifts between tasks degrade reuse of successful task trajectories; existing methods lose performance or incur fine-tuning costs. A training-free approach leveraging context-trajectory correlation aims for precise adaptation.

Method: Identify a context-trajectory correlation and perform trajectory adaptation in the agent's hidden-state representation space, guiding past trajectories toward the target context without finetuning. The method operates at inference-time to transform trajectories in a shared latent space.

Result: Across diverse benchmarks, Bifrost consistently outperforms existing trajectory reuse and finetuned self-improvement methods, showing effective leverage of past experiences despite substantial context shifts.

Conclusion: Context shifts correlate with trajectory shifts; representation-space trajectory adaptation enables effective reuse of prior experiences without training, improving performance across tasks.

Abstract: Autonomous agents excel in self-improvement through reflection and iterative refinement, which reuse successful task trajectories as in-context examples to assist subsequent reasoning. However, shifting across tasks often introduces a context mismatch. Hence, existing approaches either discard the trajectories or manipulate them using heuristics, leading to a non-negligible fine-tuning cost or unguaranteed performance. To bridge this gap, we reveal a context-trajectory correlation, where shifts of context are highly parallel with shifts of trajectory. Based on this finding, we propose BrIdge contextual gap FoR imprOvised trajectory STeering (Bifrost), a training-free method that leverages context differences to precisely guide the adaptation of previously solved trajectories towards the target task, mitigating the misalignment caused by context shifts. Our trajectory adaptation is conducted at the representation level using agent hidden states, ensuring trajectory transformation accurately aligns with the target context in a shared space. Across diverse benchmarks, Bifrost consistently outperforms existing trajectory reuse and finetuned self-improvement methods, demonstrating that agents can effectively leverage past experiences despite substantial context shifts.

</details>


### [265] [Principled Confidence Estimation for Deep Computed Tomography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05812)
*Matteo Gätzner,Johannes Kirschner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A principled uncertainty-quantification framework for CT reconstructions under a realistic Beer–Lambert Poisson forward model, using sequential likelihood mixing to produce confidence regions with coverage guarantees that apply to both classical and deep-learning reconstructions, yielding tighter regions for deep methods, enabling hallucination detection and interpretable visualizations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty estimates in CT reconstructions are crucial for clinical decision-making, especially with deep-learning methods prone to hallucinations. There is a need for principled, coverage-guaranteed uncertainty quantification that respects the physics of CT and is compatible with both traditional and modern recon reconstruction algorithms.

Method: Adopts the sequential likelihood mixing framework to construct confidence regions with theoretical coverage under a log-linear forward model with Poisson noise (Beer–Lambert law). The framework is general and applies to classical reconstruction algorithms, U-Nets, U-Net ensembles, and diffusion models. Confidence regions are visualized and used to detect hallucinations.

Result: Deep-learning reconstructions yield substantially tighter confidence regions than classical methods while preserving coverage guarantees. The approach enables hallucination detection and provides interpretable confidence visualizations, suggesting deep models can be both accurate estimators and reliable uncertainty-quantified tools.

Conclusion: The work integrates deep CT reconstructions into a principled uncertainty-quantification framework with coverage guarantees, demonstrating tighter uncertainty bounds, practical hallucination detection, and interpretability—advancing uncertainty-aware clinical imaging with deep models.

Abstract: We present a principled framework for confidence estimation in computed tomography (CT) reconstruction. Based on the sequential likelihood mixing framework (Kirschner et al., 2025), we establish confidence regions with theoretical coverage guarantees for deep-learning-based CT reconstructions. We consider a realistic forward model following the Beer-Lambert law, i.e., a log-linear forward model with Poisson noise, closely reflecting clinical and scientific imaging conditions. The framework is general and applies to both classical algorithms and deep learning reconstruction methods, including U-Nets, U-Net ensembles, and generative Diffusion models. Empirically, we demonstrate that deep reconstruction methods yield substantially tighter confidence regions than classical reconstructions, without sacrificing theoretical coverage guarantees. Our approach allows the detection of hallucinations in reconstructed images and provides interpretable visualizations of confidence regions. This establishes deep models not only as powerful estimators, but also as reliable tools for uncertainty-aware medical imaging.

</details>


### [266] [Where Does Warm-Up Come From? Adaptive Scheduling for Norm-Constrained Optimizers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05813)
*Artem Riabinin,Andrey Veprikov,Arman Bolatov,Martin Takáč,Aleksandr Beznosikov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes adaptive learning-rate warm-up for norm-constrained optimizers with a generalized smoothness condition; provides convergence guarantees and a practical warm-up scheduler, validated on LLaMA pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To obtain principled, automatic learning-rate warm-up/decay schedules for norm-constrained optimizers without manual tuning, aligning theory with practice.

Method: Introduce a generalized smoothness assumption where local curvature decreases with suboptimality; prove convergence with an appropriate learning rate; derive a practical warm-up scheduler that relies on standard hyperparameters and auto-adjusts at training start.

Result: Convergence guarantees under the proposed assumption; a practical adaptive warm-up scheduler that matches or surpasses manually tuned warm-ups across LLaMA pretraining setups; code released.

Conclusion: Theory-driven warm-up scheduling enables competitive performance without extra hyperparameter search and is applicable to norm-constrained optimizers in large-scale pretraining.

Abstract: We study adaptive learning rate scheduling for norm-constrained optimizers (e.g., Muon and Lion). We introduce a generalized smoothness assumption under which local curvature decreases with the suboptimality gap and empirically verify that this behavior holds along optimization trajectories. Under this assumption, we establish convergence guarantees under an appropriate choice of learning rate, for which warm-up followed by decay arises naturally from the proof rather than being imposed heuristically.
  Building on this theory, we develop a practical learning rate scheduler that relies only on standard hyperparameters and adapts the warm-up duration automatically at the beginning of training. We evaluate this method on large language model pretraining with LLaMA architectures and show that our adaptive warm-up selection consistently outperforms or at least matches the best manually tuned warm-up schedules across all considered setups, without additional hyperparameter search. Our source code is available at https://github.com/brain-lab-research/llm-baselines/tree/warmup

</details>


### [267] [Synthesizing Realistic Test Data without Breaking Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05833)
*Laura Plein,Alexi Turcotte,Arina Hallemans,Andreas Zeller*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A privacy-preserving synthetic test data generator inspired by GANs, using a fuzzer-based test generator and a discriminator to match original distributions without direct data leakage; shows promising utility on four datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for synthetic datasets that replicate the statistical properties of original data while preserving confidentiality; traditional GAN-based synthetic data can leak information about the training data and suffer from limited accuracy. The work aims to generate test data with the same distributions indirectly using specification-driven generation.

Method: A generation step with a test generator (fuzzer) that creates test samples from input specifications with preserved constraints; a discriminator evaluates how close generated samples are to the original data; iterative evolution of samples to identify 'good' samples that match distributions while preserving privacy.

Result: Evaluated on four datasets used in state-of-the-art evaluations; experiments indicate the approach can produce synthetic data with high utility while preserving privacy, demonstrating the potential of the method.

Conclusion: The approach shows promise for privacy-preserving synthetic test data that preserves similar distributions to the original data; further work could include formal privacy guarantees, quantitative privacy-utility metrics, and broader evaluations.

Abstract: There is a need for synthetic training and test datasets that replicate statistical distributions of original datasets without compromising their confidentiality. A lot of research has been done in leveraging Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) for synthetic data generation. However, the resulting models are either not accurate enough or are still vulnerable to membership inference attacks (MIA) or dataset reconstruction attacks since the original data has been leveraged in the training process.
