Specifically, on encoder-only architectures, this reduction in trainable parameters leads to a negligible predictive performance drop on the GLUE benchmark. On decoder-only architectures, we achieve a small drop or even improvements in the predictive performance on mathematical problem-solving capabilities and coding tasks. Finally, this effectiveness extends to multimodal models, for which we also observe competitive results relative to fine-tuning with LoRA modules in all layers. Code is available at: https://github.com/c2d-usp/Layer-wise-LoRA-with-CKA

</details>


### [295] [Orthogonal Self-Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05996)
*Leo Zhang,James Martens*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Orthogonal Self-Attention (OSA) replaces Softmax SSA with an orthogonal attention matrix obtained by exponentiating a skew-symmetric matrix derived from queries and keys, enabling linear-time, skipless Transformers with well-conditioned Jacobians.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address instability of Softmax Self-Attention (rank collapse, poorly-conditioned Jacobians) in skipless architectures, aiming to train without skip connections or normalization layers in Transformers.

Method: Parameterize attention as M = exp(S) where S is skew-symmetric and formed from query-key values; use the low-rank structure of Q and K to compute M efficiently, achieving linear time/memory in sequence length; provide an initialization scheme that ensures the Jacobian of OSA is well-conditioned.

Result: Demonstrates practical implementability of OSA, with computational and memory cost scaling linearly with sequence length and an initialization strategy that yields well-conditioned Jacobians; empirical results are not detailed in the abstract.

Conclusion: OSA offers a promising alternative to SSA for skipless Transformer architectures, potentially improving trainability and stability; further empirical evaluation across tasks is needed to validate performance and robustness.

Abstract: Softmax Self-Attention (SSA) is a key component of Transformer architectures. However, when utilised within skipless architectures, which aim to improve representation learning, recent work has highlighted the inherent instability of SSA due to inducing rank collapse and poorly-conditioned Jacobians. In this work, we design a novel attention mechanism: Orthogonal Self-Attention (OSA), which aims to bypass these issues with SSA, in order to allow for (non-causal) Transformers without skip connections and normalisation layers to be more easily trained. In particular, OSA parametrises the attention matrix to be orthogonal via mapping a skew-symmetric matrix, formed from query-key values, through the matrix exponential. We show that this can be practically implemented, by exploiting the low-rank structure of our query-key values, resulting in the computational complexity and memory cost of OSA scaling linearly with sequence length. Furthermore, we derive an initialisation scheme for which we prove ensures that the Jacobian of OSA is well-conditioned.

</details>


### [296] [On Computation and Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05999)
*Raj Ghugare,Michał Bortkiewicz,Alicja Ziarko,Benjamin Eysenbach*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Compute, not just parameters, governs RL capability: a minimal variable-compute architecture yields improved performance and longer-horizon generalization, outperforming fixed-parameter or deeper networks on 31 tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Formalize compute-bounded policies to separate compute from parameter count; address whether more compute helps RL even with fixed architectures, enabling generalization to longer horizons.

Method: Formally define compute-bounded policies; prove that increased compute expands solvable tasks and horizon generalization; propose a minimal, adjustable-compute architecture built on algorithmic learning and model-free planning; evaluate on 31 RL tasks comparing compute-scaled architectures to standard feedforward and deep residual nets with up to 5× more parameters.

Result: Theoretically, more compute enlarges the set of solvable problems and horizons; empirically, the proposed architecture benefits from more compute, achieving stronger performance with increased compute and better longer-horizon generalization than standard networks, even when those networks have up to five times as many parameters.

Conclusion: Compute is a distinct resource in RL beyond parameter count; a minimal variable-compute architecture provides practical gains and better generalization across diverse tasks, illustrating the value of compute-bounded policy design.

Abstract: How does the amount of compute available to a reinforcement learning (RL) policy affect its learning? Can policies using a fixed amount of parameters, still benefit from additional compute? The standard RL framework does not provide a language to answer these questions formally. Empirically, deep RL policies are often parameterized as neural networks with static architectures, conflating the amount of compute and the number of parameters. In this paper, we formalize compute bounded policies and prove that policies which use more compute can solve problems and generalize to longer-horizon tasks that are outside the scope of policies with less compute. Building on prior work in algorithmic learning and model-free planning, we propose a minimal architecture that can use a variable amount of compute. Our experiments complement our theory. On a set 31 different tasks spanning online and offline RL, we show that $(1)$ this architecture achieves stronger performance simply by using more compute, and $(2)$ stronger generalization on longer-horizon test tasks compared to standard feedforward networks or deep residual network using up to 5 times more parameters.

</details>


### [297] [Curiosity is Knowledge: Self-Consistent Learning and No-Regret Optimization with Active Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06029)
*Yingke Li,Anjali Parashar,Enlu Zhou,Chuchu Fan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sufficient curiosity in EFE-minimizing agents yields both coherent learning (Bayesian posterior consistency) and no-regret decision-making (bounded cumulative regret), unifying active inference with Bayesian experimental design and Bayesian optimization; provides practical guidelines for tuning the epistemic-pragmatic trade-off and validates them on real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the paradox in active inference where too little curiosity causes myopic exploitation and too much causes unnecessary exploration; establish when the EFE objective yields both effective learning and optimal control, and connect AIF to established Bayesian frameworks.

Method: Theoretical analysis proving a sufficient-curiosity condition guarantees Bayesian posterior consistency and bounded regret for EFE-minimizing agents. The analysis explores dependence on initial uncertainty, identifiability, and objective alignment, and situates AIF within Bayesian experimental design and Bayesian optimization. It derives practical guidelines for tuning the epistemic-pragmatic trade-off and validates these insights through real-world experiments.

Result: A formal guarantee that sufficient curiosity leads to both self-consistent learning and no-regret optimization in EFE-minimizing agents; a unified theoretical framework linking AIF with Bayesian design/optimization; practical tuning guidelines; empirical validation on real-world tasks.

Conclusion: Active inference can be grounded within Bayesian experimentation and optimization theory. The work yields actionable guidelines for balancing exploration and exploitation in hybrid learning-optimization problems and demonstrates robustness through real-world experiments.

Abstract: Active inference (AIF) unifies exploration and exploitation by minimizing the Expected Free Energy (EFE), balancing epistemic value (information gain) and pragmatic value (task performance) through a curiosity coefficient. Yet it has been unclear when this balance yields both coherent learning and efficient decision-making: insufficient curiosity can drive myopic exploitation and prevent uncertainty resolution, while excessive curiosity can induce unnecessary exploration and regret. We establish the first theoretical guarantee for EFE-minimizing agents, showing that a single requirement--sufficient curiosity--simultaneously ensures self-consistent learning (Bayesian posterior consistency) and no-regret optimization (bounded cumulative regret). Our analysis characterizes how this mechanism depends on initial uncertainty, identifiability, and objective alignment, thereby connecting AIF to classical Bayesian experimental design and Bayesian optimization within one theoretical framework. We further translate these theories into practical design guidelines for tuning the epistemic-pragmatic trade-off in hybrid learning-optimization problems, validated through real-world experiments.

</details>


### [298] [AP-OOD: Attention Pooling for Out-of-Distribution Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06031)
*Claus Hofmann,Christian Huber,Bernhard Lehner,Daniel Klotz,Sepp Hochreiter,Werner Zellinger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes AP-OOD, a semi-supervised NLP OOD detector that aggregates token embeddings beyond simple averaging; achieves state-of-the-art unsupervised OOD performance on text benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ODD detection is essential for reliable deployment. Simple token-aggregation methods may miss discriminative token-level cues; exploiting token information and semi-supervised learning with limited outlier data can improve OOD discrimination in NLP.

Method: AP-OOD aggregates token embeddings more richly than a simple mean, leveraging token-level information. It is a semi-supervised framework that interpolates between unsupervised and supervised objectives, allowing the use of limited auxiliary outlier data.

Result: In unsupervised setting, FPR95 on XSUM drops from 27.84% to 4.67%; on WMT15 En-Fr from 77.08% to 70.37%, achieving new state-of-the-art performance.

Conclusion: AP-OOD advances text OOD detection by effective token-level aggregation and a flexible semi-supervised training regime, yielding strong empirical gains and broad potential applicability, with room for further improvement via more outlier data and architecture refinements.

Abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, which maps high-dimensional data into a scalar OOD score, is critical for the reliable deployment of machine learning models. A key challenge in recent research is how to effectively leverage and aggregate token embeddings from language models to obtain the OOD score. In this work, we propose AP-OOD, a novel OOD detection method for natural language that goes beyond simple average-based aggregation by exploiting token-level information. AP-OOD is a semi-supervised approach that flexibly interpolates between unsupervised and supervised settings, enabling the use of limited auxiliary outlier data. Empirically, AP-OOD sets a new state of the art in OOD detection for text: in the unsupervised setting, it reduces the FPR95 (false positive rate at 95% true positives) from 27.84% to 4.67% on XSUM summarization, and from 77.08% to 70.37% on WMT15 En-Fr translation.

</details>


### [299] [Can vision language models learn intuitive physics from interaction?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06033)
*Luca M. Schulze Buschoff,Konstantinos Voudouris,Can Demircan,Eric Schulz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reinforcement-learning–based interaction with an environment improves task performance but does not yield robust, generalizable physical intuitions; cross-task generalization remains poor regardless of training method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Grounded in cognitive science, the authors argue that real physical understanding requires interaction with an environment to learn dynamics, which supervised fine-tuning alone may not capture.

Method: Compare supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement-learning–driven interaction for learning physics tasks in vision-language models; evaluate within-task performance and cross-task generalization across related tasks with shared visual and physical principles.

Result: Ask: Interaction improves within-task performance but fails to produce generalizable physical intuitions; models trained on a single task do not reliably generalize to related tasks, even with environment interaction, indicating a lack of robust cross-task physical rules.

Conclusion: Environment interaction alone is insufficient to induce generalizable physical intuitions in vision-language models; alternative approaches (e.g., stronger inductive biases or structured representations) may be necessary for cross-task physical generalization.

Abstract: Pre-trained vision language models do not have good intuitions about the physical world. Recent work has shown that supervised fine-tuning can improve model performance on simple physical tasks. However, fine-tuned models do not appear to learn robust physical rules that can generalize to new contexts. Based on research in cognitive science, we hypothesize that models need to interact with an environment to properly learn its physical dynamics. We train models that learn through interaction with the environment using reinforcement learning. While learning from interaction allows models to improve their within-task performance, it fails to produce models with generalizable physical intuitions. We find that models trained on one task do not reliably generalize to related tasks, even if the tasks share visual statistics and physical principles, and regardless of whether the models are trained through interaction.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [300] [Artificial Intelligence as Strange Intelligence: Against Linear Models of Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04986)
*Kendra Chilson,Eric Schwitzgebel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a nonlinear, nonunified model of AI intelligence with 'familiar' and 'strange' intelligence; argues against linear progress and single general intelligence; discusses implications for adversarial testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the linear model of AI progress and refine how we evaluate AI capacities by introducing distinct intelligence modalities and a nonreductive general-intelligence framework.

Method: Conceptual analysis introducing two notions ('familiar' vs 'strange' intelligence) and a nonlinear framework; argumentation defending this model and its test-evaluation implications.

Result: A nonlinear view where general intelligence is not a single scalar but the ability to achieve diverse goals across diverse environments; 'strange intelligence' combines domain-specific strengths and weaknesses; predictions about evaluation and adversarial testing.

Conclusion: Errors by capable systems do not by themselves indicate lack of broad general intelligence; success in one task does not justify broad capacities; evaluation should account for domain-specific strengths and potential surprising errors.

Abstract: We endorse and expand upon Susan Schneider's critique of the linear model of AI progress and introduce two novel concepts: "familiar intelligence" and "strange intelligence". AI intelligence is likely to be strange intelligence, defying familiar patterns of ability and inability, combining superhuman capacities in some domains with subhuman performance in other domains, and even within domains sometimes combining superhuman insight with surprising errors that few humans would make. We develop and defend a nonlinear model of intelligence on which "general intelligence" is not a unified capacity but instead the ability to achieve a broad range of goals in a broad range of environments, in a manner that defies nonarbitrary reduction to a single linear quantity. We conclude with implications for adversarial testing approaches to evaluating AI capacities. If AI is strange intelligence, we should expect that even the most capable systems will sometimes fail in seemingly obvious tasks. On a nonlinear model of AI intelligence, such errors on their own do not demonstrate a system's lack of outstanding general intelligence. Conversely, excellent performance on one type of task, such as an IQ test, cannot warrant assumptions of broad capacities beyond that task domain.

</details>


### [301] [DeepRead: Document Structure-Aware Reasoning to Enhance Agentic Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05014)
*Zhanli Li,Huiwen Tian,Lvzhou Luo,Yixuan Cao,Ping Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DeepRead is a structure-aware, multi-turn document reasoning agent for long-document QA. It indexes PDFs at the paragraph level with section/order coordinates and uses two tools (Retrieve and ReadSection) to localize and read in a structure-preserving way, yielding significant gains over flat, one-shot agentic search and revealing a locate-then-read strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long documents contain inherent hierarchical and sequential discourse priors (sections, headings, and paragraph order) that are underutilized by flat retrieval in agentic search. Exploiting these priors can improve evidence selection and reasoning in retrieval-augmented generation for long-document QA.

Method: Convert PDFs to structured Markdown via an LLM-based OCR that preserves headings and paragraph boundaries. Index documents at the paragraph level and assign each paragraph a coordinate-style metadata key encoding its section identity and intra-section order. Provide two tools: (1) Retrieve to localize relevant paragraphs with structural coordinates and lightweight scanning context, and (2) ReadSection to read contiguously and in order within a specified section and paragraph range. Integrate these tools with the LLM to perform multi-turn reasoning.

Result: Empirical results show significant improvements over Search-o1-style agentic search in document QA. The combination of retrieval and reading tools yields synergistic gains, and a fine-grained behavioral analysis reveals a human-like locate-then-read paradigm.

Conclusion: Structure-aware, paragraph-level indexing and dedicated retrieval/reading tools enable effective long-document QA, validating the value of leveraging document-native priors (hierarchy and sequence) in multi-turn LLM-based reasoning.

Abstract: With the rapid progress of tool-using and agentic large language models (LLMs), Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is evolving from one-shot, passive retrieval into multi-turn, decision-driven evidence acquisition. Despite strong results in open-domain settings, existing agentic search frameworks commonly treat long documents as flat collections of chunks, underutilizing document-native priors such as hierarchical organization and sequential discourse structure. We introduce DeepRead, a structure-aware, multi-turn document reasoning agent that explicitly operationalizes these priors for long-document question answering. DeepRead leverages LLM-based OCR model to convert PDFs into structured Markdown that preserves headings and paragraph boundaries. It then indexes documents at the paragraph level and assigns each paragraph a coordinate-style metadata key encoding its section identity and in-section order. Building on this representation, DeepRead equips the LLM with two complementary tools: a Retrieve tool that localizes relevant paragraphs while exposing their structural coordinates (with lightweight scanning context), and a ReadSection tool that enables contiguous, order-preserving reading within a specified section and paragraph range. Our experiments demonstrate that DeepRead achieves significant improvements over Search-o1-style agentic search in document question answering. The synergistic effect between retrieval and reading tools is also validated. Our fine-grained behavioral analysis reveals a reading and reasoning paradigm resembling human-like ``locate then read'' behavior.

</details>


### [302] [MINT: Minimal Information Neuro-Symbolic Tree for Objective-Driven Knowledge-Gap Reasoning and Active Elicitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05048)
*Zeyu Fang,Tian Lan,Mahdi Imani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MINT combines a neuro-symbolic tree with self-play and LLM-assisted query elicitation to manage knowledge gaps in open-world planning, achieving near-expert performance with few questions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-world planning under incomplete information requires efficient elicitation of human inputs; existing methods struggle to quantify the impact of knowledge gaps and to plan optimal queries.

Method: Construct Minimal Information Neuro-Symbolic Tree (MINT) that enumerates propositions about possible human–AI interactions; consult a neural planning policy to estimate planning uncertainty due to remaining knowledge gaps; use self-play to optimize elicitation strategies; employ a large language model to search and summarize MINT's reasoning and curate queries to elicit human inputs; analyze returns under an extended MDP framework with knowledge gaps.

Result: Across three benchmarks with unseen/unknown objects of increasing realism, MINT-based planning attains near-expert returns by asking a limited number of questions per task, while significantly improving rewards and success rates compared with baselines.

Conclusion: MINT provides a principled, scalable framework for adaptive human elicitation in object-driven planning, linking symbolic reasoning with neural policy uncertainty and LLM-assisted query curation; demonstrated gains in planning performance under knowledge gaps, with avenues for scalability and robustness in future work.

Abstract: Joint planning through language-based interactions is a key area of human-AI teaming. Planning problems in the open world often involve various aspects of incomplete information and unknowns, e.g., objects involved, human goals/intents -- thus leading to knowledge gaps in joint planning. We consider the problem of discovering optimal interaction strategies for AI agents to actively elicit human inputs in object-driven planning. To this end, we propose Minimal Information Neuro-Symbolic Tree (MINT) to reason about the impact of knowledge gaps and leverage self-play with MINT to optimize the AI agent's elicitation strategies and queries. More precisely, MINT builds a symbolic tree by making propositions of possible human-AI interactions and by consulting a neural planning policy to estimate the uncertainty in planning outcomes caused by remaining knowledge gaps. Finally, we leverage LLM to search and summarize MINT's reasoning process and curate a set of queries to optimally elicit human inputs for best planning performance. By considering a family of extended Markov decision processes with knowledge gaps, we analyze the return guarantee for a given MINT with active human elicitation. Our evaluation on three benchmarks involving unseen/unknown objects of increasing realism shows that MINT-based planning attains near-expert returns by issuing a limited number of questions per task while achieving significantly improved rewards and success rates.

</details>


### [303] [Evaluating Large Language Models on Solved and Unsolved Problems in Graph Theory: Implications for Computing Education](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05059)
*Adithya Kulkarni,Mohna Chakraborty,Jay Bagga*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs can handle solved graph theory tasks and offer exploratory reasoning for open problems; useful for conceptual work but not for generating novel proofs, requiring verification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate reliability of LLMs for mathematically rigorous thinking in graph theory and computing education.

Method: Eight-stage evaluation protocol modeling authentic mathematical inquiry; tests on a solved gracefulness of line graphs problem and an open problem; analysis of definitions, recall, strategies, proof construction; accuracy judged by expert.

Result: Solved problem: correct definitions, relevant structures, accurate recall, valid proof verified by graph theory expert. Open problem: coherent interpretation and plausible exploratory strategies; no solution produced; no fabrication; acknowledged uncertainty due to prompting instructions.

Conclusion: LLMs support exploration of established material but show limited capacity for novel mathematical insight or deep structural reasoning; educational implication: guide students to use LLMs for conceptual exploration while relying on independent verification and rigorous argumentation for formal problem solving.

Abstract: Large Language Models are increasingly used by students to explore advanced material in computer science, including graph theory. As these tools become integrated into undergraduate and graduate coursework, it is important to understand how reliably they support mathematically rigorous thinking. This study examines the performance of a LLM on two related graph theoretic problems: a solved problem concerning the gracefulness of line graphs and an open problem for which no solution is currently known. We use an eight stage evaluation protocol that reflects authentic mathematical inquiry, including interpretation, exploration, strategy formation, and proof construction.
  The model performed strongly on the solved problem, producing correct definitions, identifying relevant structures, recalling appropriate results without hallucination, and constructing a valid proof confirmed by a graph theory expert. For the open problem, the model generated coherent interpretations and plausible exploratory strategies but did not advance toward a solution. It did not fabricate results and instead acknowledged uncertainty, which is consistent with the explicit prompting instructions that directed the model to avoid inventing theorems or unsupported claims.
  These findings indicate that LLMs can support exploration of established material but remain limited in tasks requiring novel mathematical insight or critical structural reasoning. For computing education, this distinction highlights the importance of guiding students to use LLMs for conceptual exploration while relying on independent verification and rigorous argumentation for formal problem solving.

</details>


### [304] [Towards Reducible Uncertainty Modeling for Reliable Large Language Model Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05073)
*Changdae Oh,Seongheon Park,To Eun Kim,Jiatong Li,Wendi Li,Samuel Yeh,Xuefeng Du,Hamed Hassani,Paul Bogdan,Dawn Song,Sharon Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: General formulation for uncertainty quantification in interactive LLM agents; shifts from uncertainty accumulation to conditional uncertainty reduction along agent trajectories, offering an actionable framework and highlighting practical implications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UQ research has focused on single-turn QA; there is a need for principled UQ in interactive, open-world agent settings to enable safe guardrails in real-world LLM applications.

Method: Formalizes agent UQ to subsume broad existing UQ setups, argues that the accumulation view breaks down in interactive contexts, and introduces a conditional uncertainty reduction process that emphasizes action interactivity; provides a conceptual framework for designing UQ in LLM agent systems.

Result: Delivers a conceptual framework and theoretical insights showing when prior uncertainty accumulation models fail and how a trajectory-level, interaction-aware view can capture reducible uncertainty; outlines practical implications and remaining open problems.

Conclusion: Offers practical guidance for deploying UQ in frontier LLM development and domain-specific applications, and identifies open research questions to advance agent UQ.

Abstract: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) for large language models (LLMs) is a key building block for safety guardrails of daily LLM applications. Yet, even as LLM agents are increasingly deployed in highly complex tasks, most UQ research still centers on single-turn question-answering. We argue that UQ research must shift to realistic settings with interactive agents, and that a new principled framework for agent UQ is needed. This paper presents the first general formulation of agent UQ that subsumes broad classes of existing UQ setups. Under this formulation, we show that prior works implicitly treat LLM UQ as an uncertainty accumulation process, a viewpoint that breaks down for interactive agents in an open world. In contrast, we propose a novel perspective, a conditional uncertainty reduction process, that explicitly models reducible uncertainty over an agent's trajectory by highlighting "interactivity" of actions. From this perspective, we outline a conceptual framework to provide actionable guidance for designing UQ in LLM agent setups. Finally, we conclude with practical implications of the agent UQ in frontier LLM development and domain-specific applications, as well as open remaining problems.

</details>


### [305] [Optimizing Mission Planning for Multi-Debris Rendezvous Using Reinforcement Learning with Refueling and Adaptive Collision Avoidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05075)
*Agni Bandyopadhyay,Gunther Waxenegger-Wilfing*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Reinforcement learning framework using masked PPO for adaptive collision avoidance in multi-debris ADR missions with small satellites; emphasizes fuel-efficient rendezvous planning, including refueling, evaluated on Iridium 33 debris scenarios; outperforms heuristic methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing Earth orbital debris increases collision risk; ADR missions with small satellites offer flexible, cost-effective means to mitigate debris. There is a need for adaptive, fuel-aware, multi-target rendezvous planning that can avoid active collision zones in real time.

Method: Proposes a masked Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) reinforcement learning agent that learns efficient rendezvous sequences with multiple debris targets, incorporating refueling strategies and adaptive collision avoidance. The framework uses simulated ADR scenarios derived from the Iridium 33 debris dataset to train and evaluate robustness across diverse orbital configurations.

Result: The RL framework reduces collision risk and improves mission efficiency compared with traditional heuristics, demonstrating fuel efficiency and reduced mission time while handling varying debris distributions. The approach shows robustness across diverse orbital scenarios and generalizes to multi-target rendezvous problems.

Conclusion: Provides a scalable, adaptive solution for planning complex multi-debris ADR missions and is applicable to other autonomous multi-target rendezvous problems in space mission planning. Future work could extend to more realistic dynamics, uncertainty modeling, and integration with onboard autonomy.

Abstract: As the orbital environment around Earth becomes increasingly crowded with debris, active debris removal (ADR) missions face significant challenges in ensuring safe operations while minimizing the risk of in-orbit collisions. This study presents a reinforcement learning (RL) based framework to enhance adaptive collision avoidance in ADR missions, specifically for multi-debris removal using small satellites. Small satellites are increasingly adopted due to their flexibility, cost effectiveness, and maneuverability, making them well suited for dynamic missions such as ADR.
  Building on existing work in multi-debris rendezvous, the framework integrates refueling strategies, efficient mission planning, and adaptive collision avoidance to optimize spacecraft rendezvous operations. The proposed approach employs a masked Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm, enabling the RL agent to dynamically adjust maneuvers in response to real-time orbital conditions. Key considerations include fuel efficiency, avoidance of active collision zones, and optimization of dynamic orbital parameters.
  The RL agent learns to determine efficient sequences for rendezvousing with multiple debris targets, optimizing fuel usage and mission time while incorporating necessary refueling stops. Simulated ADR scenarios derived from the Iridium 33 debris dataset are used for evaluation, covering diverse orbital configurations and debris distributions to demonstrate robustness and adaptability. Results show that the proposed RL framework reduces collision risk while improving mission efficiency compared to traditional heuristic approaches.
  This work provides a scalable solution for planning complex multi-debris ADR missions and is applicable to other multi-target rendezvous problems in autonomous space mission planning.

</details>


### [306] [VERA-MH: Reliability and Validity of an Open-Source AI Safety Evaluation in Mental Health](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05088)
*Kate H. Bentley,Luca Belli,Adam M. Chekroud,Emily J. Ward,Emily R. Dworkin,Emily Van Ark,Kelly M. Johnston,Will Alexander,Millard Brown,Matt Hawrilenko*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VERA-MH is validated for automated AI safety evaluation in mental health, showing strong alignment with clinical raters and high reliability of an LLM judge; supports its use and open-source availability, with notes on generalizability and robustness needing further work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the urgent need for a reliable, evidence-based automated safety benchmark for AI in mental health, focusing on suicide risk detection and response.

Method: Simulated conversations between LLM-based user agents and general-purpose AI chatbots. Licensed mental health clinicians rate conversations for safe/unsafe behavior and user-agent realism using a rubric. An LLM-based judge applies the same rubric to the same set of conversations. Comparisons assess inter-rater reliability (IRR) among clinicians, between clinicians and consensus, and between clinicians and the LLM judge.

Result: Clinician inter-rater reliability (IRR) = 0.77, indicating substantial agreement. The LLM judge IRR with clinical consensus = 0.81, indicating strong alignment. The LLM judge aligns with consensus overall and in key conditions. Clinicians also rated user-agents as realistic.

Conclusion: VERA-MH demonstrates clinical validity and reliability as an automated AI safety evaluation for mental health; it is open-source and scalable. Future work will examine generalizability and robustness of the approach.

Abstract: Millions now use leading generative AI chatbots for psychological support. Despite the promise related to availability and scale, the single most pressing question in AI for mental health is whether these tools are safe. The Validation of Ethical and Responsible AI in Mental Health (VERA-MH) evaluation was recently proposed to meet the urgent need for an evidence-based automated safety benchmark. This study aimed to examine the clinical validity and reliability of the VERA-MH evaluation for AI safety in suicide risk detection and response. We first simulated a large set of conversations between large language model (LLM)-based users (user-agents) and general-purpose AI chatbots. Licensed mental health clinicians used a rubric (scoring guide) to independently rate the simulated conversations for safe and unsafe chatbot behaviors, as well as user-agent realism. An LLM-based judge used the same scoring rubric to evaluate the same set of simulated conversations. We then compared rating alignment across (a) individual clinicians and (b) clinician consensus and the LLM judge, and (c) examined clinicians' ratings of user-agent realism. Individual clinicians were generally consistent with one another in their safety ratings (chance-corrected inter-rater reliability [IRR]: 0.77), thus establishing a gold-standard clinical reference. The LLM judge was strongly aligned with this clinical consensus (IRR: 0.81) overall and within key conditions. Clinician raters generally perceived the user-agents to be realistic. For the potential mental health benefits of AI chatbots to be realized, attention to safety is paramount. Findings from this human evaluation study support the clinical validity and reliability of VERA-MH: an open-source, fully automated AI safety evaluation for mental health. Further research will address VERA-MH generalizability and robustness.

</details>


### [307] [Evaluating Robustness and Adaptability in Learning-Based Mission Planning for Active Debris Removal](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05091)
*Agni Bandyopadhyay,Günther Waxenegger-Wilfing*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Three planners for constrained ADR rendezvous in LEO are compared: nominal PPO, domain-randomized PPO, and MCTS. Domain randomization boosts robustness to distribution shifts; MCTS is robust to constraint changes but computationally heavy. Overall, a hybrid approach—training-time diversity plus online planning—is promising.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous Active Debris Removal requires efficient, feasible mission planning under strict fuel and duration constraints, with robustness to varying debris fields and mission parameters.

Method: Evaluate three planners in a high-fidelity orbital simulation (with refueling and realistic transfers) over 300 test cases across nominal, reduced-fuel, and reduced-time scenarios. Compare: (i) nominal masked PPO trained on fixed missions, (ii) domain-randomized masked PPO trained across varying constraints, (iii) plain Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) baseline.

Result: Nominal PPO excels under matching training conditions but degrades under distributional shifts. Domain-randomized PPO shows improved adaptability with only moderate nominal performance loss. MCTS handles constraint changes best due to online replanning but is orders of magnitude slower computationally. Trade-off observed between speed of learned policies and adaptability of search-based methods; a hybrid approach may be promising.

Conclusion: A hybrid strategy that combines training-time diversity with online planning could yield resilient ADR planners, balancing fast policy execution with adaptability to changing constraints.

Abstract: Autonomous mission planning for Active Debris Removal (ADR) must balance efficiency, adaptability, and strict feasibility constraints on fuel and mission duration. This work compares three planners for the constrained multi-debris rendezvous problem in Low Earth Orbit: a nominal Masked Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) policy trained under fixed mission parameters, a domain-randomized Masked PPO policy trained across varying mission constraints for improved robustness, and a plain Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) baseline. Evaluations are conducted in a high-fidelity orbital simulation with refueling, realistic transfer dynamics, and randomized debris fields across 300 test cases in nominal, reduced fuel, and reduced mission time scenarios. Results show that nominal PPO achieves top performance when conditions match training but degrades sharply under distributional shift, while domain-randomized PPO exhibits improved adaptability with only moderate loss in nominal performance. MCTS consistently handles constraint changes best due to online replanning but incurs orders-of-magnitude higher computation time. The findings underline a trade-off between the speed of learned policies and the adaptability of search-based methods, and suggest that combining training-time diversity with online planning could be a promising path for future resilient ADR mission planners.

</details>


### [308] [GAMMS: Graph based Adversarial Multiagent Modeling Simulator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05105)
*Rohan Patil,Jai Malegaonkar,Xiao Jiang,Andre Dion,Gaurav S. Sukhatme,Henrik I. Christensen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GAMMS is a graph-based, lightweight, open-source multi-agent simulator designed for fast prototyping across graph-structured domains, with policy-agnostic agents and plug-in visualization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for scalable, accessible simulators for multi-agent coordination and adversarial modeling. High-fidelity simulators are computationally expensive and ill-suited for rapid iteration and large-scale deployments. Graph-structured environments and integration with ML tools demand a lightweight, extensible platform to lower barriers to entry.

Method: GAMMS uses a graph-based architecture with a lightweight, extensible, integration-first design. It provides built-in visualization with minimal configuration, supports external tools (machine learning libraries, planning solvers), and is agnostic to policy type (heuristic, optimization-based, learning-based including LLMs). It is open-source and designed to run on standard hardware.

Result: The framework enables efficient, scalable simulations with fast visualization feedback and easy integration with ML libraries and planners. It is suitable for domains like urban road networks and communications, supports diverse policy types including learning-based agents, and is openly available for researchers to prototype and evaluate multi-agent systems.

Conclusion: GAMMS lowers the barrier to entry for researchers, enabling rapid experimentation and innovation in multi-agent systems, autonomous planning, and adversarial modeling, with broad applicability and real-world grounding potential.

Abstract: As intelligent systems and multi-agent coordination become increasingly central to real-world applications, there is a growing need for simulation tools that are both scalable and accessible. Existing high-fidelity simulators, while powerful, are often computationally expensive and ill-suited for rapid prototyping or large-scale agent deployments. We present GAMMS (Graph based Adversarial Multiagent Modeling Simulator), a lightweight yet extensible simulation framework designed to support fast development and evaluation of agent behavior in environments that can be represented as graphs. GAMMS emphasizes five core objectives: scalability, ease of use, integration-first architecture, fast visualization feedback, and real-world grounding. It enables efficient simulation of complex domains such as urban road networks and communication systems, supports integration with external tools (e.g., machine learning libraries, planning solvers), and provides built-in visualization with minimal configuration. GAMMS is agnostic to policy type, supporting heuristic, optimization-based, and learning-based agents, including those using large language models. By lowering the barrier to entry for researchers and enabling high-performance simulations on standard hardware, GAMMS facilitates experimentation and innovation in multi-agent systems, autonomous planning, and adversarial modeling. The framework is open-source and available at https://github.com/GAMMSim/GAMMS/

</details>


### [309] [Understanding LLM Evaluator Behavior: A Structured Multi-Evaluator Framework for Merchant Risk Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05110)
*Liang Wang,Junpeng Wang,Chin-chia Michael Yeh,Yan Zheng,Jiarui Sun,Xiran Fan,Xin Dai,Yujie Fan,Yiwei Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A multi-evaluator framework assesses LLMs as judges in MCC-based merchant risk; results show model-specific self-biases, anonymization reduces bias, partial alignment with human judgment, and ground-truth validation supports the approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify reliability and bias of LLM-based evaluation in payment-risk settings and establish a judge-independent reference metric.

Method: Five frontier LLMs generate and cross-evaluate MCC risk rationales under attributed and anonymized conditions using a five-criterion rubric with Monte-Carlo scoring; a consensus-deviation metric provides judge-independent scoring; evaluation by 26 payment-industry experts; ground-truth validation using payment-network data.

Result: Substantial heterogeneity in self-evaluation bias across models; anonymization reduces bias by 25.8%. Experts rate LLM judges +0.46 above human consensus. GPT-5.1 and Claude 4.5 Sonnet show negative bias aligning closer to human judgment; Gemini-2.5 Pro and Grok 4 show positive bias. Four models show statistically significant alignment with ground-truth (Spearman rho 0.56–0.77).

Conclusion: The framework is replicable for evaluating LLM-as-a-judge in payment-risk workflows and highlights the need for bias-aware protocols in operational settings; it can capture genuine quality but requires careful bias management.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used as evaluators of reasoning quality, yet their reliability and bias in payments-risk settings remain poorly understood. We introduce a structured multi-evaluator framework for assessing LLM reasoning in Merchant Category Code (MCC)-based merchant risk assessment, combining a five-criterion rubric with Monte-Carlo scoring to evaluate rationale quality and evaluator stability. Five frontier LLMs generate and cross-evaluate MCC risk rationales under attributed and anonymized conditions. To establish a judge-independent reference, we introduce a consensus-deviation metric that eliminates circularity by comparing each judge's score to the mean of all other judges, yielding a theoretically grounded measure of self-evaluation and cross-model deviation. Results reveal substantial heterogeneity: GPT-5.1 and Claude 4.5 Sonnet show negative self-evaluation bias (-0.33, -0.31), while Gemini-2.5 Pro and Grok 4 display positive bias (+0.77, +0.71), with bias attenuating by 25.8 percent under anonymization. Evaluation by 26 payment-industry experts shows LLM judges assign scores averaging +0.46 points above human consensus, and that the negative bias of GPT-5.1 and Claude 4.5 Sonnet reflects closer alignment with human judgment. Ground-truth validation using payment-network data shows four models exhibit statistically significant alignment (Spearman rho = 0.56 to 0.77), confirming that the framework captures genuine quality. Overall, the framework provides a replicable basis for evaluating LLM-as-a-judge systems in payment-risk workflows and highlights the need for bias-aware protocols in operational financial settings.

</details>


### [310] [Democratic Preference Alignment via Sortition-Weighted RLHF](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05113)
*Suvadip Sana,Jinzhou Wu,Martin T. Wells*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DemPO uses algorithmic sortition to create demographically representative human preferences for fine-tuning AI, via Hard Panel (quota-sampled mini-public) and Soft Panel (reweighted data).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address demographic biases in RLHF-like signals by ensuring the preference data reflects a diverse, representative public rather than convenience samples.

Method: Hard Panel trains only on a quota-satisfied mini-public selected via sortition. Soft Panel reweights all preferences by each rater's inclusion probability under the sortition lottery; Soft Panel objective equals the Hard Panel objective in expectation. Evaluation on Llama models (1B–8B params) across six aggregation methods, using public preference data paired with demographics and a 75-clause constitution elicited from a representative US panel.

Result: Hard Panel consistently ranks first across aggregation methods; Soft Panel consistently outperforms the unweighted baseline, with larger effect sizes as model capacity grows.

Conclusion: Enforcing demographic representativeness at data collection yields models whose behavior better reflects values elicited from representative publics, outperforming post hoc corrections.

Abstract: Whose values should AI systems learn? Preference based alignment methods like RLHF derive their training signal from human raters, yet these rater pools are typically convenience samples that systematically over represent some demographics and under represent others. We introduce Democratic Preference Optimization, or DemPO, a framework that applies algorithmic sortition, the same mechanism used to construct citizen assemblies, to preference based fine tuning. DemPO offers two training schemes. Hard Panel trains exclusively on preferences from a quota satisfying mini public sampled via sortition. Soft Panel retains all data but reweights each rater by their inclusion probability under the sortition lottery. We prove that Soft Panel weighting recovers the expected Hard Panel objective in closed form. Using a public preference dataset that pairs human judgments with rater demographics and a seventy five clause constitution independently elicited from a representative United States panel, we evaluate Llama models from one billion to eight billion parameters fine tuned under each scheme. Across six aggregation methods, the Hard Panel consistently ranks first and the Soft Panel consistently outperforms the unweighted baseline, with effect sizes growing as model capacity increases. These results demonstrate that enforcing demographic representativeness at the preference collection stage, rather than post hoc correction, yields models whose behavior better reflects values elicited from representative publics.

</details>


### [311] [SocialVeil: Probing Social Intelligence of Language Agents under Communication Barriers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05115)
*Keyang Xuan,Pengda Wang,Chongrui Ye,Haofei Yu,Tal August,Jiaxuan You*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces SocialVeil, a social-learning environment to simulate communication barriers in LLM interactions and evaluates their impact with two barrier-aware metrics; finds substantial degradation in mutual understanding and increased confusion under three disruption types; adaptation strategies offer only modest gains; validated barrier fidelity with human raters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks for LLM social interaction assume ideal communication, limiting analysis of robustness to realistic communication breakdowns. There is a need to diagnose whether LLMs can maintain and repair interactions when semantic vagueness, sociocultural mismatch, or emotional interference disrupt dialogue.

Method: Develop SocialVeil grounded in a literature review of communication challenges. Implement three disruption types: semantic vagueness, sociocultural mismatch, emotional interference. Define two barrier-aware metrics: unresolved confusion and mutual understanding. Conduct 720 scenarios across four frontier LLMs. Use human evaluations (ICC ~0.78, Pearson r ~0.80) to validate barrier fidelity. Test adaptation strategies (Repair Instruction, Interactive learning) to assess potential mitigation.

Result: Barriers consistently impair performance: mutual understanding drops by over 45%; unresolved confusion increases by ~50%. Human fidelity measures indicate good barrier realism (ICC ≈ 0.78, Pearson ≈ 0.80). Adaptation strategies yield only modest improvements, still far from barrier-free performance.

Conclusion: A step toward more realistic social-interaction benchmarking for LLMs, enabling exploration of social intelligence under imperfect communication. The findings motivate further development of barrier-robust interaction strategies and broader evaluation in socially rich settings.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly evaluated in interactive environments to test their social intelligence. However, existing benchmarks often assume idealized communication between agents, limiting our ability to diagnose whether LLMs can maintain and repair interactions in more realistic, imperfect settings. To close this gap, we present \textsc{SocialVeil}, a social learning environment that can simulate social interaction under cognitive-difference-induced communication barriers. Grounded in a systematic literature review of communication challenges in human interaction, \textsc{SocialVeil} introduces three representative types of such disruption, \emph{semantic vagueness}, \emph{sociocultural mismatch}, and \emph{emotional interference}. We also introduce two barrier-aware evaluation metrics, \emph{unresolved confusion} and \emph{mutual understanding}, to evaluate interaction quality under impaired communication. Experiments across 720 scenarios and four frontier LLMs show that barriers consistently impair performance, with mutual understanding reduced by over 45\% on average, and confusion elevated by nearly 50\%. Human evaluations validate the fidelity of these simulated barriers (ICC$\approx$0.78, Pearson r$\approx$0.80). We further demonstrate that adaptation strategies (Repair Instruction and Interactive learning) only have a modest effect far from barrier-free performance. This work takes a step toward bringing social interaction environments closer to real-world communication, opening opportunities for exploring the social intelligence of LLM agents.

</details>


### [312] [CAST-CKT: Chaos-Aware Spatio-Temporal and Cross-City Knowledge Transfer for Traffic Flow Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05133)
*Abdul Joseph Fofanah,Lian Wen,David Chen,Alpha Alimamy Kamara,Zhongyi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CAST-CKT is a chaos-aware cross-city few-shot traffic-prediction framework that uses a chaos analyser to drive regime-adaptive temporal modeling, adaptive spatial topology, and chaotic-consistency-based knowledge transfer, enabling horizon-specific, uncertainty-quantified predictions with improved generalization and interpretable regime analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traffic forecasting across cities in data-scarce settings is hard due to nonlinear chaotic dynamics and domain shifts; existing methods struggle to capture chaos for effective few-shot learning.

Method: Introduce a chaos analyser to quantify traffic predictability regimes; develop chaos-aware attention for regime-adaptive temporal modeling; implement adaptive topology learning for dynamic spatial dependencies; enforce chaotic consistency-based cross-city alignment for knowledge transfer; support horizon-specific predictions with uncertainty quantification; provide theoretical generalization bounds; release code.

Result: Empirical results on four cross-city few-shot benchmarks show CAST-CKT significantly outperforms state-of-the-art in MAE and RMSE; provides interpretable regime analysis and horizon-specific uncertainty; theoretical analysis indicates improved generalization bounds.

Conclusion: CAST-CKT offers a robust, interpretable framework for cross-city few-shot traffic prediction, achieving strong generalization and effective knowledge transfer with the added benefit of regime-aware insights.

Abstract: Traffic prediction in data-scarce, cross-city settings is challenging due to complex nonlinear dynamics and domain shifts. Existing methods often fail to capture traffic's inherent chaotic nature for effective few-shot learning. We propose CAST-CKT, a novel Chaos-Aware Spatio-Temporal and Cross-City Knowledge Transfer framework. It employs an efficient chaotic analyser to quantify traffic predictability regimes, driving several key innovations: chaos-aware attention for regime-adaptive temporal modelling; adaptive topology learning for dynamic spatial dependencies; and chaotic consistency-based cross-city alignment for knowledge transfer. The framework also provides horizon-specific predictions with uncertainty quantification. Theoretical analysis shows improved generalisation bounds. Extensive experiments on four benchmarks in cross-city few-shot settings show CAST-CKT outperforms state-of-the-art methods by significant margins in MAE and RMSE, while offering interpretable regime analysis. Code is available at https://github.com/afofanah/CAST-CKT.

</details>


### [313] [HugRAG: Hierarchical Causal Knowledge Graph Design for RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05143)
*Nengbo Wang,Tuo Liang,Vikash Singh,Chaoda Song,Van Yang,Yu Yin,Jing Ma,Jagdip Singh,Vipin Chaudhary*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HugRAG introduces causal gating across hierarchical modules to improve graph-based RAG by modeling explicit causal relations, reducing spurious correlations, and enabling scalable, cross-module reasoning; it outperforms baselines on multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph-based RAG often relies on surface-level node matching and lacks explicit causal modeling, leading to unfaithful or spurious answers. Existing causal extensions are limited to local or single-document contexts and modular graphs cause information isolation, hindering scalability and cross-module causal reasoning.

Method: HugRAG rethinks knowledge organization for graph-based RAG through causal gating across hierarchical modules, explicitly modeling causal relationships to suppress spurious correlations while enabling scalable reasoning over large-scale knowledge graphs.

Result: Extensive experiments show HugRAG consistently outperforms competitive graph-based RAG baselines across multiple datasets and evaluation metrics.

Conclusion: Establishes a principled foundation for structured, scalable, and causally grounded RAG systems.

Abstract: Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) has enhanced large language models by enabling access to external knowledge, with graph-based RAG emerging as a powerful paradigm for structured retrieval and reasoning. However, existing graph-based methods often over-rely on surface-level node matching and lack explicit causal modeling, leading to unfaithful or spurious answers. Prior attempts to incorporate causality are typically limited to local or single-document contexts and also suffer from information isolation that arises from modular graph structures, which hinders scalability and cross-module causal reasoning. To address these challenges, we propose HugRAG, a framework that rethinks knowledge organization for graph-based RAG through causal gating across hierarchical modules. HugRAG explicitly models causal relationships to suppress spurious correlations while enabling scalable reasoning over large-scale knowledge graphs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HugRAG consistently outperforms competitive graph-based RAG baselines across multiple datasets and evaluation metrics. Our work establishes a principled foundation for structured, scalable, and causally grounded RAG systems.

</details>


### [314] [First Proof](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05192)
*Mohammed Abouzaid,Andrew J. Blumberg,Martin Hairer,Joe Kileel,Tamara G. Kolda,Paul D. Nelson,Daniel Spielman,Nikhil Srivastava,Rachel Ward,Shmuel Weinberger,Lauren Williams*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Authors evaluate AI performance on research-level mathematics by releasing ten questions from their own work, with encrypted answers for a short period.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess current AI capabilities in advanced mathematics and identify gaps relative to research-level problems.

Method: Assemble ten questions that emerged during authors' research, not publicly released; present to AI systems to answer; keep correct answers encrypted temporarily to prevent leakage; likely compare AI responses to known answers.

Result: Not provided in abstract excerpt; intended to yield benchmarking results later; initial description focuses on setup.

Conclusion: The work frames a benchmarking exercise for AI math proficiency at high level, potentially enabling cross-system comparisons and identification of algorithmic weaknesses.

Abstract: To assess the ability of current AI systems to correctly answer research-level mathematics questions, we share a set of ten math questions which have arisen naturally in the research process of the authors. The questions had not been shared publicly until now; the answers are known to the authors of the questions but will remain encrypted for a short time.

</details>


### [315] [Traceable Cross-Source RAG for Chinese Tibetan Medicine Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05195)
*Fengxian Chen,Zhilong Tao,Jiaxuan Li,Yunlong Li,Qingguo Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A RAG system for Chinese Tibetan medicine using three heterogeneous KBs (encyclopedia, classics, clinical papers); introduces DAKS for KB routing and budgeted retrieval and an alignment graph for evidence fusion, enabling cross-KB verification with a lightweight generator; shows improved CrossEv@5, with maintained faithfulness and citation correctness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain with multiple heterogeneous knowledge bases where density biases (encyclopedia entries) can dominate retrieval and hinder authoritative evidence; need traceability, reduced hallucinations, and cross-KB verification.

Method: Two complementary components: (1) DAKS for KB routing and budgeted retrieval to reduce density bias and prioritize authoritative sources; (2) an alignment graph to guide evidence fusion and coverage-aware packing to improve cross-KB evidence coverage without naive concatenation. Generation is done with a lightweight model, openPangu-Embedded-7B.

Result: Experiments show consistent improvements in routing quality and cross-KB evidence coverage; the full system achieves the best CrossEv@5 while maintaining faithfulness and citation correctness.

Conclusion: The proposed approach enhances cross-KB evidence coverage and faithfulness in RAG across multiple domain-specific KBs, leveraging targeted routing, budgeting, and structured evidence fusion with an efficient generator.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) promises grounded question answering, yet domain settings with multiple heterogeneous knowledge bases (KBs) remain challenging. In Chinese Tibetan medicine, encyclopedia entries are often dense and easy to match, which can dominate retrieval even when classics or clinical papers provide more authoritative evidence. We study a practical setting with three KBs (encyclopedia, classics, and clinical papers) and a 500-query benchmark (cutoff $K{=}5$) covering both single-KB and cross-KB questions. We propose two complementary methods to improve traceability, reduce hallucinations, and enable cross-KB verification. First, DAKS performs KB routing and budgeted retrieval to mitigate density-driven bias and to prioritize authoritative sources when appropriate. Second, we use an alignment graph to guide evidence fusion and coverage-aware packing, improving cross-KB evidence coverage without relying on naive concatenation. All answers are generated by a lightweight generator, \textsc{openPangu-Embedded-7B}. Experiments show consistent gains in routing quality and cross-KB evidence coverage, with the full system achieving the best CrossEv@5 while maintaining strong faithfulness and citation correctness.

</details>


### [316] [Surgery: Mitigating Harmful Fine-Tuning for Large Language Models via Attention Sink](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05228)
*Guozhi Liu,Weiwei Lin,Tiansheng Huang,Ruichao Mo,Qi Mu,Xiumin Wang,Li Shen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Surgery: a fine-tuning defense using sink divergence suppression to steer attention heads away from learning harmful patterns, improving safety defense on several benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Harmful fine-tuning risks safety alignment; a robust method is needed to mitigate harmful pattern learning in LLMs.

Method: Measure sink divergence for each attention head; observe two signs of sink divergence; hypothesize that heads learning harmful patterns are separable by sign; introduce a regularizer to suppress sink divergence and redirect heads to the negative sign; implement the Surgery defense during fine-tuning; evaluate on safety benchmarks.

Result: Defense improves performance by 5.90%, 11.25%, and 9.55% on BeaverTails, HarmBench, and SorryBench respectively; source code released.

Conclusion: Attention-head sink-divergence sign correlates with harmful-pattern learning; targeted regularization can mitigate such learning, making fine-tuning safer.

Abstract: Harmful fine-tuning can invalidate safety alignment of large language models, exposing significant safety risks. In this paper, we utilize the attention sink mechanism to mitigate harmful fine-tuning. Specifically, we first measure a statistic named \emph{sink divergence} for each attention head and observe that \emph{different attention heads exhibit two different signs of sink divergence}. To understand its safety implications, we conduct experiments and find that the number of attention heads of positive sink divergence increases along with the increase of the model's harmfulness when undergoing harmful fine-tuning. Based on this finding, we propose a separable sink divergence hypothesis -- \emph{attention heads associating with learning harmful patterns during fine-tuning are separable by their sign of sink divergence}. Based on the hypothesis, we propose a fine-tuning-stage defense, dubbed Surgery. Surgery utilizes a regularizer for sink divergence suppression, which steers attention heads toward the negative sink divergence group, thereby reducing the model's tendency to learn and amplify harmful patterns. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Surgery improves defense performance by 5.90\%, 11.25\%, and 9.55\% on the BeaverTails, HarmBench, and SorryBench benchmarks, respectively. Source code is available on https://github.com/Lslland/Surgery.

</details>


### [317] [Explainable AI: A Combined XAI Framework for Explaining Brain Tumour Detection Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05240)
*Patrick McGonagle,William Farrelly,Kevin Curran*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A CNN for brain tumor detection on BraTS 2021 uses integrated XAI (GRAD-CAM, LRP, SHAP) to provide multi-level explanations, achieving 91.24% accuracy and enabling identification of full and partial tumors, with enhanced interpretability versus single XAI methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve transparency and trust in AI-driven brain imaging by combining multiple XAI techniques to offer layered, pixel-level explanations that cover both full and partial tumor appearances.

Method: Train a custom CNN on BraTS 2021; apply GRAD-CAM for broad spatial regions, LRP for pixel-level relevance, and SHAP for feature contribution attribution; integrate explanations to produce layered, comprehensive insights; evaluate on tumor vs non-tumor classification (and capability to detect partial tumors).

Result: Achieved 91.24% accuracy in distinguishing tumour vs non-tumour regions; integrated XAI approach yielded more comprehensive explanations and superior explanatory power compared to any single XAI method, including cases with partial tumour visibility.

Conclusion: Integrated XAI techniques can enhance transparency and trust in AI-based medical imaging, improving reliability and interpretability of brain tumor detection models by providing multi-scale, complementary explanations.

Abstract: This study explores the integration of multiple Explainable AI (XAI) techniques to enhance the interpretability of deep learning models for brain tumour detection. A custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) was developed and trained on the BraTS 2021 dataset, achieving 91.24% accuracy in distinguishing between tumour and non-tumour regions. This research combines Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (GRAD-CAM), Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP) and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) to provide comprehensive insights into the model's decision-making process. This multi-technique approach successfully identified both full and partial tumours, offering layered explanations ranging from broad regions of interest to pixel-level details. GRAD-CAM highlighted important spatial regions, LRP provided detailed pixel-level relevance and SHAP quantified feature contributions. The integrated approach effectively explained model predictions, including cases with partial tumour visibility thus showing superior explanatory power compared to individual XAI methods. This research enhances transparency and trust in AI-driven medical imaging analysis by offering a more comprehensive perspective on the model's reasoning. The study demonstrates the potential of integrated XAI techniques in improving the reliability and interpretability of AI systems in healthcare, particularly for critical tasks like brain tumour detection.

</details>


### [318] [Automatic Cognitive Task Generation for In-Situ Evaluation of Embodied Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05249)
*Xinyi He,Ying Yang,Chuanjian Fu,Sihan Guo,Songchun Zhu,Lifeng Fan,Zhenliang Zhang,Yujia Peng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes TEA, a two-stage, in-situ task generation framework for unseen 3D environments to benchmark embodied agents; it generates numerous, physically reasonable tasks via interaction and evolution on a task graph, revealing gaps in current models compared to humans.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks suffer from data contamination and lack scene specificity, failing to assess agent performance in unseen 3D environments. There is a need for dynamic, context-aware evaluation that reflects real-world variability.

Method: Two-stage system: (1) Interaction stage, where the agent interacts with the environment to create a loop of task execution and task generation; (2) Evolution stage, where task graphs allow recombination and reuse of existing tasks to generate new ones without external data.

Result: Across 10 unseen scenes, TEA generated 87,876 tasks in two cycles, validated as physically reasonable and capable of assessing essential daily cognitive abilities. State-of-the-art models, though strong on public benchmarks, underperform on basic perception, show poor 3D interaction awareness, and are highly sensitive to task type in reasoning, highlighting a need for in-situ evaluation.

Conclusion: In-situ evaluation in unseen environments is essential before real-world deployment. TEA enables scalable, context-rich task generation and benchmarking, better aligning agent evaluation with real-world demands.

Abstract: As general intelligent agents are poised for widespread deployment in diverse households, evaluation tailored to each unique unseen 3D environment has become a critical prerequisite. However, existing benchmarks suffer from severe data contamination and a lack of scene specificity, inadequate for assessing agent capabilities in unseen settings. To address this, we propose a dynamic in-situ task generation method for unseen environments inspired by human cognition. We define tasks through a structured graph representation and construct a two-stage interaction-evolution task generation system for embodied agents (TEA). In the interaction stage, the agent actively interacts with the environment, creating a loop between task execution and generation that allows for continuous task generation. In the evolution stage, task graph modeling allows us to recombine and reuse existing tasks to generate new ones without external data. Experiments across 10 unseen scenes demonstrate that TEA automatically generated 87,876 tasks in two cycles, which human verification confirmed to be physically reasonable and encompassing essential daily cognitive capabilities. Benchmarking SOTA models against humans on our in-situ tasks reveals that models, despite excelling on public benchmarks, perform surprisingly poorly on basic perception tasks, severely lack 3D interaction awareness and show high sensitivity to task types in reasoning. These sobering findings highlight the necessity of in-situ evaluation before deploying agents into real-world human environments.

</details>


### [319] [Beyond Cosine Similarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05266)
*Xinbo Ai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces recos, a similarity metric that normalizes the dot product by the sorted components of vectors, yielding a tighter bound than cosine and capturing ordinal concordance; empirically superior to cosine on STS benchmarks across 11 embedding models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cosine similarity, rooted in the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, restricts similarity to linear relationships and struggles with nonlinear, ordinal structures in real-world semantic spaces. A tighter dot-product bound motivates a similarity that can handle broader relational patterns.

Method: Derives a tighter upper bound for the dot product beyond Cauchy–Schwarz and defines recos as the dot product normalized by the vector components arranged in sorted order. The approach is validated by extensive experiments across static, contextualized, and universal embedding models on standard STS benchmarks.

Result: Recos consistently outperforms traditional cosine similarity, achieving higher correlation with human judgments across 11 embedding models on STS benchmarks.

Conclusion: Recos is a principled, empirically superior alternative for semantic similarity in complex embedding spaces, offering improved accuracy for semantic analysis.

Abstract: Cosine similarity, the standard metric for measuring semantic similarity in vector spaces, is mathematically grounded in the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, which inherently limits it to capturing linear relationships--a constraint that fails to model the complex, nonlinear structures of real-world semantic spaces. We advance this theoretical underpinning by deriving a tighter upper bound for the dot product than the classical Cauchy-Schwarz bound. This new bound leads directly to recos, a similarity metric that normalizes the dot product by the sorted vector components. recos relaxes the condition for perfect similarity from strict linear dependence to ordinal concordance, thereby capturing a broader class of relationships. Extensive experiments across 11 embedding models--spanning static, contextualized, and universal types--demonstrate that recos consistently outperforms traditional cosine similarity, achieving higher correlation with human judgments on standard Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) benchmarks. Our work establishes recos as a mathematically principled and empirically superior alternative, offering enhanced accuracy for semantic analysis in complex embedding spaces.

</details>


### [320] [Hallucination-Resistant Security Planning with a Large Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05279)
*Kim Hammar,Tansu Alpcan,Emil Lupu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A principled, iterative LLM-based decision-support framework for security incident response that controls hallucinations via a consistency threshold and abstention, uses external feedback (digital twin) with in-context learning, provides theoretical guarantees, and empirically reduces recovery times (up to 30%) on four public datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are unreliable and prone to hallucinations in security management tasks; need a framework that ensures consistency with system constraints and uses external feedback to refine actions.

Method: Iterative loop where the LLM proposes actions, they are checked for consistency with constraints and lookahead predictions; if inconsistent, actions are abstained from and external feedback is gathered (e.g., via a digital twin); feedback is used to refine candidates through in-context learning; a consistency threshold controls hallucination risk; a bound on the regret of ICL is established under certain assumptions.

Result: Applied to an incident response use case (response and recovery planning from system logs); evaluated on four public datasets; framework reduces recovery times by up to 30% compared to frontier LLMs.

Conclusion: The framework offers controllable hallucination risk and improved incident response performance, supported by theoretical guarantees and positive empirical results; effectiveness depends on the quality of external feedback (digital twin) and proper threshold tuning; future work could explore broader security tasks and robustness.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are promising tools for supporting security management tasks, such as incident response planning. However, their unreliability and tendency to hallucinate remain significant challenges. In this paper, we address these challenges by introducing a principled framework for using an LLM as decision support in security management. Our framework integrates the LLM in an iterative loop where it generates candidate actions that are checked for consistency with system constraints and lookahead predictions. When consistency is low, we abstain from the generated actions and instead collect external feedback, e.g., by evaluating actions in a digital twin. This feedback is then used to refine the candidate actions through in-context learning (ICL). We prove that this design allows to control the hallucination risk by tuning the consistency threshold. Moreover, we establish a bound on the regret of ICL under certain assumptions. To evaluate our framework, we apply it to an incident response use case where the goal is to generate a response and recovery plan based on system logs. Experiments on four public datasets show that our framework reduces recovery times by up to 30% compared to frontier LLMs.

</details>


### [321] [Position: Universal Time Series Foundation Models Rest on a Category Error](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05287)
*Xilin Dai,Wanxu Cai,Zhijian Xu,Qiang Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The abstract challenges universal time-series foundation models, arguing a category error between structural containers and semantic modalities. It presents an Autoregressive Blindness Bound showing history-only models cannot predict intervention-driven regime shifts, and proposes a Causal Control Agent that orchestrates specialized solvers via external context. It advocates shifting benchmarks from zero-shot accuracy to drift adaptation speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Time-series domains (e.g., finance, fluid dynamics) have inherently different generative processes and non-stationarities. Monolithic, universal models risk poor generalization under distributional drift and regime changes. A robust approach requires context-aware, controllable systems that leverage domain expertise.

Method: Proposes a theoretical Autoregressive Blindness Bound establishing limits of history-only models for predicting interventions. Introduces a Causal Control Agent (CCA) architecture that uses external context to coordinate a hierarchy of specialized solvers—from frozen domain experts to lightweight Just-in-Time adaptors.

Result: A theoretical limit is derived and an architectural blueprint for a modular, context-aware system is proposed. The work is conceptual and does not report empirical results in the abstract.

Conclusion: Encourage refocusing benchmarks toward drift adaptation speed and adopting a control-theoretic, context-aware paradigm with specialized solvers rather than universal, time-series foundation models.

Abstract: This position paper argues that the pursuit of "Universal Foundation Models for Time Series" rests on a fundamental category error, mistaking a structural Container for a semantic Modality. We contend that because time series hold incompatible generative processes (e.g., finance vs. fluid dynamics), monolithic models degenerate into expensive "Generic Filters" that fail to generalize under distributional drift.
