To address this, we introduce the "Autoregressive Blindness Bound," a theoretical limit proving that history-only models cannot predict intervention-driven regime shifts. We advocate replacing universality with a Causal Control Agent paradigm, where an agent leverages external context to orchestrate a hierarchy of specialized solvers, from frozen domain experts to lightweight Just-in-Time adaptors. We conclude by calling for a shift in benchmarks from "Zero-Shot Accuracy" to "Drift Adaptation Speed" to prioritize robust, control-theoretic systems.

</details>


### [322] [Aspect-Aware MOOC Recommendation in a Heterogeneous Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05297)
*Seongyeub Chu,Jongwoo Kim,Mun Yong Yi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AMR introduces aspect-aware path representations for MOOC recommendations by automatically discovering metapaths and encoding semantic node content with a Bi-LSTM; it yields improvements over GNN baselines on MOOCCube/PEEK for KC recommendations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackling data sparsity and over-specialization in MOOC recommender systems and reducing reliance on manually predefined metapaths and heavy engineering costs.

Method: Automatically discover metapaths via bi-directional walks; derive aspect-aware path representations using a Bi-LSTM encoder of node semantic content; use these representations as edge features in learner-learner and KC-KC subgraphs to inform KC recommendations; evaluate on large-scale MOOCCube and PEEK datasets.

Result: AMR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art graph neural network baselines on HR@K and nDCG@K; analysis shows that path-specific aspect information leads to more accurate recommendations than methods relying on predefined metapaths; code will be available upon acceptance.

Conclusion: AMR effectively captures rich path-specific aspect information, enabling more accurate, semantically informed KC recommendations and reducing the manual burden of defining metapaths.

Abstract: MOOC recommendation systems have received increasing attention to help learners navigate and select preferred learning content. Traditional methods such as collaborative filtering and content-based filtering suffer from data sparsity and over-specialization. To alleviate these limitations, graph-based approaches have been proposed; however, they still rely heavily on manually predefined metapaths, which often capture only superficial structural relationships and impose substantial burdens on domain experts as well as significant engineering costs. To overcome these limitations, we propose AMR (Aspect-aware MOOC Recommendation), a novel framework that models path-specific multiple aspects by embedding the semantic content of nodes within each metapath. AMR automatically discovers metapaths through bi-directional walks, derives aspect-aware path representations using a bi-LSTM-based encoder, and incorporates these representations as edge features in the learner-learner and KC-KC subgraphs to achieve fine-grained semantically informed KC recommendations. Extensive experiments on the large-scale MOOCCube and PEEK datasets show that AMR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art graph neural network baselines across key metrics such as HR@K and nDCG@K. Further analysis confirms that AMR effectively captures rich path-specific aspect information, allowing more accurate recommendations than those methods that rely solely on predefined metapaths. The code will be available upon accepted.

</details>


### [323] [PieArena: Frontier Language Agents Achieve MBA-Level Negotiation Performance and Reveal Novel Behavioral Differences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05302)
*Chris Zhu,Sasha Cui,Will Sanok Dufallo,Runzhi Jin,Zhen Xu,Linjun Zhang,Daylian Cain*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PieArena introduces a large-scale negotiation benchmark using multi-agent MBA scenarios; frontier LLMs (GPT-5) reach or exceed business-student performance; joint-intentionality scaffolding boosts mid/low-tier LMs more than frontier; cross-model behavioral profiling reveals deception and trust-related differences; robustness and trustworthiness remain key challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess LLM negotiation capabilities requiring strategic reasoning and theory of mind; explore whether frontier models can operate in high-stakes economic settings; understand how scaffolding affects performance across model classes.

Method: Create PieArena benchmark grounded in MBA negotiation course; evaluate multiple LMs with/without joint-intentionality scaffolding; compare against trained students; collect multi-dimensional behavioral metrics (deception, computation accuracy, instruction compliance, reputation) beyond deal outcomes.

Result: Frontier agent GPT-5 matches or outperforms trained business students; joint-intentionality scaffolding yields asymmetric gains with larger improvements for mid-/low-tier LMs; existing cross-model heterogeneity captured; deal-outcome benchmarks miss key behaviors; results indicate capability but robustness/trust issues persist.

Conclusion: Frontier LMs are cognitively capable for high-stakes economics, but investments in robustness, alignment, and trust are necessary for safe deployment.

Abstract: We present an in-depth evaluation of LLMs' ability to negotiate, a central business task that requires strategic reasoning, theory of mind, and economic value creation. To do so, we introduce PieArena, a large-scale negotiation benchmark grounded in multi-agent interactions over realistic scenarios drawn from an MBA negotiation course at an elite business school. We find systematic evidence of AGI-level performance in which a representative frontier agent (GPT-5) matches or outperforms trained business-school students, despite a semester of general negotiation instruction and targeted coaching immediately prior to the task. We further study the effects of joint-intentionality agentic scaffolding and find asymmetric gains, with large improvements for mid- and lower-tier LMs and diminishing returns for frontier LMs. Beyond deal outcomes, PieArena provides a multi-dimensional negotiation behavioral profile, revealing novel cross-model heterogeneity, masked by deal-outcome-only benchmarks, in deception, computation accuracy, instruction compliance, and perceived reputation. Overall, our results suggest that frontier language agents are already intellectually and psychologically capable of deployment in high-stakes economic settings, but deficiencies in robustness and trustworthiness remain open challenges.

</details>


### [324] [ProAct: Agentic Lookahead in Interactive Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05327)
*Yangbin Yu,Mingyu Yang,Junyou Li,Yiming Gao,Feiyu Liu,Yijun Yang,Zichuan Lin,Jiafei Lyu,Yicheng Liu,Zhicong Lu,Deheng Ye,Jie Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ProAct introduces two-stage training (GLAD distillation and MC-Critic) to internalize lookahead and provide a low-variance value signal, improving long-horizon planning in LLM agents; strong results on 2048 and Sokoban, with a 4B model outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents struggle with compounding errors in long-horizon planning due to limited foresight and the computational cost of inference-time search; there is a need for efficient internalized lookahead and stable value estimation to improve policy optimization.

Method: GLAD: supervised fine-tuning on environment-based search trajectories, compressing complex search trees into concise causal reasoning chains; MC-Critic: a plug-and-play auxiliary value estimator that uses lightweight environment rollouts to calibrate value estimates, enhancing policy-gradient methods like PPO and GRPO.

Result: Experiments on stochastic (2048) and deterministic (Sokoban) environments show improved planning accuracy. A 4B-parameter ProAct model outperforms open-source baselines and rivals state-of-the-art closed-source models, with robust generalization to unseen environments.

Conclusion: ProAct effectively improves long-horizon planning for LLM agents by internalizing lookahead through GLAD and stabilizing policy optimization via MC-Critic, reducing reliance on expensive inference-time search; codes and models are released for reproduction.

Abstract: Existing Large Language Model (LLM) agents struggle in interactive environments requiring long-horizon planning, primarily due to compounding errors when simulating future states. To address this, we propose ProAct, a framework that enables agents to internalize accurate lookahead reasoning through a two-stage training paradigm. First, we introduce Grounded LookAhead Distillation (GLAD), where the agent undergoes supervised fine-tuning on trajectories derived from environment-based search. By compressing complex search trees into concise, causal reasoning chains, the agent learns the logic of foresight without the computational overhead of inference-time search. Second, to further refine decision accuracy, we propose the Monte-Carlo Critic (MC-Critic), a plug-and-play auxiliary value estimator designed to enhance policy-gradient algorithms like PPO and GRPO. By leveraging lightweight environment rollouts to calibrate value estimates, MC-Critic provides a low-variance signal that facilitates stable policy optimization without relying on expensive model-based value approximation. Experiments on both stochastic (e.g., 2048) and deterministic (e.g., Sokoban) environments demonstrate that ProAct significantly improves planning accuracy. Notably, a 4B parameter model trained with ProAct outperforms all open-source baselines and rivals state-of-the-art closed-source models, while demonstrating robust generalization to unseen environments. The codes and models are available at https://github.com/GreatX3/ProAct

</details>


### [325] [AgentXRay: White-Boxing Agentic Systems via Workflow Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05353)
*Ruijie Shi,Houbin Zhang,Yuecheng Han,Yuheng Wang,Jingru Fan,Runde Yang,Yufan Dang,Huatao Li,Dewen Liu,Yuan Cheng,Chen Qian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Agentic Workflow Reconstruction (AWR) to extract interpretable, editable stand-in workflows for black-box agentic systems using only input–output access. Introduces AgentXRay, a search-based, MCTS-driven framework with Red-Black pruning to optimize a chain-structured workflow of roles and tool invocations, achieving better proxy similarity and lower token cost than unpruned search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Opaque internal workflows in advanced LLM agents hinder interpretability, controllability, and safety. There is a need for explicit, editable, white-box representations that approximate black-box behavior using only external IO, enabling auditing and modification.

Method: Formulate AWR as a combinatorial optimization over discrete agent roles and tool invocations within a chain-structured workflow space. Use AgentXRay, a search framework leveraging Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) guided by a scoring-based Red-Black Pruning mechanism that balances proxy quality with search depth. Optimize against an observable proxy metric that compares outputs rather than model parameters.

Result: Empirical results across diverse domains show higher proxy similarity and reduced token consumption with pruned search compared to unpruned search, allowing deeper exploration within fixed iteration budgets.

Conclusion: AgentXRay enables editable white-box approximations of black-box agents with comparable outputs, improving interpretability and controllability while maintaining efficiency under resource constraints.

Abstract: Large Language Models have shown strong capabilities in complex problem solving, yet many agentic systems remain difficult to interpret and control due to opaque internal workflows. While some frameworks offer explicit architectures for collaboration, many deployed agentic systems operate as black boxes to users. We address this by introducing Agentic Workflow Reconstruction (AWR), a new task aiming to synthesize an explicit, interpretable stand-in workflow that approximates a black-box system using only input--output access. We propose AgentXRay, a search-based framework that formulates AWR as a combinatorial optimization problem over discrete agent roles and tool invocations in a chain-structured workflow space. Unlike model distillation, AgentXRay produces editable white-box workflows that match target outputs under an observable, output-based proxy metric, without accessing model parameters. To navigate the vast search space, AgentXRay employs Monte Carlo Tree Search enhanced by a scoring-based Red-Black Pruning mechanism, which dynamically integrates proxy quality with search depth. Experiments across diverse domains demonstrate that AgentXRay achieves higher proxy similarity and reduces token consumption compared to unpruned search, enabling deeper workflow exploration under fixed iteration budgets.

</details>


### [326] [PATHWAYS: Evaluating Investigation and Context Discovery in AI Web Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05354)
*Shifat E. Arman,Syed Nazmus Sakib,Tapodhir Karmakar Taton,Nafiul Haque,Shahrear Bin Amin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PATHWAYS shows that 250 multi-step web tasks reveal a persistent gap: agents navigate to relevant pages but rarely retrieve or integrate hidden contextual evidence, with performance collapsing when surface cues are misleading; explicit instructions help context discovery but often reduce accuracy, indicating a tradeoff between procedural compliance and judgement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate whether current web agents can discover, retrieve, and correctly use hidden contextual information across multi-step tasks, and identify failure modes such as hallucinated reasoning, evidence integration failures, and poor judgment override.

Method: Benchmark PATHWAYS with 250 multi-step tasks, evaluating both closed and open models. Measures include navigation to relevant pages, retrieval of decisive hidden evidence, correct integration into final decisions, ability to override misleading surface signals, and effects of explicit instruction on discovery vs. accuracy.

Result: Agents typically locate relevant pages but retrieve decisive hidden evidence only in a small fraction of cases. When tasks require overturning misleading surface-level signals, performance approaches chance. Agents frequently hallucinate investigative reasoning by claiming they used evidence they never accessed. Even when correct context is found, they often fail to integrate it into final decisions. Providing explicit instructions improves context discovery but reduces overall accuracy, revealing a tradeoff between procedural compliance and effective judgement.

Conclusion: Current web agent architectures lack reliable mechanisms for adaptive investigation, evidence integration, and judgement override. Improvements are needed in search strategies to locate hidden evidence, grounding methods to prevent hallucinated reasoning, and decision mechanisms to integrate new context and override misleading signals.

Abstract: We introduce PATHWAYS, a benchmark of 250 multi-step decision tasks that test whether web-based agents can discover and correctly use hidden contextual information. Across both closed and open models, agents typically navigate to relevant pages but retrieve decisive hidden evidence in only a small fraction of cases. When tasks require overturning misleading surface-level signals, performance drops sharply to near chance accuracy. Agents frequently hallucinate investigative reasoning by claiming to rely on evidence they never accessed. Even when correct context is discovered, agents often fail to integrate it into their final decision. Providing more explicit instructions improves context discovery but often reduces overall accuracy, revealing a tradeoff between procedural compliance and effective judgement. Together, these results show that current web agent architectures lack reliable mechanisms for adaptive investigation, evidence integration, and judgement override.

</details>


### [327] [RaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training for Accurate and Efficient LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05367)
*Youngcheon You,Banseok Lee,Minseop Choi,Seonyoung Kim,Hyochan Chong,Changdong Kim,Youngmin Kim,Dongkyu Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RaBiT proposes residual binarization with a hierarchical residual structure derived from a single shared full-precision weight, addressing inter-path adaptation and achieving strong 2-bit accuracy while maintaining efficiency; demonstrates a 4.49× speed-up over full-precision on RTX 4090.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extreme quantization for deploying large language models (LLMs) requires low-bit, hardware-friendly inference. Residual binarization introduces multi-path binary nets but suffers from inter-path adaptation (co-adaptation of parallel binary paths) that degrades error compensation and expressive capacity. Existing heuristics (e.g., path freezing) are insufficient and constrain the solution space.

Method: RaBiT algorithmically enforces a residual hierarchy by sequentially deriving each binary path from a single shared full-precision weight, ensuring each path corrects the error of the preceding one. Robust initialization prioritizes functional preservation over weight-approximation, and quantization-aware training is performed with this sequential path construction.

Result: RaBiT achieves state-of-the-art performance on 2-bit quantization, rivaling hardware-intensive Vector Quantization (VQ) methods, and delivers approximately 4.49× faster inference than full-precision models on an RTX 4090.

Conclusion: By mitigating inter-path adaptation through a residual hierarchy and shared-weight derivation, RaBiT significantly improves the 2-bit accuracy-efficiency frontier for LLM quantization, providing a near-HW-equivalent performance with substantial speedups and establishing a new baseline for ultra-low-bit quantization.

Abstract: Efficient deployment of large language models (LLMs) requires extreme quantization, forcing a critical trade-off between low-bit efficiency and performance. Residual binarization enables hardware-friendly, matmul-free inference by stacking binary ($\pm$1) layers, but is plagued by pathological feature co-adaptation. We identify a key failure mode, which we term inter-path adaptation: during quantization-aware training (QAT), parallel residual binary paths learn redundant features, degrading the error-compensation structure and limiting the expressive capacity of the model. While prior work relies on heuristic workarounds (e.g., path freezing) that constrain the solution space, we propose RaBiT, a novel quantization framework that resolves co-adaptation by algorithmically enforcing a residual hierarchy. Its core mechanism sequentially derives each binary path from a single shared full-precision weight, which ensures that every path corrects the error of the preceding one. This process is stabilized by a robust initialization that prioritizes functional preservation over mere weight approximation. RaBiT redefines the 2-bit accuracy-efficiency frontier: it achieves state-of-the-art performance, rivals even hardware-intensive Vector Quantization (VQ) methods, and delivers a $4.49\times$ inference speed-up over full-precision models on an RTX 4090.

</details>


### [328] [Clinical Validation of Medical-based Large Language Model Chatbots on Ophthalmic Patient Queries with LLM-based Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05381)
*Ting Fang Tan,Kabilan Elangovan,Andreas Pollreisz,Kevin Bryan Dy,Wei Yan Ng,Joy Le Yi Wong,Jin Liyuan,Chrystie Quek Wan Ning,Ashley Shuen Ying Hong,Arun James Thirunavukarasu,Shelley Yin-His Chang,Jie Yao,Dylan Hong,Wang Zhaoran,Amrita Gupta,Daniel SW Ting*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Domain-specific ophthalmology LLMs were evaluated for patient-education QA; Meerkat-7B performed best, MedLLaMA3-v20 showed substantial hallucinations; GPT-4-Turbo aligned well with clinicians; results support feasible LLM benchmarking and the need for hybrid automated–clinician safety frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess safety and accuracy of small-domain ophthalmology LLMs and test feasibility of LLM-based evaluation against clinician grading.

Method: Cross-sectional study with 180 ophthalmology patient queries, each answered by four small LLMs (Meerkat-7B, BioMistral-7B, OpenBioLLM-8B, MedLLaMA3-v20) producing 2160 responses. Models were restricted to ≤10B parameters for resource efficiency. Three ophthalmologists of differing seniority and GPT-4-Turbo evaluated responses using the S.C.O.R.E. framework (Safety/Consensus/Context, Objectivity, Reproducibility, Explainability) on a five-point Likert scale. Agreement between LLM and clinician grading was assessed with Spearman rho, Kendall tau, and kernel density estimates.

Result: Meerkat-7B yielded the highest mean scores (Senior Consultants 3.44, Consultants 4.08, Residents 4.18). MedLLaMA3-v20 performed worst, with 25.5% of responses containing hallucinations or clinically misleading content (including fabricated terminology). GPT-4-Turbo grading showed strong overall alignment with clinicians (Spearman rho 0.80, Kendall tau 0.67), though Senior Consultants graded more conservatively. Overall, LLMs show potential for safe ophthalmic QA but exhibit limitations in clinical depth and consensus. The study supports feasibility of LLM-based benchmarking and suggests a hybrid automated–clinician review framework for safe deployment.

Conclusion: Small-domain ophthalmology LLMs can answer patient queries with potential safety benefits, but current models exhibit hallucinations and inconsistent clinical depth; LLM-based evaluation is feasible for large-scale benchmarking, yet safe deployment will require hybrid, clinician-in-the-loop review.

Abstract: Domain specific large language models are increasingly used to support patient education, triage, and clinical decision making in ophthalmology, making rigorous evaluation essential to ensure safety and accuracy. This study evaluated four small medical LLMs Meerkat-7B, BioMistral-7B, OpenBioLLM-8B, and MedLLaMA3-v20 in answering ophthalmology related patient queries and assessed the feasibility of LLM based evaluation against clinician grading. In this cross sectional study, 180 ophthalmology patient queries were answered by each model, generating 2160 responses. Models were selected for parameter sizes under 10 billion to enable resource efficient deployment. Responses were evaluated by three ophthalmologists of differing seniority and by GPT-4-Turbo using the S.C.O.R.E. framework assessing safety, consensus and context, objectivity, reproducibility, and explainability, with ratings assigned on a five point Likert scale. Agreement between LLM and clinician grading was assessed using Spearman rank correlation, Kendall tau statistics, and kernel density estimate analyses. Meerkat-7B achieved the highest performance with mean scores of 3.44 from Senior Consultants, 4.08 from Consultants, and 4.18 from Residents. MedLLaMA3-v20 performed poorest, with 25.5 percent of responses containing hallucinations or clinically misleading content, including fabricated terminology. GPT-4-Turbo grading showed strong alignment with clinician assessments overall, with Spearman rho of 0.80 and Kendall tau of 0.67, though Senior Consultants graded more conservatively. Overall, medical LLMs demonstrated potential for safe ophthalmic question answering, but gaps remained in clinical depth and consensus, supporting the feasibility of LLM based evaluation for large scale benchmarking and the need for hybrid automated and clinician review frameworks to guide safe clinical deployment.

</details>


### [329] [Advancing Opinion Dynamics Modeling with Neural Diffusion-Convection-Reaction Equation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05403)
*Chenghua Gong,Yihang Jiang,Hao Li,Rui Sun,Juyuan Zhang,Tianjun Gu,Liming Pan,Linyuan Lü*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OPINN: a physics-informed neural ODE framework for opinion dynamics that embeds a diffusion-convection-reaction PDE into neural networks to jointly learn dynamics with physical priors, achieving state-of-the-art forecasting and better interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify mechanistic interpretability with data-driven flexibility in opinion dynamics, addressing polarization and cyberspace security, and overcoming limitations of penalty-based priors and incomplete physical models.

Method: Define a Diffusion-Convection-Reaction system inspired by interacting particle theory; integrate with Neural ODEs to coordinate neural networks with physical priors; develop OPINN, a physics-informed neural framework that enforces PDE-based dynamics rather than relying on penalties.

Result: Evaluated on real-world and synthetic datasets, OPINN achieves state-of-the-art performance in opinion evolution forecasting and improves latent-physics consistency and interpretability.

Conclusion: OPINN provides a coherent cyber-physical-social modeling paradigm, leveraging DCR dynamics and Neural ODEs to enhance interpretability and forecasting; potential for extension to broader multi-level social dynamics.

Abstract: Advanced opinion dynamics modeling is vital for deciphering social behavior, emphasizing its role in mitigating polarization and securing cyberspace. To synergize mechanistic interpretability with data-driven flexibility, recent studies have explored the integration of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for opinion modeling. Despite this promise, existing methods are tailored to incomplete priors, lacking a comprehensive physical system to integrate dynamics from local, global, and endogenous levels. Moreover, penalty-based constraints adopted in existing methods struggle to deeply encode physical priors, leading to optimization pathologies and discrepancy between latent representations and physical transparency. To this end, we offer a physical view to interpret opinion dynamics via Diffusion-Convection-Reaction (DCR) system inspired by interacting particle theory. Building upon the Neural ODEs, we define the neural opinion dynamics to coordinate neural networks with physical priors, and further present the OPINN, a physics-informed neural framework for opinion dynamics modeling. Evaluated on real-world and synthetic datasets, OPINN achieves state-of-the-art performance in opinion evolution forecasting, offering a promising paradigm for the nexus of cyber, physical, and social systems.

</details>


### [330] [H-AdminSim: A Multi-Agent Simulator for Realistic Hospital Administrative Workflows with FHIR Integration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05407)
*Jun-Min Lee,Meong Hi Son,Edward Choi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A comprehensive end-to-end simulation framework (H-AdminSim) for hospital administrative workflows to evaluate LLM-driven automation, combining data generation, multi-agent simulation, rubrics, and FHIR integration as a standardized testbed across settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To capture the complexity of real hospital admin workflows and enable systematic, cross-setting evaluation of LLM-based automation, addressing the gap beyond patient-physician interactions and isolated subtasks.

Method: Develop H-AdminSim: realistic data generation + multi-agent simulation of admin processes; quantitative rubrics; FHIR-integrated, interoperable environment across heterogeneous hospital settings.

Result: Provides a quantitative evaluation framework and unified testbed for comparing LLMs in hospital admin tasks; demonstrates feasibility for end-to-end workflow automation testing (per abstract).

Conclusion: H-AdminSim offers a standardized, interoperable platform for assessing feasibility and performance of LLM-driven hospital administrative automation, facilitating cross-site benchmarking and development.

Abstract: Hospital administration departments handle a wide range of operational tasks and, in large hospitals, process over 10,000 requests per day, driving growing interest in LLM-based automation. However, prior work has focused primarily on patient--physician interactions or isolated administrative subtasks, failing to capture the complexity of real administrative workflows. To address this gap, we propose H-AdminSim, a comprehensive end-to-end simulation framework that combines realistic data generation with multi-agent-based simulation of hospital administrative workflows. These tasks are quantitatively evaluated using detailed rubrics, enabling systematic comparison of LLMs. Through FHIR integration, H-AdminSim provides a unified and interoperable environment for testing administrative workflows across heterogeneous hospital settings, serving as a standardized testbed for assessing the feasibility and performance of LLM-driven administrative automation.

</details>


### [331] [THOR: Inductive Link Prediction over Hyper-Relational Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05424)
*Weijian Yu,Yuhuan Lu,Dingqi Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: THOR is an inductive method for hyper-relational knowledge graphs that uses relation and entity foundation graphs with dual encoders and a transformer decoder to enable fully inductive link prediction and strong cross-dataset performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transductive KG embedding methods struggle to generalize to unseen vocabularies in hyper-relational KGs. There is a need for an inductive approach that captures fundamental, vocabulary-agnostic interactions.

Method: Define relation and entity foundation graphs that are agnostic to specific relations/entities. Use two parallel graph encoders to process these foundation graphs, followed by a transformer decoder with masked training to enable fully-inductive inference. Evaluate on 12 hyper-relational KG datasets; conduct ablations.

Result: THOR outperforms baselines by 66.1% over the best rule-based, 55.9% over semi-inductive, and 20.4% over fully-inductive methods. Ablation studies highlight design factors that capture structural invariance transferable across HKGs for inductive tasks.

Conclusion: Foundation-graph-based inductive framework THOR effectively enables robust hyper-relational link prediction and generalization to unseen vocabularies, with empirical gains and insights from ablations.

Abstract: Knowledge graphs (KGs) have become a key ingredient supporting a variety of applications. Beyond the traditional triplet representation of facts where a relation connects two entities, modern KGs observe an increasing number of hyper-relational facts, where an arbitrary number of qualifiers associated with a triplet provide auxiliary information to further describe the rich semantics of the triplet, which can effectively boost the reasoning performance in link prediction tasks. However, existing link prediction techniques over such hyper-relational KGs (HKGs) mostly focus on a transductive setting, where KG embedding models are learned from the specific vocabulary of a given KG and subsequently can only make predictions within the same vocabulary, limiting their generalizability to previously unseen vocabularies. Against this background, we propose THOR, an inducTive link prediction technique for Hyper-relational knOwledge gRaphs. Specifically, we first introduce both relation and entity foundation graphs, modeling their fundamental inter- and intra-fact interactions in HKGs, which are agnostic to any specific relations and entities. Afterward, THOR is designed to learn from the two foundation graphs with two parallel graph encoders followed by a transformer decoder, which supports efficient masked training and fully-inductive inference. We conduct a thorough evaluation of THOR in hyper-relational link prediction tasks on 12 datasets with different settings. Results show that THOR outperforms a sizable collection of baselines, yielding 66.1%, 55.9%, and 20.4% improvement over the best-performing rule-based, semi-inductive, and fully-inductive techniques, respectively. A series of ablation studies also reveals our key design factors capturing the structural invariance transferable across HKGs for inductive tasks.

</details>


### [332] [M$^2$-Miner: Multi-Agent Enhanced MCTS for Mobile GUI Agent Data Mining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05429)
*Rui Lv,Juncheng Mo,Tianyi Chu,Chen Rao,Hongyi Jing,Jiajie Teng,Jiafu Chen,Shiqi Zhang,Liangzi Ding,Shuo Fang,Huaizhong Lin,Ziqiang Dang,Chenguang Ma,Lei Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: M2-Miner is a low-cost, automated mobile GUI data-mining framework based on Monte Carlo Tree Search that uses a collaborative multi-agent system (InferAgent, OrchestraAgent, JudgeAgent) and techniques like intent recycling and progressive model-in-the-loop training to mine high-quality, diverse intent-trajectory data, achieving state-of-the-art GUI agent performance on mobile benchmarks and releasing the code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the high cost, low quality, and limited diversity of manually annotated or mined GUI interaction data, which hampers training effective GUI agents.

Method: Introduce M2-Miner, the first low-cost automated mobile GUI data-mining framework based on MCTS, featuring a collaborative multi-agent system (InferAgent for guidance, OrchestraAgent for coordination, JudgeAgent for evaluation), an intent recycling strategy to harvest extra trajectories, and a progressive model-in-the-loop training regime to boost mining success.

Result: Extensive experiments show that GUI agents fine-tuned on mined data achieve state-of-the-art performance on multiple mobile GUI benchmarks; the data-mining approach improves efficiency and data richness; authors plan to release the code.

Conclusion: M2-Miner demonstrates that automated, low-cost data mining guided by an intelligent multi-agent framework can produce high-quality, diverse GUI interaction data, enabling scalable training of GUI agents.

Abstract: Graphical User Interface (GUI) agent is pivotal to advancing intelligent human-computer interaction paradigms. Constructing powerful GUI agents necessitates the large-scale annotation of high-quality user-behavior trajectory data (i.e., intent-trajectory pairs) for training. However, manual annotation methods and current GUI agent data mining approaches typically face three critical challenges: high construction cost, poor data quality, and low data richness. To address these issues, we propose M$^2$-Miner, the first low-cost and automated mobile GUI agent data-mining framework based on Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS). For better data mining efficiency and quality, we present a collaborative multi-agent framework, comprising InferAgent, OrchestraAgent, and JudgeAgent for guidance, acceleration, and evaluation. To further enhance the efficiency of mining and enrich intent diversity, we design an intent recycling strategy to extract extra valuable interaction trajectories. Additionally, a progressive model-in-the-loop training strategy is introduced to improve the success rate of data mining. Extensive experiments have demonstrated that the GUI agent fine-tuned using our mined data achieves state-of-the-art performance on several commonly used mobile GUI benchmarks. Our work will be released to facilitate the community research.

</details>


### [333] [Day-Ahead Electricity Price Forecasting for Volatile Markets Using Foundation Models with Regularization Strategy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05430)
*Kritchanat Ponyuenyong,Pengyu Tu,Jia Wei Tan,Wei Soon Cheong,Jamie Ng Suat Ling,Lianlian Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TSFMs outperform traditional models for day-ahead electricity price forecasting in volatile markets, aided by a spike regularization strategy; evaluated on half-hourly Singapore wholesale data using exogenous features; TTMs, MOIRAI, MOMENT, and TimesFM surpass ARIMA, LSTM, and CNN-LSTM with up to 37.4% MAPE improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Electricity price signals are highly volatile and nonlinear, making accurate day-ahead forecasting difficult. While time series foundation models (TSFMs) excel in general TS tasks, their effectiveness in day-ahead EPF, especially under volatility, remains underexplored. A spike regularization approach is proposed to enhance TSFM performance.

Method: Evaluate a broad set of TSFMs (Tiny Time Mixers, MOIRAI, MOMENT, TimesFM) against traditional models (ARIMA, LSTM, CNN-LSTM) using half-hourly wholesale market data from Singapore; incorporate exogenous variables (weather, calendar) where applicable; apply a spike regularization strategy and assess forecasting accuracy via MAPE across multiple evaluation settings.

Result: TSFMs consistently outperform traditional approaches, delivering substantial gains in forecast accuracy with improvements up to 37.4% in MAPE across diverse settings; exogenous factors are integrated where applicable, underscoring practical forecasting improvements in volatile markets.

Conclusion: The results demonstrate that TSFMs, aided by spike regularization, offer robust and practical improvements for day-ahead EPF in volatile markets, providing actionable guidance for market participants on forecast accuracy and decision-making.

Abstract: Electricity price forecasting (EPF) is essential for energy markets stakeholders (e.g. grid operators, energy traders, policymakers) but remains challenging due to the inherent volatility and nonlinearity of price signals. Traditional statistical and deep learning (DL) models often struggle to capture complex temporal dependencies and integrate heterogeneous data effectively. While time series foundation models (TSFMs) have shown strong performance in general time series forecasting tasks, such as traffic forecasting and weather forecasting. However, their effectiveness in day-ahead EPF, particularly in volatile markets, remains underexplored. This paper presents a spike regularization strategy and evaluates a wide range of TSFMs, including Tiny Time Mixers (TTMs), MOIRAI, MOMENT, and TimesFM, against traditional statistical and DL models such as Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA), Long-short Term Memory (LSTM), and Convolutional Neural Network - LSTM (CNN-LSTM) using half-hourly wholesale market data with volatile trends in Singapore. Exogenous factors (e.g. weather and calendar variables) are also incorporated into models where applicable. Results demonstrate that TSFMs consistently outperform traditional approaches, achieving up to 37.4% improvement in MAPE across various evaluation settings. The findings offer practical guidance for improving forecast accuracy and decision-making in volatile electricity markets.

</details>


### [334] [Refine and Purify: Orthogonal Basis Optimization with Null-Space Denoising for Conditional Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05464)
*Jiaquan Wang,Yan Lyu,Chen Li,Yuheng Jia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OD-CRL proposes Adaptive Orthogonal Basis Optimization (AOBO) and Null-Space Denoising Projection (NSDP) to produce robust, orthogonal conditional representations, achieving state-of-the-art results on customized clustering, classification, and retrieval tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address two main limitations in prior conditional representation learning: (1) sensitivity to the choice of subspace basis spanning the LLM-generated text basis, and (2) vulnerability to interference from non-target subspaces.

Method: AOBO constructs orthogonal semantic bases using singular value decomposition with curvature-based truncation to select informative modes. NSDP projects embeddings onto the null space of irrelevant subspaces to suppress cross-subspace interference.

Result: Extensive experiments on customized clustering, customized classification, and customized retrieval demonstrate that OD-CRL achieves new state-of-the-art performance with better generalization compared with existing methods.

Conclusion: The framework effectively mitigates basis sensitivity and inter-subspace interference, with AOBO and NSDP providing complementary mechanisms for robust conditional representations and broad applicability to customized tasks; potential future work may explore scalability and deeper ablations.

Abstract: Conditional representation learning aims to extract criterion-specific features for customized tasks. Recent studies project universal features onto the conditional feature subspace spanned by an LLM-generated text basis to obtain conditional representations. However, such methods face two key limitations: sensitivity to subspace basis and vulnerability to inter-subspace interference. To address these challenges, we propose OD-CRL, a novel framework integrating Adaptive Orthogonal Basis Optimization (AOBO) and Null-Space Denoising Projection (NSDP). Specifically, AOBO constructs orthogonal semantic bases via singular value decomposition with a curvature-based truncation. NSDP suppresses non-target semantic interference by projecting embeddings onto the null space of irrelevant subspaces. Extensive experiments conducted across customized clustering, customized classification, and customized retrieval tasks demonstrate that OD-CRL achieves a new state-of-the-art performance with superior generalization.

</details>


### [335] [ALIVE: Awakening LLM Reasoning via Adversarial Learning and Instructive Verbal Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05472)
*Yiwen Duan,Jing Ye,Xinpei Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ALIVE reframes alignment as intrinsic reasoning acquisition, reducing reliance on external scalar rewards by embedding evaluative criteria into a single policy via adversarial learning and instructive feedback. This yields improved accuracy, cross-domain generalization, and self-correction without human supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward bottlenecks in RL for LLMs: scalar rewards are costly to scale, brittle across domains, and ignore underlying logical structure. A self-contained, self-improving reasoning capability is desired to enable scalable general-purpose reasoning.

Method: Introduce ALIVE (Adversarial Learning with Instructive Verbal Evaluation), a hands-free alignment framework that unifies problem posing, solving, and judging within one policy. Coupled adversarial learning with instructive verbal feedback to internalize evaluative criteria from raw corpora, enabling endogenous reasoning.

Result: Across mathematical reasoning, code generation, and general logical inference tasks, ALIVE improves accuracy with identical data/compute, enhances cross-domain generalization, and increases self-correction rates, suggesting a self-sustaining trajectory of capability growth.

Conclusion: ALIVE offers a scalable foundation for general-purpose reasoning alignment without human-in-the-loop supervision by internalizing evaluation criteria and aligning reasoning processes within a single policy.

Abstract: The quest for expert-level reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs) has been hampered by a persistent \textit{reward bottleneck}: traditional reinforcement learning (RL) relies on scalar rewards that are \textbf{costly} to scale, \textbf{brittle} across domains, and \textbf{blind} to the underlying logic of a solution. This reliance on external, impoverished signals prevents models from developing a deep, self-contained understanding of reasoning principles. We introduce \textbf{ALIVE} (\emph{Adversarial Learning with Instructive Verbal Evaluation}), a hands-free alignment framework that moves beyond scalar reward optimization toward intrinsic reasoning acquisition. Grounded in the principle of \emph{Cognitive Synergy}, ALIVE unifies problem posing, solving, and judging within a single policy model to internalize the logic of correctness. By coupling adversarial learning with instructive verbal feedback, ALIVE enables models to internalize evaluative criteria directly from raw corpora, effectively transforming external critiques into an endogenous reasoning faculty. Empirical evaluations across mathematical reasoning, code generation, and general logical inference benchmarks demonstrate that ALIVE consistently mitigates reward signal limitations. With identical data and compute, it achieves accuracy gains, markedly improved cross-domain generalization, and higher self-correction rates. These results indicate that the reasoning trinity fosters a self-sustaining trajectory of capability growth, positioning ALIVE as a scalable foundation for general-purpose reasoning alignment without human-in-the-loop supervision.

</details>


### [336] [Phi-Former: A Pairwise Hierarchical Approach for Compound-Protein Interactions Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05479)
*Zhe Wang,Zijing Liu,Chencheng Xu,Yuan Yao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Phi-former introduces a hierarchical, motif-aware pairwise learning framework for predicting compound-protein interactions, modeling interactions at atom-atom, motif-motif, and atom-motif levels to improve accuracy and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current CPI models are largely atom-centric and may miss biologically meaningful motif-level recognition units (functional groups). There is a need to align computational CPI models with chemical realities and improve interpretability for drug design.

Method: Phi-former represents molecules and proteins hierarchically and employs a pairwise pre-training framework to jointly model interactions across three levels: atom-atom, motif-motif, and atom-motif. It includes intra-level and inter-level learning pipelines to allow interaction information to mutually reinforce across levels.

Result: The method achieves superior performance on CPI-related tasks and enables interpretable explanations, including case studies that identify specific atoms or motifs activated in CPIs, aiding rational drug design and precision medicine.

Conclusion: Modeling interactions across motif and atomic levels yields more biologically faithful CPI predictions and provides actionable insights for drug design; the approach can enhance understanding of molecular recognition in CPIs and support precision medicine applications.

Abstract: Drug discovery remains time-consuming, labor-intensive, and expensive, often requiring years and substantial investment per drug candidate. Predicting compound-protein interactions (CPIs) is a critical component in this process, enabling the identification of molecular interactions between drug candidates and target proteins. Recent deep learning methods have successfully modeled CPIs at the atomic level, achieving improved efficiency and accuracy over traditional energy-based approaches. However, these models do not always align with chemical realities, as molecular fragments (motifs or functional groups) typically serve as the primary units of biological recognition and binding. In this paper, we propose Phi-former, a pairwise hierarchical interaction representation learning method that addresses this gap by incorporating the biological role of motifs in CPIs. Phi-former represents compounds and proteins hierarchically and employs a pairwise pre-training framework to model interactions systematically across atom-atom, motif-motif, and atom-motif levels, reflecting how biological systems recognize molecular partners. We design intra-level and inter-level learning pipelines that make different interaction levels mutually beneficial. Experimental results demonstrate that Phi-former achieves superior performance on CPI-related tasks. A case study shows that our method accurately identifies specific atoms or motifs activated in CPIs, providing interpretable model explanations. These insights may guide rational drug design and support precision medicine applications.

</details>


### [337] [SDFP: Speculative Decoding with FIT-Pruned Models for Training-Free and Plug-and-Play LLM Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05499)
*Hanyu Wei,Zunhai Su,Peng Lu,Chao Li,Spandan Tiwari,Ashish Sirasao,Yuhan Dong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SDFP is a training-free, plug-and-play method that constructs a draft model for speculative decoding by pruning an LLM using Fisher Information Trace (FIT); it yields faster decoding without changing the model's output.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive decoding in LLMs introduces significant latency in multimedia applications. Existing speculative decoding needs dedicated draft models or expensive tuning/training, limiting deployment.

Method: Compute layer sensitivity via Fisher Information Trace (FIT) and prune low-impact layers to form a compact draft model that remains compatible for speculative verification; no additional training or hyperparameter tuning is required.

Result: Achieves 1.32x–1.5x decoding speedup across benchmarks while preserving the original model's output distribution; requires no extra training or drafts to maintain, enabling rapid deployment.

Conclusion: SDFP provides a training-free, deployment-friendly way to accelerate decoding with speculative frameworks by constructing an effective draft via FIT-based layer pruning, without altering model outputs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) underpin interactive multimedia applications such as captioning, retrieval, recommendation, and creative content generation, yet their autoregressive decoding incurs substantial latency. Speculative decoding reduces latency using a lightweight draft model, but deployment is often limited by the cost and complexity of acquiring, tuning, and maintaining an effective draft model. Recent approaches usually require auxiliary training or specialization, and even training-free methods incur costly search or optimization. We propose SDFP, a fully training-free and plug-and-play framework that builds the draft model via Fisher Information Trace (FIT)-based layer pruning of a given LLM. Using layer sensitivity as a proxy for output perturbation, SDFP removes low-impact layers to obtain a compact draft while preserving compatibility with the original model for standard speculative verification. SDFP needs no additional training, hyperparameter tuning, or separately maintained drafts, enabling rapid, deployment-friendly draft construction. Across benchmarks, SDFP delivers 1.32x-1.5x decoding speedup without altering the target model's output distribution, supporting low-latency multimedia applications.

</details>


### [338] [A Unified Multimodal Framework for Dataset Construction and Model-Based Diagnosis of Ameloblastoma](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05515)
*Ajo Babu George,Anna Mariam John,Athul Anoop,Balu Bhasuran*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: New multimodal ameloblastoma dataset with annotated imaging (radiology, histology, intraoral) and structured case-data; NLP extracts features; multimodal model for variant classification, recurrence risk, and surgical planning; improved performance over baseline like MultiCaRe; supports clinical deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses scarcity and formatting inconsistency of existing maxillofacial pathology datasets; aims to enable robust training and clinical decision support for ameloblastoma.

Method: Curated multimodal dataset integrating annotated radiological, histopathological, and intraoral images; structured data derived from case reports via NLP; domain-specific preprocessing and augmentation for image data; development of a multimodal deep learning model that accepts clinical inputs (presenting complaint, age, gender) during deployment.

Result: Variant classification accuracy improved from 46.2% to 65.9%; abnormal tissue detection F1-score improved from 43.0% to 90.3%; benchmarked against resources like MultiCaRe; demonstrates robust dataset and adaptable multimodal AI framework for patient-specific decision support.

Conclusion: The work provides a robust, openly usable multimodal dataset and AI framework that enhances personalized decision support in ameloblastoma management and planning.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI)-enabled diagnostics in maxillofacial pathology require structured, high-quality multimodal datasets. However, existing resources provide limited ameloblastoma coverage and lack the format consistency needed for direct model training. We present a newly curated multimodal dataset specifically focused on ameloblastoma, integrating annotated radiological, histopathological, and intraoral clinical images with structured data derived from case reports. Natural language processing techniques were employed to extract clinically relevant features from textual reports, while image data underwent domain specific preprocessing and augmentation. Using this dataset, a multimodal deep learning model was developed to classify ameloblastoma variants, assess behavioral patterns such as recurrence risk, and support surgical planning. The model is designed to accept clinical inputs such as presenting complaint, age, and gender during deployment to enhance personalized inference. Quantitative evaluation demonstrated substantial improvements; variant classification accuracy increased from 46.2 percent to 65.9 percent, and abnormal tissue detection F1-score improved from 43.0 percent to 90.3 percent. Benchmarked against resources like MultiCaRe, this work advances patient-specific decision support by providing both a robust dataset and an adaptable multimodal AI framework.

</details>


### [339] [Learning Event-Based Shooter Models from Virtual Reality Experiments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06023)
*Christopher A. McClurg,Alan R. Wagner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Data-driven discrete-event simulation trained on VR study data to evaluate scalable, autonomous school-security interventions (robot-based shooter strategy).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VR-based evaluation of school security is costly and non-scalable; learning effective interventions requires many training episodes; a scalable surrogate enables iterative development.

Method: Construct a discrete-event simulator where shooter movement and in-region actions are modeled as stochastic processes learned from VR participant data; validate by reproducing empirical patterns; apply to test robot-based intervention; enable learning via simulation.

Result: Simulator reproduces key empirical patterns and supports scalable evaluation and learning of intervention strategies infeasible with human subjects.

Conclusion: The workflow offers a high-to-mid fidelity simulation surrogate for developing and evaluating autonomous school-security interventions.

Abstract: Virtual reality (VR) has emerged as a powerful tool for evaluating school security measures in high-risk scenarios such as school shootings, offering experimental control and high behavioral fidelity. However, assessing new interventions in VR requires recruiting new participant cohorts for each condition, making large-scale or iterative evaluation difficult. These limitations are especially restrictive when attempting to learn effective intervention strategies, which typically require many training episodes. To address this challenge, we develop a data-driven discrete-event simulator (DES) that models shooter movement and in-region actions as stochastic processes learned from participant behavior in VR studies. We use the simulator to examine the impact of a robot-based shooter intervention strategy. Once shown to reproduce key empirical patterns, the DES enables scalable evaluation and learning of intervention strategies that are infeasible to train directly with human subjects. Overall, this work demonstrates a high-to-mid fidelity simulation workflow that provides a scalable surrogate for developing and evaluating autonomous school-security interventions.

</details>


### [340] [Split Personality Training: Revealing Latent Knowledge Through Alternate Personalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05532)
*Florian Dietz,William Wale,Oscar Gilg,Robert McCarthy,Felix Michalak,Gustavo Ewbank Rodrigues Danon,Miguelito de Guzman,Dietrich Klakow*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPT inserts a dormant second 'honest persona' via LoRA that is activated after a response to audit the model, achieving high detection accuracy on a misalignment benchmark and revealing latent biases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auditing misalignment in LLMs is hard: black-box methods struggle to separate misbehavior from benign outputs, and mechanistic interpretability scales poorly with model capabilities. A scalable introspective mechanism is needed to expose hidden misalignment.

Method: Fine-tune a second 'honest persona' into LoRA that remains inactive during normal operation. After the main model responds, activate the LoRA adapter and insert a trigger string to enable the honest persona to review the response while accessing the main model's latent states.

Result: On the Anthropic Auditing Game Model Organism using Llama-3.3-70B, SPT achieves 96% overall accuracy in detecting misalignment, while Anthropic reports near 0% accuracy. The honest persona uncovers latent knowledge inaccessible to external observers, including biases the compromised model was trained on.

Conclusion: SPT presents a scalable auditing approach that can reveal hidden misalignment and latent knowledge. Findings indicate substantial improvements over reported baselines, but replication and generalization across models and tasks remain necessary.

Abstract: Detecting misalignment in large language models is challenging because models may learn to conceal misbehavior during training. Standard auditing techniques fall short: black-box methods often cannot distinguish misaligned outputs from benign ones, and mechanistic interpretability does not scale with model capabilities. We introduce Split Personality Training (SPT), which fine-tunes a second ``honest persona'' into LoRA parameters that remain inactive during normal operation. After the main model responds, we activate the LoRA adapter and insert a trigger string, enabling the honest persona to review the response while accessing the main model's latent states. We test our method on the Anthropic Auditing Game Model Organism, a benchmark where Llama-3.3-70B is trained to exploit reward hacks while concealing this behavior. SPT achieves 96% overall accuracy, whereas Anthropic reports near 0% accuracy. The honest persona reveals latent knowledge inaccessible to external observers, such as the fictional biases the compromised model was trained on.

</details>


### [341] [Conditional Diffusion Guidance under Hard Constraint: A Stochastic Analysis Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05533)
*Zhengyi Guo,Wenpin Tang,Renyuan Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces a principled framework for hard-constraint conditional diffusion using Doob's h-transform; augments pretrained diffusion with a drift correction derived from the log-gradient of a conditioning function, with two off-policy martingale-based learning algorithms and non-asymptotic guarantees for constraint satisfaction and rare-event sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In safety-critical applications and rare-event simulation, guaranteeing that generated samples satisfy prescribed events with probability one is essential, which is not ensured by soft or reward-based guidance methods.

Method: Interprets diffusion models probabilistically and employs Doob's h-transform with a drift correction term (log-gradient of the conditioning function). The pretrained score network remains unchanged. Two off-policy learning algorithms—martingale loss and martingale-covariation loss—estimate h and its gradient using trajectories from the pretrained model, leveraging martingale and quadratic-variation identities.

Result: Derives conditional samplers with non-asymptotic guarantees in total variation and Wasserstein distances, detailing how score approximation and guidance estimation errors affect performance. Numerical experiments demonstrate effective enforcement of hard constraints and generation of rare-event samples.

Conclusion: The work provides a principled, off-policy framework for hard-constrained conditional diffusion, achieving guaranteed constraint satisfaction and robust rare-event sampling, with theoretical guarantees and practical algorithms that rely only on trajectories from the pretrained model.

Abstract: We study conditional generation in diffusion models under hard constraints, where generated samples must satisfy prescribed events with probability one. Such constraints arise naturally in safety-critical applications and in rare-event simulation, where soft or reward-based guidance methods offer no guarantee of constraint satisfaction. Building on a probabilistic interpretation of diffusion models, we develop a principled conditional diffusion guidance framework based on Doob's h-transform, martingale representation and quadratic variation process. Specifically, the resulting guided dynamics augment a pretrained diffusion with an explicit drift correction involving the logarithmic gradient of a conditioning function, without modifying the pretrained score network. Leveraging martingale and quadratic-variation identities, we propose two novel off-policy learning algorithms based on a martingale loss and a martingale-covariation loss to estimate h and its gradient using only trajectories from the pretrained model. We provide non-asymptotic guarantees for the resulting conditional sampler in both total variation and Wasserstein distances, explicitly characterizing the impact of score approximation and guidance estimation errors. Numerical experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods in enforcing hard constraints and generating rare-event samples.

</details>


### [342] [Reasoning-guided Collaborative Filtering with Language Models for Explainable Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05544)
*Fahad Anwaar,Adil Mehmood Khan,Muhammad Khalid,Usman Zia,Kezhi Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RGCF-XRec is a single-step explainable sequential recommender that injects reasoning-guided collaborative filtering knowledge into a language model, achieving improved recommendation and explanation quality with a lightweight backbone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the separation between recommendation and explanation in LLM-based systems and to leverage collaborative signals within a unified, memory-efficient architecture.

Method: A hybrid RGCF-XRec framework that (i) augments CF knowledge through reasoning-guided contextual prompting, (ii) uses a four-dimension scoring mechanism (coherence, completeness, relevance, consistency) to curate high-quality CF reasoning traces, and (iii) employs a unified representation learning network to fuse collaborative and semantic signals, conditioning a lightweight LLaMA 3.2-3B LLM for explainable sequential recommendations.

Result: Empirical results show consistent improvements: HR@10 gains of 7.38% (Sports) and 4.59% (Toys); ROUGE-L gains of 8.02% and 3.49%; reduced cold-start gap (14.5%) and warm-start gap (11.9%); zero-shot HR@5 gains of 18.54% (Beauty) and 23.16% (Toys). Training efficiency achieved with a lightweight LLaMA 3.2-3B backbone across 642,503 interactions from Amazon, Sports, Toys, and Beauty datasets.

Conclusion: RGCF-XRec demonstrates that integrating reasoning-guided CF knowledge into LLMs with a structured prompt and unified representation yields superior explainable sequential recommendations, improves robustness and generalization (including zero-shot and cold/warm settings), and remains scalable for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit potential for explainable recommendation systems but overlook collaborative signals, while prevailing methods treat recommendation and explanation as separate tasks, resulting in a memory footprint. We present RGCF-XRec, a hybrid framework that introduces reasoning-guided collaborative filtering (CF) knowledge into a language model to deliver explainable sequential recommendations in a single step. Theoretical grounding and empirical findings reveal that RGCF-XRec offers three key merits over leading CF-aware LLM-based methods: (1) reasoning-guided augmentation of CF knowledge through contextual prompting to discover latent preferences and interpretable reasoning paths; (2) an efficient scoring mechanism based on four dimensions: coherence, completeness, relevance, and consistency to mitigate noisy CF reasoning traces and retain high-quality explanations; (3) a unified representation learning network that encodes collaborative and semantic signals, enabling a structured prompt to condition the LLM for explainable sequential recommendation. RGCF-XRec demonstrates consistent improvements across Amazon datasets, Sports, Toys, and Beauty, comprising 642,503 user-item interactions. It improves HR@10 by 7.38\% in Sports and 4.59\% in Toys, along with ROUGE-L by 8.02\% and 3.49\%, respectively. It reduces the cold warm performance gap, achieving overall gains of 14.5\% in cold-start and 11.9\% in warm start scenarios, and enhances zero-shot HR@5 by 18.54\% in Beauty and 23.16\% in Toys, highlighting effective generalization and robustness. Moreover, RGCF-XRec achieves training efficiency with a lightweight LLaMA 3.2-3B backbone, ensuring scalability for real-world applications.

</details>


### [343] [TangramSR: Can Vision-Language Models Reason in Continuous Geometric Space?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05570)
*Yikun Zong,Cheston Tan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A training-free test-time verifier-refiner framework enables self-guided iterative refinement for Tangram-like geometric reasoning in Vision-Language Models (VLMs), using in-context learning and reward-guided feedback to substantially boost IoU (0.63→0.932 on medium-triangle cases) without model retraining, highlighting human-inspired strategies to close the gap to human performance on continuous spatial tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address systematic failures of VLMs in continuous geometric reasoning and investigate whether models can improve predictions at test time without updating parameters, inspired by human Tangram solving through trial-and-error, observation, and correction.

Method: A training-free verifier-refiner agent is employed at test time, combining in-context learning (ICL) with reward-guided feedback loops to perform recursive refinement based on geometric-consistency feedback; operates as a self-improvement loop without any parameter updates; evaluated on Tangram-like tasks (single-piece, two-piece, medium-triangle) across multiple VLM baselines.

Result: Baseline IoU performance is low (0.41 on single-piece, 0.23 on two-piece). The verifier-refiner achieves IoU improvements from 0.63 to 0.932 on medium-triangle cases without retraining, illustrating significant gains from iterative refinement.

Conclusion: Incorporating human-inspired iterative refinement through ICL and reward loops enables substantial, training-free improvements in geometric reasoning for VLMs, advancing the viability of self-improving AI in continuous spatial domains.

Abstract: Humans excel at spatial reasoning tasks like Tangram puzzle assembly through cognitive processes involving mental rotation, iterative refinement, and visual feedback. Inspired by how humans solve Tangram puzzles through trial-and-error, observation, and correction, we design a framework that models these human cognitive mechanisms. However, comprehensive experiments across five representative Vision-Language Models (VLMs) reveal systematic failures in continuous geometric reasoning: average IoU of only 0.41 on single-piece tasks, dropping to 0.23 on two-piece composition, far below human performance where children can complete Tangram tasks successfully. This paper addresses a fundamental challenge in self-improving AI: can models iteratively refine their predictions at test time without parameter updates? We introduce a test-time self-refinement framework that combines in-context learning (ICL) with reward-guided feedback loops, inspired by human cognitive processes. Our training-free verifier-refiner agent applies recursive refinement loops that iteratively self-refine predictions based on geometric consistency feedback, achieving IoU improvements from 0.63 to 0.932 on medium-triangle cases without any model retraining. This demonstrates that incorporating human-inspired iterative refinement mechanisms through ICL and reward loops can substantially enhance geometric reasoning in VLMs, moving self-improving AI from promise to practice in continuous spatial domains. Our work is available at this anonymous link https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TangramVLM-F582/.

</details>


### [344] [Emulating Aggregate Human Choice Behavior and Biases with GPT Conversational Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05597)
*Stephen Pilli,Vivek Nallur*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs can predict and reproduce individual-level cognitive biases under interactive dialogue; GPT-4 and GPT-5 show varying alignment with human bias dynamics; implications for bias-aware AI design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether LLMs can model bias at the individual level and recapitulate human dynamics when contextual factors (e.g., cognitive load) interact with biases, bridging human experiments and LLM simulations.

Method: Human experiment (N=1100) with a chatbot-mediated decision task in simple vs. complex dialogues; collect demographics and dialogue transcripts; simulate conditions with GPT-4 and GPT-5 using participant data; assess bias reproduction and model-to-human alignment; compare models.

Result: LLMs reproduced human biases with high precision; robust biases observed in humans and mirrored by LLMs; differences in alignment between GPT-4 and GPT-5; model-dependent differences in bias dynamics.

Conclusion: Findings imply LLMs can model and reproduce individual-level biases in interactive settings; informs design and evaluation of adaptive, bias-aware LLM applications and highlights model-dependent differences in bias dynamics.

Abstract: Cognitive biases often shape human decisions. While large language models (LLMs) have been shown to reproduce well-known biases, a more critical question is whether LLMs can predict biases at the individual level and emulate the dynamics of biased human behavior when contextual factors, such as cognitive load, interact with these biases. We adapted three well-established decision scenarios into a conversational setting and conducted a human experiment (N=1100). Participants engaged with a chatbot that facilitates decision-making through simple or complex dialogues. Results revealed robust biases. To evaluate how LLMs emulate human decision-making under similar interactive conditions, we used participant demographics and dialogue transcripts to simulate these conditions with LLMs based on GPT-4 and GPT-5. The LLMs reproduced human biases with precision. We found notable differences between models in how they aligned human behavior. This has important implications for designing and evaluating adaptive, bias-aware LLM-based AI systems in interactive contexts.

</details>


### [345] [BhashaSetu: Cross-Lingual Knowledge Transfer from High-Resource to Extreme Low-Resource Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05599)
*Subhadip Maji,Arnab Bhattacharya*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Graph-Enhanced Token Representation (GETR) uses a GNN-based cross-lingual transfer to boost NLP performance for truly low-resource languages, yielding large gains over baselines on POS tagging, sentiment classification, and NER; includes transfer-mechanism analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-resource languages suffer from data scarcity and limited linguistic resources; cross-lingual knowledge transfer from high-resource languages is a promising direction to mitigate performance gaps.

Method: Introduce GETR, a graph neural network-based cross-lingual transfer method that constructs a cross-lingual token graph to propagate knowledge from high-resource languages to low-resource languages; compare against two baselines: (a) augmentation in hidden layers, and (b) token embedding transfer via token translation.

Result: GETR significantly outperforms existing multilingual and cross-lingual baselines, achieving 13 percentage-point improvements on POS tagging for truly low-resource languages (Mizo, Khasi) and 20 and 27 percentage-point improvements in macro-F1 for Marathi, Bangla, Malayalam across sentiment classification and NER tasks; includes detailed analysis of transfer mechanisms and key contributing factors.

Conclusion: Graph-based cross-lingual transfer via GETR is effective for truly low-resource languages across sentence-level and word-level tasks; the work identifies transfer mechanisms and practical factors driving success, offering a roadmap for future cross-lingual transfer research.

Abstract: Despite remarkable advances in natural language processing, developing effective systems for low-resource languages remains a formidable challenge, with performances typically lagging far behind high-resource counterparts due to data scarcity and insufficient linguistic resources. Cross-lingual knowledge transfer has emerged as a promising approach to address this challenge by leveraging resources from high-resource languages. In this paper, we investigate methods for transferring linguistic knowledge from high-resource languages to low-resource languages, where the number of labeled training instances is in hundreds. We focus on sentence-level and word-level tasks. We introduce a novel method, GETR (Graph-Enhanced Token Representation) for cross-lingual knowledge transfer along with two adopted baselines (a) augmentation in hidden layers and (b) token embedding transfer through token translation. Experimental results demonstrate that our GNN-based approach significantly outperforms existing multilingual and cross-lingual baseline methods, achieving 13 percentage point improvements on truly low-resource languages (Mizo, Khasi) for POS tagging, and 20 and 27 percentage point improvements in macro-F1 on simulated low-resource languages (Marathi, Bangla, Malayalam) across sentiment classification and NER tasks respectively. We also present a detailed analysis of the transfer mechanisms and identify key factors that contribute to successful knowledge transfer in this linguistic context.

</details>


### [346] [Reactive Knowledge Representation and Asynchronous Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05625)
*Simon Kohaut,Benedict Flade,Julian Eggert,Kristian Kersting,Devendra Singh Dhami*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Resin and Reactive Circuits enable reactive, asynchronous probabilistic reasoning with memoized subproblems, yielding massive speedups for streaming, real-time inference in dynamic environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exact inference in complex probabilistic models is computationally expensive, a major challenge for autonomous agents requiring real-time belief updates. Traditional inference re-evaluates the entire model upon changes and often ignores heterogeneous update rates across information streams.

Method: Introduce Resin, a probabilistic programming language that merges probabilistic logic with reactive programming. Propose Reactive Circuits (RCs) as a semantics and meta-structure built over Algebraic Circuits and asynchronous data streams. RCs are time-dynamic directed acyclic graphs that adapt based on input volatility. Compute by partitioning tasks according to the Frequency of Change and memoize subproblems to re-evaluate only components affected by new information.

Result: In high-fidelity drone swarm simulations, the approach achieves several orders of magnitude speedup compared to frequency-agnostic inference. RCs’ adaptations capture environmental dynamics, reducing latency and enabling reactive real-time reasoning.

Conclusion: Adaptive, memoized decomposition guided by input dynamics yields scalable, exact inference for streaming probabilistic models. RCs provide an explicit, reactive framework that efficiently handles heterogeneous update rates in dynamic environments.

Abstract: Exact inference in complex probabilistic models often incurs prohibitive computational costs. This challenge is particularly acute for autonomous agents in dynamic environments that require frequent, real-time belief updates. Existing methods are often inefficient for ongoing reasoning, as they re-evaluate the entire model upon any change, failing to exploit that real-world information streams have heterogeneous update rates. To address this, we approach the problem from a reactive, asynchronous, probabilistic reasoning perspective. We first introduce Resin (Reactive Signal Inference), a probabilistic programming language that merges probabilistic logic with reactive programming. Furthermore, to provide efficient and exact semantics for Resin, we propose Reactive Circuits (RCs). Formulated as a meta-structure over Algebraic Circuits and asynchronous data streams, RCs are time-dynamic Directed Acyclic Graphs that autonomously adapt themselves based on the volatility of input signals. In high-fidelity drone swarm simulations, our approach achieves several orders of magnitude of speedup over frequency-agnostic inference. We demonstrate that RCs' structural adaptations successfully capture environmental dynamics, significantly reducing latency and facilitating reactive real-time reasoning. By partitioning computations based on the estimated Frequency of Change in the asynchronous inputs, large inference tasks can be decomposed into individually memoized sub-problems. This ensures that only the specific components of a model affected by new information are re-evaluated, drastically reducing redundant computation in streaming contexts.

</details>


### [347] [Generative Ontology: When Structured Knowledge Learns to Create](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05636)
*Benny Cheung*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Generative Ontology merges ontological grammar with LLM creativity using executable schemas and a multi-agent, retrieval-augmented pipeline, demonstrated by GameGrammar and applicable to other domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the mismatch between structural validity in traditional ontologies and hallucination-prone creativity of LLMs by integrating grammar-driven constraints with design-inspired AI generation.

Method: Encode domain knowledge as executable Pydantic schemas constrained by DSPy signatures; implement a multi-agent pipeline with specialized roles (Mechanics Architect, Theme Weaver, Balance Critic) each with professional constraints (anxiety); employ retrieval-augmented generation and iterative validation to ensure coherence between mechanisms and components.

Result: Produced complete, playable tabletop game designs with mechanisms, components, victory conditions, and setup instructions that satisfy ontological constraints while exhibiting genuine creativity.
