Conclusion: The approach generalizes to any domain with expert vocabulary, validity constraints, and exemplars (e.g., music, software, culinary arts); constraints enable creativity by providing a grammar that enables structured generation.

Abstract: Traditional ontologies excel at describing domain structure but cannot generate novel artifacts. Large language models generate fluently but produce outputs that lack structural validity, hallucinating mechanisms without components, goals without end conditions. We introduce Generative Ontology, a framework that synthesizes these complementary strengths: ontology provides the grammar; the LLM provides the creativity.
  Generative Ontology encodes domain knowledge as executable Pydantic schemas that constrain LLM generation via DSPy signatures. A multi-agent pipeline assigns specialized roles to different ontology domains: a Mechanics Architect designs game systems, a Theme Weaver integrates narrative, a Balance Critic identifies exploits. Each agent carrying a professional "anxiety" that prevents shallow, agreeable outputs. Retrieval-augmented generation grounds novel designs in precedents from existing exemplars, while iterative validation ensures coherence between mechanisms and components.
  We demonstrate the framework through GameGrammar, a system for generating complete tabletop game designs. Given a thematic prompt ("bioluminescent fungi competing in a cave ecosystem"), the pipeline produces structurally complete, playable game specifications with mechanisms, components, victory conditions, and setup instructions. These outputs satisfy ontological constraints while remaining genuinely creative.
  The pattern generalizes beyond games. Any domain with expert vocabulary, validity constraints, and accumulated exemplars (music composition, software architecture, culinary arts) is a candidate for Generative Ontology. We argue that constraints do not limit creativity but enable it: just as grammar makes poetry possible, ontology makes structured generation possible.

</details>


### [348] [Graph-based Agent Memory: Taxonomy, Techniques, and Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05665)
*Chang Yang,Chuang Zhou,Yilin Xiao,Su Dong,Luyao Zhuang,Yujing Zhang,Zhu Wang,Zijin Hong,Zheng Yuan,Zhishang Xiang,Shengyuan Chen,Huachi Zhou,Qinggang Zhang,Ninghao Liu,Jinsong Su,Xinrun Wang,Yi Chang,Xiao Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A comprehensive survey of graph-based agent memory for LLM-driven agents, detailing taxonomy, lifecycle techniques (extraction, storage, retrieval, evolution), resources, applications, and future challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memory is central to enabling long-horizon tasks in LLM-based agents; graphs provide relational modeling, hierarchical organization, and efficient retrieval, justifying a focused survey.

Method: Systematic review that (1) defines a taxonomy of agent memory, (2) analyzes graph-based memory across the memory lifecycle (extraction, storage, retrieval, evolution), (3) surveys open-source libraries and benchmarks, (4) discusses applications, and (5) identifies challenges and future directions, with a compiled resource list on GitHub.

Result: Offers actionable insights and a consolidated resource base to guide development of graph-based agent memory systems and benchmarks.

Conclusion: Graph-based memory is a promising approach for scalable, persistent agent memory, but notable research gaps remain; the survey outlines key challenges and directions to advance the field.

Abstract: Memory emerges as the core module in the Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents for long-horizon complex tasks (e.g., multi-turn dialogue, game playing, scientific discovery), where memory can enable knowledge accumulation, iterative reasoning and self-evolution. Among diverse paradigms, graph stands out as a powerful structure for agent memory due to the intrinsic capabilities to model relational dependencies, organize hierarchical information, and support efficient retrieval. This survey presents a comprehensive review of agent memory from the graph-based perspective. First, we introduce a taxonomy of agent memory, including short-term vs. long-term memory, knowledge vs. experience memory, non-structural vs. structural memory, with an implementation view of graph-based memory. Second, according to the life cycle of agent memory, we systematically analyze the key techniques in graph-based agent memory, covering memory extraction for transforming the data into the contents, storage for organizing the data efficiently, retrieval for retrieving the relevant contents from memory to support reasoning, and evolution for updating the contents in the memory. Third, we summarize the open-sourced libraries and benchmarks that support the development and evaluation of self-evolving agent memory. We also explore diverse application scenarios. Finally, we identify critical challenges and future research directions. This survey aims to offer actionable insights to advance the development of more efficient and reliable graph-based agent memory systems. All the related resources, including research papers, open-source data, and projects, are collected for the community in https://github.com/DEEP-PolyU/Awesome-GraphMemory.

</details>


### [349] [Determining Energy Efficiency Sweet Spots in Production LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05695)
*Hiari Pizzini Cavagna,Andrea Proia,Giacomo Madella,Giovanni B. Esposito,Francesco Antici,Daniele Cesarini,Zeynep Kiziltan,Andrea Bartolini*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Analytical Transformer energy model captures non-linear efficiency regimes; validated with TensorRT-LLM on H100 across 1B–9B models; reveals sweet spots in input/output lengths; enables energy-aware truncation/adaptive generation; mean absolute percentage error ~1.79%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing energy proxies are linear in sequence lengths and fail to capture non-linear compute/memory behavior; a non-linear model is needed to accurately forecast energy and guide deployment decisions.

Method: Derives an analytical complexity-based model from Transformer compute and memory access characteristics; calibrates against empirical energy measurements using TensorRT-LLM on NVIDIA H100 across multiple models (OPT, LLaMA, Gemma, Falcon, Qwen2, Granite) with input/output lengths from 64 to 4096 tokens; reports MAPE.

Result: Model achieves mean MAPE of 1.79% across configurations; identifies non-linear energy curves with peak efficiency at short-to-moderate inputs and mid-length outputs; long inputs or very short outputs reduce efficiency; aligning sequence lengths to these 'Sweet Spots' can substantially reduce energy consumption; supports truncation, summarization, and adaptive generation in production systems.

Conclusion: The analytical energy model accurately characterizes LLM energy-efficiency curves and can guide energy-aware deployment. Future work could extend validation to broader hardware, larger models, and dynamic runtime adaptations.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) inference is central in modern AI applications, making it critical to understand their energy footprint. Existing approaches typically estimate energy consumption through simple linear functions of input and output sequence lengths, yet our observations reveal clear Energy Efficiency regimes: peak efficiency occurs with short-to-moderate inputs and medium-length outputs, while efficiency drops sharply for long inputs or very short outputs, indicating a non-linear dependency. In this work, we propose an analytical model derived from the computational and memory-access complexity of the Transformer architecture, capable of accurately characterizing the efficiency curve as a function of input and output lengths. To assess its accuracy, we evaluate energy consumption using TensorRT-LLM on NVIDIA H100 GPUs across a diverse set of LLMs ranging from 1B to 9B parameters, including OPT, LLaMA, Gemma, Falcon, Qwen2, and Granite, tested over input and output lengths from 64 to 4096 tokens, achieving a mean MAPE of 1.79%. Our results show that aligning sequence lengths with these efficiency "Sweet Spots" can substantially reduce energy usage, supporting informed truncation, summarization, and adaptive generation strategies in production systems.

</details>


### [350] [Nonlinearity as Rank: Generative Low-Rank Adapter with Radial Basis Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05709)
*Yihao Ouyang,Shiwei Li,Haozhao Wang,Xiandi Luo,Zhuoqi Hu,Yuetong Song,Qiyu Qin,Yichen Li,Ruixuan Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GenLoRA replaces explicit low-rank basis vectors in LoRA with nonlinear basis-vector generation using latent vectors and lightweight radial basis functions (RBFs), achieving higher effective ranks with fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard LoRA’s explicit-rank paradigm causes parameter growth as model capacity increases; observed redundancy in basis vectors suggests they can be compactly represented by nonlinear functions.

Method: For each low-rank matrix, maintain a latent vector. Use a set of lightweight radial basis functions to synthesize the basis vectors; each RBF requires far fewer parameters than an explicit basis vector, enabling parameter-efficient generation of the low-rank updates.

Result: Experiments across multiple datasets and architectures show GenLoRA achieves higher effective LoRA ranks under smaller parameter budgets, leading to superior fine-tuning performance.

Conclusion: Nonlinear basis-vector generation via RBFs is an effective, parameter-efficient alternative to explicit basis vectors in LoRA, enabling higher effective capacity with fewer parameters; code is available.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) approximates the update of a pretrained weight matrix using the product of two low-rank matrices. However, standard LoRA follows an explicit-rank paradigm, where increasing model capacity requires adding more rows or columns (i.e., basis vectors) to the low-rank matrices, leading to substantial parameter growth. In this paper, we find that these basis vectors exhibit significant parameter redundancy and can be compactly represented by lightweight nonlinear functions. Therefore, we propose Generative Low-Rank Adapter (GenLoRA), which replaces explicit basis vector storage with nonlinear basis vector generation. Specifically, GenLoRA maintains a latent vector for each low-rank matrix and employs a set of lightweight radial basis functions (RBFs) to synthesize the basis vectors. Each RBF requires far fewer parameters than an explicit basis vector, enabling higher parameter efficiency in GenLoRA. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and architectures show that GenLoRA attains higher effective LoRA ranks under smaller parameter budgets, resulting in superior fine-tuning performance. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/GenLoRA-1519.

</details>


### [351] [Anchored Policy Optimization: Mitigating Exploration Collapse Via Support-Constrained Rectification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05717)
*Tianyi Wang,Long Li,Hongcan Guo,Yibiao Chen,Yixia Li,Yong Wang,Yun Chen,Guanhua Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RLVR can lead to Recursive Space Contraction (RSC), an irreversible collapse of valid alternatives due to positive sharpening and negative squeezing. Anchored Policy Optimization (APO) replaces global Shape Matching with Support Coverage on a Safe Manifold, enabling elastic recovery and maintaining diversity. Theoretical guarantees show gradient alignment for maximizing support coverage; empirical results on math benchmarks show improved Pass@1 and restored Pass@K diversity, surpassing standard policy gradient methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify systemic pathology in reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RSC) that causes pruning to collapse valid option spaces. Limitations of KL regularization due to rigid Shape Matching, which conflicts with necessary sharpening for correctness. Need a method that preserves coverage of viable actions while permitting aggressive optimization.

Method: Propose Anchored Policy Optimization (APO): replace global Shape Matching with Support Coverage by defining a Safe Manifold based on the reference model's high-confidence support. Allow aggressive sharpening for efficiency and apply a restorative force only during error correction to avoid collapse. Provide a theoretical derivation showing gradient alignment with the objective of maximizing support coverage (Elastic Recovery). Demonstrate empirically on mathematical benchmarks that APO improves Pass@1 and recovers Pass@K diversity.

Result: APO yields a gradient-aligned mechanism that maintains broad support coverage and enables elastic recovery of valid branches. Empirical results show significant gains in Pass@1 and restoration of diversity typically lost by standard policy gradient methods.

Conclusion: APO effectively mitigates the RSC pathology by shifting from global density matching to targeted support coverage, enabling efficient sharpening without sacrificing validity. It breaks the accuracy-diversity trade-off in RL with verifiable rewards and preserves diversity while improving accuracy.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) is increasingly viewed as a tree pruning mechanism. However, we identify a systemic pathology termed Recursive Space Contraction (RSC), an irreversible collapse driven by the combined dynamics of positive sharpening and negative squeezing, where the sampling probability of valid alternatives vanishes. While Kullback-Leibler (KL) regularization aims to mitigate this, it imposes a rigid Shape Matching constraint that forces the policy to mimic the reference model's full density, creating a gradient conflict with the sharpening required for correctness. We propose Anchored Policy Optimization (APO), shifting the paradigm from global Shape Matching to Support Coverage. By defining a Safe Manifold based on the reference model's high-confidence support, APO permits aggressive sharpening for efficiency while selectively invoking a restorative force during error correction to prevent collapse. We theoretically derive that APO serves as a gradient-aligned mechanism to maximize support coverage, enabling an Elastic Recovery that re-inflates valid branches. Empirical evaluations on mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that APO breaks the accuracy-diversity trade-off, significantly improving Pass@1 while restoring the Pass@K diversity typically lost by standard policy gradient methods.

</details>


### [352] [Mitigating Hallucination in Financial Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Fine-Grained Knowledge Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05723)
*Taoye Yin,Haoyuan Hu,Yaxin Fan,Xinhao Chen,Xinya Wu,Kai Deng,Kezun Zhang,Feng Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A RL-based framework (RLFKV) improves faithfulness in financial RAG by verifying atomic knowledge units against retrieved documents, with an informativeness reward to avoid reward hacking; tested on FDD and FDD-ANT with consistent improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In finance, time-sensitive retrieval-augmented generation often hallucinate despite access to retrieved documents. A fine-grained verification and stability reward is needed to align responses with retrieved sources and to prevent overly terse outputs.

Method: Decompose financial responses into atomic knowledge units (AKUs), evaluate the correctness of each unit against retrieved materials to compute a fine-grained faithful reward, and add an informativeness reward to ensure the policy maintains sufficient knowledge content relative to a base model.

Result: Experiments on the public FDD task and the FDD-ANT dataset show consistent improvements, indicating better faithfulness and alignment with retrieved documents.

Conclusion: RLFKV effectively improves faithfulness in financial RAG by combining fine-grained knowledge verification with an informativeness incentive, and demonstrates generalization to a new dataset (FDD-ANT).

Abstract: In financial Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, models frequently rely on retrieved documents to generate accurate responses due to the time-sensitive nature of the financial domain. While retrieved documents help address knowledge gaps, model-generated responses still suffer from hallucinations that contradict the retrieved information. To mitigate this inconsistency, we propose a Reinforcement Learning framework enhanced with Fine-grained Knowledge Verification (RLFKV). Our method decomposes financial responses into atomic knowledge units and assesses the correctness of each unit to compute the fine-grained faithful reward. This reward offers more precise optimization signals, thereby improving alignment with the retrieved documents. Additionally, to prevent reward hacking (e.g., overly concise replies), we incorporate an informativeness reward that encourages the policy model to retain at least as many knowledge units as the base model. Experiments conducted on the public Financial Data Description (FDD) task and our newly proposed FDD-ANT dataset demonstrate consistent improvements, confirming the effectiveness of our approach.

</details>


### [353] [LeakBoost: Perceptual-Loss-Based Membership Inference Attack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05748)
*Amit Kravchik Taub,Fred M. Grabovski,Guy Amit,Yisroel Mirsky*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LeakBoost uses an activation-space perceptual-loss to synthesize interrogation images that reveal membership signals, enabling a detector to perform substantially better in white-box membership inference attacks with minimal modifications to the detector.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static MIA signals (loss/confidence) underutilize the dynamic behavior of models under probing. There is a need for a modular, efficient framework to expose hidden membership signals by actively interrogating internal representations.

Method: For each candidate input, optimize a perceptual objective in activation space to generate an interrogation image that amplifies differences between training members and non-members. The image is then passed to an off-the-shelf membership detector (without changing the detector). This framework can boost existing MIAs and shows that deeper layers and short, low-learning-rate optimization yield stronger leakage; gradient-based detectors gain most.

Result: AUC improved from ~0.53–0.62 to ~0.81–0.88; TPR at 1% FPR increases by over an order of magnitude. Results hold across image classification datasets and diverse architectures. Sensitivity analysis highlights deeper layers and fast, low-rate optimization as key factors; improvements concentrate in gradient-based detectors.

Conclusion: LeakBoost is a modular, computationally efficient framework for probing privacy risks in white-box settings, advancing dynamic membership inference by leveraging activation-space optimization to reveal hidden signals.

Abstract: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) aim to determine whether a sample was part of a model's training set, posing serious privacy risks for modern machine-learning systems. Existing MIAs primarily rely on static indicators, such as loss or confidence, and do not fully leverage the dynamic behavior of models when actively probed. We propose LeakBoost, a perceptual-loss-based interrogation framework that actively probes a model's internal representations to expose hidden membership signals. Given a candidate input, LeakBoost synthesizes an interrogation image by optimizing a perceptual (activation-space) objective, amplifying representational differences between members and non-members. This image is then analyzed by an off-the-shelf membership detector, without modifying the detector itself. When combined with existing membership inference methods, LeakBoost achieves substantial improvements at low false-positive rates across multiple image classification datasets and diverse neural network architectures. In particular, it raises AUC from near-chance levels (0.53-0.62) to 0.81-0.88, and increases TPR at 1 percent FPR by over an order of magnitude compared to strong baseline attacks. A detailed sensitivity analysis reveals that deeper layers and short, low-learning-rate optimization produce the strongest leakage, and that improvements concentrate in gradient-based detectors. LeakBoost thus offers a modular and computationally efficient way to assess privacy risks in white-box settings, advancing the study of dynamic membership inference.

</details>


### [354] [RocqSmith: Can Automatic Optimization Forge Better Proof Agents?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05762)
*Andrei Kozyrev,Nikita Khramov,Denis Lochmelis,Valerio Morelli,Gleb Solovev,Anton Podkopaev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Automatic AI agent optimization methods were tested on a Rocq proof-generation agent in automatic theorem proving. Few-shot bootstrapping emerged as the most consistently effective optimization, but none of the tested optimizers matched a carefully engineered state-of-the-art proof agent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether automated optimization of agent parameters, prompts, knowledge, and control strategies can meaningfully improve real-world formal-verification agents, reducing reliance on manual tuning.

Method: Compare multiple automated agent optimizers on the task of optimizing a Rocq proof-generation agent; evaluate impact on prompts, contextual knowledge, and control strategies; benchmark against a manually engineered state-of-the-art proof agent.

Result: Several optimizers produced measurable improvements; few-shot bootstrapping was the most consistently effective; however, performance did not reach that of the carefully engineered state-of-the-art agent.

Conclusion: Automated optimization offers gains but cannot yet replace expert engineering for high-end formal-verification agents; further work is needed to close the gap to expert-crafted systems.

Abstract: This work studies the applicability of automatic AI agent optimization methods to real-world agents in formal verification settings, focusing on automated theorem proving in Rocq as a representative and challenging domain. We evaluate how different automatic agent optimizers perform when applied to the task of optimizing a Rocq proof-generation agent, and assess whether parts of the fine-grained tuning of agentic systems, such as prompt design, contextual knowledge, and control strategies, can be automated. Our results show that while several optimizers yield measurable improvements, simple few-shot bootstrapping is the most consistently effective; however, none of the studied methods matches the performance of a carefully engineered state-of-the-art proof agent.

</details>


### [355] [RL-VLA$^3$: Reinforcement Learning VLA Accelerating via Full Asynchronism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05765)
*Zhong Guan,Haoran Sun,Yongjian Guo,Shuai Di,Xiaodong Bai,Jing Long,Tianyun Zhao,Mingxi Luo,Chen Zhou,Yucheng Guo,Qiming Yang,Wanting Xu,Wen Huang,Yunxuan Ma,Hongke Zhao,Likang Wu,Xiaotie Deng,Xi Xiao,Sheng Wen,Yicheng Gong,Junwu Xiong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A fully asynchronous training framework for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models decouples environment interaction, rollout generation, and policy updates to boost training throughput and scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synchronous RL-based training causes underutilization and limited throughput during environment interaction, rollout, and updates in VLA models; an asynchronous, decoupled pipeline could improve efficiency.

Method: Proposes a multi-level asynchronous architecture: parallel asynchronous environment interaction/trajectory collection, streaming policy generation, and decoupled scheduling for updates; draws from asynchronous optimization ideas in large-model RL; validates across LIBERO benchmark with multiple models/environments; scalability tested from 8 to 256 GPUs.

Result: Throughput improvements of up to 59.25% over synchronous baselines; up to 126.67% with optimized separation; ablation studies confirm the contribution of each asynchronous component; scaling-law validation demonstrates strong scalability across 8–256 GPUs.

Conclusion: A fully asynchronous policy training framework significantly enhances throughput and scalability for VLA RL training and can effectively utilize large-scale GPU clusters.

Abstract: In recent years, Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a crucial pathway towards general embodied intelligence, yet their training efficiency has become a key bottleneck. Although existing reinforcement learning (RL)-based training frameworks like RLinf can enhance model generalization, they still rely on synchronous execution, leading to severe resource underutilization and throughput limitations during environment interaction, policy generation (rollout), and model update phases (actor). To overcome this challenge, this paper, for the first time, proposes and implements a fully-asynchronous policy training framework encompassing the entire pipeline from environment interaction, rollout generation, to actor policy updates. Systematically drawing inspiration from asynchronous optimization ideas in large model RL, our framework designs a multi-level decoupled architecture. This includes asynchronous parallelization of environment interaction and trajectory collection, streaming execution for policy generation, and decoupled scheduling for training updates. We validated the effectiveness of our method across diverse VLA models and environments. On the LIBERO benchmark, the framework achieves throughput improvements of up to 59.25\% compared to existing synchronous strategies. When deeply optimizing separation strategies, throughput can be increased by as much as 126.67\%. We verified the effectiveness of each asynchronous component via ablation studies. Scaling law validation across 8 to 256 GPUs demonstrates our method's excellent scalability under most conditions.

</details>


### [356] [FiMI: A Domain-Specific Language Model for Indian Finance Ecosystem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05794)
*Aboli Kathar,Aman Kumar,Anusha Kamath,Araveeti Srujan,Ashish Sharma,Chandra Bhushan,Dilip Asbe,Divya Sorate,Duddu Prasanth Kumar,Evan Acharya,Harsh Sharma,Hrithik Kadam,Kanishk Singla,Keyur Doshi,Kiran Praveen,Kolisetty Krishna SK,Krishanu Adhikary,Lokesh MPT,Mayurdeep Sonowal,Nadeem Shaikh,Navya Prakash,Nimit Kothari,Nitin Kukreja,Prashant Devadiga,Rakesh Paul,Ratanjeet Pratap Chauhan,Raunak Kalani,Raviraj Joshi,Shamanth MH,Shantanu Pandey,Shubham Soni,Siddharth Dixit,Smriti Jopat,Sunil Patel,Suraj Singh,Suvradip Paul,Tulasi Pilla,Utkarsh Vaidya,Vineeth Nambiar,Vishal Kanvaty,Yatharth Dedhia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FiMI is a domain-specific financial LLM for India with two variants (Base and Instruct) built on Mistral Small 24B. It is trained via a multi-stage pipeline on 68B tokens (financial, multilingual English/Hindi/Hinglish, and synthetic data), followed by instruction fine-tuning and domain-specific supervised fine-tuning for multi-turn, tool-driven workflows. FiMI Base shows +20% finance reasoning over Mistral Small 24B Base; FiMI Instruct shows +87% improvement in domain-specific tool-calling over Mistral Small 24B Instruct, while maintaining general benchmark parity with similarly-sized models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill the need for a domain-focused AI in Indian digital payments by delivering finance-domain reasoning and tool-driven multi-turn dialogue across multiple languages, tailored to real-world workflows like dispute resolution and mandate lifecycle management.

Method: Multi-stage training: (1) continuous pre-training on 68B tokens of curated financial data across English, Hindi, and Hinglish plus synthetic data; (2) instruction fine-tuning; (3) domain-specific supervised fine-tuning targeting multi-turn, tool-driven conversations that mirror real-world workflows.

Result: FiMI Base achieves a 20% improvement on a finance reasoning benchmark relative to the Mistral Small 24B Base model. FiMI Instruct outperforms the Mistral Small 24B Instruct model by 87% on domain-specific tool-calling. In aggregate, FiMI maintains comparable performance to similarly-sized models on general benchmarks.

Conclusion: FiMI demonstrates strong domain specialization for the Indian financial domain with two variants, delivering notable gains in finance reasoning and tool interaction while preserving general capabilities; this suggests effective integration of domain-specific data and multi-stage training to support real-world workflows in Indian digital payments.

Abstract: We present FiMI (Finance Model for India), a domain-specialized financial language model developed for Indian digital payment systems. We develop two model variants: FiMI Base and FiMI Instruct. FiMI adapts the Mistral Small 24B architecture through a multi-stage training pipeline, beginning with continuous pre-training on 68 Billion tokens of curated financial, multilingual (English, Hindi, Hinglish), and synthetic data. This is followed by instruction fine-tuning and domain-specific supervised fine-tuning focused on multi-turn, tool-driven conversations that model real-world workflows, such as transaction disputes and mandate lifecycle management. Evaluations reveal that FiMI Base achieves a 20% improvement over the Mistral Small 24B Base model on finance reasoning benchmark, while FiMI Instruct outperforms the Mistral Small 24B Instruct model by 87% on domain-specific tool-calling. Moreover, FiMI achieves these significant domain gains while maintaining comparable performance to models of similar size on general benchmarks.

</details>


### [357] [NEX: Neuron Explore-Exploit Scoring for Label-Free Chain-of-Thought Selection and Model Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05805)
*Kang Chen,Zhuoka Feng,Sihan Zhao,Kai Xiong,Junjie Nian,Yaoning Wang,Changyi Xiao,Yixin Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces NEX, an unsupervised E-X phase detector for LLM reasoning; uses spikes in newly activated neurons and a two-state HMM to rank candidate outputs without labels via a Good-Mass Fraction; demonstrates predictive power for downstream accuracy and variant ranking across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the bottleneck shift from generation to selection in LLM reasoning and the lack of supervised signals for target distributions; provide an interpretable, label-free scoring framework.

Method: White-box, label-free approach. Uses sparse activation caches to detect exploration (E) by spikes in newly activated MLP neurons per token. Applies a sticky two-state HMM to infer E/X phases and credits E-introduced neurons by their reuse in the following exploitation (X) span. Produces interpretable neuron weights and a Good-Mass Fraction score to rank candidate responses and merged variants without task answers.

Result: On reasoning benchmarks and Qwen3 merge families, NEX computed on a small unlabeled activation set predicts downstream accuracy and identifies better variants. Further validated with human annotations and supported by causal evidence via Effective-vs-Redundant neuron transfer.

Conclusion: NEX provides interpretable neuron-level signals for reasoning dynamics, enabling unsupervised scoring and variant ranking. The framework generalizes across reasoning benchmarks and merged model families, supporting E–X alternation as a principled aid to selection rather than generation.

Abstract: Large language models increasingly spend inference compute sampling multiple chain-of-thought traces or searching over merged checkpoints. This shifts the bottleneck from generation to selection, often without supervision on the target distribution. We show entropy-based exploration proxies follow an inverted-U with accuracy, suggesting extra exploration can become redundant and induce overthinking. We propose NEX, a white-box label-free unsupervised scoring framework that views reasoning as alternating E-phase (exploration) and X-phase (exploitation). NEX detects E-phase as spikes in newly activated MLP neurons per token from sparse activation caches, then uses a sticky two-state HMM to infer E-X phases and credits E-introduced neurons by whether they are reused in the following X span. These signals yield interpretable neuron weights and a single Good-Mass Fraction score to rank candidate responses and merged variants without task answers. Across reasoning benchmarks and Qwen3 merge families, NEX computed on a small unlabeled activation set predicts downstream accuracy and identifies better variants; we further validate the E-X signal with human annotations and provide causal evidence via "Effective-vs-Redundant" neuron transfer.

</details>


### [358] [STProtein: predicting spatial protein expression from multi-omics data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05811)
*Zhaorui Jiang,Yingfang Yuan,Lei Hu,Wei Pang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: STProtein uses graph neural networks with multi-task learning to predict spatial protein expression from spatial transcriptomics and other accessible spatial multi-omics, addressing the scarcity of spatial proteomics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spatial proteomics data are scarce due to technical limitations and high costs, creating data imbalance that hinders integrative analysis; predictive frameworks can leverage abundant spatial transcriptomics to impute protein expression.

Method: Graph neural networks with a multi-task learning framework to integrate spatial transcriptomics (and possibly other data) to predict unobserved spatial protein expression; designed to handle cross-modality transfer and spatial context.

Result: The abstract claims accurate prediction of unknown spatial protein expression, but provides no empirical results or benchmarks.

Conclusion: STProtein has potential to mitigate proteomics scarcity, accelerate spatial multi-omics integration, reveal spatial patterns and relationships, and catalyze discoveries in life sciences.

Abstract: The integration of spatial multi-omics data from single tissues is crucial for advancing biological research. However, a significant data imbalance impedes progress: while spatial transcriptomics data is relatively abundant, spatial proteomics data remains scarce due to technical limitations and high costs. To overcome this challenge we propose STProtein, a novel framework leveraging graph neural networks with multi-task learning strategy. STProtein is designed to accurately predict unknown spatial protein expression using more accessible spatial multi-omics data, such as spatial transcriptomics. We believe that STProtein can effectively addresses the scarcity of spatial proteomics, accelerating the integration of spatial multi-omics and potentially catalyzing transformative breakthroughs in life sciences. This tool enables scientists to accelerate discovery by identifying complex and previously hidden spatial patterns of proteins within tissues, uncovering novel relationships between different marker genes, and exploring the biological "Dark Matter".

</details>


### [359] [TKG-Thinker: Towards Dynamic Reasoning over Temporal Knowledge Graphs via Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05818)
*Zihao Jiang,Miao Peng,Zhenyan Shan,Wenjie Xu,Ben Liu,Gong Chen,Ziqi Gao,Min Peng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduce TKG-Thinker, an autonomous planning and adaptive retrieval agent for temporal KG QA, achieving state-of-the-art results via a dual training regime (SFT with chain-of-thought followed by RL) across multiple LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM prompting for temporal KGQA suffers from reasoning hallucinations under complex temporal constraints and from static prompting that hinders autonomy and generalization; there is a need for dynamic interaction with TKG environments and goal-directed planning.

Method: Propose TKG-Thinker with autonomous planning and adaptive retrieval for reasoning over TKGs. Train via dual-training: (1) Supervised Fine-Tuning with chain-of-thought data to instill planning; (2) Reinforcement Learning with multi-dimensional rewards to refine reasoning policies under temporal constraints; enable dynamic multi-turn interactions with TKGs.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets using three open-source LLMs; demonstrates strong generalization across complex TKGQA settings.

Conclusion: TKG-Thinker enables effective autonomous planning and adaptive retrieval for robust temporal reasoning in TKGQA, offering improved performance and generalization over static prompting baselines.

Abstract: Temporal knowledge graph question answering (TKGQA) aims to answer time-sensitive questions by leveraging temporal knowledge bases. While Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate significant potential in TKGQA, current prompting strategies constrain their efficacy in two primary ways. First, they are prone to reasoning hallucinations under complex temporal constraints. Second, static prompting limits model autonomy and generalization, as it lack optimization through dynamic interaction with temporal knowledge graphs (TKGs) environments. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{TKG-Thinker}, a novel agent equipped with autonomous planning and adaptive retrieval capabilities for reasoning over TKGs. Specifically, TKG-Thinker performs in-depth temporal reasoning through dynamic multi-turn interactions with TKGs via a dual-training strategy. We first apply Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with chain-of thought data to instill core planning capabilities, followed by a Reinforcement Learning (RL) stage that leverages multi-dimensional rewards to refine reasoning policies under intricate temporal constraints. Experimental results on benchmark datasets with three open-source LLMs show that TKG-Thinker achieves state-of-the-art performance and exhibits strong generalization across complex TKGQA settings.

</details>


### [360] [Learning Compact Boolean Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05830)
*Shengpu Wang,Yuhao Mao,Yani Zhang,Martin Vechev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The abstract introduces three strategies to build compact, accurate Boolean neural networks: learn connections without extra parameters, a locality-aware Boolean convolution with fewer operations, and adaptive discretization to better convert continuous networks to Boolean form, yielding a Pareto-improved accuracy vs. computation frontier (up to 37× fewer Boolean ops) on vision tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between high-performing floating-point networks and resource-constrained deployments by developing efficient Boolean networks that retain accuracy.

Method: 1) Learn connections with no extra parameters and negligible overhead; 2) Propose a locality-exploiting Boolean convolution architecture that reduces Boolean operations; 3) Introduce adaptive discretization to mitigate accuracy loss when binarizing/qualitizing networks. Implementations are demonstrated on standard vision benchmarks.

Result: The proposed methods collectively produce a significantly improved Pareto front for accuracy versus computation compared with prior state-of-the-art, achieving better accuracy with up to 37 times fewer Boolean operations.

Conclusion: The work presents a cohesive set of techniques to make Boolean networks markedly more efficient without sacrificing accuracy, suggesting strong practical potential for edge/embedded ML where compute is limited.

Abstract: Floating-point neural networks dominate modern machine learning but incur substantial inference cost, motivating interest in Boolean networks for resource-constrained settings. However, learning compact and accurate Boolean networks is challenging due to their combinatorial nature. In this work, we address this challenge from three different angles: learned connections, compact convolutions and adaptive discretization. First, we propose a novel strategy to learn efficient connections with no additional parameters and negligible computational overhead. Second, we introduce a novel convolutional Boolean architecture that exploits the locality with reduced number of Boolean operations than existing methods. Third, we propose an adaptive discretization strategy to reduce the accuracy drop when converting a continuous-valued network into a Boolean one. Extensive results on standard vision benchmarks demonstrate that the Pareto front of accuracy vs. computation of our method significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art, achieving better accuracy with up to 37x fewer Boolean operations.

</details>


### [361] [OmniVideo-R1: Reinforcing Audio-visual Reasoning with Query Intention and Modality Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05847)
*Zhangquan Chen,Jiale Tao,Ruihuang Li,Yihao Hu,Ruitao Chen,Zhantao Yang,Xinlei Yu,Haodong Jing,Manyuan Zhang,Shuai Shao,Biao Wang,Qinglin Lu,Ruqi Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OmniVideo-R1: a reinforced framework for omnivideo understanding that uses query-intensive grounding via self-supervised learning and modality-attentive fusion via contrastive learning, yielding strong generalization across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address pervasive challenges in audio-visual and mixed-modality reasoning in omnivideo models by enabling better grounding and fusion of modalities using self-supervised and contrastive objectives.

Method: Two strategies: (1) query-intensive grounding based on self-supervised paradigms; (2) modality-attentive fusion built on contrastive learning. A reinforced framework that integrates these components to improve multi-modality reasoning.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks show consistent improvements over strong baselines, indicating effectiveness and robust generalization.

Conclusion: OmniVideo-R1 advances omni-modal understanding by combining self-supervised grounding and contrastive fusion within a reinforced framework, yielding improved mixed-modality reasoning and generalization.

Abstract: While humans perceive the world through diverse modalities that operate synergistically to support a holistic understanding of their surroundings, existing omnivideo models still face substantial challenges on audio-visual understanding tasks. In this paper, we propose OmniVideo-R1, a novel reinforced framework that improves mixed-modality reasoning. OmniVideo-R1 empowers models to "think with omnimodal cues" by two key strategies: (1) query-intensive grounding based on self-supervised learning paradigms; and (2) modality-attentive fusion built upon contrastive learning paradigms. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that OmniVideo-R1 consistently outperforms strong baselines, highlighting its effectiveness and robust generalization capabilities.

</details>


### [362] [BABE: Biology Arena BEnchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05857)
*Junting Zhou,Jin Chen,Linfeng Hao,Denghui Cao,Zheyu Wang,Qiguang Chen,Chaoyou Fu,Jiaze Chen,Yuchen Wu,Ge Zhang,Mingxuan Wang,Wenhao Huang,Tong Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces BABE, a biology arena benchmark to evaluate AI experimental reasoning using peer-reviewed papers and real-world studies, emphasizing causal reasoning and cross-scale inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Biology AI benchmarks often lack evaluation of integrating experimental results with contextual knowledge; there is a need for authentic scientific reasoning assessment to gauge AI's potential to assist biological research.

Method: Constructs tasks from peer‑reviewed literature and real‑world biological studies; focuses on causal reasoning and cross‑scale inference; provides a framework for evaluation and scoring.

Result: Conceptual proposal that defines the BABE benchmark and its evaluation framework; no empirical results reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: BABE offers a realistic measure of an AI system's ability to reason like practicing scientists and could drive development of biologically capable AI; aims to capture the interdisciplinary and complex nature of biological inquiry.

Abstract: The rapid evolution of large language models (LLMs) has expanded their capabilities from basic dialogue to advanced scientific reasoning. However, existing benchmarks in biology often fail to assess a critical skill required of researchers: the ability to integrate experimental results with contextual knowledge to derive meaningful conclusions. To address this gap, we introduce BABE(Biology Arena BEnchmark), a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate the experimental reasoning capabilities of biological AI systems. BABE is uniquely constructed from peer-reviewed research papers and real-world biological studies, ensuring that tasks reflect the complexity and interdisciplinary nature of actual scientific inquiry. BABE challenges models to perform causal reasoning and cross-scale inference. Our benchmark provides a robust framework for assessing how well AI systems can reason like practicing scientists, offering a more authentic measure of their potential to contribute to biological research.

</details>


### [363] [Beyond Manual Planning: Seating Allocation for Large Organizations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05875)
*Anton Ipsen,Michael Cashmore,Kirsty Fielding,Nicolas Marchesotti,Parisa Zehtabi,Daniele Magazzeni,Manuela Veloso*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces the Hierarchical Seating Allocation Problem (HSAP) to optimally seat hierarchically structured teams on a floor plan, using PRM/RRT-based distance calculations combined with heuristic search and dynamic programming, solved via integer programming; validated on varied instance sizes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large, hierarchically organized entities need nearby seating of related teams to foster collaboration; current manual replanning is infrequent and often suboptimal, hindering agile workspace optimization.

Method: Compute scalable seat-to-seat distances with probabilistic road maps (PRM) and rapidly-exploring random trees (RRT), then solve HSAP via heuristic search and dynamic programming within an integer programming formulation, delivering an end-to-end framework for seating allocations.

Result: Demonstrates scalability and effectiveness across different instance sizes; evaluates the PRM-based distance framework and resulting allocations using quantitative metrics and qualitative assessments.

Conclusion: Proposes a scalable, end-to-end solution for HSAP that automates seating allocation, enabling frequent, near-optimal layouts that cluster related teams together on the floor plan.

Abstract: We introduce the Hierarchical Seating Allocation Problem (HSAP) which addresses the optimal assignment of hierarchically structured organizational teams to physical seating arrangements on a floor plan. This problem is driven by the necessity for large organizations with large hierarchies to ensure that teams with close hierarchical relationships are seated in proximity to one another, such as ensuring a research group occupies a contiguous area. Currently, this problem is managed manually leading to infrequent and suboptimal replanning efforts. To alleviate this manual process, we propose an end-to-end framework to solve the HSAP. A scalable approach to calculate the distance between any pair of seats using a probabilistic road map (PRM) and rapidly-exploring random trees (RRT) which is combined with heuristic search and dynamic programming approach to solve the HSAP using integer programming. We demonstrate our approach under different sized instances by evaluating the PRM framework and subsequent allocations both quantitatively and qualitatively.

</details>


### [364] [Agent2Agent Threats in Safety-Critical LLM Assistants: A Human-Centric Taxonomy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05877)
*Lukas Stappen,Ahmet Erkan Turan,Johann Hagerer,Georg Groh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentHeLLM offers a threat-modeling framework that separates asset identification from attack-path analysis for LLM-enabled automotive agents, introducing a harm-oriented, victim-centric asset taxonomy and a graph-based model that distinguishes poison paths from trigger paths, plus an open-source Attack Path Generator using bi-level search to automate multi-stage threat discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address a gap in safety-critical systems engineering where assets and attack paths are intertwined, by providing a formal separation of concerns and a structured methodology for threat discovery in LLM-assisted vehicles.

Method: Propose the AgentHeLLM framework with (1) a victim-modeling-based asset taxonomy inspired by universal human rights, (2) a formal graph model separating poison paths (malicious data propagation) from trigger paths (activation actions), and (3) an open-source Attack Path Generator that automates multi-stage threat discovery via a bi-level search strategy.

Result: Demonstrates practical applicability of the framework through an open-source tool that generates attack paths and supports multi-stage threat discovery, illustrating a rigorous separation of concerns for asset protection and attack-path analysis.

Conclusion: AgentHeLLM advances safety-focused threat modeling for LLM-based automotive agents by formalizing asset-vulnerability separation, enabling systematic, automated threat discovery and potentially improving resilience against adversarial interactions and inter-agent manipulation.

Abstract: The integration of Large Language Model (LLM)-based conversational agents into vehicles creates novel security challenges at the intersection of agentic AI, automotive safety, and inter-agent communication. As these intelligent assistants coordinate with external services via protocols such as Google's Agent-to-Agent (A2A), they establish attack surfaces where manipulations can propagate through natural language payloads, potentially causing severe consequences ranging from driver distraction to unauthorized vehicle control. Existing AI security frameworks, while foundational, lack the rigorous "separation of concerns" standard in safety-critical systems engineering by co-mingling the concepts of what is being protected (assets) with how it is attacked (attack paths). This paper addresses this methodological gap by proposing a threat modeling framework called AgentHeLLM (Agent Hazard Exploration for LLM Assistants) that formally separates asset identification from attack path analysis. We introduce a human-centric asset taxonomy derived from harm-oriented "victim modeling" and inspired by the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, and a formal graph-based model that distinguishes poison paths (malicious data propagation) from trigger paths (activation actions). We demonstrate the framework's practical applicability through an open-source attack path suggestion tool AgentHeLLM Attack Path Generator that automates multi-stage threat discovery using a bi-level search strategy.

</details>


### [365] [A Guide to Large Language Models in Modeling and Simulation: From Core Techniques to Critical Challenges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05883)
*Philippe J. Giabbanelli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Provide concise guidance for using LLMs in Modeling & Simulation with attention to pitfalls and evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used in M&S but can incur subtle issues if misapplied; need practical, principled guidance to avoid unnecessary complexity and preserve information.

Method: Synthesize common pitfalls (non-determinism, data scaling, RAG/LoRA, decomposition of data, hyperparameters) and propose diagnostic strategies and empirical evaluation practices; deliver a heuristic framework for when/how to use LLMs in M&S.

Result: A structured guidance framework (principles, checklists, and evaluation protocols) for practitioners to design M&S workflows with LLMs, clarifying decisions about data, prompts, model modifications, and evaluation.

Conclusion: If followed, the guidance helps modelers make informed, effective, and efficient use of LLMs in M&S while mitigating risks of deteriorating performance or misaligned outputs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have rapidly become familiar tools to researchers and practitioners. Concepts such as prompting, temperature, or few-shot examples are now widely recognized, and LLMs are increasingly used in Modeling & Simulation (M&S) workflows. However, practices that appear straightforward may introduce subtle issues, unnecessary complexity, or may even lead to inferior results. Adding more data can backfire (e.g., deteriorating performance through model collapse or inadvertently wiping out existing guardrails), spending time on fine-tuning a model can be unnecessary without a prior assessment of what it already knows, setting the temperature to 0 is not sufficient to make LLMs deterministic, providing a large volume of M&S data as input can be excessive (LLMs cannot attend to everything) but naive simplifications can lose information. We aim to provide comprehensive and practical guidance on how to use LLMs, with an emphasis on M&S applications. We discuss common sources of confusion, including non-determinism, knowledge augmentation (including RAG and LoRA), decomposition of M&S data, and hyper-parameter settings. We emphasize principled design choices, diagnostic strategies, and empirical evaluation, with the goal of helping modelers make informed decisions about when, how, and whether to rely on LLMs.

</details>


### [366] [Quantum Reinforcement Learning with Transformers for the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05920)
*Eva Andrés*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Quantum-enhanced RL (A2C) for CVRP comparing classical, full quantum, and hybrid models; hybrids show best performance in distance, compactness, and route overlap, with quantum models delivering more structured routing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether quantum or hybrid quantum-classical RL improves solution quality and robustness for a hard combinatorial optimization problem (CVRP) and whether transformer-based attention can capture interdependencies among vehicles and clients in quantum settings.

Method: Implement Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C) with three variants: classical (fully classical), full quantum (all components quantum), and hybrid (quantum components integrated with classical ones). Use transformer architectures with self- and cross-attention to model relationships among depot, vehicles, and clients. Evaluate on CVRP with 20 clients and 4 vehicles, 10 independent runs. Metrics: routing distance, route compactness, route overlap. Compare learning performance and provide qualitative visualizations of routes.

Result: All three approaches learn effective policies; quantum-enhanced models outperform classical baseline and show more robust route organization; hybrid architecture achieves the best overall performance across all metrics; qualitative visuals suggest more structured and coherent routing with quantum approaches.

Conclusion: Hybrid quantum-classical RL shows promise for complex combinatorial optimization like CVRP; quantum techniques can enhance policy learning and route organization, warranting further study on scalability, generalization, and real hardware implementation.

Abstract: This paper addresses the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) by comparing classical and quantum Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches. An Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C) agent is implemented in classical, full quantum, and hybrid variants, integrating transformer architectures to capture the relationships between vehicles, clients, and the depot through self- and cross-attention mechanisms. The experiments focus on multi-vehicle scenarios with capacity constraints, considering 20 clients and 4 vehicles, and are conducted over ten independent runs. Performance is assessed using routing distance, route compactness, and route overlap. The results show that all three approaches are capable of learning effective routing policies. However, quantum-enhanced models outperform the classical baseline and produce more robust route organization, with the hybrid architecture achieving the best overall performance across distance, compactness, and route overlap. In addition to quantitative improvements, qualitative visualizations reveal that quantum-based models generate more structured and coherent routing solutions. These findings highlight the potential of hybrid quantum-classical reinforcement learning models for addressing complex combinatorial optimization problems such as the CVRP.

</details>


### [367] [Geographically-aware Transformer-based Traffic Forecasting for Urban Motorway Digital Twins](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05983)
*Krešimir Kušić,Vinny Cahill,Ivana Dusparic*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GATTF improves motorway traffic forecasting by integrating geography-informed relationships via mutual information into a Transformer-based model, evaluated on Geneva data, outperforming a standard Transformer without added complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prediction of motorway traffic is spatio-temporally complex and non-linear; improving forecasting accuracy is essential for proactive traffic management and digital-twin effectiveness.

Method:  Introduce GATTF, a Geographically-aware Transformer-based model that leverages mutual information to capture geographical relationships among distributed traffic sensors and integrates this into the Transformer framework without increasing overall model complexity.

Result: Empirical evaluation on Geneva motorway data shows that incorporating MI-based geographical awareness improves forecasting accuracy over a standard Transformer.

Conclusion: Geographical relationships captured via mutual information enhance forecast accuracy in a Transformer framework for motorway traffic, without adding model complexity, highlighting the importance of geography-aware spatio-temporal modeling.

Abstract: The operational effectiveness of digital-twin technology in motorway traffic management depends on the availability of a continuous flow of high-resolution real-time traffic data. To function as a proactive decision-making support layer within traffic management, a digital twin must also incorporate predicted traffic conditions in addition to real-time observations. Due to the spatio-temporal complexity and the time-variant, non-linear nature of traffic dynamics, predicting motorway traffic remains a difficult problem. Sequence-based deep-learning models offer clear advantages over classical machine learning and statistical models in capturing long-range, temporal dependencies in time-series traffic data, yet limitations in forecasting accuracy and model complexity point to the need for further improvements. To improve motorway traffic forecasting, this paper introduces a Geographically-aware Transformer-based Traffic Forecasting GATTF model, which exploits the geographical relationships between distributed sensors using their mutual information (MI). The model has been evaluated using real-time data from the Geneva motorway network in Switzerland and results confirm that incorporating geographical awareness through MI enhances the accuracy of GATTF forecasting compared to a standard Transformer, without increasing model complexity.

</details>


### [368] [Speech Emotion Recognition Leveraging OpenAI's Whisper Representations and Attentive Pooling Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06000)
*Ali Shendabadi,Parnia Izadirad,Mostafa Salehi,Mahmoud Bijankhan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Whisper-based features with attention-based pooling enable effective SER with lightweight models, achieving state-of-the-art on Persian ShEMO and competitive results on IEMOCAP; intermediate Whisper layers can be advantageous for Persian.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SER datasets are limited in size and standardization; leveraging large pre-trained models like Whisper can extract rich representations, while efficient pooling methods preserve emotional content during dimensionality reduction.

Method: Proposes two attention-based pooling schemes—Multi-head Attentive Average Pooling and QKV Pooling—to downscale Whisper representations for SER. Experiments use Whisper Tiny and Small encoders on English (IEMOCAP) and Persian (ShEMO) datasets, with analysis across Whisper encoder layers.

Result: The multi-head QKV architecture achieves state-of-the-art unweighted accuracy on ShEMO, with a 2.47% improvement. Intermediate Whisper encoder layers often yield better SER performance for Persian, and the approach offers a lightweight alternative to larger models like HuBERT X-Large.

Conclusion: Whisper is a viable representation extractor for SER, and attentional pooling techniques effectively reduce dimensionality while preserving emotion-relevant information; results motivate cross-lingual SER using transcription-free features and efficient architectures.

Abstract: Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) research has faced limitations due to the lack of standard and sufficiently large datasets. Recent studies have leveraged pre-trained models to extract features for downstream tasks such as SER. This work explores the capabilities of Whisper, a pre-trained ASR system, in speech emotion recognition by proposing two attention-based pooling methods, Multi-head Attentive Average Pooling and QKV Pooling, designed to efficiently reduce the dimensionality of Whisper representations while preserving emotional features. We experiment on English and Persian, using the IEMOCAP and ShEMO datasets respectively, with Whisper Tiny and Small. Our multi-head QKV architecture achieves state-of-the-art results on the ShEMO dataset, with a 2.47% improvement in unweighted accuracy. We further compare the performance of different Whisper encoder layers and find that intermediate layers often perform better for SER on the Persian dataset, providing a lightweight and efficient alternative to much larger models such as HuBERT X-Large. Our findings highlight the potential of Whisper as a representation extractor for SER and demonstrate the effectiveness of attention-based pooling for dimension reduction.

</details>


### [369] [AgenticPay: A Multi-Agent LLM Negotiation System for Buyer-Seller Transactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06008)
*Xianyang Liu,Shangding Gu,Dawn Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A new benchmark and simulation framework, AgenticPay, for evaluating multi-agent, language-mediated buyer-seller negotiation across 110+ tasks; current LLMs show gaps in long-horizon strategic negotiation; provides metrics, datasets, and a foundation for language-based market interaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of principled, language-enabled evaluation benchmarks for economic interactions among multiple agents. The work targets language-mediated negotiation where sellers and buyers have private constraints and product-dependent valuations, requiring multi-round natural language bargaining rather than numeric bids.

Method: Introduce AgenticPay, a benchmark and simulation framework for multi-agent buyer-seller negotiation driven by natural language. Models markets with private constraints and valuations; supports 110+ tasks ranging from bilateral bargaining to many-to-many markets; includes structured action extraction and metrics for feasibility, efficiency, and welfare.

Result: Empirical benchmarking shows substantial gaps in negotiation performance for state-of-the-art LLMs (both proprietary and open-weight). Highlights challenges in long-horizon strategic reasoning; establishes AgenticPay as a foundation for studying agentic commerce and language-based market interaction.

Conclusion: AgenticPay provides a principled benchmark and framework to study language-based economic interaction among multiple agents, enabling evaluation of negotiation capabilities and market dynamics; it exposes current limitations of LLMs and offers a resources (code/dataset) for continued research.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based agents are increasingly expected to negotiate, coordinate, and transact autonomously, yet existing benchmarks lack principled settings for evaluating language-mediated economic interaction among multiple agents. We introduce AgenticPay, a benchmark and simulation framework for multi-agent buyer-seller negotiation driven by natural language. AgenticPay models markets in which buyers and sellers possess private constraints and product-dependent valuations, and must reach agreements through multi-round linguistic negotiation rather than numeric bidding alone. The framework supports a diverse suite of over 110 tasks ranging from bilateral bargaining to many-to-many markets, with structured action extraction and metrics for feasibility, efficiency, and welfare. Benchmarking state-of-the-art proprietary and open-weight LLMs reveals substantial gaps in negotiation performance and highlights challenges in long-horizon strategic reasoning, establishing AgenticPay as a foundation for studying agentic commerce and language-based market interaction. Code and dataset are available at the link: https://github.com/SafeRL-Lab/AgenticPay.

</details>


### [370] [DyTopo: Dynamic Topology Routing for Multi-Agent Reasoning via Semantic Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06039)
*Yuxing Lu,Yucheng Hu,Xukai Zhao,Jiuxin Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DyTopo: manager-guided dynamic sparse graphs for multi-agent LLM coordination per round, improving performance and offering interpretable traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed, trajectory-wide communication patterns fail to match stage-specific needs in iterative reasoning; need adaptive routing and interpretable coordination.

Method: Per round, a central manager sets the round goal; agents output lightweight natural-language queries (needs) and offers (keys); DyTopo embeds these descriptors and performs semantic matching to form a sparse directed graph; messages are routed along induced edges; evaluated on code generation and mathematical reasoning benchmarks across four LLM backbones.

Result: DyTopo yields consistent improvement over the strongest baseline (avg. +6.2) across tasks and backbones; also provides an interpretable coordination trace via evolving graphs that shows how communication pathways reconfigure over rounds.

Conclusion: Dynamic, manager-guided sparse routing enables adaptive, interpretable multi-agent coordination in LLM systems and improves accuracy on iterative reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems built from prompted large language models can improve multi-round reasoning, yet most existing pipelines rely on fixed, trajectory-wide communication patterns that are poorly matched to the stage-dependent needs of iterative problem solving. We introduce DyTopo, a manager-guided multi-agent framework that reconstructs a sparse directed communication graph at each round. Conditioned on the manager's round goal, each agent outputs lightweight natural-language query (need) and \key (offer) descriptors; DyTopo embeds these descriptors and performs semantic matching, routing private messages only along the induced edges. Across code generation and mathematical reasoning benchmarks and four LLM backbones, DyTopo consistently outperforms over the strongest baseline (avg. +6.2). Beyond accuracy, DyTopo yields an interpretable coordination trace via the evolving graphs, enabling qualitative inspection of how communication pathways reconfigure across rounds.

</details>
